The spontaneous spreading and mixing of particles from a region of higher concentration to lower concentration due to their random kinetic motion. Rate of diffusion: gases (fastest) > liquids > solids (slowest). Example: smell of a perfume spreading across a room.

The continuous, random, zigzag movement of microscopic particles (like pollen grains or smoke particles) suspended in a fluid. It is caused by unequal bombardment of the particle by surrounding fluid molecules from different directions. First observed by botanist Robert Brown in 1827.

The heat energy absorbed or released by a substance during a change of state at constant temperature. The energy goes into breaking or forming intermolecular bonds rather than raising temperature. Latent heat of fusion (melting/freezing) and latent heat of vaporisation (boiling/condensing) are the two types.

The change of state from liquid to gas that occurs at the surface of a liquid at any temperature below its boiling point. Only the most energetic surface molecules escape. Evaporation causes cooling — the remaining liquid loses its most energetic particles, so average kinetic energy (and temperature) drops.

The direct change of state from solid to gas (or gas to solid) without passing through the liquid state. Examples: dry ice (solid CO₂) sublimes at room temperature; iodine crystals sublime on gentle heating; naphthalene (mothballs) sublimes slowly.

laws_and_formulas.name: Relationship between Kinetic Energy and Temperature
laws_and_formulas.description: The average kinetic energy of particles is directly proportional to the absolute temperature. Doubling the absolute temperature doubles the average kinetic energy of particles.

laws_and_formulas.name: Absolute Zero
laws_and_formulas.description: The temperature at which particle motion theoretically ceases completely. It is the lowest possible temperature. Kelvin scale: T(K) = T(°C) + 273.

key_points: Evidence for Kinetic Theory: (1) Brownian motion shows random particle movement, (2) diffusion shows particles move and mix, (3) gas pressure shows particles bombard container walls, (4) dissolving shows particles intermingle.
Changes of state: Melting (solid → liquid), Freezing (liquid → solid), Evaporation/Boiling (liquid → gas), Condensation (gas → liquid), Sublimation (solid → gas), Deposition (gas → solid).
During a change of state, temperature remains constant even though heat is being added or removed — the energy goes into changing intermolecular bonding, not increasing kinetic energy.
Factors that increase rate of evaporation: higher temperature, larger surface area, lower humidity, wind/air movement, lower atmospheric pressure.
Diffusion is fastest in gases because gas particles have the most kinetic energy and the least intermolecular attraction; it is slowest in solids because particles are tightly bound.
Gas pressure arises from billions of molecules bombarding the container walls per second. Higher temperature → faster molecules → more frequent, harder collisions → higher pressure.
Plasma is sometimes called the fourth state of matter: an ionised gas at extremely high temperatures (e.g., inside stars, lightning bolts, fluorescent tubes) where electrons are stripped from atoms.

Intermolecular forces: strongest in solids, moderate in liquids, negligible in gases. These forces determine melting points and boiling points — substances with stronger forces have higher melting/boiling points.
Compressibility: Gases are highly compressible (large empty spaces between particles); liquids are nearly incompressible; solids are almost entirely incompressible.

8|physics|2_Physical_Quantities_and_Meas.overview: Physics is a science built on measurement. Every physical quantity we study — length, mass, time, temperature, force — must be measured precisely and expressed with the correct unit. The international community of scientists agreed on a standard system called the International System of Units (SI) to ensure that measurements made anywhere in the world can be compared and understood universally. This chapter teaches you how to measure accurately, how to choose the right instrument, and how to express quantities with appropriate precision — skills that underpin all of science and engineering.

key_concepts.definition: Any property of matter or energy that can be measured and expressed as a number with a unit. Examples: length, mass, time, temperature, speed, force, energy. Every physical quantity has a numerical value and a unit — writing '5' without a unit is meaningless in physics.

Seven quantities that cannot be defined in terms of other physical quantities. They form the foundation of all measurements. The seven SI base quantities are: length (metre, m), mass (kilogram, kg), time (second, s), electric current (ampere, A), temperature (kelvin, K), amount of substance (mole, mol), and luminous intensity (candela, cd).

Quantities obtained by mathematically combining fundamental quantities through multiplication or division. Examples: Area = length × length (m²), Volume = length³ (m³), Speed = length/time (m/s), Force = mass × acceleration (kg·m/s² = Newton), Density = mass/volume (kg/m³).

The globally agreed standard system of units adopted in 1960, replacing older systems like CGS (centimetre-gram-second) and FPS (foot-pound-second). SI units ensure consistency in scientific communication worldwide.

The smallest measurement that an instrument can reliably make. It determines the precision of the instrument. Least count = (value of 1 main scale division) ÷ (number of vernier/secondary divisions). A ruler with mm markings has a least count of 1 mm.

A precision measuring instrument used to measure lengths, diameters, and depths to 0.1 mm accuracy. It has a main scale and a sliding vernier scale. The reading = main scale reading + (vernier scale division coinciding × least count).

A precision instrument for measuring very small lengths (wire diameter, thickness of a sheet) to 0.01 mm accuracy. It works on the principle of a screw — one full rotation of the thimble advances the spindle by the pitch (usually 0.5 mm). Reading = main scale + thimble scale reading × least count.

The difference between the measured value and the true value. Systematic errors (e.g., zero error in a screw gauge) affect all readings the same way and can be corrected. Random errors vary unpredictably and are reduced by taking multiple readings and averaging.

The digits in a measurement that carry meaningful information about its precision. All non-zero digits are significant. Zeros between non-zero digits are significant. Trailing zeros after a decimal point are significant. Example: 3.040 has 4 significant figures.

laws_and_formulas.name: Speed
laws_and_formulas.description: A derived quantity. SI unit: metres per second (m/s). Distance is in metres, time in seconds.

laws_and_formulas.name: Density
laws_and_formulas.description: A derived quantity. SI unit: kg/m³. Also commonly expressed as g/cm³. Water has a density of 1000 kg/m³ (1 g/cm³).

laws_and_formulas.name: Unit Conversions (Length)
laws_and_formulas.description: Standard metric prefix relationships. Prefix kilo (k) = 10³, centi (c) = 10⁻², milli (m) = 10⁻³, micro (μ) = 10⁻⁶.

key_points: The seven fundamental SI quantities are: length, mass, time, electric current, thermodynamic temperature, amount of substance, and luminous intensity. All other physical quantities are derived from these.
A measurement is only complete when it includes both a numerical value AND a unit. '10' is incomplete; '10 metres' is a proper measurement.

Precision vs Accuracy: Precision refers to how repeatable a measurement is (small random error); Accuracy refers to how close a measurement is to the true value (small systematic error). An instrument can be precise but inaccurate (consistently wrong in the same direction).
Zero error in screw gauge/vernier callipers must be corrected: if the zero error is positive, subtract it from the reading; if negative, add it to the reading.
To measure the diameter of a sphere or cylinder, place it between the jaws of the vernier callipers — this gives a more accurate result than using a ruler alone.
The least count of a metre rule is 1 mm; vernier callipers is 0.1 mm; screw gauge is 0.01 mm.
Scientific notation is used for very large or very small numbers: e.g., the diameter of a hydrogen atom ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ m; the distance from Earth to Sun ≈ 1.5 × 10¹¹ m.
When adding or subtracting measurements, the result should have the same number of decimal places as the measurement with the fewest decimal places.

8|physics|3_Force_and_Pressure.overview: A force is a push or a pull — it is the fundamental agent of change in the physical world. Forces make stationary objects move, moving objects stop, and objects change shape or direction. When a force acts over an area, we call it pressure, and pressure is what allows a sharp knife to cut easily, a dam to hold back water, or a hydraulic press to lift a car. This chapter explores the nature of contact and non-contact forces, how to calculate pressure in solids and fluids, and how atmospheric pressure shapes everyday phenomena from drinking through a straw to weather patterns.

key_concepts.definition: A push or pull on an object that can cause it to start moving, stop moving, change speed, change direction, or change shape. Force is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. SI unit: Newton (N). 1 Newton = the force needed to give a mass of 1 kg an acceleration of 1 m/s².

Forces that act only when two objects are physically touching. Examples: friction (opposes sliding motion between surfaces), normal reaction (surface pushes back perpendicular to itself), tension (force in a stretched rope or string), muscular force (applied by muscles).

Forces that act between objects without physical contact, across a distance. Examples: gravitational force (attraction between masses), magnetic force (between magnets or between a magnet and magnetic material), electrostatic force (between charged objects).

The force acting per unit area on a surface. A large force spread over a large area produces less pressure than the same force concentrated on a small area. SI unit: Pascal (Pa) = N/m². Example: a sharp nail exerts enormous pressure because all the force is concentrated at a tiny tip area.

Pressure in a liquid or gas that increases with depth. At a given depth, fluid pressure acts equally in all directions (up, down, sideways). It depends on the depth (h), density of the fluid (ρ), and gravitational acceleration (g), but NOT on the shape or cross-section of the container.

The pressure exerted by the weight of the atmosphere (column of air) above a surface. At sea level: approximately 101,325 Pa (1 atmosphere = 760 mm of mercury). It decreases with altitude as the column of air above becomes shorter and less dense.

Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid. This is the principle behind hydraulic machines: a small force on a small piston creates the same pressure throughout the fluid, producing a large force on a large piston.

When an object is partially or fully immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of fluid displaced by the object. Buoyant force = ρ_fluid × V_submerged × g. An object floats if its average density is less than the fluid density.

The upward force exerted by a fluid on any object immersed in it. It arises because pressure at the bottom of an object is greater than at its top, creating a net upward force. Objects appear lighter in water due to upthrust — apparent weight = actual weight − upthrust.

laws_and_formulas.name: Pressure Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Pressure = Force ÷ Area. P in Pascals (Pa), F in Newtons (N), A in square metres (m²). To increase pressure: increase force or decrease area. To decrease pressure: decrease force or increase area.

laws_and_formulas.name: Pressure in a Liquid
laws_and_formulas.description: Pressure at a depth h in a liquid of density ρ. h = depth in metres, ρ = density in kg/m³, g = 10 m/s². This pressure is in addition to atmospheric pressure acting on the liquid's surface.

laws_and_formulas.name: Pascal's Law (Hydraulics)
laws_and_formulas.description: Force amplification in hydraulic systems. If A₂ is 10 times larger than A₁, the output force F₂ is 10 times the input force F₁. Used in hydraulic jacks, car brakes, hydraulic presses.

laws_and_formulas.name: Archimedes' Principle
laws_and_formulas.formula: Upthrust = Weight of fluid displaced = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g
laws_and_formulas.description: The buoyant force equals the weight of fluid the object pushes aside. If upthrust ≥ object's weight, the object floats.

key_points: Forces have two categories: contact (friction, tension, normal reaction, muscular) and non-contact (gravity, magnetic, electrostatic). Contact forces require physical touching; non-contact forces act through a field.
A sharp knife, needle, or nail concentrates force on a tiny area, producing enormous pressure. Snow shoes and tractor tyres spread force over a large area to reduce pressure and prevent sinking.
Liquid pressure increases with depth but is independent of the shape of the container (Pascal's paradox). The pressure at the same depth is the same throughout a connected fluid.
Atmospheric pressure is measured with a barometer (mercury or aneroid). The mercury barometer shows ~760 mm Hg at sea level. Aneroid barometers are used in aircraft altimeters.
A hydraulic system multiplies force but not energy — the small piston moves a large distance while the large piston moves a small distance. Work input = Work output (ideal case).
Objects float when upthrust equals their weight — i.e., when their average density equals the fluid density. A ship floats because its hollow steel hull makes its average density less than water.
Submarines use ballast tanks: filling them with water increases average density to sink; pumping water out decreases average density to surface.
The barometric pressure drops by ~1.2 Pa for every 10 m rise in altitude — mountaineers experience lower air pressure at high altitudes, which is why water boils at lower temperatures there.

Friction: a contact force that opposes relative motion between surfaces. Static friction (prevents motion) > kinetic friction (during sliding). Friction produces heat (energy loss) but is essential for walking, brakes, and writing.

8|physics|4_Energy.overview: Energy is the capacity to do work — it is what makes things happen in the universe. Without energy, there would be no motion, no heat, no light, no chemical reactions, and no life. Energy exists in many forms: mechanical, thermal, chemical, electrical, nuclear, and radiant, and it can be converted from one form to another. The most fundamental law governing energy is the Law of Conservation of Energy, which states that energy can never be created or destroyed — only transformed. Understanding energy enables engineers to design engines, scientists to understand stars, and everyone to make informed decisions about electricity use.

key_concepts.definition: The ability to do work. Energy is a scalar quantity. SI unit: Joule (J). 1 Joule = 1 Newton × 1 metre. Energy is stored in various forms and can be transferred or transformed but never created or destroyed.

The energy possessed by an object due to its motion. Every moving object — a thrown ball, flowing river, moving air — has kinetic energy. KE depends on the mass and speed of the object: heavier objects and faster objects have more KE. KE = ½mv².

Stored energy that an object has due to its position or condition. Gravitational PE: energy stored by raising an object to a height (a boulder on a cliff edge). Elastic PE: energy stored in a stretched or compressed spring or elastic band. Chemical PE: energy stored in chemical bonds (food, fuel, batteries).

Energy stored by an object due to its height above a reference point (usually the ground). When the object falls, this energy converts to kinetic energy. GPE = mgh, where m is mass (kg), g is gravitational acceleration (10 m/s²), h is height (m).

The sum of kinetic energy and gravitational potential energy of a system. In the absence of friction or air resistance, mechanical energy is conserved — as an object falls, KE increases at the same rate as PE decreases, keeping total mechanical energy constant.

Work is done when a force causes displacement in the direction of the force. If you push a wall that doesn't move, you do zero work despite the effort. Work = Force × Displacement × cos θ, where θ is the angle between force and displacement. SI unit: Joule (J). 1 J = 1 N × 1 m.

The rate at which work is done or energy is transferred. A powerful machine does more work per second. SI unit: Watt (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. A 100 W bulb uses 100 joules every second. Also expressed as: Power = Force × Velocity.

Energy cannot be created or destroyed; it can only be converted from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system always remains constant. In practice, 'useful' energy is often converted to heat by friction, but total energy is always conserved.

Renewable sources are replenished naturally (solar, wind, hydroelectric, geothermal, tidal). Non-renewable sources are finite and cannot be quickly replenished (coal, oil, natural gas, nuclear fission fuels). Renewable sources produce little or no greenhouse gases.

laws_and_formulas.name: Kinetic Energy
laws_and_formulas.description: m = mass in kg, v = speed in m/s, KE in Joules. Doubling the speed quadruples the kinetic energy (v² relationship). This is why high-speed crashes are so much more destructive.

laws_and_formulas.name: Gravitational Potential Energy
laws_and_formulas.description: m = mass (kg), g = 10 m/s² (or 9.8 m/s²), h = height above reference point (m). GPE in Joules. A 1 kg object raised 1 m gains 10 J of GPE.

laws_and_formulas.name: Work Done
laws_and_formulas.description: F = force (N), d = displacement (m), θ = angle between force and displacement. When force and displacement are in the same direction (θ = 0°), W = F × d. No work is done if there is no displacement, or if force is perpendicular to displacement.

laws_and_formulas.name: Power
laws_and_formulas.description: P = power (W), W = work done (J), t = time (s). Also: P = F × v (force × velocity). 1 kilowatt (kW) = 1000 W; 1 horsepower ≈ 746 W.

laws_and_formulas.name: Conservation of Mechanical Energy
laws_and_formulas.formula: KE + PE = constant  →  ½mv² + mgh = constant
laws_and_formulas.description: In the absence of friction, total mechanical energy is constant. At the highest point of a pendulum swing, KE = 0 and PE is maximum; at the lowest point, PE = 0 and KE is maximum.

key_points: Energy transformations are happening constantly: a car engine converts chemical energy (fuel) → thermal energy → mechanical energy; a solar panel converts radiant energy → electrical energy; a battery converts chemical energy → electrical energy.
The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² − ½mu².
Doubling mass doubles KE; doubling speed quadruples KE (because KE ∝ v²). This is why speed limits matter more than weight limits for road safety.

A swinging pendulum: at the highest point, all energy is PE; at the lowest point, all energy is KE; at intermediate points, energy is a mixture of KE and PE. Total mechanical energy stays constant (ignoring air resistance).

Efficiency = (Useful energy output ÷ Total energy input) × 100%. No machine is 100% efficient — some energy is always lost as heat due to friction. Lubrication reduces friction and improves efficiency.
Hydroelectric power: water at height has GPE → flows down → GPE converts to KE of water → turns turbines → KE to electrical energy. Clean and renewable.

Fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) contain chemical energy from ancient organic matter. Burning releases CO₂, contributing to the greenhouse effect and climate change. This is why energy efficiency and renewables matter.
The kilowatt-hour (kWh) is the unit of electrical energy used in billing: 1 kWh = 3,600,000 J = 3.6 MJ. A 1 kW appliance running for 1 hour uses 1 kWh of electrical energy.

8|physics|5_Light_Energy.overview: Light is a form of electromagnetic radiation that our eyes can detect, and it is fundamental to how we see and understand the world around us. It travels at an astonishing 300,000 kilometres per second — the fastest speed possible in the universe. Light travels in straight lines (rectilinear propagation), and when it meets a surface or passes between materials, it reflects, refracts, or is absorbed. These behaviours explain why mirrors form images, why a straw appears bent in water, why rainbows form, and how lenses in spectacles, cameras, and telescopes work. Optics — the study of light — is one of the oldest and most practically useful branches of physics.

key_concepts.definition: Light travels in straight lines in a uniform medium. Evidence: formation of sharp shadows, the pinhole camera image, eclipses of the Sun and Moon. It is why you cannot see around corners (without a mirror) and why solar eclipses occur only when the Moon is precisely between the Earth and Sun.

The bouncing back of light when it strikes a surface. Regular (specular) reflection occurs from smooth, shiny surfaces (mirrors) and produces clear images. Diffuse reflection occurs from rough surfaces (paper, walls) and scatters light in all directions — this is why we can see non-luminous objects from any angle.

(1) The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection (∠i = ∠r). (2) The incident ray, reflected ray, and normal to the surface at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane. These laws apply to all types of reflecting surfaces.

A plane mirror forms a virtual, erect, laterally inverted image of the same size as the object, located as far behind the mirror as the object is in front. The image cannot be projected on a screen (virtual). Lateral inversion: left appears right and right appears left.

Concave mirrors curve inward (like the inside of a spoon) and converge reflected rays — used in torches, headlights, shaving mirrors, satellite dishes. Convex mirrors curve outward (like the back of a spoon) and diverge reflected rays — they always form virtual, diminished, erect images and are used as rear-view mirrors and security mirrors because of their wide field of view.

The bending of light as it passes from one transparent medium to another of different optical density (e.g., air to water, air to glass). Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium (speed decreases) and away from the normal when entering a less dense medium (speed increases). Refraction explains the apparent bending of a straw in water and the depth of a pool appearing shallower than it is.

The quantitative law of refraction: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂, where n is the refractive index of each medium and θ is the angle with the normal. The refractive index of a medium n = speed of light in vacuum ÷ speed of light in that medium. Glass has n ≈ 1.5, water n ≈ 1.33.

White light is a mixture of all colours of the visible spectrum (VIBGYOR: violet, indigo, blue, green, yellow, orange, red). When white light passes through a prism, each colour refracts by a different amount (violet most, red least) because each colour has a slightly different speed in glass — this separates white light into its spectrum. A rainbow is dispersion by water droplets.

When light travels from a denser medium to a less dense medium (e.g., glass to air) at an angle greater than the critical angle, no refraction occurs — all light is reflected back inside. This principle is used in optical fibres (internet cables, endoscopes) and sparkling of diamonds (cut so light undergoes total internal reflection inside).

laws_and_formulas.name: Laws of Reflection
laws_and_formulas.description: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection. Both angles measured from the normal to the surface at the point of incidence.

laws_and_formulas.name: Refractive Index
laws_and_formulas.formula: n = sin i ÷ sin r  =  speed in vacuum ÷ speed in medium
laws_and_formulas.description: n is the refractive index (dimensionless). A higher refractive index means light slows more in that medium. n(vacuum) = 1; n(air) ≈ 1; n(water) ≈ 1.33; n(glass) ≈ 1.5; n(diamond) ≈ 2.42.

laws_and_formulas.name: Mirror Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Relates focal length (f), image distance (v), and object distance (u) for curved mirrors. Sign convention: distances measured from pole; real distances negative for mirrors (using real-is-negative convention).

laws_and_formulas.name: Speed of Light
laws_and_formulas.description: Speed of light in vacuum/air. This is the universal speed limit — nothing with mass can reach or exceed this speed.

key_points: Light travels in straight lines — evidence includes: sharp shadows (umbra and penumbra), pin-hole camera, solar and lunar eclipses (Moon, Earth, and Sun must be in a straight line).

Characteristics of image in a plane mirror: virtual (cannot be caught on screen), erect (right side up), same size as object, laterally inverted, located as far behind the mirror as the object is in front.

Concave mirror uses: torch/headlight reflector (object at focus → parallel reflected beam), shaving/makeup mirror (object inside focus → magnified upright virtual image), solar cooker (parallel rays converge at focus → high temperature).
Convex mirror uses: rear-view mirrors in vehicles (gives wide field of view, upright image, always virtual and diminished), security mirrors in shops and at road bends.

Refraction effects in daily life: a pool appears shallower than it is (light bends away from normal as it exits water); a straw in water looks bent; mirages (light bends in layers of air with different temperatures); twinkling of stars (light refracts in moving atmospheric layers).

The order of colours in a spectrum from a prism: Violet (bends most) → Indigo → Blue → Green → Yellow → Orange → Red (bends least). Memory aid: VIBGYOR. Violet has the shortest wavelength and highest frequency of visible light.

Optical fibre works by total internal reflection: light sent into a glass fibre undergoes repeated total internal reflection and travels along the fibre even around bends, with minimal loss. Used for high-speed internet and medical endoscopes.

A convex lens converges light rays (converging lens); a concave lens diverges light rays (diverging lens). Convex lenses are used in magnifying glasses, cameras, and correcting long-sightedness. Concave lenses correct short-sightedness.

8|physics|6_Heat_Transfer.overview: Heat is a form of energy that transfers from a region of higher temperature to one of lower temperature. Unlike temperature (which measures the average kinetic energy of particles), heat refers to the total thermal energy being transferred. Heat can move in three fundamentally different ways: conduction (through direct particle contact in solids), convection (through fluid movement in liquids and gases), and radiation (through electromagnetic waves that require no medium). Understanding heat transfer is essential for designing everything from home insulation and cooking utensils to spacecraft heat shields and medical imaging devices.

key_concepts.definition: Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance (measured in °C or K). Heat is the thermal energy transferred between bodies due to a temperature difference (measured in Joules). A large bowl of warm soup has more heat than a small spoon of boiling water, even though the spoon's temperature is higher.

Heat transfer through a material by direct collision between neighbouring particles without the material itself moving. Occurs best in solids, especially metals. In metals, free electrons carry thermal energy rapidly from hot to cold regions. Non-metals and gases are poor conductors (good insulators) because they lack free electrons and have widely spaced particles.

Heat transfer through a fluid (liquid or gas) by the actual movement of the fluid itself. When a fluid is heated, it expands, becomes less dense, and rises. Cooler, denser fluid sinks to replace it, creating a convection current. Convection is responsible for sea breezes, room heating by radiators, and large-scale atmospheric and oceanic circulation.

Heat transfer by electromagnetic waves (infrared radiation) that can travel through a vacuum — no medium required. All objects above absolute zero emit radiation. Hotter objects emit more intensely. Dark, matt surfaces are good absorbers and emitters; shiny, light surfaces are poor absorbers and good reflectors. The Sun heats the Earth entirely by radiation through the vacuum of space.

A measure of how well a material conducts heat. High conductivity: silver, copper, aluminium (metals). Low conductivity: wood, glass, plastic, wool, air. Air is an excellent insulator — this is why double-glazed windows, woolly jumpers, and foam packaging are effective at reducing heat loss.

The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of 1 kg of a substance by 1°C (or 1 K). Symbol: c. SI unit: J/(kg·K) or J/(kg·°C). Water has an unusually high specific heat capacity (4200 J/kg·K), which is why oceans warm up and cool down slowly, moderating coastal climates.

When substances are heated, their particles vibrate more vigorously and push each other further apart — the substance expands. Solids expand least; gases expand most for the same temperature rise. Thermal expansion must be accounted for in engineering: gaps between railway lines, expansion joints in bridges, bimetallic strips in thermostats.

The atmosphere absorbs infrared radiation emitted by the Earth's surface and re-radiates it back, warming the planet. This natural process is essential for life. However, increased concentrations of greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, water vapour) from human activities are enhancing this effect, causing global warming.

laws_and_formulas.name: Heat Energy Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Q = heat energy (J), m = mass (kg), c = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K), ΔT = temperature change (K or °C). Example: to heat 2 kg of water by 10°C needs Q = 2 × 4200 × 10 = 84,000 J.

laws_and_formulas.name: Temperature Conversion
laws_and_formulas.description: Kelvin is the SI unit of temperature. Absolute zero = 0 K = −273°C. The Kelvin scale is used in physics equations. Celsius is used in everyday science.

laws_and_formulas.name: Newton's Law of Cooling
laws_and_formulas.description: The rate at which a hot object cools is proportional to the temperature difference between the object and its surroundings. Objects cool faster when the temperature difference is large, slower as they approach room temperature.

key_points: Conduction needs a medium and occurs through particle vibration/collision. Best in metals (free electrons), worst in insulators like wood, glass, plastic. That is why metal feels cold (conducts heat away from your hand quickly) but wood at the same temperature feels warm.

Convection needs a fluid — it cannot occur in solids because particles are fixed. Land and sea breezes are caused by differential heating and convection: land heats/cools faster than sea. Hot air rises over land during day (sea breeze blows in); reverses at night.

Radiation does not need any medium — it travels through vacuum at the speed of light (3×10⁸ m/s). All objects above absolute zero radiate; the hotter the object, the more radiation emitted. Infrared cameras detect heat radiation from objects.

Good absorbers of radiation = good emitters. Dark, rough/matt surfaces absorb and emit radiation best. Shiny, light/smooth surfaces reflect radiation and are poor emitters. Thermos flask uses a shiny inner surface and vacuum to minimise all three modes of heat transfer.
Thermos flask design: (1) vacuum between walls eliminates conduction and convection; (2) silvered walls minimise radiation. Result: hot drinks stay hot for hours.

Applications of thermal expansion: bimetallic strip (two metals bonded together expand differently when heated, bending to make or break a circuit — used in thermostats and fire alarms), gaps in railway tracks (prevent buckling in summer), overhead power lines are loosely strung (tighten in winter, slack in summer).

Water has the highest specific heat capacity of common substances (4200 J/kg·K), making it an ideal coolant for car engines and for moderating climates. Coastal areas have milder climates than inland areas because the sea absorbs and releases heat slowly.

The three processes compared: Conduction — needs solid/medium, particles don't move, slow in non-metals. Convection — needs fluid, particles physically move, creates currents. Radiation — needs no medium, travels at light speed, can travel through vacuum.

8|physics|7_Sound.overview: Sound is a mechanical wave that transfers energy through matter by causing particles to vibrate. Unlike light, sound cannot travel through a vacuum — it needs a medium (solid, liquid, or gas). Sound travels fastest through solids (where particles are closely packed) and slowest through gases. When you speak, your vocal cords vibrate and create pressure variations in the air that travel to a listener's ear, causing the eardrum to vibrate and ultimately creating the sensation of hearing. Understanding sound helps us design concert halls, reduce noise pollution, use ultrasound in medical imaging, and appreciate the physics behind every musical note.

key_concepts.definition: Sound travels as a longitudinal wave — the particles of the medium vibrate parallel to the direction of wave travel, creating alternating regions of compression (particles pushed together, high pressure) and rarefaction (particles spread apart, low pressure). This is different from transverse waves (like light or water waves) where vibration is perpendicular to travel direction.

Frequency is the number of complete vibrations (cycles) per second. SI unit: Hertz (Hz). Pitch is the perceived highness or lowness of a sound, determined by frequency. High frequency = high pitch (e.g., whistle); low frequency = low pitch (e.g., bass drum). Human hearing range: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz.

Amplitude is the maximum displacement of a particle from its equilibrium position during vibration. Loudness is the perceived volume of sound, determined by amplitude. Greater amplitude = louder sound. Loudness is measured in decibels (dB). Whisper ≈ 20 dB; normal conversation ≈ 60 dB; jet engine ≈ 140 dB (causes ear damage).

Sound travels at about 340 m/s in air at room temperature (20°C). It travels faster in warmer air, faster in liquids (~1500 m/s in water), and fastest in solids (~5000 m/s in steel). Sound travels much faster in dense, rigid materials because particles are closer together and transfer vibrations more quickly.

An echo is the reflection of sound from a hard surface (wall, cliff, building). For an echo to be heard distinctly, the reflecting surface must be at least 17 metres away (so reflected sound returns after at least 1/10 second, which is the minimum persistence of hearing). Used in SONAR (Sound Navigation and Ranging) to measure distances underwater.

When an object is made to vibrate at its natural frequency by another vibrating object, the amplitude of vibration becomes very large — this is resonance. A wine glass shatters when a singer hits the exact right frequency. Bridges can resonate dangerously in the wind (the Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse, 1940). Resonance is exploited in musical instruments.

Sound with frequency above 20,000 Hz (above the human hearing range). Bats use ultrasound (echolocation) to navigate and hunt. Medical ultrasound scans use high-frequency sound waves reflected from internal organs/tissue to create images (safe, no radiation). Industrial uses: cleaning jewellery, detecting cracks in metal.

Unwanted or excessive sound that causes physical and psychological harm. Prolonged exposure to sound above 85 dB causes permanent hearing damage. Sources: traffic, construction, aircraft, industry. Reduction: sound-absorbing materials, earmuffs, distance from sources, soundproofing walls with soft materials.

laws_and_formulas.name: Wave Speed Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Speed (m/s) = frequency (Hz) × wavelength (m). For sound in air: v ≈ 340 m/s. If frequency doubles, wavelength halves (speed stays constant in the same medium).

laws_and_formulas.name: Echo Distance Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: d = distance to the reflecting surface (m), v = speed of sound (m/s), t = time for echo to return (s). Divided by 2 because sound travels to the object and back. Used in SONAR and ultrasound ranging.

laws_and_formulas.name: Time Period and Frequency
laws_and_formulas.description: T = time period (s) — time for one complete vibration. f = frequency (Hz). Example: if a tuning fork vibrates 440 times per second (440 Hz, musical note A), its time period T = 1/440 ≈ 0.00227 s.

key_points: Sound is a mechanical, longitudinal wave. It requires a material medium — it cannot travel through vacuum. This is why space is completely silent (no air) and why astronauts cannot hear explosions in space.

Speed of sound increases with temperature and with the density/rigidity of the medium. Sound travels ~340 m/s in air, ~1500 m/s in water, ~5100 m/s in steel. Sound is faster in solids > liquids > gases.
Pitch depends on frequency; loudness depends on amplitude. Two sounds can have the same loudness (amplitude) but different pitch (frequency), or the same pitch but different loudness.

Infrasound (below 20 Hz): some animals detect it — elephants communicate using infrasound, and infrasound from earthquakes and volcanic eruptions can travel vast distances. Ultrasound (above 20,000 Hz): bats, dolphins use it for echolocation; doctors use it for safe prenatal imaging.
SONAR uses the echo principle: a sound pulse is sent out, and the time until the echo returns is used to calculate the distance to the sea floor or a submarine. d = (v × t)/2.

Musical notes: frequency determines pitch, amplitude determines loudness, and the waveform (presence of harmonics/overtones) determines the quality (timbre) that makes a violin sound different from a guitar at the same pitch.

The Doppler Effect: when a sound source moves towards you, the pitch sounds higher (waves compressed); when it moves away, the pitch sounds lower (waves stretched). You hear this with a passing ambulance siren — high pitch approaching, drops as it passes.

Sound insulation uses soft, porous materials (foam, carpets, curtains, glass wool) that absorb sound energy rather than reflecting it. Hard, smooth surfaces reflect sound well (echo) — this is why empty rooms echo more than furnished ones.

8|physics|8_Electricity_and_Magnetism.overview: Electricity and magnetism are two aspects of a single fundamental force — electromagnetism — that governs the behaviour of charged particles. Electricity flows through the circuits that power every device around us, from phones and computers to hospitals and factories. Magnetism arises from the movement of electric charges and is responsible for compasses, electric motors, generators, and data storage. Understanding how electric circuits work, how charges flow, how to calculate resistance and power, and how magnets behave gives you the tools to understand and design the technological world. This is arguably the most practically important topic in Class 8 Physics.

key_concepts.definition: A fundamental property of matter. There are two types: positive (protons) and negative (electrons). Like charges repel; unlike charges attract. The unit is the Coulomb (C). An electron carries a charge of −1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C. Objects become charged by gaining or losing electrons; they never gain or lose protons in everyday situations.

The rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor. In metal conductors, current is the flow of free electrons (from negative to positive terminal internally). By convention, current direction is from positive to negative terminal (opposite to electron flow). SI unit: Ampere (A). Measured with an ammeter connected in series.

The work done per unit charge to move a charge between two points in a circuit. It is the 'electrical pressure' that drives current through a circuit. SI unit: Volt (V). 1 V = 1 J/C. Measured with a voltmeter connected in parallel across a component. A battery's EMF (electromotive force) is the voltage it can provide per unit charge.

The opposition offered by a conductor to the flow of electric current. Caused by collisions between flowing electrons and the stationary ions of the conductor lattice. SI unit: Ohm (Ω). Factors affecting resistance: length (longer → more resistance), cross-sectional area (thicker → less resistance), material (copper < iron), temperature (resistance of metals increases with temperature).

For a metallic conductor at constant temperature, the current through it is directly proportional to the potential difference across it. V ∝ I, so V/I = constant = R. This means: doubling the voltage doubles the current; doubling the resistance halves the current. Materials that obey Ohm's law are called ohmic conductors (e.g., metal wire); those that don't are non-ohmic (e.g., filament bulb, diode, LDR).

Series circuit: components connected end-to-end in a single loop. Same current flows through all; total resistance R = R₁ + R₂ + R₃; voltage divides across components. If one component fails, the whole circuit breaks. Parallel circuit: components connected across the same two points (multiple branches). Same voltage across all; total resistance decreases; currents divide. If one branch fails, others continue working. Houses are wired in parallel.

The rate at which electrical energy is converted to other forms (heat, light, mechanical). P = V × I = I²R = V²/R. Unit: Watt (W). A 60W bulb converts 60 joules of electrical energy to heat and light every second. Electrical energy consumed = P × t, measured in kWh for electricity bills.

Magnets have two poles: North (N) and South (S). Like poles repel; unlike poles attract. Magnetic field lines run from N to S outside the magnet (and S to N inside). The Earth is a giant magnet — the geographic north pole is near a magnetic south pole, which is why compass needles (N-seeking) point north. Magnetic materials: iron, nickel, cobalt (ferromagnetic). Magnets can be permanent (steel) or temporary (soft iron).

A temporary magnet created by passing electric current through a coil of wire (solenoid) wrapped around an iron core. The magnetic field exists only when current flows. Strength can be increased by: more turns of wire, greater current, and using a soft iron core. Uses: electric bell, loudspeaker, relay, MRI machines, scrapyard cranes, electric motors.

laws_and_formulas.name: Ohm's Law
laws_and_formulas.description: V = potential difference (V), I = current (A), R = resistance (Ω). Rearranged: I = V/R (current increases with voltage, decreases with resistance); R = V/I.

laws_and_formulas.name: Series Resistance
laws_and_formulas.description: Total resistance in series = sum of individual resistances. Adding more components in series always increases total resistance and decreases current.

laws_and_formulas.name: Parallel Resistance
laws_and_formulas.description: Total resistance in parallel is always less than the smallest individual resistance. Adding more branches in parallel decreases total resistance and increases total current drawn.

laws_and_formulas.name: Electric Power
laws_and_formulas.formula: P = V × I = I² × R = V² ÷ R
laws_and_formulas.description: P = power (W), V = voltage (V), I = current (A), R = resistance (Ω). Electrical energy E = P × t (Joules, or in kWh for billing: E(kWh) = P(kW) × t(hours)).

laws_and_formulas.name: Electrical Energy
laws_and_formulas.formula: E = P × t = V × I × t
laws_and_formulas.description: E = energy (J or kWh). 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J. Cost of electricity = energy used (kWh) × price per unit (kWh).

key_points: Current flows from the positive terminal to the negative terminal outside the battery (conventional current), but electrons actually flow from negative to positive. The convention was fixed before electrons were discovered.

Ohm's Law (V = IR) applies to ohmic conductors at constant temperature. The resistance of a metallic conductor increases with temperature (more vibration impedes electron flow). At very low temperatures, some materials become superconductors (zero resistance).

In a series circuit: current is the same everywhere; voltage splits proportionally to resistance; total resistance is the sum of all resistances. In parallel: voltage is the same across all branches; current divides; total resistance is less than the smallest branch.
Household wiring is in parallel: all appliances get the same 230V (or 120V); each can be switched independently; adding more appliances doesn't change the voltage but increases total current drawn.

Fuses and circuit breakers protect circuits from excessive current. A fuse contains a thin wire that melts if current exceeds its rating, breaking the circuit. Earth wire provides a safe path to ground in case of a fault, preventing electric shock.

Electromagnet strength increases with: (1) more turns of wire in the coil, (2) greater current through the coil, (3) using a soft iron core (iron magnetises strongly when current flows and demagnetises quickly when switched off).
The right-hand rule for a solenoid: curl the right-hand fingers in the direction of current flow; the thumb points toward the North pole of the electromagnet.

Electromagnetic induction (Faraday's Law): moving a magnet in or out of a coil (or changing the current in a nearby coil) induces an EMF in the coil. This is the principle of the electric generator — converting mechanical energy to electrical energy. The motor does the reverse — electrical energy to mechanical energy.
Electricity bill calculation: Power (kW) × Time (hours) = Energy (kWh). Multiply by cost per kWh to get the bill. A 2 kW heater running for 3 hours uses 6 kWh of energy.

8|chemistry|1_Matter.overview: Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space. All matter around us exists in three physical states — solid, liquid, and gas — each distinguished by the arrangement and energy of its particles. The kinetic theory of matter explains that particles are in constant random motion, and the speed of this motion determines the state of matter. Matter can change from one state to another through processes such as melting, boiling, condensation, and freezing, collectively called interconversion of states. Diffusion — the spontaneous mixing of particles of one substance with another — is direct evidence of the kinetic nature of matter and is faster in gases than in liquids.

key_concepts.definition: Matter is defined as anything that has mass and occupies space (volume). It is made up of tiny particles called atoms or molecules. Examples include air, water, iron, and wood — even invisible gases like oxygen are matter because they have mass and volume.

The kinetic theory states that all matter is composed of tiny particles that are in constant, random motion. The energy of this motion (kinetic energy) determines the state of matter. Higher temperature means greater kinetic energy and faster-moving particles. This theory explains properties like diffusion, expansion, and changes of state.

In a solid, particles are closely packed in a regular arrangement and held together by strong intermolecular forces. They vibrate about fixed positions but cannot move freely. Solids have a definite shape and volume, are incompressible, and have the highest density among the three states. Examples: ice, iron, wood, salt crystals.

In a liquid, particles are close together but not in a fixed arrangement; they can slide past one another. Intermolecular forces are weaker than in solids. Liquids have a definite volume but no definite shape — they take the shape of their container. They flow, have moderate density, and are nearly incompressible. Examples: water, mercury, alcohol.

In a gas, particles are far apart and move rapidly in all directions with negligible intermolecular forces. Gases have no definite shape or volume and expand to fill any container completely. They are highly compressible and have very low density. Examples: oxygen, nitrogen, carbon dioxide, steam.

Diffusion is the spontaneous, gradual mixing of particles of one substance with particles of another due to their random kinetic motion, occurring from a region of higher concentration to lower concentration. It takes place without any external force. Example: the smell of perfume spreading across a room, or a drop of ink spreading in water. Diffusion is fastest in gases and slowest in solids.

Matter can change from one physical state to another by absorbing or releasing heat energy, a process called interconversion. The processes include melting (solid to liquid), freezing (liquid to solid), vaporisation/boiling (liquid to gas), condensation (gas to liquid), sublimation (solid directly to gas, e.g., dry ice, naphthalene), and deposition (gas directly to solid).

The melting point is the fixed temperature at which a solid changes to a liquid at standard atmospheric pressure (e.g., ice melts at 0°C). The boiling point is the fixed temperature at which a liquid changes to a gas (e.g., water boils at 100°C). These are characteristic physical properties used to identify pure substances.

Latent heat is the heat energy absorbed or released during a change of state without any change in temperature. Latent heat of fusion is absorbed during melting; latent heat of vaporisation is absorbed during boiling. During these processes, the absorbed energy is used to overcome intermolecular forces rather than raise temperature, which is why the temperature remains constant during a change of state.

Sublimation is the direct conversion of a solid into vapour (gas) without passing through the liquid state, upon heating. The reverse process — gas converting directly to solid — is called deposition or reverse sublimation. Common examples of sublimating substances include iodine, naphthalene (camphor), ammonium chloride, and dry ice (solid carbon dioxide).

laws_and_formulas.name: Charles's Law (qualitative context)
laws_and_formulas.description: The volume of a fixed mass of gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature when pressure is kept constant. This explains why gases expand on heating — particles move faster and push walls further apart.

laws_and_formulas.name: Boyle's Law (qualitative context)
laws_and_formulas.description: At constant temperature, the pressure of a fixed mass of gas is inversely proportional to its volume. Compressing a gas into a smaller volume increases its pressure because particles collide with the walls more frequently.

key_points: All matter is made up of tiny particles (atoms or molecules) that are in constant random motion — this is the central idea of the kinetic theory.
The three states of matter — solid, liquid, and gas — differ in the arrangement, spacing, and energy of their particles.
Solids have fixed shape and volume; liquids have fixed volume but no fixed shape; gases have neither fixed shape nor fixed volume.
Interconversion of states occurs when heat energy is added or removed, causing particles to gain or lose kinetic energy.
Melting point and boiling point are fixed temperatures (at standard pressure) characteristic of pure substances and used to test purity.
During a change of state (melting or boiling), temperature remains constant even as heat is supplied — this heat is called latent heat.
Diffusion is evidence that particles are in constant motion. It is fastest in gases (particles far apart, high speed) and slowest in solids.
Sublimation is the direct solid-to-gas conversion; examples include iodine, naphthalene, ammonium chloride, and dry ice.
Increasing temperature increases the kinetic energy of particles, causing faster diffusion and eventually a change of state.
Intermolecular forces of attraction are strongest in solids (hence rigid shape) and weakest in gases (hence no fixed shape or volume).

8|chemistry|2_Physical_and_Chemical_Chan.overview: Changes in matter are broadly classified into physical changes and chemical changes. In a physical change, only the physical properties of a substance (shape, size, state) change while its chemical composition remains the same; no new substance is formed, and the change is generally reversible. In a chemical change, one or more new substances with entirely different properties are formed, and the original substance cannot be easily recovered; these changes are generally irreversible. Recognising a chemical change involves looking for characteristic signs such as evolution of gas, change in colour, formation of a precipitate, change in temperature, or emission of light.

key_concepts.definition: A physical change is a change in which only the physical form of a substance alters — its shape, size, colour, or state — but its chemical composition (the type of molecules) remains unchanged. No new substance is produced. Physical changes are generally reversible. Examples: melting of ice to water, dissolving sugar in water, cutting paper, stretching a rubber band, magnetising iron.

A chemical change (also called a chemical reaction) is a change in which one or more new substances with entirely different chemical properties are formed. The original substances (reactants) are consumed, and the products usually cannot be converted back to reactants by simple physical means. Chemical changes are generally irreversible. Examples: burning of wood, rusting of iron, cooking of food, souring of milk, digestion.

Physical changes are typically reversible — the original substance can be recovered by reversing the conditions. For example, water frozen to ice can be melted back to water. Chemical changes are generally irreversible because entirely new substances are formed with different chemical bonds. For example, burned paper (ash + gases) cannot be converted back to paper.

One sign of a chemical change is the production of a gas, often seen as bubbling or fizzing. For example, when dilute hydrochloric acid is added to zinc metal, hydrogen gas is produced with vigorous bubbling. When vinegar reacts with baking soda, carbon dioxide gas is evolved — this is a chemical change because a new substance (CO₂) is formed.

A permanent change in colour during a reaction is a sign of a chemical change, indicating that a new substance has been formed. For example, iron turning reddish-brown when it rusts (formation of iron oxide), or a copper coin turning green (formation of copper carbonate/copper hydroxide) are chemical changes. A temporary colour change that can be reversed is more likely a physical change.

A precipitate is an insoluble solid that forms when two solutions are mixed and react chemically. Its formation is a sign of a chemical change. For example, when silver nitrate solution is added to common salt (sodium chloride) solution, a white precipitate of silver chloride forms immediately, indicating a chemical change.

A chemical change is often accompanied by absorption or release of heat (or sometimes light). Reactions that release heat (exothermic) cause the surroundings to warm up — for example, burning of fuels, neutralisation of acids and bases. Reactions that absorb heat (endothermic) cause cooling — for example, dissolving ammonium nitrate in water. A temperature change is an important indicator of a chemical change.

Exothermic changes release heat energy to the surroundings, making the surroundings warmer. Examples: combustion (burning), neutralisation, respiration. Endothermic changes absorb heat energy from the surroundings, making the surroundings cooler. Examples: photosynthesis, thermal decomposition, dissolving certain salts in water. These are mostly associated with chemical changes.

Rusting is a slow chemical change in which iron reacts with oxygen and water (moisture) in the air to form hydrated iron(III) oxide (rust), which is reddish-brown and flaky. The chemical formula of rust is Fe₂O₃·xH₂O. Rusting is irreversible; rust is a completely new substance with properties different from iron. Rusting can be prevented by painting, galvanising, or alloying.

Burning or combustion is a rapid chemical change in which a substance reacts with oxygen to produce heat and light, forming entirely new products (oxides). For example, burning of magnesium ribbon in air produces white magnesium oxide (a new solid) with a bright white flame. Combustion is irreversible and is accompanied by evolution of heat and light.

laws_and_formulas.name: Law of Conservation of Mass
laws_and_formulas.formula: Mass of reactants = Mass of products
laws_and_formulas.description: In any chemical change, matter is neither created nor destroyed. The total mass of all reactants equals the total mass of all products. This law, proposed by Antoine Lavoisier, is fundamental to understanding chemical changes and is used to balance chemical equations.

key_points: Physical changes alter only physical properties (shape, size, state); no new substance is formed, and the change is generally reversible.
Chemical changes produce one or more new substances with different properties and are generally irreversible.
Signs of a chemical change include: evolution of gas, change in colour, formation of a precipitate, change in temperature, and emission of light or sound.
Melting, dissolving, and cutting are physical changes; burning, rusting, souring of milk, and digestion are chemical changes.
In a physical change, the chemical composition (molecular identity) of the substance does not change.
Rusting of iron is a slow chemical change requiring both oxygen and moisture; it forms iron oxide, a new substance.
Burning (combustion) is a fast chemical change that releases heat and light; the products are completely different from the original substance.
Exothermic reactions release energy (heat) to surroundings; endothermic reactions absorb energy from surroundings.
The Law of Conservation of Mass states that in a chemical reaction, the total mass of products equals the total mass of reactants.
A change of state (melting, boiling) is a physical change because the chemical composition of the substance is unchanged even though its physical form changes.
Cooking food involves chemical changes — proteins denature and new compounds form — which is why cooked food cannot be 'uncooked'.

8|chemistry|3_Elements_Compounds_and_Mix.overview: Pure substances are classified as elements or compounds, while impure substances are called mixtures. An element is the simplest form of matter that cannot be broken down further by any chemical method; elements are made of only one type of atom. A compound is a pure substance formed by the chemical combination of two or more elements in a fixed ratio by mass, and it has properties entirely different from its constituent elements. A mixture contains two or more substances combined physically in any proportion, retaining their individual properties, and can be separated by physical methods. Understanding this classification is fundamental to chemistry and materials science.

key_concepts.definition: An element is a pure substance made up of only one type of atom that cannot be broken down into simpler substances by any chemical method. There are 118 known elements, each represented by a unique chemical symbol. Elements are classified as metals (e.g., iron Fe, copper Cu, sodium Na), non-metals (e.g., oxygen O, carbon C, sulphur S), and metalloids/semi-metals (e.g., silicon Si, arsenic As, germanium Ge).

Metals are elements that are typically lustrous (shiny), malleable (can be beaten into sheets), ductile (can be drawn into wires), good conductors of heat and electricity, and solid at room temperature (except mercury, which is liquid). Metals are found on the left side of the periodic table. Examples: iron, copper, gold, silver, aluminium, sodium, calcium. Most metals react with oxygen to form basic oxides.

Non-metals are elements that are generally dull (non-lustrous), brittle when solid, poor conductors of heat and electricity, and can exist as solids, liquids, or gases at room temperature. They are found on the right side of the periodic table. Examples: carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), sulphur (S), chlorine (Cl), phosphorus (P). Non-metals react with oxygen to form acidic oxides.

Metalloids are elements that have properties intermediate between metals and non-metals. They may be semiconductors — conducting electricity better than non-metals but not as well as metals. Metalloids are important in the electronics industry. Examples: silicon (Si), germanium (Ge), arsenic (As), antimony (Sb), boron (B). Silicon is widely used in computer chips and solar cells.

A compound is a pure substance formed by the chemical combination of two or more elements in a definite ratio by mass. A compound has fixed composition, a definite melting/boiling point, and properties entirely different from its constituent elements. For example, water (H₂O) is a compound of hydrogen (a flammable gas) and oxygen (a gas that supports combustion), but water itself is used to extinguish fires. Compounds can only be separated into elements by chemical methods.

A mixture is a combination of two or more substances (elements or compounds) that are physically mixed together in any proportion without any chemical reaction occurring. Each component retains its own properties. Mixtures do not have a fixed composition or definite melting/boiling point. They can be separated by physical methods such as filtration, evaporation, distillation, and chromatography. Examples: air (mixture of gases), sea water, soil, alloys like brass.

A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout — the components are mixed so thoroughly that they cannot be distinguished visually, and any sample taken from the mixture has the same composition. Also called solutions. Examples: salt solution (salt dissolved in water), sugar solution, air, alloys (brass, bronze, steel). The solute particles are too small to be seen or filtered.

A heterogeneous mixture has a non-uniform composition — different parts of the mixture have different compositions and properties, and the components can often be seen separately. Examples: sand in water, oil and water, iron filings and sulphur powder, a salad, gravel and soil. Components of a heterogeneous mixture can often be separated mechanically or by simple physical methods.

Mixtures can be separated into their components by exploiting differences in physical properties. Common methods include: filtration (to separate insoluble solids from liquids), evaporation (to recover dissolved solids from solutions), distillation (to separate miscible liquids with different boiling points), chromatography (to separate mixtures of dyes/pigments), magnetic separation (to separate magnetic substances), and fractional distillation (to separate crude oil into fractions).

A compound is formed by chemical combination of elements in a fixed ratio; it has a definite composition, definite physical properties, and its properties differ from its constituent elements. A mixture has variable composition, its components retain their own properties, has no definite melting/boiling point, and can be separated by physical methods. For example, iron filings and sulphur powder form a mixture (separable by magnet) but when heated they form iron sulphide, a compound (inseparable by magnet).

laws_and_formulas.name: Law of Definite Proportions
laws_and_formulas.formula: Elements in a compound are always present in the same ratio by mass
laws_and_formulas.description: Also called Proust's Law. A pure compound always contains the same elements combined in the same proportions by mass, regardless of its source or method of preparation. For example, water always contains hydrogen and oxygen in a mass ratio of 1:8, whether it comes from a river, rain, or is made in the laboratory.

laws_and_formulas.name: Law of Conservation of Mass (for mixtures)
laws_and_formulas.formula: Mass of mixture = Sum of masses of all components
laws_and_formulas.description: When a mixture is formed by combining components, no mass is gained or lost. The total mass of the mixture equals the sum of the masses of the individual components mixed together, since no chemical reaction occurs.

key_points: Elements are the simplest pure substances made of one type of atom; they cannot be split further by chemical means. There are 118 known elements.
Elements are classified as metals, non-metals, and metalloids based on their physical and chemical properties.
Metals are lustrous, malleable, ductile, and good conductors; non-metals are generally dull and poor conductors; metalloids have intermediate properties.
A compound is formed by the chemical combination of two or more elements in a fixed ratio by mass; its properties are entirely different from its constituent elements.
Compounds have definite composition and definite physical properties (fixed melting and boiling points); they can only be separated by chemical methods.
A mixture contains two or more substances combined physically; components retain their own properties and can be separated by physical methods.
Homogeneous mixtures (solutions) have uniform composition throughout; heterogeneous mixtures have non-uniform composition.

Key separation methods: filtration (solid from liquid), evaporation (solute from solvent), distillation (liquids with different boiling points), magnetic separation (magnetic from non-magnetic), chromatography (dissolved pigments).
Air is a homogeneous mixture of nitrogen (~78%), oxygen (~21%), and other gases; it is NOT a compound because its composition varies and components can be separated.
Iron sulphide (FeS) formed from iron and sulphur is a compound — it has different properties from iron and sulphur and cannot be separated by a magnet.
The Law of Definite Proportions states that a compound always has the same elements in the same mass ratio, regardless of the source.

8|chemistry|4_Atomic_Structure.overview: The atom is the fundamental unit of matter and was once thought to be indivisible. Modern understanding reveals that atoms are composed of three subatomic particles: protons (positively charged, in the nucleus), neutrons (no charge, in the nucleus), and electrons (negatively charged, orbiting the nucleus in shells). The atomic number of an element is the number of protons in its nucleus and defines the identity of the element. The mass number is the total number of protons and neutrons. Electrons are arranged in energy shells (K, L, M, N) following specific rules, and this electronic configuration determines the chemical behaviour and valency of an element.

key_concepts.definition: An atom is the smallest particle of an element that retains the chemical properties of that element and can take part in a chemical reaction. Atoms are extremely small — the radius of a hydrogen atom is about 0.053 nanometres (5.3 × 10⁻¹¹ m). Atoms of the same element are identical in mass and properties; atoms of different elements differ in mass and properties. Atoms combine to form molecules or compounds.

A proton is a positively charged subatomic particle found in the nucleus of an atom. It carries a charge of +1 (relative charge) and has a relative mass of 1 atomic mass unit (amu). The number of protons in the nucleus (atomic number) is unique to each element and defines what element an atom is. For example, all carbon atoms have 6 protons; all oxygen atoms have 8 protons.

A neutron is an electrically neutral (no charge) subatomic particle found in the nucleus alongside protons. It has a relative mass of 1 amu (approximately equal to that of a proton). Neutrons contribute to the mass of the atom but not to its charge. The number of neutrons can vary among atoms of the same element, giving rise to isotopes. For example, carbon-12 has 6 neutrons and carbon-14 has 8 neutrons.

An electron is a negatively charged subatomic particle that orbits the nucleus in fixed energy levels called shells or orbits. It carries a charge of –1 (relative charge) and has a negligible mass (about 1/1836 the mass of a proton). In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons. Electrons in the outermost shell (valence electrons) determine how an atom bonds with others.

The atomic number (Z) of an element is the number of protons in the nucleus of one atom of that element. It is unique to each element and determines the element's identity and position in the periodic table. In a neutral atom, the atomic number also equals the number of electrons. For example, sodium (Na) has atomic number 11, meaning it has 11 protons and 11 electrons in a neutral atom.

The mass number (A) of an atom is the total number of protons and neutrons in its nucleus. Since electrons have negligible mass, the mass number gives an approximate atomic mass in atomic mass units (amu). The number of neutrons = Mass number – Atomic number. For example, a sodium atom with mass number 23 and atomic number 11 has 23 – 11 = 12 neutrons.

Electrons in an atom are arranged in concentric energy levels called shells or orbits, labelled K, L, M, N (or 1, 2, 3, 4) from innermost to outermost. Each shell can hold a maximum number of electrons: K shell = 2, L shell = 8, M shell = 18, N shell = 32 (general rule: 2n² electrons, where n is the shell number). The outermost shell can hold a maximum of 8 electrons. The arrangement is written as: number of electrons in each shell from K outward.

Valence electrons are the electrons present in the outermost (valence) shell of an atom. They determine the chemical properties, bonding behaviour, and valency of an element. For example, sodium (electronic configuration 2, 8, 1) has 1 valence electron and tends to lose it to form Na⁺. Chlorine (2, 8, 7) has 7 valence electrons and tends to gain 1 to form Cl⁻. Elements with 8 valence electrons (noble gases) are chemically unreactive.

Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have the same atomic number (same number of protons) but different mass numbers (different number of neutrons). Isotopes have identical chemical properties because they have the same number of electrons and protons, but slightly different physical properties (e.g., density, rate of diffusion). Examples: Carbon-12 (6 protons, 6 neutrons) and Carbon-14 (6 protons, 8 neutrons); Hydrogen (1 proton, 0 neutrons), Deuterium (1 proton, 1 neutron), Tritium (1 proton, 2 neutrons).

The nucleus is the extremely small, dense, positively charged centre of an atom containing protons and neutrons. Despite occupying a tiny fraction of the atom's volume, the nucleus contains almost all the atom's mass. The nucleus is held together by the strong nuclear force, which overcomes the electrostatic repulsion between positively charged protons. Rutherford's gold-foil experiment (1911) established the existence of the nucleus.

laws_and_formulas.name: Number of Neutrons
laws_and_formulas.description: The number of neutrons (n) in an atom equals its mass number (A) minus its atomic number (Z). For example, an atom of chlorine with A = 35 and Z = 17 has 35 – 17 = 18 neutrons.

laws_and_formulas.name: Maximum Electrons in a Shell
laws_and_formulas.description: The maximum number of electrons that can occupy the nth shell is given by 2n². K shell (n=1): 2×1²=2; L shell (n=2): 2×2²=8; M shell (n=3): 2×3²=18. However, the outermost shell of any atom can never have more than 8 electrons (octet rule).

laws_and_formulas.name: Atomic Neutrality
laws_and_formulas.formula: Number of protons = Number of electrons (in a neutral atom)
laws_and_formulas.description: In an electrically neutral atom, the positive charge of protons is exactly balanced by the negative charge of electrons. If an atom gains electrons, it becomes a negatively charged ion (anion); if it loses electrons, it becomes a positively charged ion (cation).

key_points: An atom consists of a central nucleus (containing protons and neutrons) surrounded by electrons revolving in shells.
Protons have +1 charge and mass 1 amu; neutrons have 0 charge and mass 1 amu; electrons have –1 charge and negligible mass (~1/1836 amu).
Atomic number (Z) = number of protons = number of electrons (in a neutral atom); it uniquely identifies the element.
Mass number (A) = number of protons + number of neutrons; number of neutrons = A – Z.
Electrons are arranged in shells K, L, M, N with maximum capacity 2, 8, 18, 32 electrons respectively (2n² rule); outermost shell maximum is 8.
Electronic configuration is written as electrons in shells from innermost to outermost, e.g., sodium (Z=11): 2, 8, 1.
Valence electrons (outermost shell electrons) determine the chemical reactivity and valency of an element.
Elements with 8 valence electrons (complete outermost shell) are noble gases and are chemically inert (e.g., neon: 2, 8).
Isotopes are atoms of the same element with same atomic number but different mass numbers (different neutron count); they have the same chemical properties.
Dalton's atomic theory proposed that atoms are indivisible; this was later disproved by the discovery of subatomic particles (electron by Thomson, proton by Rutherford, neutron by Chadwick).
Rutherford's alpha-particle scattering experiment demonstrated that most of an atom's mass and all its positive charge are concentrated in a tiny, dense nucleus.

8|chemistry|5_Language_of_Chemistry.overview: Chemistry has its own language — a system of symbols, formulae, and equations that allows chemists worldwide to communicate information about substances and reactions concisely and unambiguously. Every element is represented by a one- or two-letter symbol, and every compound is represented by a chemical formula showing the types and numbers of atoms present. Valency is the combining capacity of an element or radical, and it determines the formula of a compound. Chemical equations represent reactions, with reactants on the left and products on the right; a balanced equation obeys the Law of Conservation of Mass by showing equal numbers of each type of atom on both sides.

key_concepts.definition: A chemical symbol is a shorthand representation of an element, consisting of one or two letters derived from the element's English or Latin name. The first letter is always a capital; the second (if present) is always lowercase. Symbols are internationally recognised. Examples: O (oxygen), H (hydrogen), C (carbon), Na (sodium, from Latin 'Natrium'), Fe (iron, from Latin 'Ferrum'), K (potassium, from Latin 'Kalium'), Au (gold, from Latin 'Aurum').

A chemical formula represents the composition of a compound using element symbols and subscript numbers to show how many atoms of each element are present in one formula unit or molecule. For example, H₂O (water) has 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom; H₂SO₄ (sulphuric acid) has 2 H, 1 S, and 4 O atoms. The formula is determined using the valencies of the combining elements or radicals.

Valency is the combining capacity of an atom or radical — it represents the number of hydrogen atoms (or equivalent) with which one atom of an element can combine, or which one atom can replace. Elements can have fixed or variable valency. For example, hydrogen and sodium have valency 1, oxygen has valency 2, aluminium has valency 3, carbon has valency 4. Iron has variable valency: 2 (ferrous) and 3 (ferric).

A radical is a group of atoms that carry a charge and behave as a single unit in chemical reactions. Radicals do not normally exist independently — they are found in compounds. Positive radicals (cations): ammonium (NH₄⁺). Negative radicals (anions): hydroxide (OH⁻, valency 1), sulphate (SO₄²⁻, valency 2), nitrate (NO₃⁻, valency 1), carbonate (CO₃²⁻, valency 2), phosphate (PO₄³⁻, valency 3). The valency of a radical equals the magnitude of its charge.

To write the formula of a compound, write the symbols of the combining ions/elements, note their valencies, then interchange (criss-cross) the valencies as subscripts. For example, for calcium chloride: Ca²⁺ and Cl⁻; criss-crossing gives CaCl₂. For aluminium oxide: Al³⁺ and O²⁻; criss-crossing gives Al₂O₃. Always simplify to the lowest whole number ratio (e.g., Ca₂O₂ simplifies to CaO).

A chemical equation is a symbolic representation of a chemical reaction, showing the reactants on the left side and the products on the right side, separated by an arrow (→). The arrow means 'reacts to form' or 'yields'. State symbols may be included: (s) solid, (l) liquid, (g) gas, (aq) aqueous solution. Example: Zn(s) + H₂SO₄(aq) → ZnSO₄(aq) + H₂(g). A chemical equation must be balanced to obey the Law of Conservation of Mass.

A chemical equation is balanced by adjusting the coefficients (numbers placed before formulae) so that the number of atoms of each element is equal on both sides of the equation. Never change the subscripts within a formula to balance — only add coefficients. Example: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O is unbalanced (2 H, 2 O on left; 2 H, 1 O on right). Balanced: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O (4 H, 2 O on each side).

A word equation expresses a chemical reaction in words, naming the reactants and products (e.g., 'zinc + sulphuric acid → zinc sulphate + hydrogen'). A symbol equation uses chemical symbols and formulae and is more precise and informative (e.g., Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂). Symbol equations can be balanced, which word equations cannot. Symbol equations are the standard way of representing reactions in chemistry.

The molecular formula shows the actual number of atoms of each element in one molecule of a compound (e.g., H₂O₂ for hydrogen peroxide). The empirical formula shows the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element (e.g., HO for hydrogen peroxide). For water (H₂O), the molecular formula and empirical formula are the same. At Class 8 level, focus is mainly on molecular formulae of common compounds.

key_concepts.term: Common Acids, Bases, and Salts — Formulae
key_concepts.definition: Key formulae to know: Hydrochloric acid = HCl; Sulphuric acid = H₂SO₄; Nitric acid = HNO₃; Sodium hydroxide = NaOH; Calcium hydroxide = Ca(OH)₂; Common salt (sodium chloride) = NaCl; Calcium carbonate = CaCO₃; Copper sulphate = CuSO₄; Ammonium chloride = NH₄Cl; Potassium permanganate = KMnO₄. These are foundational for writing and balancing equations.

laws_and_formulas.name: Criss-Cross Rule for Formulae
laws_and_formulas.description: To write the formula of a compound formed between element/radical A (valency x) and element/radical B (valency y), interchange the valencies as subscripts: AᵧBₓ. Then simplify to the lowest ratio. Example: Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ → Mg₂O₂ → simplified to MgO.

laws_and_formulas.name: Law of Conservation of Mass (for balancing)
laws_and_formulas.formula: Σ atoms of each element (left) = Σ atoms of each element (right)
laws_and_formulas.description: A chemical equation must be balanced so that the total number of atoms of each element is the same on both sides. This reflects the fact that atoms are neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction — only rearranged.

key_points: Chemical symbols are one or two letter representations of elements; the first letter is always capitalised and the second (if any) is always lowercase.
