Some symbols come from Latin names: Na (Natrium/sodium), Fe (Ferrum/iron), K (Kalium/potassium), Cu (Cuprum/copper), Pb (Plumbum/lead), Au (Aurum/gold), Ag (Argentum/silver).
Valency is the combining capacity of an element or radical; it equals the number of hydrogen atoms (or equivalent) it can combine with or replace.
A radical is a group of atoms carrying an overall charge that acts as a single unit; its valency equals its charge magnitude (e.g., SO₄²⁻ has valency 2).
Formulae are written using the criss-cross method: interchange the valencies as subscripts and simplify to the lowest whole-number ratio.
Chemical equations show reactants on the left and products on the right, separated by an arrow; state symbols (s, l, g, aq) are added for clarity.
Balancing an equation requires adjusting coefficients (NOT subscripts) so atom counts are equal on both sides.
A balanced equation provides information about: the reactants and products, their relative moles (stoichiometry), and states of matter.
Common variable-valency metals: iron (2 or 3), copper (1 or 2), tin (2 or 4). The valency is indicated by Roman numerals: iron(II) vs iron(III).
Important radicals and their valencies: OH⁻ (1), NO₃⁻ (1), HCO₃⁻ (1), NH₄⁺ (1), SO₄²⁻ (2), CO₃²⁻ (2), SO₃²⁻ (2), PO₄³⁻ (3).

8|chemistry|6_Chemical_Reactions.overview: A chemical reaction involves the breaking of bonds in reactants and the formation of new bonds in products, resulting in new substances with different properties. Chemical reactions are classified based on the nature of the chemical change involved: combination reactions, decomposition reactions, displacement (single replacement) reactions, double displacement (double decomposition) reactions, and oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions. Reactions are also classified by energy exchange as exothermic (heat-releasing) or endothermic (heat-absorbing). Understanding these types helps predict products and conditions required for various reactions.

key_concepts.definition: A combination reaction is one in which two or more reactants combine to form a single product. General form: A + B → AB. Examples: Burning of magnesium in oxygen: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO (magnesium oxide); Formation of water: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O; Formation of sulphur dioxide: S + O₂ → SO₂. These reactions are often exothermic. The single product is always more complex than the reactants.

A decomposition reaction is one in which a single compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances. It is the reverse of a combination reaction. General form: AB → A + B. Energy (heat, light, or electricity) is usually required. Examples: Thermal decomposition of calcium carbonate: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (used in lime kilns); Electrolysis of water: 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂; Decomposition of hydrogen peroxide: 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂.

A displacement reaction occurs when a more reactive element displaces (pushes out) a less reactive element from its salt solution. General form: A + BC → AC + B (where A is more reactive than B). Examples: Zinc displaces copper from copper sulphate solution: Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu (the blue solution turns colourless and a red-brown copper deposit forms); Iron displaces copper from copper sulphate: Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu. Reactivity series determines which element displaces which.

In a double displacement reaction, two compounds react and exchange their ions (or radicals) to form two new compounds. General form: AB + CD → AD + CB. These often produce a precipitate, water, or a gas. Examples: AgNO₃ + NaCl → AgCl↓ + NaNO₃ (white precipitate of AgCl forms); BaCl₂ + Na₂SO₄ → BaSO₄↓ + 2NaCl; NaOH + HCl → NaCl + H₂O (neutralisation). Reactions producing precipitates are called precipitation reactions.

Oxidation is a chemical process involving the gain of oxygen, the loss of hydrogen, or the loss of electrons by a substance. The substance that is oxidised is called the reducing agent (it reduces the other substance). Examples: Burning of carbon: C + O₂ → CO₂ (carbon gains oxygen — oxidised); Rusting of iron is oxidation of iron; Conversion of H₂S to S (sulphur gains no hydrogen when H₂S loses H — oxidation of sulphur species).

Reduction is a chemical process involving the loss of oxygen, the gain of hydrogen, or the gain of electrons by a substance. The substance that is reduced is called the oxidising agent (it oxidises the other substance). Examples: Reduction of copper oxide by hydrogen: CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O (CuO loses oxygen — reduced); Reduction of iron oxide to iron in a blast furnace: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂.

Oxidation and reduction always occur simultaneously in a reaction — whenever one substance is oxidised, another is reduced. Such reactions are called oxidation-reduction reactions or redox reactions. The oxidising agent gains electrons (is itself reduced); the reducing agent loses electrons (is itself oxidised). Example: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. Magnesium is oxidised (gains O, loses electrons); oxygen is reduced (gains electrons).

An exothermic reaction releases heat energy to the surroundings. The products have lower energy than the reactants, and the excess energy is released as heat (and sometimes light). The surroundings become warmer. Examples: Combustion of fuels (burning of coal, wood, LPG), neutralisation of acids with bases, respiration in cells, rusting of iron (very slowly). These reactions generally do not need continuous heating once started.

An endothermic reaction absorbs heat energy from the surroundings. The products have higher energy than the reactants, and the surroundings become cooler. Examples: Photosynthesis (plants absorbing sunlight), thermal decomposition of calcium carbonate (heating limestone), dissolution of ammonium nitrate in water, melting of ice. These reactions require a continuous supply of energy to proceed.

The reactivity series (activity series) is a list of metals arranged in decreasing order of their chemical reactivity. More reactive metals (higher in the series) can displace less reactive metals from their salt solutions. Order (high to low): K, Na, Ca, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe, Ni, Sn, Pb, H, Cu, Hg, Ag, Au, Pt. Metals above hydrogen can displace hydrogen from dilute acids; metals below hydrogen cannot. This series is used to predict displacement reactions.

laws_and_formulas.name: General Combination Reaction
laws_and_formulas.description: Two or more reactants combine to form a single, more complex product. The forward direction is combination (synthesis). This is often exothermic. Example: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O.

laws_and_formulas.name: General Decomposition Reaction
laws_and_formulas.description: A single compound breaks down into two or more simpler products, usually requiring energy input. Example: 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂ (electrolysis) or CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (thermal decomposition).

laws_and_formulas.name: General Displacement Reaction
laws_and_formulas.formula: A + BC → AC + B (A more reactive than B)
laws_and_formulas.description: A more reactive element displaces a less reactive one from a compound in solution. Determined by position in the reactivity series. Example: Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu (zinc is more reactive than copper).

laws_and_formulas.name: General Double Displacement
laws_and_formulas.description: Two compounds exchange partners (ions or radicals). Often involves precipitation, neutralisation, or gas evolution. Example: AgNO₃ + NaCl → AgCl↓ + NaNO₃.

key_points: Chemical reactions are classified as: combination, decomposition, displacement (single), and double displacement — based on how reactants and products are related.
Combination reactions: two or more substances combine to form one product (A + B → AB); often exothermic.
Decomposition reactions: one compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances; usually require energy input (heat, light, or electricity).
Displacement reactions: a more reactive element displaces a less reactive element from its compound; determined by the reactivity series.
Double displacement reactions: two compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds; often accompanied by precipitate, gas, or water formation.
Oxidation is gain of oxygen / loss of hydrogen / loss of electrons; reduction is loss of oxygen / gain of hydrogen / gain of electrons.
Oxidation and reduction always occur together (redox reactions); the oxidising agent is reduced and the reducing agent is oxidised.
Exothermic reactions release heat (surroundings warm up): combustion, neutralisation, respiration; endothermic reactions absorb heat (surroundings cool down): photosynthesis, thermal decomposition.
The reactivity series ranks metals by reactivity; a metal higher in the series can displace one lower in the series from its salt solution.
Neutralisation is a double displacement reaction between an acid and a base to form a salt and water: acid + base → salt + water.
Thermal decomposition of CaCO₃ (limestone) to produce quicklime (CaO) and CO₂ is an important industrial endothermic decomposition reaction.

8|chemistry|7_Carbon_and_its_Compounds.overview: Carbon is a non-metallic element with atomic number 6 and the unique ability to form millions of compounds due to its tetravalency (4 valence electrons) and catenation (ability to form long chains by bonding with other carbon atoms). Carbon exists in nature in several distinct physical forms called allotropes — diamond, graphite, and fullerene — each with vastly different properties despite being made only of carbon atoms. Carbon is also the basis of all living matter and organic chemistry. Organic compounds are carbon-containing compounds, with hydrocarbons (compounds of only carbon and hydrogen) being the simplest class. Methane (CH₄) is the simplest hydrocarbon and the main component of natural gas.

key_concepts.definition: Allotropes are different physical forms of the same element that differ in the arrangement of atoms. Carbon has three main allotropes: diamond, graphite, and fullerene (Buckminsterfullerene). Despite being made entirely of carbon atoms, these allotropes have drastically different properties — diamond is the hardest natural substance, graphite is soft and slippery, and fullerene has a cage-like structure. The different properties arise solely from the different atomic arrangements.

In diamond, each carbon atom is bonded to four other carbon atoms in a rigid, three-dimensional tetrahedral network structure. This gives diamond extreme hardness (hardest natural substance — 10 on Mohs scale), very high melting point, and makes it a poor conductor of electricity (no free electrons). Diamond is transparent and has a very high refractive index, making it valuable as a gemstone. Industrial diamonds are used to cut, grind, and bore other hard materials.

In graphite, carbon atoms are arranged in flat, hexagonal layers (like a honeycomb). Within each layer, each carbon bonds to three others, leaving one electron free per carbon atom. These free electrons allow graphite to conduct electricity. The layers are held together by weak van der Waals forces and can slide over one another easily, making graphite soft and slippery. It is used as a lubricant, in pencil 'lead' (mixed with clay), and as electrodes in electrolysis.

Fullerene is an allotrope of carbon in which 60 carbon atoms are arranged in a spherical cage resembling a football (soccer ball), made of 20 hexagons and 12 pentagons. Discovered in 1985, it was named after architect Buckminster Fuller. Fullerenes (also called buckyballs) are being studied for use in drug delivery, lubricants, superconductors, and nanotechnology. Carbon nanotubes are cylindrical fullerene-related structures with exceptional strength and conductivity.

Catenation is the unique property of carbon atoms to bond with other carbon atoms to form long chains, branched chains, and ring structures. This gives carbon the ability to form an almost unlimited variety of compounds — over 10 million organic compounds are known. No other element shows catenation to this extent, which is why carbon chemistry (organic chemistry) is a vast separate branch of chemistry.

Carbon has 4 valence electrons and a valency of 4, meaning each carbon atom can form 4 covalent bonds with other atoms (carbon or other elements). This tetravalency, combined with catenation, allows carbon to form an enormous diversity of structures: straight chains, branched chains, rings, and double or triple bonds. For example, in methane (CH₄), carbon forms 4 bonds with 4 hydrogen atoms.

Organic compounds are compounds primarily containing carbon, usually bonded with hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and other elements. They are the basis of all living matter. Organic chemistry was originally defined as the study of compounds produced by living organisms, but now includes any carbon compound (except simple ones like CO₂, CO, carbonates, and carbides, which are classified as inorganic). Examples: methane, ethanol, glucose, proteins, fats, plastics, petrol.

Hydrocarbons are organic compounds containing only carbon and hydrogen atoms. They are the simplest organic compounds and are the main components of fossil fuels (petroleum, natural gas). Hydrocarbons are classified as: saturated (alkanes — only single C–C bonds, e.g., methane CH₄, ethane C₂H₆) and unsaturated (alkenes — contain C=C double bonds, e.g., ethene C₂H₄; alkynes — contain C≡C triple bonds, e.g., ethyne C₂H₂).

Methane is the simplest hydrocarbon with the molecular formula CH₄. It is a colourless, odourless, highly flammable gas at room temperature. Methane is the main component of natural gas (~95%) and biogas and is a major fuel source. It burns in oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. The carbon in methane has a tetrahedral arrangement of 4 hydrogen atoms. Methane is also a greenhouse gas.

Ethanol (ethyl alcohol) is a simple organic compound with the molecular formula C₂H₅OH. It is the alcohol present in alcoholic beverages and is also used as a solvent, antiseptic, and fuel additive (E10 petrol). Ethanol is produced by fermentation of sugars (C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂) by yeast, or by hydration of ethene. It is a colourless liquid with a characteristic smell and burns with a clean blue flame. It is miscible with water in all proportions.

laws_and_formulas.name: Combustion of Methane
laws_and_formulas.description: Methane burns completely in excess oxygen to form carbon dioxide and water, releasing a large amount of heat energy. This is the basis of its use as a fuel in natural gas. Incomplete combustion in limited oxygen produces carbon monoxide (CO) and carbon (soot).

laws_and_formulas.name: Fermentation of Glucose to Ethanol
laws_and_formulas.description: Glucose is converted to ethanol and carbon dioxide by yeast enzymes (zymase) in the absence of oxygen. This anaerobic process is called fermentation and is used to produce alcoholic beverages, bioethanol fuel, and bread (CO₂ makes dough rise).

laws_and_formulas.name: Combustion of Ethanol
laws_and_formulas.description: Ethanol burns in oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water. It is a cleaner fuel than many hydrocarbons. The products are the same as in any complete combustion of a carbon-hydrogen-oxygen compound.

key_points: Carbon (atomic number 6, valency 4) is unique in its ability to form millions of compounds due to tetravalency and catenation.
Catenation is the ability of carbon atoms to bond with each other to form chains and rings — no other element shows this property to the same extent.
Allotropes are different physical forms of the same element; carbon's main allotropes are diamond, graphite, and fullerene.
Diamond: each C bonded to 4 others in a 3D network — hardest natural substance, poor conductor of electricity, used as gemstone and cutting tool.
Graphite: C atoms in flat hexagonal layers — soft, good conductor of electricity, used as lubricant, pencil lead, and electrode.
Fullerene (C₆₀): spherical cage of 60 C atoms arranged in hexagons and pentagons — studied for nanotechnology and drug delivery applications.
Organic compounds contain carbon (usually also hydrogen); over 10 million organic compounds are known. Simple compounds like CO₂ and CaCO₃ are classified as inorganic.
Hydrocarbons contain only C and H; they are classified as alkanes (saturated, single bonds), alkenes (unsaturated, double bonds), and alkynes (unsaturated, triple bonds).
Methane (CH₄) is the simplest alkane and the main component of natural gas; it burns to give CO₂ and H₂O.
Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) is produced by fermentation of sugars by yeast; it is used as a fuel, solvent, and antiseptic.
Fossil fuels (coal, petroleum, natural gas) are formed from the remains of ancient organisms and are primarily composed of carbon compounds.

8|chemistry|8_Water.overview: Water (H₂O) is the most abundant and important compound on Earth, covering about 71% of the Earth's surface and making up 60–70% of the human body. It is sometimes called the 'universal solvent' because it dissolves a wide variety of substances. Water exists in three states — solid (ice), liquid (water), and gas (water vapour/steam) — and circulates continuously through the environment via the water cycle. Natural water is rarely pure; it contains dissolved salts, making it hard or soft. Water can be purified by various methods for different uses. Water conservation is critical because only about 2.5% of Earth's water is fresh water, and much of it is inaccessible.

key_concepts.definition: Pure water is a colourless, odourless, tasteless liquid at room temperature. It has a boiling point of 100°C and a freezing point of 0°C at standard atmospheric pressure. Water has an unusually high specific heat capacity (4.18 J g⁻¹ °C⁻¹), meaning it absorbs and releases large amounts of heat with minimal temperature change — important for climate regulation. It has maximum density at 4°C. Ice is less dense than liquid water, which is why ice floats — a vital property for aquatic life in winter.

Water is called the 'universal solvent' because it can dissolve a greater variety of substances than any other liquid. This is due to its polar nature (unequal sharing of electrons in H–O bonds creates partial positive and negative charges). Water dissolves ionic compounds (like NaCl, dissolving Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions) and polar molecular compounds (like sugar, HCl). Non-polar substances (oils, fats, waxes) do not dissolve in water. This solvent property makes water essential for biological processes.

Hard water is water that does not lather readily with soap and instead forms a white, sticky scum. Hardness is caused by dissolved calcium and magnesium salts: calcium bicarbonate Ca(HCO₃)₂, calcium sulphate CaSO₄, magnesium bicarbonate Mg(HCO₃)₂, and magnesium sulphate MgSO₄. Temporary hardness (caused by bicarbonates) can be removed by boiling; permanent hardness (caused by sulphates/chlorides) cannot be removed by boiling and requires chemical treatment.

Soft water is water that lathers easily with soap and produces no scum. It does not contain significant amounts of dissolved calcium or magnesium salts. Rainwater and distilled water are naturally soft. Soft water is preferred for washing, cooking (preserves taste of food), and industrial processes (prevents boiler scale). Hard water can be softened (converted to soft water) by boiling (temporary hardness), addition of washing soda (Na₂CO₃), ion exchange, or distillation.

Temporary hardness is caused by dissolved calcium and magnesium bicarbonates. It can be removed by: (1) Boiling — Ca(HCO₃)₂ decomposes to insoluble CaCO₃ (scale/fur) + H₂O + CO₂; the insoluble CaCO₃ is filtered off. (2) Adding slaked lime (calcium hydroxide): Ca(HCO₃)₂ + Ca(OH)₂ → 2CaCO₃↓ + 2H₂O (Clarke's process). The precipitate is filtered to give soft water.

Permanent hardness is caused by dissolved calcium/magnesium sulphates and chlorides, which do not decompose on boiling. Removal methods: (1) Adding washing soda (Na₂CO₃): CaSO₄ + Na₂CO₃ → CaCO₃↓ + Na₂SO₄; the insoluble carbonate is filtered off. (2) Ion exchange (Permutit/zeolite) process: water is passed through a bed of zeolite (sodium aluminium silicate) which exchanges Na⁺ for Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions. (3) Distillation: produces pure water but is expensive for large volumes.

Drinking water is purified from natural sources by: (1) Sedimentation — allowing suspended particles to settle. (2) Filtration through sand and gravel to remove fine particles. (3) Chlorination — adding chlorine gas or bleaching powder to kill bacteria and other pathogens; this is the most common method. (4) Boiling — kills bacteria (effective at home level). (5) Distillation — produces the purest water by evaporating and re-condensing, removing all dissolved salts and microorganisms.

The water cycle (hydrological cycle) is the continuous movement of water between the Earth's surface and the atmosphere. Key processes: Evaporation — water from oceans, lakes, rivers evaporates due to solar heat; Transpiration — plants release water vapour (collectively: evapotranspiration); Condensation — water vapour rises, cools, and condenses to form clouds; Precipitation — water falls as rain, hail, or snow; Collection — water collects in rivers, lakes, oceans, and groundwater. The cycle purifies and redistributes water continuously.

Water pollution is the contamination of water bodies (rivers, lakes, groundwater, oceans) with harmful substances. Major causes: industrial effluents (heavy metals, chemicals), agricultural runoff (fertilisers, pesticides), sewage and domestic waste, oil spills, and plastic dumping. Effects: health hazards (cholera, typhoid, dysentery from contaminated drinking water), death of aquatic organisms (from eutrophication and oxygen depletion), and disruption of ecosystems. Clean water is essential for human health and biodiversity.

Water conservation is the careful and efficient use of water to prevent its wastage. Only about 2.5% of Earth's water is fresh, and of that, most is locked in glaciers and ice caps. Methods: rainwater harvesting, drip irrigation in agriculture, fixing leaks in pipes, reusing grey water for gardening, reducing industrial water use, and treating and recycling wastewater. Conservation is critical to ensure water availability for future generations and to protect ecosystems.

laws_and_formulas.name: Decomposition of Calcium Bicarbonate (boiling to remove temporary hardness)
laws_and_formulas.description: When water containing dissolved calcium bicarbonate is boiled, it decomposes to produce insoluble calcium carbonate (scale/limescale), water, and carbon dioxide. The insoluble CaCO₃ can be removed by filtration, yielding soft water. This is the chemistry of kettles developing limescale in hard-water areas.

laws_and_formulas.name: Clarke's Process (lime softening)
laws_and_formulas.description: Adding calculated amounts of slaked lime (calcium hydroxide) to hard water containing calcium bicarbonate precipitates calcium carbonate. The precipitate is filtered off, leaving soft water. This is used on a large scale in water treatment plants.

laws_and_formulas.name: Softening with Washing Soda (permanent hardness)
laws_and_formulas.description: Washing soda (sodium carbonate) reacts with calcium sulphate (which causes permanent hardness) to form insoluble calcium carbonate and soluble sodium sulphate. After filtering off the CaCO₃ precipitate, the water is softened. The same principle applies to MgSO₄ and CaCl₂.

key_points: Pure water is colourless, odourless, and tasteless; it boils at 100°C and freezes at 0°C at standard atmospheric pressure.
Water has maximum density at 4°C; ice is less dense than liquid water and floats — this is critical for aquatic life in cold climates.
Water is called the 'universal solvent' because its polar nature allows it to dissolve a large variety of ionic and polar covalent compounds.
Hard water does not lather well with soap; it contains dissolved Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ salts (bicarbonates, sulphates, chlorides).

Temporary hardness (bicarbonates) can be removed by boiling or adding slaked lime (Clarke's process); permanent hardness (sulphates/chlorides) is removed by washing soda, ion exchange, or distillation.
Water is purified for drinking by sedimentation, filtration, and chlorination; distillation gives the purest water.
The water cycle involves evaporation (from water bodies), transpiration (from plants), condensation (forming clouds), precipitation (rain/snow), and collection in water bodies.
Water pollution from industrial waste, sewage, and agricultural runoff causes serious health hazards (waterborne diseases) and destroys aquatic ecosystems.
Only ~2.5% of Earth's water is fresh; conservation through rainwater harvesting, efficient irrigation, and wastewater recycling is essential.
Scale (limescale) in kettles and boilers is insoluble CaCO₃ formed when temporary hard water is boiled — it reduces heating efficiency and can damage equipment.

8|chemistry|9_Hydrogen.overview: Hydrogen is the lightest and most abundant element in the universe, with atomic number 1, symbol H, and atomic mass 1 amu. At standard conditions, it exists as a diatomic gas (H₂) — colourless, odourless, and tasteless. Hydrogen is an extremely reactive and highly flammable gas. It can be prepared in the laboratory by the reaction of dilute acids with active metals, or by the electrolysis of water. Industrially, it is produced by steam reforming of natural gas. Hydrogen has numerous applications as a fuel, reducing agent in metallurgy, and in the manufacture of ammonia (Haber process). The hydrogen fuel cell, which produces electricity from hydrogen and oxygen with water as the only by-product, is being developed as a clean energy technology.

key_concepts.definition: Hydrogen (H₂) is the lightest known element and gas — density about 14 times less than air, which is why it rises rapidly and was formerly used in airships (though its flammability caused disasters). It is colourless, odourless, and tasteless. It is sparingly soluble in water. Hydrogen has the lowest boiling point of any element except helium (–253°C). It diffuses very rapidly through small gaps because of its low molecular mass. It turns moist blue litmus paper red if hydrogen chloride gas is present, but pure hydrogen has no effect on litmus.

Hydrogen is combustible (burns readily) but is not a supporter of combustion (does not help other things burn — unlike oxygen). It burns in air/oxygen with a pale blue, almost invisible flame to produce water: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. A mixture of hydrogen and air is explosive (hydrogen-air mixture ignites with a 'squeaky pop' — the test for hydrogen). Hydrogen is a good reducing agent — it reduces metal oxides to metals by removing their oxygen. For example: CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O.

The standard laboratory test for hydrogen gas: bring a burning splint near the mouth of the test tube or gas jar containing the gas. Hydrogen burns with a characteristic 'squeaky pop' (sharp 'pop' sound) as it ignites and reacts with oxygen in the air. This squeaky pop is produced by the rapid combustion of a hydrogen-air mixture. No other gas gives this specific test result, making it a reliable identification test for hydrogen.

key_concepts.term: Laboratory Preparation from Acids and Metals
key_concepts.definition: In the laboratory, hydrogen is prepared by reacting a dilute acid with an active metal. Dilute sulphuric acid (or dilute hydrochloric acid) is added to zinc granules in a conical flask: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑ or Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂↑. Zinc is preferred over iron (too slow) and magnesium (too fast and expensive). Dilute sulphuric acid is preferred over dilute nitric acid (which does not give hydrogen — it acts as an oxidising agent). The gas is collected by downward displacement of water or upward displacement of air (as hydrogen is lighter).

Water is a poor conductor of electricity in pure form. By adding dilute sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) or sodium hydroxide (NaOH) to improve conductivity, water can be electrolysed. On passing electric current: hydrogen is produced at the cathode (negative electrode) and oxygen at the anode (positive electrode). 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂. The volume of hydrogen produced is twice the volume of oxygen (ratio 2:1). This is a demonstration of the composition of water.

Industrially, hydrogen is produced on a large scale by steam reforming of natural gas (methane). Methane is reacted with steam at high temperature (700–1000°C) over a nickel catalyst: CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂. This is followed by the water-gas shift reaction: CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂. The CO₂ is removed, leaving hydrogen. Steam reforming is the most common industrial process for hydrogen production, used to make ammonia, methanol, and hydrogen fuel.

Hydrogen is a powerful reducing agent — it removes oxygen from metal oxides (reduces them), thereby producing the pure metal. This is used in metallurgy. Examples: Reduction of copper oxide: CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O (black CuO turns to shiny red-brown copper); Reduction of iron oxide: Fe₂O₃ + 3H₂ → 2Fe + 3H₂O. In these reactions, hydrogen gains oxygen (is oxidised) and the metal oxide loses oxygen (is reduced). This makes hydrogen both a reducing agent and a fuel.

Hydrogen is an excellent fuel because it has a very high energy content per kilogram (about 3 times that of petrol) and its combustion produces only water — no carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide, or other pollutants. 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O + energy. Hydrogen is used as rocket fuel (liquid hydrogen in space vehicles). However, challenges include: storage (it must be stored under high pressure or as liquid at extremely low temperatures), production (currently mostly from fossil fuels), and distribution infrastructure.

A hydrogen fuel cell is an electrochemical device that converts chemical energy from hydrogen and oxygen directly into electrical energy, with water as the only by-product. The reaction is: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O + electricity. At the anode, hydrogen is oxidised (H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻); at the cathode, oxygen is reduced (O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ → 2H₂O). Fuel cells are clean, highly efficient, and are being developed for use in electric vehicles, portable power, and buildings. They emit no greenhouse gases if the hydrogen comes from renewable sources.

Hydrogen has diverse industrial and commercial uses: (1) Manufacture of ammonia by the Haber process (N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃) — used for fertilisers. (2) Manufacture of methanol and hydrochloric acid. (3) Hydrogenation of vegetable oils (unsaturated liquid oils + H₂ → saturated solid fats, used to make margarine). (4) As a rocket fuel and fuel cell fuel. (5) As a reducing agent in metallurgy to extract metals from their oxides. (6) Filling weather balloons (due to low density, but helium is safer).

laws_and_formulas.name: Laboratory Preparation of Hydrogen
laws_and_formulas.description: Zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid to produce zinc sulphate (in solution) and hydrogen gas (which bubbles off). This is the standard laboratory preparation. Alternatively: Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂↑ (using dilute hydrochloric acid).

laws_and_formulas.name: Combustion of Hydrogen
laws_and_formulas.description: Hydrogen burns in oxygen (or air) to produce water. This is a highly exothermic reaction. The product is water only — no carbon dioxide or other pollutants. This makes hydrogen a clean fuel. A hydrogen-air mixture produces a characteristic 'squeaky pop' on ignition.

laws_and_formulas.name: Electrolysis of Water
laws_and_formulas.description: When water is electrolysed (using dilute H₂SO₄ or NaOH as electrolyte), hydrogen is produced at the cathode and oxygen at the anode in a 2:1 volume ratio. This confirms that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen.

laws_and_formulas.name: Reduction of Copper Oxide by Hydrogen
laws_and_formulas.description: When hydrogen gas is passed over hot black copper oxide, the oxide is reduced to shiny red-brown copper and water is formed. Hydrogen acts as the reducing agent (it gains oxygen and is itself oxidised to water). This demonstrates hydrogen's reducing power.

laws_and_formulas.name: Haber Process (industrial use of H₂)
laws_and_formulas.description: The Haber process combines nitrogen (from air) with hydrogen (from steam reforming of natural gas) at high temperature (~450°C), high pressure (~200 atm), and with an iron catalyst to produce ammonia. Ammonia is the basis of most synthetic nitrogen fertilisers. This is the most important industrial use of hydrogen.

key_points: Hydrogen (H₂) is the lightest and most abundant element in the universe; it is a colourless, odourless, highly flammable diatomic gas at room temperature.
Hydrogen burns with a pale blue flame producing only water: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O; a hydrogen-air mixture produces a characteristic 'squeaky pop' on ignition — the laboratory test for hydrogen.
Hydrogen is combustible but is NOT a supporter of combustion — it does not help other substances burn (unlike oxygen).
Laboratory preparation: dilute sulphuric acid (or HCl) + zinc granules → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑. Zinc is the preferred metal; dilute HNO₃ is NOT used as it does not produce hydrogen.
Hydrogen can be prepared by electrolysis of water (acidified or alkaline): hydrogen at cathode, oxygen at anode, in a 2:1 volume ratio.
Industrially, hydrogen is produced by steam reforming of methane: CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (at high temperature over Ni catalyst).
Hydrogen is a powerful reducing agent — it removes oxygen from metal oxides to give the pure metal and water (e.g., CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O).
Hydrogen is an excellent fuel with very high energy content and produces only water on combustion — no CO₂ or other pollutants (clean fuel).
The hydrogen fuel cell converts H₂ and O₂ directly to electricity and water; it is a clean and efficient energy technology for vehicles and power generation.
Key industrial uses: Haber process (ammonia synthesis for fertilisers), hydrogenation of oils to make margarine, manufacture of HCl and methanol, rocket fuel.
Challenges of hydrogen as a fuel: costly production (mostly from fossil fuels currently), difficult storage (high pressure or cryogenic liquid), and lack of distribution infrastructure.

8|biology|1_Transport_of_Food_and_Minera.overview: Plants, unlike animals, lack a circulatory system with a pump, yet they transport water, minerals, and manufactured food across their entire body — from roots deep in the soil to leaves high in the canopy. This chapter examines the two specialised conducting tissues, xylem and phloem, which together form the vascular system of a plant. Xylem carries water and dissolved mineral salts upward from the roots, while phloem distributes the sugars produced by photosynthesis to every living cell. Understanding the mechanisms behind this transport — transpiration pull, root pressure, and active loading into the phloem — reveals how plants function as remarkably efficient biological systems despite being stationary organisms.

key_concepts.definition: Xylem is the water-conducting vascular tissue of plants, composed of dead, hollow cells (vessel elements and tracheids) arranged end-to-end to form continuous tubes. It transports water and dissolved mineral salts absorbed by the roots upward to the leaves and other aerial parts. Because xylem cells are non-living at maturity, transport through them is entirely physical, driven by transpiration pull and root pressure.

Phloem is the food-conducting vascular tissue made up of living cells, principally sieve tube elements and companion cells. It transports dissolved organic solutes — mainly sucrose produced during photosynthesis — from the leaves (source) to all other parts of the plant such as roots, fruits, and seeds (sinks). Unlike xylem transport, phloem translocation is an active, energy-requiring process.

Transpiration is the process by which water evaporates from the aerial surfaces of a plant — predominantly through tiny pores called stomata on the leaf surface. As water vapour escapes, it creates a water deficit in the leaf cells, which draws water upward from the xylem by cohesion-tension. Transpiration plays a crucial role in mineral absorption and helps cool the plant, but excessive transpiration can lead to wilting.

Transpiration pull is the primary mechanism that drives the ascent of water through the xylem. Water molecules have strong cohesive forces between them, forming a continuous, unbroken column inside the xylem tubes. When water evaporates from leaf cells, it creates a negative pressure (tension) at the top of the column, pulling the entire water column upward — much like liquid being drawn through a straw.

Root pressure is a pushing force generated in the roots due to the active absorption of mineral ions into the root tissues. This creates a high solute concentration inside the root xylem, causing water to enter by osmosis from the surrounding soil. Root pressure is most significant at night when transpiration is low, and it can cause guttation — the exudation of water droplets from leaf tips.

Osmosis is the movement of water molecules from a region of higher water potential (dilute solution) to a region of lower water potential (concentrated solution) through a selectively permeable membrane. In plants, osmosis drives the entry of water from soil into root hair cells, where the cell sap is more concentrated than the surrounding soil water. This process is fundamental to the absorption stage of water transport.

Stomata (singular: stoma) are microscopic pores found mainly on the lower surface of leaves, each surrounded by a pair of specialised guard cells. They regulate the exchange of gases (CO₂ and O₂) for photosynthesis and respiration, and also control the rate of water vapour loss during transpiration. Guard cells swell with water to open the pore and lose water to close it, allowing the plant to regulate transpiration.

Plants absorb mineral ions such as nitrates, phosphates, and potassium from the soil through their root hair cells. Most mineral absorption occurs by active transport — a process that requires energy (ATP) to move ions against a concentration gradient from the dilute soil solution into the root cells. Minerals then pass into the xylem and are carried upward with the transpiration stream to where they are needed.

Translocation is the movement of dissolved organic substances, particularly sucrose and amino acids, through the phloem from regions of production (sources, mainly mature leaves) to regions of utilisation or storage (sinks, such as roots, flowers, fruits, and developing leaves). This bidirectional transport is driven by differences in turgor pressure between source and sink cells, a mechanism described by the pressure-flow hypothesis.

Guttation is the exudation of drops of liquid water (not water vapour) from the tips and margins of leaves, usually seen in the early morning. It occurs when root pressure is high and transpiration is low — typically at night — forcing xylem sap out through specialised pores called hydathodes located at leaf tips. Guttation water contains dissolved minerals and differs from dew, which is condensed atmospheric moisture.

key_points: Vascular tissue in plants consists of xylem (water and mineral transport, upward) and phloem (food transport, bidirectional), forming a continuous network from root to leaf.
Water enters root hair cells by osmosis because root cell sap has a higher solute concentration than surrounding soil water; root hairs increase the surface area for efficient absorption.

The transpiration pull (cohesion-tension mechanism) is the main force responsible for the ascent of water through xylem; cohesion between water molecules keeps the column intact while tension created by evaporation at the leaf surface pulls it upward.
Transpiration rate is affected by temperature, humidity, wind speed, light intensity, and the number of open stomata; plants close stomata during drought to reduce water loss.
Root pressure acts as a supplementary force for water rise and is responsible for guttation observed as droplets at leaf tips on humid mornings.
Mineral salts are absorbed mainly by active transport, requiring cellular energy; this distinguishes mineral absorption from water absorption (which is passive via osmosis).
Phloem translocation is an active process — sieve tube elements are living cells assisted by companion cells that supply ATP for loading sugars at source cells.
The pressure-flow hypothesis explains phloem transport: high osmotic pressure at the source (leaves) and low osmotic pressure at the sink (roots/fruits) creates a pressure gradient that drives flow.
Wilting occurs when the rate of water loss by transpiration exceeds the rate of water absorption by roots, causing loss of turgor pressure in leaf cells.
Deforestation reduces transpiration at a landscape level, contributing to reduced rainfall and altered water cycles in an ecosystem.

8|biology|2_Reproduction_in_Plants.overview: Reproduction is the fundamental biological process by which living organisms produce new individuals of their own kind, ensuring the continuity of the species. In plants, reproduction occurs through two broad strategies: asexual reproduction, which does not involve the fusion of gametes and produces offspring genetically identical to the parent, and sexual reproduction, which involves the production and fusion of male and female gametes to generate genetically diverse offspring. This chapter explores vegetative propagation — one of the most common forms of asexual reproduction in plants — as well as the intricacies of sexual reproduction including flower structure, pollination, fertilisation, seed formation, fruit development, and the diverse mechanisms by which seeds and fruits are dispersed to new locations. Understanding plant reproduction has enormous agricultural significance, as it underpins techniques used in crop improvement and horticulture.

key_concepts.definition: Vegetative reproduction is a form of asexual reproduction in which new plants arise from vegetative (non-reproductive) parts of the parent plant such as roots, stems, or leaves, without the involvement of seeds or spores. Examples include the formation of runners in strawberry, bulbs in onion, rhizomes in ginger, tubers in potato, and leaf buds in Bryophyllum. All offspring are genetically identical to the parent (clones), making this method useful for preserving desirable traits.

Asexual reproduction is a mode of reproduction that involves only one parent and does not require the fusion of gametes. The offspring produced are genetically identical to the parent. In lower plants, asexual reproduction occurs by fragmentation (in algae such as Spirogyra), budding (in yeast), and spore formation (in fungi like Rhizopus and in mosses and ferns). Asexual reproduction is rapid and allows quick colonisation of a favourable environment.

Sexual reproduction involves the formation and fusion of male gametes (contained in pollen) and female gametes (ovules inside the ovary) to form a zygote, which develops into a seed. This process introduces genetic variation in the offspring, which is the raw material for evolution and adaptation. The flower is the reproductive organ of angiosperms, containing the stamen (male) and pistil or carpel (female) parts.

Pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anther of a stamen to the stigma of a pistil. When pollen is transferred within the same flower or to another flower on the same plant, it is called self-pollination. When pollen is transferred from a flower on one plant to a flower on a genetically different plant of the same species, it is called cross-pollination. Cross-pollination is facilitated by agents such as wind (anemophily), insects (entomophily), water (hydrophily), and birds (ornithophily).

Fertilisation is the fusion of the male gamete (from the pollen tube) with the female gamete (egg cell in the ovule) to form a diploid zygote. In angiosperms, double fertilisation occurs: one male gamete fuses with the egg cell to form the zygote, and another fuses with the two polar nuclei to form the triploid primary endosperm nucleus, which develops into the nutritive endosperm tissue. After fertilisation, the ovule becomes the seed and the ovary becomes the fruit.

A seed is a mature ovule containing the embryo (a miniature plant) along with stored food (cotyledons or endosperm) and enclosed within a protective seed coat (testa). The embryo consists of the radicle (embryonic root), plumule (embryonic shoot), and one or two seed leaves (cotyledons). Seeds of dicotyledonous plants (e.g., bean) have two cotyledons that store food, while monocotyledonous seeds (e.g., maize) have one cotyledon.

Germination is the process by which a dormant seed resumes active growth and develops into a seedling under favourable conditions. The essential requirements for germination are adequate water, a suitable temperature, and oxygen. Water activates enzymes that break down stored food reserves; oxygen is needed for cellular respiration to supply energy; temperature affects enzyme activity. In epigeal germination (e.g., bean), cotyledons are carried above the soil, whereas in hypogeal germination (e.g., maize), cotyledons remain below the soil.

Seed dispersal is the process by which seeds or fruits are scattered away from the parent plant to reduce competition for resources and to colonise new habitats. The main agents of dispersal are wind (e.g., light, winged fruits of maple; feathery fruits of dandelion), water (e.g., coconut), animals (e.g., hooked fruits like Xanthium that cling to fur; fleshy fruits eaten and seeds excreted), and self-dispersal mechanisms (e.g., explosive dehiscence in pea and Impatiens).

Artificial vegetative propagation refers to techniques developed by humans to propagate plants using vegetative parts deliberately, to multiply desired varieties quickly and reliably. Common methods include cutting (rose, sugarcane), layering (jasmine, strawberry), grafting (mango, apple — joining a scion onto a rootstock), and budding (citrus). These techniques are widely used in horticulture and agriculture because they produce true-to-type plants faster than seed propagation.

The flower is the reproductive structure of angiosperms, typically composed of four whorls: the calyx (sepals, which protect the flower bud), corolla (petals, which attract pollinators), androecium (stamens — the male organs, each consisting of an anther on a filament), and gynoecium or pistil (the female organ, consisting of stigma, style, and ovary containing ovules). Flowers may be complete (having all four whorls) or incomplete, and bisexual (hermaphrodite) or unisexual.

key_points: Plants reproduce asexually (no gametes, genetically identical offspring) and sexually (gamete fusion, genetically diverse offspring); both strategies are advantageous in different environments.
Vegetative propagation can occur naturally via runners (strawberry), bulbs (onion), corms (colocasia), rhizomes (ginger), tubers (potato), and stem buds (Bryophyllum leaves).
Artificial propagation methods — cutting, layering, grafting, budding — allow rapid multiplication of commercially valuable plants while preserving exact parental characteristics.
The stamen (anther + filament) is the male reproductive organ; the pistil/carpel (stigma + style + ovary) is the female reproductive organ of a flower.

Self-pollination occurs within the same plant; cross-pollination occurs between different plants of the same species and promotes genetic variation; insects, wind, water, and birds are key pollinating agents.
After pollination, the pollen grain germinates on the stigma, growing a pollen tube down the style to deliver the male gamete to the ovule inside the ovary.
Double fertilisation is unique to angiosperms: one male gamete fuses with the egg (forming zygote → embryo) and another fuses with polar nuclei (forming endosperm — the food store in the seed).
The ovule matures into the seed (containing embryo + food reserve + testa), while the ovary wall (pericarp) becomes the fruit wall.
Seed dispersal by wind, water, animals, and explosive mechanisms prevents overcrowding around the parent plant and enables plants to colonise new territories.
Conditions necessary for seed germination are water, warmth (appropriate temperature), and oxygen; light is not essential during germination itself but is required for subsequent photosynthesis.
Spore formation in ferns, mosses, and fungi is a simple form of asexual reproduction — spores are single-celled units that germinate directly into new individuals under suitable conditions.

8|biology|3_Reproduction_in_Animals.overview: Reproduction in animals ensures the survival of species across generations and exhibits far greater diversity in mechanism and strategy than in most other kingdoms of life. Animals reproduce either asexually — through processes such as binary fission, budding, and regeneration that involve a single parent — or sexually, through the production and fusion of specialised sex cells called gametes. Sexual reproduction is dominant in most animal groups and introduces genetic variation that is vital for adaptation and evolution. This chapter examines the contrasts between asexual and sexual reproduction, the distinction between internal and external fertilisation, the differences between oviparous (egg-laying) and viviparous (live-bearing) animals, and introduces the essentials of human reproduction including the roles of the male and female reproductive systems, fertilisation, development of the embryo, and birth.

key_concepts.definition: Asexual reproduction in animals involves a single parent and produces offspring that are genetically identical to the parent. Common methods include binary fission (splitting of a single parent into two, as in Amoeba and Paramecium), budding (outgrowth from the parent body that detaches as a new individual, as in Hydra), and regeneration (regrowth of lost body parts that can develop into complete new organisms, as in Planaria and starfish). Asexual reproduction is rapid but produces no genetic variation.

Sexual reproduction involves the production of haploid gametes — spermatozoa (male) and ova or eggs (female) — by meiosis, followed by their fusion (fertilisation) to produce a diploid zygote that develops into a new individual. This process introduces genetic recombination and variation among offspring, which is crucial for adaptation to changing environments. Sexual reproduction is the dominant mode in most animals, including all vertebrates.

External fertilisation occurs when eggs and sperm are released into the external environment — typically water — and fertilisation takes place outside the body of the female. This method is common in aquatic animals such as fish (e.g., rohu, trout) and amphibians (e.g., frogs). Because eggs are unprotected, large numbers are produced to compensate for high mortality rates. The aquatic medium helps prevent the gametes from drying out.

Internal fertilisation occurs when the male deposits sperm directly into the female's reproductive tract, where fertilisation takes place inside the female's body. This strategy is common in terrestrial animals (reptiles, birds, and mammals) and reduces dependence on a watery external environment. Internal fertilisation generally involves the production of fewer offspring, but each has a greater chance of survival due to parental protection during early development.

Oviparous animals are those that reproduce by laying eggs, in which the embryo develops outside the mother's body. The egg contains yolk as a food reserve for the developing embryo and is typically enclosed in a protective shell (as in birds and reptiles) or a jelly-like coat (as in frogs and fish). Birds are the most familiar oviparous animals; they incubate their eggs to maintain the temperature needed for embryonic development.

Viviparous animals give birth to live young that have developed inside the mother's uterus, nourished directly via the placenta throughout gestation. Most mammals, including humans, dogs, cows, and whales, are viviparous. The placenta allows exchange of nutrients, oxygen, and waste products between the maternal and foetal blood without the two bloods directly mixing. Viviparity greatly increases the chances of offspring survival compared to egg-laying.

The human male reproductive system consists of a pair of testes (which produce spermatozoa and the hormone testosterone), the epididymis (where sperm mature and are stored), the vas deferens (which carries sperm), accessory glands including the seminal vesicles, prostate gland, and Cowper's glands (which produce fluids that form semen), and the penis (which delivers semen into the female reproductive tract during copulation).

The human female reproductive system comprises a pair of ovaries (which produce ova and the hormones oestrogen and progesterone), the fallopian tubes or oviducts (which carry eggs from the ovary to the uterus and where fertilisation typically occurs), the uterus (where the fertilised egg implants and the foetus develops), the cervix (lower narrow part of the uterus), and the vagina (the birth canal). The menstrual cycle, regulated by hormones, prepares the uterus monthly for a potential pregnancy.

key_concepts.term: Fertilisation and Embryo Development in Humans
key_concepts.definition: Human fertilisation occurs when one spermatozoon penetrates and fuses with the ovum, typically in the fallopian tube, forming a zygote. The zygote undergoes repeated cell divisions (cleavage) as it travels to the uterus, forming a ball of cells called a blastocyst that implants in the thickened uterine wall (endometrium). The embryo is nourished first by the endometrium and then through the placenta, developing through embryonic and foetal stages over approximately 40 weeks (gestation period) before birth.

The placenta is a specialised disc-shaped organ that develops in the uterus during pregnancy, connecting the developing foetus to the mother's uterine wall. It allows oxygen and nutrients from the mother's blood to diffuse into the foetal blood, and carbon dioxide and metabolic wastes to pass in the opposite direction, all without the two bloodstreams mixing directly. The placenta also secretes hormones such as human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) and progesterone to maintain pregnancy.

key_points: Asexual reproduction (fission, budding, regeneration) involves one parent, is rapid, and produces genetically identical offspring; sexual reproduction involves two parents, gamete fusion, and produces genetically varied offspring.
Binary fission in Amoeba and Paramecium divides the parent into two equal daughter cells; budding in Hydra produces an outgrowth that eventually separates as a new individual.

External fertilisation (fish, frogs) occurs in water and requires large numbers of eggs to compensate for high losses; internal fertilisation (birds, mammals) occurs inside the female body and requires fewer eggs due to greater protection.
Oviparous animals (birds, reptiles, most fish) lay eggs in which the embryo develops externally; viviparous animals (most mammals) retain the embryo inside the uterus, nourished by the placenta.
In frogs, both eggs and sperm are released into water simultaneously during amplexus (the mating embrace), and fertilisation occurs externally — tadpoles hatch from the eggs.

In birds, internal fertilisation produces a shelled egg with albumen (white) for water and protein, yolk for nutrients, and a hard calcium carbonate shell for protection; the parent incubates the egg for warmth.
In humans, a single ovum is released from the ovary during ovulation (approximately day 14 of the menstrual cycle); it can be fertilised only within 12–24 hours.
The fertilised egg (zygote) divides to form an embryo that implants in the uterine wall; failure to implant results in the menstrual flow.
The placenta serves as the interface for exchange of nutrients, oxygen, and wastes between mother and foetus; the umbilical cord contains blood vessels connecting the foetus to the placenta.
Human gestation lasts approximately 9 months (40 weeks); at birth, uterine contractions expel the baby followed by the placenta (afterbirth).

Metamorphosis in frogs (tadpole → frog) and insects (caterpillar → pupa → butterfly) represents a dramatic change in body form during development — a feature absent in direct development seen in reptiles, birds, and mammals.

8|biology|4_Ecosystem.overview: An ecosystem is a self-sustaining unit of nature comprising a community of living organisms (the biotic component) interacting with each other and with the non-living physical environment (the abiotic component) in a defined space. The study of ecosystems — a branch of biology called ecology — examines how energy flows through communities via food chains and food webs, how matter cycles through the environment, and how the diverse populations of organisms maintain a dynamic balance that sustains life. From a tropical rainforest to a freshwater pond, every ecosystem demonstrates complex interdependencies: producers capture solar energy and form the base of all food relationships; consumers transfer energy up through trophic levels; and decomposers recycle nutrients back into the soil. Understanding ecosystem dynamics is essential for appreciating environmental issues such as habitat destruction, biodiversity loss, and climate change.

key_concepts.definition: An ecosystem is a functional unit of nature that encompasses all the living organisms (biotic components — plants, animals, microorganisms) in a given area, together with the non-living physical factors (abiotic components — sunlight, temperature, water, soil, air) that influence them, and the interactions among all these components. Ecosystems can range from a small pond or a forest patch to an entire ocean or the global biosphere. They are characterised by continuous energy flow and matter cycling.

Biotic components are the living parts of an ecosystem, including all plants, animals, fungi, and microorganisms. They are grouped into producers (autotrophs that make their own food through photosynthesis or chemosynthesis), consumers (heterotrophs that obtain energy by eating other organisms), and decomposers (organisms such as bacteria and fungi that break down dead organic matter and return nutrients to the soil). The interactions among biotic components — predation, competition, mutualism, parasitism — shape the structure of the community.

Abiotic components are the non-living physical and chemical factors of an ecosystem that directly influence the survival and distribution of organisms. These include solar radiation (energy source for producers), temperature (affects metabolic rates and distribution), water (essential for all life), soil composition (determines nutrient availability and rooting), air (provides CO₂ for photosynthesis and O₂ for respiration), and pH. The climate of a region largely determines which biotic communities can exist there.

Producers are organisms that manufacture their own organic food from inorganic raw materials using an external energy source. Green plants, algae, and photosynthetic bacteria are photoautotrophs — they use sunlight, carbon dioxide, and water to produce glucose via photosynthesis. Producers form the first trophic level and are the foundation of all food chains; all the energy available to consumers ultimately derives from producers. Without producers, no ecosystem can be sustained.

Consumers are organisms that cannot make their own food and must obtain energy by consuming other organisms. Primary consumers (herbivores, e.g., grasshoppers, deer) eat producers directly. Secondary consumers (carnivores or omnivores, e.g., frogs, foxes) eat primary consumers. Tertiary consumers eat secondary consumers, and so on. Omnivores like humans feed at multiple trophic levels. Each feeding level is called a trophic level, and energy is lost (as heat) at each transfer — typically only 10% is passed on.

Decomposers are microorganisms — primarily bacteria and fungi — that break down the complex organic molecules in dead organisms and animal waste into simpler inorganic compounds such as nitrates, phosphates, and carbon dioxide. This process of decomposition releases nutrients back into the soil and atmosphere, making them available once again for producers to absorb. Without decomposers, nutrients would remain locked in dead matter and ecosystems would collapse due to nutrient depletion.

A food chain is a linear sequence that shows the transfer of energy and nutrients from one organism to the next through feeding relationships, starting with a producer and ending with a top consumer. For example: grass → grasshopper → frog → snake → eagle. Each arrow represents the direction of energy flow. In any food chain, the number of links is usually limited to 4–5 because energy is lost at each trophic level, meaning insufficient energy remains to support further levels.

A food web is a complex network of interconnected food chains within an ecosystem, showing the real feeding relationships where most organisms eat more than one type of food and are eaten by more than one predator. Food webs are more accurate representations of energy flow in nature than simple food chains. They demonstrate the interdependence of species: the removal of one species can have cascading effects (trophic cascades) on many others throughout the web.

Energy flows through an ecosystem in one direction — from producers to successive consumers — and is not recycled (unlike matter). At each trophic level, approximately 90% of the energy is lost as heat through respiration, movement, and metabolic processes; only about 10% is incorporated into new biomass and is available to the next level. This is known as the 10% law or the law of ecological efficiency, and it explains why food chains rarely exceed 4–5 links and why there are always far more producers than top predators in any ecosystem.

Ecological balance (or ecological equilibrium) refers to the stable, self-regulating state of an ecosystem in which the populations of different species remain relatively stable over time due to natural checks and balances — predator-prey relationships, competition, and the availability of resources. Human activities such as deforestation, pollution, introduction of invasive species, and overexploitation of resources disrupt this balance, leading to loss of biodiversity, extinction of species, and degradation of ecosystem services.

key_points: An ecosystem consists of biotic components (producers, consumers, decomposers) and abiotic components (sunlight, water, temperature, soil, air) that interact with each other.
Producers (green plants) capture solar energy through photosynthesis and form the base of all food relationships in an ecosystem; without them, no consumer could survive.
Consumers are classified by trophic level: herbivores (primary consumers) eat plants; carnivores (secondary consumers) eat herbivores; top carnivores (tertiary consumers) have no natural predators.
Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) break down dead organic matter, releasing nutrients back into the soil and atmosphere — they are essential for nutrient cycling in any ecosystem.

A food chain shows a single pathway of energy transfer (e.g., grass → deer → tiger); a food web shows the interconnection of many food chains, better representing the real complexity of feeding relationships.

The 10% law states that only about 10% of energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next; this means that fewer individuals can be supported at each successive level, forming an ecological pyramid.

An ecological pyramid of numbers shows decreasing numbers of organisms at each higher trophic level; a pyramid of biomass shows decreasing total mass; a pyramid of energy always shows decreasing energy — it is never inverted.
Matter (carbon, nitrogen, water) cycles continuously within and between ecosystems — unlike energy, it is neither created nor destroyed but transformed and recycled.

A balanced ecosystem maintains stable populations through natural mechanisms: when prey populations increase, predator populations rise in response, which then reduces prey numbers, creating a cyclical regulation.
Human activities — deforestation, pollution, hunting, introduction of alien species — disrupt ecological balance, triggering population imbalances, habitat loss, and ultimately extinction of species.

Aquatic ecosystems include ponds, rivers, lakes, and oceans; terrestrial ecosystems include forests, grasslands, and deserts — each has characteristic producers, consumers, and decomposers adapted to local conditions.
Biodiversity (variety of species within an ecosystem) increases ecosystem resilience — more species means more alternative pathways for energy flow, so the loss of one species is less catastrophic.

8|biology|5_Endocrine_System.overview: The endocrine system is the body's chemical messaging network, responsible for coordinating a vast range of physiological processes — from growth and metabolism to reproduction and the response to stress — through the secretion of hormones directly into the bloodstream. Unlike the nervous system, which transmits electrical signals at high speed for rapid, short-term responses, the endocrine system works more slowly but produces effects that are widespread and long-lasting. This chapter examines the major endocrine glands of the human body — the pituitary, thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal glands, pancreatic islets, gonads, and others — identifying the hormones they produce and the specific functions those hormones regulate. A central concept is the negative feedback mechanism by which hormone levels in the blood are kept within precise limits, ensuring homeostasis.

key_concepts.definition: A hormone is a chemical messenger produced by an endocrine gland and secreted directly into the bloodstream, through which it is carried to a distant target organ or tissue where it exerts a specific regulatory effect. Hormones are effective in very small concentrations and act by binding to specific receptor proteins on or inside target cells. Unlike enzymes (which act locally), hormones travel through the circulation and can affect cells far from their site of production.

An endocrine gland (ductless gland) is a gland that secretes its products (hormones) directly into the surrounding interstitial fluid and thence into the blood, rather than through a duct. This distinguishes endocrine glands from exocrine glands (e.g., salivary glands, sweat glands) which secrete products through ducts to a surface. Examples of endocrine glands include the pituitary, thyroid, adrenal glands, pancreatic islets of Langerhans, and gonads.

The pituitary gland is a pea-sized gland situated at the base of the brain beneath the hypothalamus, often called the 'master gland' because it secretes hormones that regulate the activity of many other endocrine glands. Its anterior lobe produces growth hormone (GH), thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), and luteinising hormone (LH), among others. Its posterior lobe stores and releases antidiuretic hormone (ADH) and oxytocin, produced by the hypothalamus.

Growth hormone (GH), also called somatotropin, is produced by the anterior pituitary gland and stimulates the growth of bones, muscles, and other tissues by promoting protein synthesis and the uptake of amino acids. Insufficient GH during childhood results in pituitary dwarfism (abnormally short stature); excess GH during childhood causes gigantism (abnormal height); excess GH in adults causes acromegaly (enlargement of hands, feet, and facial features).

The thyroid gland is a butterfly-shaped gland in the neck that produces thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3), iodine-containing hormones that regulate the body's metabolic rate — the rate at which cells use oxygen and produce energy. Thyroxine also influences growth, development of the nervous system, and body temperature. Deficiency of thyroxine in adults causes hypothyroidism (goitre, sluggish metabolism, weight gain); excess causes hyperthyroidism (rapid metabolism, weight loss, bulging eyes in Grave's disease). Iodine in diet is essential for thyroxine synthesis.

The adrenal (suprarenal) glands are a pair of glands sitting atop each kidney, each consisting of an outer cortex and an inner medulla. The adrenal medulla produces adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline in response to stress, fear, or excitement — the 'fight-or-flight' hormones that increase heart rate, dilate airways, redirect blood to muscles, and mobilise glucose from glycogen stores, preparing the body for immediate action. The adrenal cortex produces corticosteroids including cortisol (stress response) and aldosterone (salt-water balance).

The pancreas is a mixed gland: its exocrine portions produce digestive enzymes (released through ducts into the small intestine), while its endocrine portions — the islets of Langerhans — produce hormones released directly into the blood. Beta cells of the islets secrete insulin, which lowers blood glucose by promoting glucose uptake into cells and stimulating glycogen synthesis in the liver. Alpha cells secrete glucagon, which raises blood glucose by stimulating glycogen breakdown in the liver. Together, insulin and glucagon maintain blood sugar homeostasis.

Diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder characterised by chronic high blood glucose (hyperglycaemia) due to insufficient insulin production (Type 1 diabetes, an autoimmune condition) or inability of cells to respond to insulin (Type 2 diabetes, often linked to obesity and lifestyle). Symptoms include excessive thirst, frequent urination, fatigue, and slow wound healing. Long-term complications affect the eyes, kidneys, nerves, and cardiovascular system. Type 1 requires insulin injections; Type 2 is managed by diet, exercise, and oral medication.

Negative feedback is the primary mechanism by which the endocrine system maintains hormone levels within a narrow optimal range (homeostasis). When the blood level of a hormone rises above the set point, it inhibits the gland (and often the pituitary or hypothalamus) from producing more of that hormone, causing its level to fall. When the level drops below the set point, inhibition is removed and production increases. This self-correcting loop ensures stable internal conditions — analogous to a thermostat regulating room temperature.

The gonads (testes in males, ovaries in females) are both reproductive organs and endocrine glands. The testes produce testosterone (an androgen), which stimulates the development of male secondary sexual characteristics (voice deepening, facial hair, muscle development) and regulates sperm production. The ovaries produce oestrogen and progesterone: oestrogen promotes female secondary sexual characteristics (breast development, widening of hips) and regulates the menstrual cycle; progesterone maintains the uterine lining during pregnancy.

key_points: The endocrine system uses hormones — chemical messengers secreted by ductless glands directly into the blood — to regulate growth, metabolism, reproduction, and response to stress over long timeframes.

The pituitary gland is the 'master gland' because it controls the activity of the thyroid, adrenal glands, and gonads via trophic hormones (TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH); the hypothalamus in turn controls the pituitary.
Growth hormone (GH) from the anterior pituitary regulates body growth; deficiency causes dwarfism; excess during growth causes gigantism; excess in adulthood causes acromegaly.

Thyroxine from the thyroid gland sets the body's metabolic rate and requires dietary iodine for its synthesis; deficiency causes goitre (enlarged thyroid) and slowed metabolism; excess causes hyperthyroidism.

The adrenal medulla secretes adrenaline in response to stress, triggering the fight-or-flight response: increased heart rate, elevated blood sugar, dilated airways, and redirected blood flow to muscles.

The islets of Langerhans in the pancreas produce insulin (lowers blood glucose — promotes cell uptake and glycogen storage) and glucagon (raises blood glucose — promotes glycogen breakdown); together they maintain blood glucose homeostasis.
Diabetes mellitus results from insufficient insulin action: Type 1 involves destruction of beta cells; Type 2 involves insulin resistance; both result in hyperglycaemia.

Negative feedback is the key control mechanism of the endocrine system — rising hormone levels inhibit further secretion, preventing dangerous excesses; falling levels remove inhibition, restoring production.
Gonadal hormones (testosterone, oestrogen, progesterone) drive puberty and control reproduction; their release is regulated by pituitary gonadotropins (FSH and LH).
The parathyroid glands (embedded in the thyroid) produce parathyroid hormone (PTH), which raises blood calcium levels by stimulating bone resorption and calcium reabsorption in the kidneys.
Unlike nerve signals (milliseconds), hormonal responses take seconds to hours to manifest but produce longer-lasting effects across multiple organ systems simultaneously.

8|biology|6_Adolescence.overview: Adolescence is the transitional phase of human development between childhood and adulthood, typically spanning the ages of 10 to 19 years, during which the body undergoes dramatic physical, hormonal, psychological, and social changes that prepare an individual for reproductive maturity. The biological onset of adolescence is marked by puberty — a period initiated and governed primarily by hormones secreted by the pituitary gland and the gonads (testes in males, ovaries in females). This chapter explores the physical changes that occur in boys and girls during puberty, the roles of testosterone and oestrogen in driving these changes, the importance of personal hygiene and nutrition during this period of rapid growth, and the emotional and behavioural changes that accompany the transition to adulthood. Understanding adolescence equips young people to manage this period healthily and confidently.

key_concepts.definition: Adolescence is the period of psychological and physical transition from childhood to adulthood, generally occurring between the ages of 10 and 19 years. It is characterised by rapid physical growth, the development of secondary sexual characteristics, cognitive maturation, and increasing emotional independence. Adolescence is not just a biological event — it also involves significant social and psychological development as individuals form their identity, establish peer relationships, and develop decision-making capacities.

Puberty is the biological process of sexual maturation during which the reproductive organs become functional and secondary sexual characteristics develop. It is triggered by the hypothalamus signalling the pituitary gland to release gonadotropins (FSH and LH), which in turn stimulate the gonads to produce sex hormones — testosterone in males and oestrogen in females. Puberty typically begins between ages 8–13 in girls and ages 9–14 in boys, with girls generally starting somewhat earlier.

Secondary sexual characteristics are physical features that distinguish males from females but are not directly part of the reproductive organs (which are primary sexual characteristics). They develop during puberty under the influence of sex hormones. In males: deepening of voice, growth of facial and body hair, broadening of shoulders, and increased muscle mass. In females: development of breasts, widening of hips, and growth of pubic and axillary hair. Both sexes experience a growth spurt and development of axillary and pubic hair.

Testosterone is the primary male sex hormone (androgen) produced by the Leydig cells of the testes, with small amounts also produced by the adrenal glands. It is responsible for the development of male secondary sexual characteristics during puberty — including deepening of the voice, growth of facial and body hair, broadening of the shoulders, increased muscle mass, and growth of the penis and testes. Testosterone also regulates sperm production (spermatogenesis) throughout adult life and influences mood and sex drive.

Oestrogen is the primary female sex hormone produced by the ovaries; it drives the development of female secondary sexual characteristics during puberty — breast development, widening of the hips, and growth of pubic and axillary hair. Oestrogen also regulates the menstrual cycle. Progesterone, also produced by the ovaries (by the corpus luteum after ovulation), prepares the uterine lining for implantation of a fertilised egg and maintains pregnancy. Both hormones fluctuate in a monthly cycle regulated by pituitary gonadotropins.

Menarche is the first occurrence of menstruation in a girl, signalling that the ovaries have begun releasing eggs and that the uterus is responding to hormones — a key milestone of female puberty. The menstrual cycle is a roughly 28-day cycle regulated by oestrogen and progesterone. It involves the maturation and release of an egg (ovulation, around day 14), preparation of the uterine lining for implantation, and if fertilisation does not occur, shedding of the uterine lining as menstrual flow.

The adolescent growth spurt is a period of accelerated increase in height and body mass that occurs during puberty, driven primarily by growth hormone and sex hormones. In girls, the growth spurt typically begins earlier (ages 10–14) than in boys (ages 12–16). During this period, long bones lengthen at the epiphyseal (growth) plates in response to hormonal signals. Adequate nutrition — especially calcium, protein, and vitamins — is critical during this phase to support healthy skeletal and muscular development.

During adolescence, increased activity of sebaceous (oil) glands and sweat glands — driven by rising sex hormones — leads to oilier skin, acne (pimples caused by blocked and infected pores), and greater body odour. Maintaining personal hygiene is therefore particularly important: regular bathing, washing the face with mild soap, changing into clean clothes daily, washing hair regularly, and using deodorant help prevent bacterial growth on the skin and body odour. Girls need specific guidance on menstrual hygiene using clean sanitary products and changing them regularly.

The rapid physical growth and hormonal changes of adolescence create increased nutritional demands. Adolescents need higher intakes of energy (carbohydrates and fats), protein (for muscle and tissue growth), calcium and vitamin D (for bone density — peak bone mass is established during adolescence), iron (especially in girls to compensate for menstrual blood loss), and vitamins including folate and vitamin C. A balanced diet rich in whole grains, pulses, dairy or calcium-rich alternatives, fruits, and vegetables supports healthy development.

Adolescence brings significant emotional and psychological changes alongside the physical ones. Young people may experience mood swings due to fluctuating hormone levels, heightened self-consciousness about body image, a stronger drive for independence from parents, and an increased need for peer acceptance. They also develop the capacity for abstract thinking, moral reasoning, and long-term planning (formal operational thought). Peer pressure, identity formation, and exploration of values and relationships are central challenges of this developmental stage.
