Understanding polygon angle sums enables solving complex geometric problems involving multi-sided figures and is foundational for more advanced topics in Euclidean geometry.

key_concepts.definition: A closed plane figure bounded by three or more straight line segments (sides). Named by number of sides: triangle (3), quadrilateral (4), pentagon (5), hexagon (6), heptagon (7), octagon (8), nonagon (9), decagon (10).

The sum of interior angles of an n-sided polygon = (n-2) × 180°. Derived by dividing the polygon into (n-2) triangles from one vertex. Triangle: 180°; Quadrilateral: 360°; Pentagon: 540°; Hexagon: 720°.
The sum of exterior angles of any convex polygon is always 360°, regardless of the number of sides. One exterior angle per vertex; exterior angle + interior angle = 180° at each vertex.

A polygon with all sides equal AND all angles equal. Each interior angle of a regular n-gon = (n-2)×180°/n. Each exterior angle = 360°/n. A regular hexagon has interior angles of 120° and exterior angles of 60°.
The number of diagonals in an n-sided polygon = n(n-3)/2. A quadrilateral has 2, a pentagon has 5, a hexagon has 9. Derived by counting connections between non-adjacent vertices.

A convex polygon has all interior angles < 180° — all diagonals lie inside. A concave (re-entrant) polygon has at least one interior angle > 180°. The exterior angle sum formula applies only to convex polygons.

A regular hexagon has all sides equal, all angles 120°, 6 lines of symmetry, rotational symmetry of order 6. Its long diagonal = 2 × side length. It can be divided into 6 equilateral triangles by joining the centre to all vertices.

In any polygon, the exterior angle at each vertex = 180° - interior angle. The number of sides n = 360°/exterior angle for regular polygons. If interior angle = 135°, exterior = 45°, n = 360°/45° = 8 (octagon).

laws_and_formulas.name: Interior Angle Sum
laws_and_formulas.description: Total sum of interior angles of an n-sided polygon.

laws_and_formulas.name: Each Interior Angle (Regular)
laws_and_formulas.description: Interior angle of a regular (equiangular) polygon with n sides.

laws_and_formulas.name: Number of Diagonals
laws_and_formulas.description: Total number of diagonals in an n-sided polygon.

key_points: Interior angle sum = (n-2) × 180°; exterior angle sum = 360° always.
At each vertex: interior angle + exterior angle = 180°.
For a regular polygon: each exterior angle = 360°/n; each interior angle = (n-2)×180°/n.
Finding n from interior angle: exterior angle = 180° - interior; n = 360°/exterior.
Pentagon: interior sum = 540°, each angle (regular) = 108°.
Hexagon: interior sum = 720°, each angle (regular) = 120°.
Number of diagonals in n-gon = n(n-3)/2.
A polygon can be divided into (n-2) non-overlapping triangles by diagonals from one vertex.
A regular polygon with n sides has n lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order n.
The only regular polygons that tile the plane perfectly: equilateral triangle, square, regular hexagon.

9|mathematics|Geometry_Quadrilaterals.overview: This chapter applies congruence and triangle properties to prove the key theorems about parallelograms — their defining properties and the conditions sufficient to identify one. The midpoint theorem for triangles extends to the concept of the median of a trapezium. Proofs are written in the standard statement-reason format, developing logical deductive skills that are central to mathematical reasoning at all levels.

key_concepts.definition: A parallelogram has: (1) opposite sides equal and parallel, (2) opposite angles equal, (3) consecutive angles supplementary, (4) diagonals that bisect each other. Each property can be proved using congruent triangles formed by a diagonal.

A quadrilateral is a parallelogram if any one of these holds: (a) both pairs of opposite sides are equal; (b) both pairs of opposite sides are parallel; (c) diagonals bisect each other; (d) one pair of sides is both equal and parallel.
The diagonals of a rectangle are equal. Proof: in rectangle ABCD, triangles ABD and BCA are congruent by SAS (AB = AB common side, ∠ABD = ∠BAC = 90°, BC = AD), so BD = AC.
The diagonals of a rhombus bisect each other at right angles. Proof: the four triangles formed by the diagonals are congruent (SSS — all sides equal), establishing perpendicularity and bisection.
The line joining the midpoints of the non-parallel sides (legs) of a trapezium is parallel to the parallel sides and equal to half their sum. This is the median (or midsegment) of the trapezium.
If three or more parallel lines cut two transversals, they intercept proportional segments. If the intercepts on one transversal are equal, the intercepts on any other transversal are also equal.

Most quadrilateral proofs use: (1) draw a diagonal to create two triangles; (2) prove the triangles congruent using a criterion; (3) conclude corresponding parts are equal by CPCTC. Identify the correct criterion from given information.

Area = base × height (perpendicular height, not the slant side). Parallelograms with the same base and between the same parallels have equal areas. Triangles with the same base and between same parallels have half the area.

laws_and_formulas.name: Area of Parallelogram
laws_and_formulas.description: Area equals the base multiplied by the perpendicular distance between the parallel sides.

laws_and_formulas.name: Trapezium Median
laws_and_formulas.formula: Median = (a + b)/2, parallel to both bases (a, b)
laws_and_formulas.description: The midsegment of a trapezium is the arithmetic mean of the two parallel sides.

key_points: Opposite sides equal and parallel; opposite angles equal; consecutive angles supplementary; diagonals bisect each other — the four properties of a parallelogram.
Any ONE of those four properties (plus quadrilateral shape) is sufficient to prove it's a parallelogram.
Rectangle: diagonals equal; rhombus: diagonals perpendicular bisectors of each other; square: both.
Proof technique: draw diagonal → create two triangles → prove congruent → CPCTC.
The median of a trapezium = (sum of parallel sides)/2, parallel to both.
Intercept theorem: equal intercepts on one transversal → equal intercepts on any transversal (if cut by equal parallel lines).
Area of parallelogram = base × height (height is perpendicular, not the side).
Parallelogram on the same base between same parallels: same area as the rectangle.
Triangle on same base between same parallels = half the parallelogram's area.
A kite has diagonals perpendicular but is NOT a parallelogram — only one diagonal is bisected.

9|mathematics|Statistics_Understanding_a.overview: Statistics is the science of collecting, organising, analysing, and interpreting numerical data. Raw data from surveys, experiments, and records must first be understood and recognised before analysis can begin. This chapter introduces the vocabulary of statistics, types of data, and the concept of a statistical variable. Building a clear conceptual framework for what data is and how it arises is the foundation for all subsequent statistical methods.

key_concepts.definition: A collection of numerical or categorical facts and figures gathered through observation, measurement, or survey. Raw data is unorganised; organised data has been sorted, grouped, or tabulated.

Data collected directly by the investigator from original sources for a specific purpose. Examples: conducting a survey, measuring heights of students, timing an experiment. More relevant but time-consuming and expensive to collect.

Data collected by someone else for a different purpose, now being reused. Examples: census data, government statistics, published research. Readily available but may not perfectly fit the current purpose.

A characteristic that can take different values. Quantitative (numerical) variables: discrete (only specific values, e.g., number of students) or continuous (any value in a range, e.g., height). Qualitative (categorical) variables: colour, gender, category.

Discrete data can only take specific isolated values (usually whole numbers): number of cars, number of students. Continuous data can take any value within a range: height, weight, temperature. This distinction affects how data is organised and displayed.

Population: the entire group being studied. Sample: a subset of the population selected for study. Sampling makes large-scale data collection feasible. A representative sample gives results that generalise to the population.
A systematic method of collecting data. Steps: define the problem → design the questionnaire → collect data → organise → analyse → interpret. Bias in any step compromises the entire analysis.
Data in its original, unorganised form as collected. A list of exam scores before sorting or grouping is raw data. The first task in statistics is to organise raw data into a more useful form.

key_points: Statistics cycle: collect → organise → analyse → interpret → present.
Primary data: collected firsthand (surveys, experiments); secondary data: collected by others (census, records).
Discrete variables: countable specific values (integer counts); continuous variables: measurable (any value in a range).
Population = whole group; sample = representative subset selected for practical study.
A good sample is random, representative, and of sufficient size.
Bias occurs when data collection systematically favours certain outcomes.
Quantitative data has numerical values; qualitative data has category labels.
Raw data must be sorted/organised before any meaningful statistics can be computed.
The purpose of the study determines what data to collect and what type of analysis is appropriate.
Statistical literacy is essential for critically evaluating claims in science, media, and public policy.

9|mathematics|Statistics_Tabulation.overview: Tabulation is the process of organising raw data into tables for easy reading and analysis. Frequency tables summarise how often each value (or range of values) occurs in a dataset. Cumulative frequency tables track running totals. Effective tabulation is the critical bridge between raw data and the graphical and numerical analyses that follow. A well-constructed table reveals patterns invisible in raw data.

key_concepts.definition: The number of times a particular value or event occurs in a dataset. A frequency distribution table lists all values (or classes) alongside their frequencies. The sum of all frequencies = total number of observations (n).
A quick method to count frequencies: marks are made in groups of five (four vertical, one diagonal) to allow easy counting. Tally marks prevent errors in counting long lists.
A table with columns for: class interval (or value), tally, frequency, and (optionally) cumulative frequency and relative frequency. Organises raw data into a compact and informative format.
The running total of frequencies: the cumulative frequency up to a class = sum of all frequencies up to and including that class. Used to determine median and draw ogives.

The fraction or percentage of the total: relative frequency = frequency/total frequency. Expressed as a decimal or percentage. Relative frequency distributions allow comparison between datasets of different sizes.
A table with two variables — one along rows, one along columns. Cell entries show the frequency for each combination. For example, gender vs. preferred subject creates a bivariate frequency table.
Lists each distinct value with its frequency. Suitable for discrete data with few distinct values. For example, number of siblings: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 with respective frequencies.
For continuous or widely spread discrete data, values are grouped into intervals. Class width = (max - min)/number of classes (typically 5-10 classes). Each class has a lower and upper limit.

laws_and_formulas.name: Total Frequency
laws_and_formulas.description: The sum of all frequencies in a distribution equals the total number of data points.

laws_and_formulas.name: Relative Frequency
laws_and_formulas.description: Frequency of a class divided by total observations; expresses proportion.

key_points: Always verify: Σf = n (sum of all frequencies = total data points).
Tally marks are used to manually count and record frequencies from raw data.
Cumulative frequency (CF) for a class = frequency of that class + sum of all previous frequencies.
The last cumulative frequency entry = total frequency n.
Relative frequency converts counts to proportions — useful for comparing datasets of different sizes.
Class intervals should be equal width (in most cases) and non-overlapping.
The convention for class boundaries: e.g., 10-20 means 10 ≤ x < 20 (lower inclusive, upper exclusive).
Mid-value (class mark) of an interval = (lower limit + upper limit)/2.
A frequency table is the starting point for all subsequent statistical analysis and graphing.
Discrete data: ungrouped table; continuous or large-range data: grouped frequency table.

9|mathematics|Statistics_Grouped_frequen.overview: When data spans a wide range, grouping into class intervals creates a grouped frequency distribution. This chapter focuses on constructing grouped frequency tables, choosing appropriate class widths, computing class marks, and calculating the mean from grouped data. Grouped frequency distributions are essential for summarising large datasets and are the basis for histograms, frequency polygons, and cumulative frequency curves (ogives).

key_concepts.definition: A range of values grouped together: e.g., 20-30 means values from 20 up to (but not including) 30. The width of each class interval is constant in most standard distributions.
The representative value of a class interval: class mark = (lower class limit + upper class limit)/2. Used as the assumed value of all data points in that class when computing the mean.

The true boundaries accounting for continuous data: lower boundary = lower limit - 0.5; upper boundary = upper limit + 0.5. This ensures no gaps between consecutive class intervals for continuous data.
Upper class boundary - lower class boundary (= upper limit - lower limit for equal-width intervals). A consistent class width makes graphical representation straightforward.
Mean = Σ(f × x) / Σf, where x = class mark and f = frequency of that class. This is an approximation since the exact data values within each class are replaced by the class mark.
The class interval with the highest frequency. The mode of grouped data is estimated to be in this class. For a histogram, the modal class corresponds to the tallest bar.
The class in which the (n/2)th observation falls. Found from the cumulative frequency table: locate the first class whose cumulative frequency exceeds n/2.
Frequency density = frequency / class width. Used on the y-axis of a histogram when class widths are unequal, ensuring bars with wider classes don't appear disproportionately large.

laws_and_formulas.name: Mean of Grouped Data
laws_and_formulas.formula: x̄ = Σ(f · x) / Σf (x = class mark)
laws_and_formulas.description: Weighted mean using class marks as representative values for each class interval.

laws_and_formulas.name: Class Mark
laws_and_formulas.description: The midpoint of a class interval, used as the representative value in mean calculation.

key_points: Class intervals should be equal-width (typically 5 or 10 units wide), non-overlapping, and exhaustive.
Class mark = (lower + upper limit)/2 — used as the representative value for the class.
Mean of grouped data: Σ(f×x)/Σf — this is an estimate, not exact.
The modal class is the class with the highest frequency.
To find the median class: locate first class where cumulative frequency > n/2.
Class boundaries: subtract 0.5 from lower limit, add 0.5 to upper limit (for continuous data with whole number limits).
Number of classes: typically 5-10; too few loses detail, too many becomes unwieldy.
For unequal class widths, use frequency density (not frequency) on the histogram's y-axis.
The sum of all relative frequencies = 1 (or 100%).
Grouped frequency distributions lose individual data values — a trade-off for manageability.

9|mathematics|Statistics_Drawing_a_histo.overview: A histogram is a bar chart for continuous grouped data where bars are adjacent (no gaps) and the area of each bar is proportional to the frequency. This chapter covers constructing histograms from grouped frequency distributions and drawing frequency polygons by connecting midpoints of histogram bars. These graphical tools reveal the shape and spread of a distribution at a glance — uniform, skewed, or bell-shaped.

key_concepts.definition: A graphical representation of a grouped frequency distribution using adjacent rectangles. The x-axis shows the class intervals; the y-axis shows frequency (or frequency density for unequal widths). No gaps between bars distinguish it from a bar chart.

Bar charts represent discrete or categorical data with gaps between bars. Histograms represent continuous grouped data with no gaps. In a histogram, the area of each bar represents frequency; in a bar chart, height represents frequency.

Mark class intervals on x-axis (using class boundaries, not limits, for continuous data). Mark frequencies (or frequency densities) on y-axis. Draw adjacent rectangles of equal width with heights equal to frequencies. Label axes and give a title.

Formed by connecting the midpoints (class marks) of the tops of histogram bars with straight lines. The polygon is closed by extending lines to the x-axis one class-width before and after the data range. Shows the shape of the distribution.

Symmetric (bell-shaped): frequencies increase then decrease symmetrically. Positively skewed: long tail to the right. Negatively skewed: long tail to the left. Uniform: all bars approximately equal height.
The modal class is the class with the tallest bar. The mode can be estimated more precisely using the heights of adjacent bars (not typically required at this level).
In a histogram, frequency = bar height × bar width (= area if width = 1). Total area of all bars = total frequency n. For frequency density y-axis: frequency = frequency density × class width.

A graph of cumulative frequency vs. upper class boundary. An 'S-shaped' curve. Used to read off the median, quartiles, and percentiles graphically. Connecting cumulative frequency points with a smooth curve gives the ogive.

key_points: Histogram: no gaps between bars — distinguishes it from bar chart.
x-axis: class intervals (boundaries); y-axis: frequency (or frequency density).
All bars have equal width for standard distributions; area ∝ frequency.
Frequency polygon: connect midpoints of bar tops; close with extensions to x-axis.
Modal class = tallest bar in the histogram.
For unequal class widths: use frequency density = frequency/class width on y-axis.
The total area of all histogram bars = total frequency n.
Ogive: plot (upper boundary, cumulative frequency); join with smooth curve.
Read median from ogive: find where cumulative frequency = n/2 and read off the x-value.
Histogram shape reveals distribution: symmetric, positively skewed, negatively skewed, or uniform.

9|mathematics|Mensuration_Area_and_perim.overview: Mensuration deals with the measurement of geometric figures — their perimeters (boundary lengths) and areas (enclosed surface). This chapter consolidates formulas for common 2D shapes: triangles, quadrilaterals, and regular polygons. Heron's formula allows area computation for any triangle given its three sides. These measurements are used in architecture, land surveying, fabric estimation, and countless practical applications.

key_concepts.definition: The total length of the boundary of a 2D figure. Measured in linear units (cm, m). For polygons, perimeter = sum of all side lengths.
Area = ½ × base × height. The height is the perpendicular distance from the base to the opposite vertex. Any side can serve as the base.
For a triangle with sides a, b, c: s = (a+b+c)/2 (semi-perimeter); Area = √[s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)]. Allows area computation without knowing the height — useful when only three sides are given.
Rectangle: l×b; Square: a²; Parallelogram: base×height; Rhombus: ½×d₁×d₂; Trapezium: ½(a+b)×h; General quadrilateral via diagonal: ½×d×(h₁+h₂).
Regular polygon with n sides of length a: Area = (n/4) × a² × cot(π/n). For a regular hexagon: Area = (3√3/2)a². Can also divide into n isosceles triangles from the centre.
A path of width w around a rectangle of dimensions l × b: outer rectangle is (l+2w) × (b+2w). Area of path = outer area - inner area. Common in problems about borders, frames, and walkways.

Complex shapes are decomposed into simpler shapes (rectangles, triangles, semicircles). Total area = sum of component areas (or larger area minus cut-out area). Sketch clearly and identify each component.
1 m = 100 cm → 1 m² = 10,000 cm². 1 km = 1000 m → 1 km² = 10⁶ m². 1 hectare = 10,000 m². Always verify units before computing and express the final answer in the required unit.

laws_and_formulas.name: Heron's Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Computes triangle area from three sides without needing height.

laws_and_formulas.name: Area of Trapezium
laws_and_formulas.description: Where a and b are the parallel sides and h is the perpendicular height.

laws_and_formulas.name: Area of Rhombus
laws_and_formulas.description: Area in terms of diagonals d₁ and d₂.

key_points: Heron's formula needs only the three side lengths — no height required.
Semi-perimeter s = (a+b+c)/2; plug into √[s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)].
Area of parallelogram = base × perpendicular height (not the slant side).
Trapezium area: ½ × (sum of parallel sides) × perpendicular height.
Rhombus area: ½ × d₁ × d₂ (from diagonals).
Path problems: outer area - inner area gives path/border area.
Break composite figures into recognisable shapes and sum areas.
1 hectare = 10,000 m² — the standard land area unit.
For a right triangle with legs a, b: Area = ½ab (no height formula needed).
Always draw and label a figure before applying any formula.

9|mathematics|Mensuration_Circle_Area_an.overview: The circle is a perfectly symmetric curve, and its measurements — circumference and area — involve the constant π (pi). This chapter covers circumference, area, arc length, sector area, and the geometry of rings (annuli). The relationship between a circle's dimensions and π appears throughout mathematics, physics, and engineering. Students also apply these formulas to composite figures involving circular and rectilinear parts.

key_concepts.definition: Radius (r): distance from centre to circumference. Diameter (d = 2r): longest chord. Chord: a line segment joining two points on the circle. Arc: a portion of the circumference. Sector: a pie-slice region bounded by two radii and an arc. Segment: region between a chord and the arc.
The perimeter of a circle: C = 2πr = πd. π ≈ 22/7 ≈ 3.14159... The circumference is the distance around the circle — what you'd measure by rolling the circle once along a flat surface.
A = πr². Derived by dividing the circle into infinitely thin triangular slices rearranged into a rectangle of height r and width πr. This is why Area = πr × r = πr².
For a sector with central angle θ (in degrees): Arc length = (θ/360°) × 2πr; Sector area = (θ/360°) × πr². The sector is a fraction θ/360 of the full circle.
The region between two concentric circles with radii R (outer) and r (inner): Area = π(R² - r²) = π(R+r)(R-r). Used for hollow cylinders, pipes, and circular borders.
Area of minor segment = Area of sector - Area of triangle formed by the two radii and the chord. Requires the central angle θ and the radius r.
Perimeter = 2r + arc length = 2r + (θ/360°) × 2πr. Includes the two radii and the arc — the total boundary of the sector.
The ratio of circumference to diameter for any circle: π = C/d. Irrational and transcendental: π ≈ 3.14159265... For calculations: use π = 22/7 (when radius is a multiple of 7) or π = 3.14.

laws_and_formulas.name: Circumference
laws_and_formulas.description: Perimeter of a circle with radius r or diameter d.

laws_and_formulas.name: Area of Circle
laws_and_formulas.description: Area enclosed by a circle of radius r.

laws_and_formulas.name: Arc Length
laws_and_formulas.description: Length of arc subtended by central angle θ at radius r.

laws_and_formulas.name: Sector Area
laws_and_formulas.description: Area of a sector with central angle θ and radius r.

key_points: C = 2πr; A = πr² — the two most fundamental circle formulas.
π = 22/7 when radius is a multiple of 7; π = 3.14 for other cases.
Arc length and sector area are θ/360 fractions of the full circle's circumference and area.
Annulus area = π(R² - r²) = π(R+r)(R-r).
Segment area = sector area - triangle area.
Semicircle: half circumference + diameter = πr + 2r (perimeter); area = πr²/2.
Quadrant: ¼ of circle; area = πr²/4; perimeter = πr/2 + 2r.
For composite figures: add/subtract circular and rectilinear areas as needed.
Speed problems with wheels: distance in one rotation = circumference = 2πr.
π is irrational — its decimal expansion neither terminates nor repeats; we use approximations.

9|mathematics|Mensuration_Surface_area_a.overview: Surface area and volume calculations extend to cones, spheres, and hemispheres at Class 9 level, in addition to cuboids and cylinders reviewed from Class 8. These formulas are central to packaging engineering, architecture, fluid dynamics, and manufacturing. Understanding how area scales as the square and volume as the cube of linear dimensions has profound implications for biology, physics, and engineering design.

key_concepts.definition: A solid with a circular base and an apex. Slant height l = √(r² + h²). Curved surface area = πrl; Total surface area = πr(r + l); Volume = ⅓πr²h. The ⅓ factor arises because three cones fill one cylinder.
A perfectly round 3D solid. All points on its surface are equidistant from the centre (radius r). Surface area = 4πr²; Volume = (4/3)πr³.
Half a sphere. Curved surface area = 2πr²; Total surface area (with flat circular face) = 2πr² + πr² = 3πr²; Volume = (2/3)πr³.
Curved surface area = 2πrh; Total surface area = 2πr(r+h); Volume = πr²h. The 'can' shape — two circles and a rectangle (when unrolled).
Total surface area = 2(lb + bh + lh); Volume = l×b×h; Diagonal = √(l²+b²+h²). A rectangular box.
l = √(r² + h²) by Pythagoras — the distance from the apex to any point on the base circumference. Not the same as the vertical height h.

A cone, hemisphere, and cylinder with the same radius r and height 2r have volumes in the ratio 1:2:3. Volume of cone : volume of hemisphere : volume of cylinder = ⅓πr²(2r) : (2/3)πr³ : πr²(2r) = 1:2:3.

key_concepts.term: Scaling of Surface Area and Volume
key_concepts.definition: If all linear dimensions are scaled by factor k: surface area scales by k² and volume by k³. Doubling the radius of a sphere quadruples its surface area and octuples its volume.

laws_and_formulas.name: Cone Volume
laws_and_formulas.description: Volume of a cone with base radius r and vertical height h.

laws_and_formulas.name: Cone Curved Surface Area
laws_and_formulas.description: Lateral surface area of a cone using slant height l.

laws_and_formulas.name: Sphere Surface Area
laws_and_formulas.description: Total surface area of a sphere of radius r.

laws_and_formulas.name: Sphere Volume
laws_and_formulas.description: Volume of a sphere of radius r.

key_points: Cone: V = ⅓πr²h; CSA = πrl; TSA = πrl + πr² = πr(l+r).
Slant height of cone: l = √(r² + h²) — Pythagoras in the cone's axial cross-section.
Sphere: SA = 4πr²; V = (4/3)πr³.
Hemisphere: CSA = 2πr²; TSA = 3πr²; V = (2/3)πr³.
Cone : hemisphere : cylinder (same r, height 2r) volumes = 1:2:3.
Surface area ∝ r²; volume ∝ r³ — why large animals struggle with heat loss (small SA:V ratio).
For a solid sphere placed in water: volume of water displaced = volume of sphere.
Converting between solids: melting a sphere into a cone — equate volumes to find new dimensions.
TSA of cone includes the base circle: TSA = πrl + πr² = πr(l + r).
1 litre = 1000 cm³; always check units when volume is given in litres.

9|mathematics|Trigonometry_Trigonometric.overview: Trigonometry is the study of relationships between angles and side lengths in triangles. The three primary trigonometric ratios — sine, cosine, and tangent — are defined for acute angles in right-angled triangles. These ratios, along with their reciprocals, are the building blocks of all of trigonometry. They are applied in navigation, engineering, physics, and any field involving angles and distances that cannot be measured directly.

key_concepts.term: Trigonometric Ratios for Acute Angle θ
key_concepts.definition: In a right-angled triangle with angle θ: sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse; cos θ = adjacent/hypotenuse; tan θ = opposite/adjacent. The hypotenuse is always the longest side, opposite the right angle.

Cosecant: csc θ = 1/sin θ = hypotenuse/opposite. Secant: sec θ = 1/cos θ = hypotenuse/adjacent. Cotangent: cot θ = 1/tan θ = adjacent/opposite. These are useful when the primary ratios are in the denominator.

key_concepts.term: tan θ in terms of sin and cos
key_concepts.definition: tan θ = sin θ / cos θ. This follows directly from the definitions: (opp/hyp) ÷ (adj/hyp) = opp/adj = tan θ.
sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 (from Pythagoras: opp² + adj² = hyp²). Also: 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ and 1 + cot²θ = csc²θ. These are fundamental identities used extensively in simplification.
For complementary angles (θ and 90°-θ): sin θ = cos(90°-θ); cos θ = sin(90°-θ); tan θ = cot(90°-θ). This is because swapping the opposite and adjacent sides swaps sin and cos.
sin 30° = ½; cos 30° = √3/2; tan 30° = 1/√3. sin 45° = cos 45° = 1/√2; tan 45° = 1. sin 60° = √3/2; cos 60° = ½; tan 60° = √3. These are derived from equilateral and isosceles right triangles.
Tables of sine, cosine, and tangent values for angles from 0° to 90° in steps of 1° (or smaller). Used before calculators — students learn to read them accurately and use mean differences.
Given an angle and one side, find another side: if hypotenuse and angle are known, opposite = hyp × sin θ, adjacent = hyp × cos θ. If one leg and angle are known, hypotenuse = opp/sin θ.

laws_and_formulas.name: Pythagorean Identity
laws_and_formulas.description: Fundamental trigonometric identity derived from Pythagoras' theorem.

laws_and_formulas.name: tan Identity
laws_and_formulas.formula: tan θ = sin θ / cos θ; 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ
laws_and_formulas.description: tan in terms of sin and cos; the second Pythagorean identity.

laws_and_formulas.name: Complementary Angle Relations
laws_and_formulas.formula: sin θ = cos(90° - θ); tan θ = cot(90° - θ)
laws_and_formulas.description: Trigonometric ratios of complementary angles are interchanged (sin↔cos, tan↔cot).

key_points: SOH-CAH-TOA: Sin = Opposite/Hypotenuse, Cos = Adjacent/Hypotenuse, Tan = Opposite/Adjacent.
sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 always (for any angle θ) — the most important trigonometric identity.
tan θ = sin θ / cos θ; cot θ = cos θ / sin θ.
Reciprocals: csc = 1/sin, sec = 1/cos, cot = 1/tan.
Complementary: sin θ = cos(90°-θ); this is why 'co' in cosine means 'complement'.
All trig ratios are positive for 0° < θ < 90°.
Given ratio, use Pythagoras to find the other side and compute remaining ratios.
Trigonometry applies to ANY right-angled triangle — not just special angles.
The angle of elevation/depression is always measured from the horizontal.

9|mathematics|Trigonometry_Trigonometric.overview: The trigonometric ratios of standard angles (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°) are exact values derived from special triangles. These exact values are essential for solving trigonometric problems without a calculator and are encountered repeatedly throughout mathematics and physics. This chapter also covers how the ratios change as the angle increases from 0° to 90°, providing intuition for the behavior of the trigonometric functions.

key_concepts.definition: The exact values: sin 0°=0, sin 30°=1/2, sin 45°=1/√2, sin 60°=√3/2, sin 90°=1. cos follows the reverse order. tan 0°=0, tan 30°=1/√3, tan 45°=1, tan 60°=√3, tan 90°=undefined.
From an equilateral triangle with side 2: drop an altitude to create two 30°-60°-90° triangles. Sides are 2, 1, √3. So: sin 30° = 1/2, cos 30° = √3/2, sin 60° = √3/2, cos 60° = 1/2.
From an isosceles right triangle with legs of length 1: hypotenuse = √2. So: sin 45° = 1/√2, cos 45° = 1/√2, tan 45° = 1.
sin 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° = √0/2, √1/2, √2/2, √3/2, √4/2 = 0, 1/2, 1/√2, √3/2, 1. This neat pattern using √0 through √4 divided by 2 is a reliable mnemonic.
tan 90° = sin 90°/cos 90° = 1/0 = undefined. As θ → 90°, the adjacent side → 0, so the ratio opposite/adjacent → ∞. The tangent function has no value at 90°.
As θ increases from 0° to 90°: sin increases (0 → 1); cos decreases (1 → 0); tan increases (0 → ∞). This mirrors how the opposite and adjacent sides change relative to the hypotenuse.
Heights and distances involving 30°, 45°, 60° angles can be solved exactly. Example: a 10m ladder at 60° to ground: height reached = 10 sin 60° = 10 × √3/2 = 5√3 m.

key_concepts.term: Trigonometric Identities Verified at Standard Angles
key_concepts.definition: The identity sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 can be verified: at 30°: (1/2)² + (√3/2)² = 1/4 + 3/4 = 1 ✓. At 45°: (1/√2)² + (1/√2)² = 1/2 + 1/2 = 1 ✓.

laws_and_formulas.name: Standard Angle Values
laws_and_formulas.description: Exact sine values for the five standard angles — cosine values are in reverse order.

key_points: Memorise: sin 0°=0, sin 30°=½, sin 45°=1/√2, sin 60°=√3/2, sin 90°=1.
cos values are the reverse: cos 0°=1, cos 30°=√3/2, cos 45°=1/√2, cos 60°=½, cos 90°=0.
30°-60°-90° triangle: sides in ratio 1:√3:2.
45°-45°-90° triangle: sides in ratio 1:1:√2.
tan 90° is undefined (division by zero — cos 90° = 0).
sin increases and cos decreases monotonically from 0° to 90°.
1/√2 = √2/2 ≈ 0.707; √3/2 ≈ 0.866 — useful approximations.
Verify identities at standard angles as a quick check.

9|mathematics|Trigonometry_Simple_2-D_pr.overview: Trigonometric ratios are applied to real-world problems involving heights, distances, and angles that cannot be measured directly. The angle of elevation is measured upward from the horizontal; the angle of depression is measured downward. This chapter develops the skill of translating verbal descriptions into right-angled triangles and solving them using the appropriate trigonometric ratio. These applications span surveying, navigation, construction, and astronomy.

key_concepts.definition: The angle formed between the horizontal line of sight and the line of sight directed upward to an object. Always measured from the horizontal. Example: looking up at the top of a tower — the angle of elevation is the angle between the horizontal and the line to the top.

The angle formed between the horizontal and the line of sight directed downward to an object. Example: from the top of a cliff, looking down at a boat — the angle of depression is measured from the horizontal downward.

The angle of elevation from point A to point B = the angle of depression from point B to point A (alternate interior angles with the horizontal as the transversal). This symmetry simplifies many problems.

Draw a clear diagram: identify the right angle, label the known angle (elevation or depression), and label the known and unknown sides. Decide which ratio to use: if hypotenuse and angle are known, use sin or cos; if two legs are involved, use tan.

The length of a shadow cast by a vertical object is related to the sun's elevation angle: tan(elevation) = height of object / length of shadow. Two similar triangles can relate shadows of two different objects.

Some problems involve two angles of elevation (or depression) to the same object from different distances. Set up two equations using tan, express the height in terms of both equations, and solve simultaneously.
An instrument used to measure angles of elevation or depression. In surveying, it is combined with a theodolite. Understanding angle measurement connects classroom trigonometry to field applications.

Height of tower/building from angle of elevation at known distance; distance between two objects from a point; height of cliff with angle of depression to a boat; height of object from two points on the same side.

laws_and_formulas.name: Height from Elevation Angle
laws_and_formulas.description: Height of an object at distance d with angle of elevation θ (when the observer and base are at the same level).

laws_and_formulas.name: Distance from Elevation
laws_and_formulas.description: Horizontal distance from observer to base of object, given height h and angle of elevation θ.

key_points: Angle of elevation: measured upward from horizontal; angle of depression: measured downward from horizontal.
Elevation from A to B = Depression from B to A (alternate angles).
tan θ = opposite/adjacent — the most used ratio in height-distance problems.
Always draw a labelled diagram before writing any equation.
Standard setup: tan(angle) = height / horizontal distance → height = distance × tan(angle).
For two-position problems: write two tan equations, equate heights, solve for distance or height.
Shadow problem: tan(sun's elevation) = height of pole / shadow length.
When the observer has height, subtract or add as appropriate to the measured height.
Use exact values (√3, 1/√3) when angles are 30°, 45°, 60°; use tables/calculator for others.
Check the answer: does the height seem reasonable given the angle and distance?

9|mathematics|Coordinate_Geometry_Depend.overview: Coordinate geometry establishes an algebraic framework for studying geometric figures. This chapter introduces the concept of variables in relationships — how one quantity (dependent variable) changes in response to another (independent variable). Understanding this cause-and-effect structure of variables is the conceptual foundation for graphing functions and interpreting graphs. These ideas connect algebra, geometry, statistics, and science.

key_concepts.definition: The variable whose value is freely chosen or controlled. It is the input variable. On a graph, it is plotted on the x-axis (horizontal axis). Example: time is independent when studying distance covered over time.

The variable whose value depends on the independent variable. It is the output — determined by the relationship once the independent variable is set. Plotted on the y-axis (vertical axis). Example: distance depends on time.

A relationship where each value of the independent variable gives exactly one value of the dependent variable. Expressed as y = f(x). Examples: y = 2x + 3; y = x²; C = 2πr (circumference as function of radius).
A systematic way to record (x, y) pairs for a relationship. Choose several values of x, calculate the corresponding y, and list them. These ordered pairs are then plotted to produce the graph.

Cost depends on quantity purchased: cost = dependent, quantity = independent. Distance depends on time: distance = dependent, time = independent. Always ask 'Which quantity causes the other to change?'

Temperature and altitude: as altitude (independent) increases, temperature (dependent) decreases. Sales and advertising spend. Many scientific laws express dependent variables as functions of independent variables.
Domain: the set of all possible values of the independent variable (x). Range: the set of all resulting values of the dependent variable (y). For y = √x: domain is x ≥ 0; range is y ≥ 0.
Correctly identifying independent and dependent variables determines: which axis to plot on, how to interpret the graph's slope and intercept, and how to read predictions from the graph.

key_points: Independent variable = input = x-axis; dependent variable = output = y-axis.
The dependent variable changes because of the independent variable.
To identify: ask 'Which variable is freely chosen?' — that is independent.
y = f(x) notation: f is the rule, x is the independent, y is the dependent variable.
A table of values lists selected (x, y) pairs before plotting.
The relationship must be one-to-one or many-to-one (not one-to-many) for it to be a function.
Real science: distance = speed × time; here time is independent, distance is dependent.
Domain restrictions arise from: division by zero, square roots of negative numbers, physical constraints.
Reading a graph: choose an x-value, read the corresponding y — you get the dependent value.
This framework underpins all of calculus: derivatives measure how the dependent variable changes per unit change in independent variable.

9|mathematics|Coordinate_Geometry_Ordere.overview: The Cartesian coordinate system, developed by René Descartes, assigns a unique pair of numbers (x, y) to every point in the plane. This bridges algebra and geometry — every algebraic relationship can be visualised as a curve in the plane, and every geometric curve can be described by an equation. This chapter covers plotting points, identifying quadrants, and the distance and midpoint formulas.

key_concepts.definition: A flat surface with two perpendicular number lines: the x-axis (horizontal) and y-axis (vertical) intersecting at the origin O(0,0). Every point is uniquely described by an ordered pair (x, y) — the x-coordinate (abscissa) and y-coordinate (ordinate).
A pair (x, y) where the order matters: (3, 2) ≠ (2, 3). The first number is always the x-coordinate, the second the y-coordinate. Ordered pairs are used to locate points precisely in the plane.
The axes divide the plane into four quadrants. Quadrant I: (+, +); Quadrant II: (-, +); Quadrant III: (-, -); Quadrant IV: (+, -). Points on the axes (not at origin) do not belong to any quadrant.
To plot (x, y): start at origin, move x units horizontally (right if +, left if -), then y units vertically (up if +, down if -). Mark the point.
Distance between A(x₁, y₁) and B(x₂, y₂): d = √[(x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)²]. Derived from Pythagoras' theorem applied to the horizontal and vertical distances between the points.
The midpoint M of segment AB where A(x₁,y₁) and B(x₂,y₂): M = ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2). The coordinates of the midpoint are the averages of the corresponding coordinates.

Three points are collinear if they lie on the same straight line. Verified by checking the area of the triangle they form = 0, or by checking equal slopes between pairs of points, or by distance: if AC = AB + BC.
A point on the x-axis has y = 0: written (a, 0). A point on the y-axis has x = 0: written (0, b). The origin is (0, 0). These are important boundary cases.

laws_and_formulas.name: Distance Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Distance between two points A(x₁, y₁) and B(x₂, y₂) in the Cartesian plane.

laws_and_formulas.name: Midpoint Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Coordinates of the midpoint of a segment joining (x₁,y₁) and (x₂,y₂).

key_points: Every point in the plane has a unique ordered pair (x, y); order matters.
x-coordinate = horizontal distance from y-axis; y-coordinate = vertical distance from x-axis.
Quadrant I: both positive; II: x negative, y positive; III: both negative; IV: x positive, y negative.
Distance formula: d = √[(Δx)² + (Δy)²] — Pythagoras in the coordinate plane.
Midpoint: average of x-coordinates, average of y-coordinates.
Points on x-axis: (a, 0); on y-axis: (0, b); origin: (0, 0).
To check collinearity: area of triangle formed = 0, or distances satisfy AB + BC = AC.
The distance formula is symmetric: d(A,B) = d(B,A).
Equidistant from two points → the point lies on the perpendicular bisector of their segment.
Coordinate geometry: the bridge between Euclid's geometry and Descartes' algebra.

9|mathematics|Coordinate_Geometry_Recogn.overview: Graphs provide visual representations of the relationship between variables. This chapter covers recognising and sketching the graphs of fundamental functions — straight lines (linear), parabolas (quadratic), and simple curves. Reading information from graphs (intercepts, slope, turning points) connects algebraic equations to visual geometric shapes. Graph recognition is a fundamental skill in algebra, calculus, physics, and data analysis.

key_concepts.term: Graph of a Linear Function (y = mx + c)
key_concepts.definition: A straight line where m is the slope (gradient) and c is the y-intercept. m > 0: line rises left to right; m < 0: line falls; m = 0: horizontal line. The line cuts the y-axis at (0, c) and the x-axis at (-c/m, 0).
The steepness of a line: m = (y₂ - y₁)/(x₂ - x₁) = rise/run. Positive slope: ascends; negative slope: descends; zero slope: horizontal; undefined slope: vertical line (x = constant).
y-intercept: where the graph crosses the y-axis (set x = 0). x-intercept (root): where the graph crosses the x-axis (set y = 0). These two intercepts and the slope fully define a linear graph.

A U-shaped curve symmetric about the y-axis. Vertex at origin (0,0). As x increases from 0, y increases rapidly. The graph is entirely above the x-axis (except at origin). y = (x-a)² shifts vertex to (a, 0).
Two curved branches in Quadrants I and III (for y = 1/x). Never crosses the axes. As x → 0, y → ∞; as x → ∞, y → 0. The axes are asymptotes.
A straight line through the origin with slope 1. All direct proportion relationships pass through the origin. y = kx for any constant k gives a line through the origin with slope k.

From a graph, we can read: x and y intercepts, slope (rise over run between two clear points), maximum/minimum points, intervals where y is increasing or decreasing, and the solution to y = k for a given k.

The solution to a system of two linear equations is the intersection point of their graphs. Parallel lines: no intersection (no solution). Same line: infinite solutions. Intersecting at one point: unique solution.

laws_and_formulas.name: Slope-Intercept Form
laws_and_formulas.description: Equation of a straight line with slope m and y-intercept c.

laws_and_formulas.name: Slope Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Gradient of a line through two points (x₁,y₁) and (x₂,y₂).

key_points: y = mx + c: straight line; m = slope, c = y-intercept.
Positive slope → rises left to right; negative → falls; zero → horizontal; undefined → vertical.
y = x²: U-shaped parabola, vertex at origin, symmetric about y-axis.
y = 1/x: rectangular hyperbola in Q1 and Q3; axes are asymptotes.
y = kx: direct proportion, straight line through origin with slope k.
To find x-intercept: set y = 0 and solve; to find y-intercept: set x = 0.
Two linear graphs intersect at the solution to the simultaneous equations.
Parallel lines (same m, different c): no intersection — no solution to the system.
Slope = rise/run = (change in y)/(change in x) between any two points on the line.
Graph recognition: know the signature shape (line, parabola, hyperbola) of each standard equation.

9|physics|Measurements_and_Experimentati.overview: Scientific estimation uses powers of 10 (orders of magnitude) to quickly compare and approximate sizes, from subatomic particles to galaxies. Understanding measurement precision and errors is essential for all experimental science.

key_concepts.definition: The power of 10 closest to a given quantity. E.g., the diameter of an atom (~10⁻¹⁰ m) is of order 10⁻¹⁰.
Approximate calculation using rounded values and orders of magnitude to get a quick answer.
Smallest measurement an instrument can reliably make. Ruler: 1 mm; Vernier: 0.01 cm; Screw gauge: 0.001 cm.
Difference between measured value and true value. Types: systematic (consistent bias) and random (unpredictable fluctuation).

key_points: The diameter of a hydrogen atom ≈ 10⁻¹⁰ m; diameter of nucleus ≈ 10⁻¹⁵ m.
Mass of electron ≈ 10⁻³⁰ kg; mass of Sun ≈ 10³⁰ kg — 60 orders of magnitude apart!
Estimation checks: Is the answer reasonable? (A bus can't weigh 10⁶ kg.)
Percentage error = (absolute error / true value) × 100%
Precision: how consistent repeated measurements are; Accuracy: how close to the true value.

9|physics|Measurements_and_Experimentati.overview: Physics is a quantitative science — every measurement must be expressed in a unit. The International System of Units (SI) provides a coherent, globally accepted framework of seven base units from which all other units are derived. This chapter introduces the SI base units, their symbols, and the prefixes used for very large or very small quantities. Standardisation of units is the bedrock of scientific communication and the reproducibility of experiments.

key_concepts.definition: The seven fundamental units: metre (m) for length, kilogram (kg) for mass, second (s) for time, ampere (A) for electric current, kelvin (K) for temperature, mole (mol) for amount of substance, candela (cd) for luminous intensity. All other units are derived from these seven.

SI unit of length. Defined as the distance light travels in a vacuum in 1/299,792,458 of a second. The metre replaced the reference platinum-iridium bar in 1983 with this light-travel definition for greater precision.

SI unit of mass. Since 2019 defined via Planck's constant (h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s). Formerly the mass of the International Prototype of the Kilogram — the only SI unit once defined by a physical artefact.
SI unit of time. Defined as 9,192,631,770 periods of the radiation emitted by caesium-133 atoms transitioning between two hyperfine ground states. Atomic clocks exploit this for extreme precision.

Multipliers that scale SI units: kilo (k) = 10³, mega (M) = 10⁶, giga (G) = 10⁹; milli (m) = 10⁻³, micro (μ) = 10⁻⁶, nano (n) = 10⁻⁹. Using prefixes avoids writing very large or very small numbers: 1 km = 1000 m.

Units formed by combining base units through multiplication or division. Speed = m/s; Force = kg·m/s² (newton); Pressure = kg/(m·s²) (pascal); Energy = kg·m²/s² (joule). Every derived unit reduces to base units.

Expressing numbers as a × 10ⁿ where 1 ≤ a < 10. Essential for very large/small values in physics: mass of Earth = 5.97×10²⁴ kg; mass of electron = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg. Enables easy comparison and calculation.

Checking equations by ensuring dimensions (M, L, T) balance on both sides. If F = ma, dimensions are [M][LT⁻²] on the right and must equal [force] on the left. A dimensionally incorrect equation is definitely wrong.

laws_and_formulas.name: Unit Conversion
laws_and_formulas.description: Standard length conversions; apply the same pattern to other SI units using prefixes.

key_points: 7 SI base units: m, kg, s, A, K, mol, cd.
All physical quantities are expressed in terms of these base units.
SI prefixes: kilo (10³), mega (10⁶), milli (10⁻³), micro (10⁻⁶), nano (10⁻⁹).
Scientific notation: a × 10ⁿ with 1 ≤ a < 10.
Derived units: speed (m/s), force (N = kg·m/s²), energy (J = kg·m²/s²).
Dimensional analysis checks the consistency of equations.
1 litre = 10⁻³ m³ = 1000 cm³ — volume conversion.
Weight is a force (measured in N); mass is measured in kg.
Temperature in Kelvin: K = °C + 273.15.
Consistency of units across an equation is a necessary (not sufficient) condition for it being correct.

9|physics|Measurements_and_Experimentati.overview: Vernier calipers and screw gauges are precision measuring instruments. Vernier calipers measure to 0.01 cm; screw gauges measure to 0.001 cm. Understanding their construction and reading them correctly is essential for practical physics.

key_concepts.definition: Instrument with a main scale and vernier scale; measures external/internal dimensions and depths to 0.01 cm.
A sliding scale with 10 divisions equal to 9 main scale divisions. LC = 1 MSD – 1 VSD = 0.1 mm.
Instrument using a fine screw; pitch = 1 mm; with 100 circular divisions → LC = 0.01 mm = 0.001 cm.
When the zero of vernier/thimble doesn't align with the reference when instrument is fully closed.

laws_and_formulas.name: Vernier Reading
laws_and_formulas.formula: = Main scale reading + (Vernier scale coincidence × LC)

laws_and_formulas.name: Screw Gauge LC
laws_and_formulas.formula: = Pitch / Number of circular scale divisions = 1mm/100 = 0.01mm

laws_and_formulas.name: Screw Gauge Reading
laws_and_formulas.formula: = Main scale + (Circular scale reading × LC) ± Zero error

key_points: Positive zero error: when jaws closed, zero of vernier is to the right of zero of main scale → subtract.
Negative zero error: zero of vernier is to the left → add.
Screw gauge backlash error: always turn screw in the same direction to avoid backlash.
Vernier caliper can also measure internal diameters (using upper jaws) and depths (depth probe).
Reading to 3 significant figures requires a precise instrument like screw gauge.

9|physics|Measurements_and_Experimentati.overview: Scientific data is best communicated through well-structured tables and graphs. A straight-line graph (y = mx + c) shows a linear relationship and allows determination of slope, intercept, and verification of physical laws.

key_concepts.definition: Organised format with columns for variable name, symbol, unit, and measured values. Independent variable first.
Visual representation of data; plot independent variable on x-axis, dependent on y-axis.
A straight line drawn so that data points are evenly distributed on both sides; represents the trend.

key_concepts.formula: m = (y₂–y₁)/(x₂–x₁); gives the rate of change between variables.

key_points: Choose scale so the graph fills at least 2/3 of the paper; mark axes with quantity, symbol, and unit.
Gradient of distance-time graph = speed; gradient of speed-time graph = acceleration.
Y-intercept (c): the value of y when x = 0; often has physical significance.
A straight-line graph through origin means y ∝ x (directly proportional).
Plotting a graph smooths out random errors — more reliable than a single measurement.

9|physics|Motion_in_One_Dimension_Distan.overview: Motion along a straight line is described by distance, displacement, speed, velocity, and acceleration. Class 9 introduces the key distinction between scalar and vector quantities in the context of motion.

key_concepts.definition: Total path length covered — scalar (no direction). SI unit: metre.
Shortest straight-line distance from start to finish with direction — vector. SI unit: metre.
Rate of change of distance = distance/time — scalar. SI unit: m/s.
Rate of change of displacement — vector. v = displacement/time. SI unit: m/s.
Rate of change of velocity — vector. a = (v–u)/t. SI unit: m/s². Negative = deceleration.

laws_and_formulas.name: Equations of motion
laws_and_formulas.formula: v = u+at; s = ut+½at²; v² = u²+2as

key_points: A body moving in a circle at constant speed still has changing velocity (direction changes) → acceleration.
Average speed = total distance / total time; Average velocity = total displacement / total time.
For uniformly accelerated motion: average velocity = (u+v)/2.
Free fall: a = g = 9.8 m/s² downward; for a body dropped from rest: u = 0.
Uniform motion: constant velocity (speed and direction); acceleration = 0.

9|physics|Motion_in_One_Dimension_Graphs.overview: Distance-time and speed-time graphs give a visual picture of motion. The slope and area of these graphs yield key motion quantities — speed, acceleration, and displacement.

key_concepts.definition: Slope = speed. Straight line → uniform motion; curve → accelerating; horizontal line → at rest.
Slope = acceleration. Area under graph = distance/displacement. Horizontal line → uniform speed; straight line → uniform acceleration.
Straight line on distance-time graph; horizontal line on speed-time graph.
Curve on distance-time; straight line (slope = a) on speed-time.

key_points: A horizontal line on d-t graph means zero speed (object at rest).
A steeper slope on d-t graph means higher speed.
Area of triangle under v-t graph for uniformly accelerated motion = ½ × base × height = ½ × t × (v–u).
A negative slope on v-t graph means deceleration (acceleration opposing motion).
For free fall from rest: d-t graph is a parabola; v-t graph is a straight line through origin.

9|physics|Motion_in_One_Dimension_Equati.overview: Three kinematic equations relate initial velocity, final velocity, acceleration, time, and displacement for uniformly accelerated motion. These are derived from graphs and form the core of Class 9 mechanics.

key_concepts.definition: Acceleration that remains constant in magnitude and direction throughout the motion.
Velocity at the start of the time interval being considered.
Velocity at the end of the time interval.

laws_and_formulas.name: Derivation 2
laws_and_formulas.formula: s = avg velocity × t = (u+v)/2 × t = ut + ½at²

key_points: All three equations require constant acceleration — cannot be used if acceleration varies.
For free fall, use a = g = 9.8 m/s² (take downward as positive).
If a body is thrown upward with speed u: at the highest point v = 0; then use v² = u² – 2gs to find height.
Time to reach maximum height: t = u/g.
The body hits the ground with the same speed it was thrown (if no air resistance).

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Newtons_First_L.overview: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) states that every body remains in its state of rest or uniform motion unless compelled by an external force. It defines inertia and the concept of force.

key_concepts.definition: The tendency of a body to resist any change in its state of rest or uniform motion. Greater mass = greater inertia.
The vector sum of all forces on an object. If net force = 0, the object is in equilibrium (at rest or uniform motion).
State where net force = 0; the body remains at rest or moves with constant velocity.

key_points: A book on a table is at rest because: weight (down) = normal force (up) → net force = 0.
A passenger jerks forward when a bus stops suddenly — inertia of motion keeps passenger moving.
A passenger jerks backward when a bus starts — inertia of rest keeps passenger stationary.
First Law gives the qualitative definition of force — something that changes the state of motion.
In space (no friction, no gravity), an object set in motion continues forever — First Law.

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Newtons_Second_.overview: Newton's Second Law quantifies the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration: F = ma. It is the most used law in mechanics for solving problems involving motion.

key_concepts.definition: Net force on a body = mass × acceleration: F = ma. The acceleration is in the direction of the net force.
SI unit of force: 1 N is the force required to accelerate 1 kg at 1 m/s². 1 N = 1 kg·m/s².
p = mv. Newton's Second Law: F = Δp/Δt (rate of change of momentum).
Impulse = F × t = Δp = m(v–u). Force applied for a short time.

laws_and_formulas.name: F = Δp/Δt
laws_and_formulas.formula: Force = rate of change of momentum

key_points: If mass is constant: F = ma (most common form).
Heavier vehicles need greater braking force to stop in the same distance (F = ma — larger m).
A fielder 'follows through' when catching a ball to increase the time of impulse, reducing force on hands.
SI unit of momentum: kg·m/s.
If F = 0, then a = 0 → confirms Newton's First Law from Second Law.

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Newtons_Third_L.overview: Newton's Third Law states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. This law explains propulsion, recoil, and everyday pushes and pulls.

key_concepts.definition: Two forces of equal magnitude and opposite direction acting on two different bodies. They act simultaneously.
Backward push experienced by a gun when a bullet is fired; action-reaction: bullet forward, gun backward.

key_points: Action and reaction ALWAYS act on different bodies — they cannot cancel each other.
Walking: foot pushes backward on ground (action) → ground pushes foot forward (reaction) → we move forward.
Swimming: hands push water backward → water pushes hands forward.
Rocket propulsion: hot gases pushed backward at high speed → rocket pushed forward.
A bird's wings push air downward → air pushes bird upward (lift).

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Gravitation.overview: Newton's Law of Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

key_concepts.definition: Universal attractive force between any two masses. Very weak — noticeable only when at least one mass is very large (planet, star).
G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²; measured by Cavendish experiment.
Acceleration of a freely falling body near Earth's surface: g = 9.8 m/s².
Gravitational force on an object: W = mg. SI unit: Newton. Weight varies with location; mass does not.

key_points: Gravitational force is always attractive — there is no gravitational repulsion.
g is less at higher altitudes (farther from Earth's centre) and at the poles vs equator.
On the Moon: g_moon = g_earth/6 → weight on Moon = 1/6 of weight on Earth.
Weightlessness in orbit: not because gravity is absent, but because the astronaut is in free fall.
G was first measured by Cavendish in 1798 using a torsion balance experiment.

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Fluids_Change_o.overview: Fluids exert pressure on all surfaces in contact. Pressure in a fluid increases with depth and is transmitted equally in all directions (Pascal's Law). This underlies hydraulic machines.

key_concepts.definition: Force per unit area: P = F/A. SI unit: Pascal (Pa = N/m²).
P = ρgh — pressure increases with depth h, density ρ, and gravity g.
Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted unchanged in all directions.
Uses Pascal's Law: small force on small piston creates large force on large piston. F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂.

key_points: A dam is thicker at the base because water pressure is greatest at the bottom.
Hydraulic brakes: driver applies small force on brake pedal → transmitted through fluid → large force on brake pads.
Pressure is the same at the same horizontal level in a connected static fluid.
Blood pressure: measured in mm of Hg; normal = 120/80 mm Hg.
Pressure does not depend on the shape or size of the container — only on depth and fluid density.

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Atmospheric_pre.overview: Earth's atmosphere exerts pressure on all objects due to the weight of the air column above. Atmospheric pressure is approximately 101,325 Pa at sea level and decreases with altitude.

key_concepts.definition: Pressure exerted by the atmosphere at sea level ≈ 1 atm = 101,325 Pa = 760 mm Hg = 1013 mbar.
Instrument for measuring atmospheric pressure; invented by Torricelli — a mercury column supported by atmospheric pressure.
The vacuum above mercury in a barometer (no air → no pressure above the mercury column).

laws_and_formulas.name: Barometer reading
laws_and_formulas.formula: P_atm = ρgh (h ≈ 76 cm Hg, ρ_Hg = 13600 kg/m³)

key_points: Water cannot be used in barometer because the tube would need to be 10.3 m tall (vs 76 cm for mercury).
Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude — that's why aircraft are pressurised.
At the top of Mt Everest (~8.8 km), atmospheric pressure is about 1/3 of sea level.
Suction cups work by atmospheric pressure: removing air from the cup creates a pressure difference.
Aneroid barometer: no liquid; uses a sealed metal box that expands/contracts with pressure changes.

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Buoyancy_Archim.overview: Any object immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the displaced fluid. This principle explains why objects float or sink and is fundamental to ship design and density measurement.

key_concepts.definition: Upward force on a submerged object = weight of fluid displaced. Always acts upward.
When a body is fully or partially submerged in a fluid, the buoyant force = weight of fluid displaced.
Weight of object in fluid = actual weight – buoyant force. Object appears lighter in fluid.
RD = density of substance / density of water. If RD < 1 → floats; RD > 1 → sinks.

laws_and_formulas.name: Condition for floating
laws_and_formulas.formula: F_b = W  →  ρ_object × V_object = ρ_fluid × V_submerged

laws_and_formulas.name: Relative Density
laws_and_formulas.formula: RD = Weight in air / (Weight in air – Weight in water)

key_points: A ship (steel) floats because its overall density (hull + air inside) is less than water.
Ice floats with ~1/10 of its volume above water (density of ice = 900 kg/m³ < 1000 kg/m³ for water).
Balloons rise because the buoyant force of air on the balloon > weight of balloon + gas.
Submarines control buoyancy by filling/emptying ballast tanks with water.
Hydrometer: instrument using Archimedes' principle to measure density of liquids.

9|physics|Heat_and_Energy_Concepts_of_he.overview: Heat is energy in transit due to temperature difference; temperature measures the degree of hotness. This section covers temperature scales, specific heat capacity, and calorimetry.

key_concepts.definition: Thermal energy transferred from hotter to cooler object due to temperature difference. SI unit: Joule.
Measure of average kinetic energy of particles. SI unit: Kelvin. Also measured in Celsius.
Heat required to raise temperature of 1 kg of substance by 1 K. Unit: J/(kg·K).
Heat needed to raise temperature of a given body by 1 K = m × c. Unit: J/K.

laws_and_formulas.name: Principle of Calorimetry
laws_and_formulas.formula: Heat lost by hot body = Heat gained by cold body (mcΔT)hot = (mcΔT)cold

key_points: Water has the highest specific heat of common substances (4200 J/kg·K) — it resists temperature change.
The sea heats and cools more slowly than land — moderating coastal climates.
Calorimeter: insulated container used to measure heat exchange between substances.
At absolute zero (0 K = –273.15°C), all molecular motion stops — lowest possible temperature.
1 calorie = 4.2 J (mechanical equivalent of heat — Joule's experiment).

9|physics|Heat_and_Energy_Anomalous_expa.overview: Unlike most substances, water contracts when cooled from 4°C to 0°C instead of expanding. This anomalous expansion has profound ecological significance — ponds freeze from the top, preserving aquatic life.

key_concepts.definition: Water expands on cooling from 4°C to 0°C. Maximum density of water = 1000 kg/m³ at 4°C.
Most substances expand on heating and contract on cooling. Solid: linear, area, volume expansion.
Fractional change in length per degree Celsius change. ΔL = L₀αΔT.

key_points: Water is densest at 4°C; ice (0°C) is less dense than liquid water → ice floats.
Lakes freeze from top downward; water at 4°C sinks to bottom, fish survive in winter.
Bimetallic strip: two metals with different α bonded together; bends when heated — used in thermostats.
Rail gaps are left between sections to allow for thermal expansion in summer.
Overhead wires sag more in summer (expansion) than in winter (contraction).

9|physics|Heat_and_Energy_Thermometers.overview: Thermometers measure temperature by exploiting the thermal expansion of liquids, electrical resistance changes, or radiation. Clinical and laboratory thermometers are the common types at Class 9 level.

key_concepts.definition: Measures human body temperature (35°C–42°C); has a constriction to prevent mercury from dropping back.
Measures wider range (–10°C to 110°C); no constriction; must be read while in contact.
Ice point (0°C): temperature of pure ice at normal pressure. Steam point (100°C): temperature of steam at 1 atm.
Uses voltage produced at junctions of two different metals to measure temperature over a wide range.

key_points: Mercury thermometer: mercury expands uniformly → reliable, easily read. Toxic — being replaced.
Alcohol thermometer: used for very low temperatures (lower freezing point than mercury, –117°C).
The constriction in a clinical thermometer lets you read after removal from the patient — it must be shaken down to reset.
Resistance thermometer: resistance of a platinum wire increases with temperature — very precise.
Infrared thermometer: measures body temperature without contact — used during COVID-19 pandemic.

9|physics|Heat_and_Energy_Energy_sources.overview: Energy exists in many forms and can be converted from one form to another. Class 9 covers conventional energy sources (fossil fuels, hydro) and renewable alternatives (solar, wind, tidal, nuclear, biomass).

key_concepts.definition: Coal, oil, natural gas — formed from ancient organic matter. Non-renewable; burning produces CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ.
Replenished naturally: solar, wind, hydro, tidal, geothermal, biomass.
Energy from the Sun; captured by solar panels (photovoltaic) or solar thermal collectors.
Released in fission (splitting heavy nuclei) or fusion (combining light nuclei); enormous energy per reaction.

key_points: Law of Conservation of Energy: energy cannot be created or destroyed; it transforms from one form to another.
Efficiency = useful energy output / total energy input × 100%.
Nuclear fission: U-235 or Pu-239 split → chain reaction → massive energy. Used in nuclear power plants.
Solar panels: photovoltaic cells convert light energy directly to electrical energy.
Hydroelectric: gravitational potential energy of water → kinetic → electrical energy via turbine and generator.

9|physics|Light_Reflection_of_light.overview: Light travels in straight lines and reflects off surfaces according to two laws: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, and the incident ray, reflected ray, and normal all lie in the same plane. These laws, combined with ray diagrams, explain image formation in plane mirrors, curved mirrors, and optical instruments. The chapter distinguishes between real and virtual images and between regular and diffuse reflection.

key_concepts.definition: 1. The angle of incidence (i) = angle of reflection (r), both measured from the normal to the surface. 2. The incident ray, reflected ray, and normal all lie in the same plane. These laws hold for all surfaces — plane, curved, rough, or smooth.
A line perpendicular to the reflecting surface at the point of incidence. All angles (of incidence, reflection, refraction) are measured from the normal, NOT from the surface.

Virtual (cannot be projected on a screen). Erect (same way up as object). Same size as object. As far behind the mirror as the object is in front. Laterally inverted (left and right are reversed). The image is formed by tracing reflected rays back to where they appear to diverge from.

Real image: formed by actual convergence of light rays; can be projected on a screen. Virtual image: formed where diverging rays appear to come from (behind mirror); cannot be projected. Plane mirrors form virtual images; concave mirrors can form real images.

Regular (specular) reflection: from a smooth surface; parallel rays reflect as parallel rays → clear image. Diffuse reflection: from a rough surface; parallel rays scatter in all directions → no clear image but the surface is visible from all angles.

Concave (converging): centre of curvature and focus on the same side as the incoming light. Convex (diverging): centre of curvature and focus on the opposite side. Focal length f = R/2 (R = radius of curvature).

1/v + 1/u = 1/f, where u = object distance, v = image distance, f = focal length. Sign convention: distances measured from the pole; distances in the direction of incident light are positive. For concave mirror, f is negative (distances behind mirror are negative).

Concave mirrors: shaving/make-up mirrors (magnified upright virtual image), dentist mirrors, telescopes, solar furnaces (concentrate parallel rays at focus). Convex mirrors: vehicle rear-view mirrors and security mirrors (wide field of view, diminished virtual image).

laws_and_formulas.name: Laws of Reflection
laws_and_formulas.formula: ∠i = ∠r (both from normal); incident ray, normal, reflected ray coplanar
laws_and_formulas.description: The two laws of reflection governing all mirror surfaces.

laws_and_formulas.name: Mirror Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Relates image distance v, object distance u, and focal length f for spherical mirrors.

key_points: ∠i = ∠r (from the normal, not the surface).
Incident ray, normal, reflected ray: all in the same plane.
Plane mirror image: virtual, erect, same size, same distance behind mirror, laterally inverted.
Real image: formed by converging rays; can be caught on screen.
Virtual image: formed by diverging rays appearing to meet; cannot be caught on screen.
Regular reflection: smooth surface → clear image. Diffuse: rough → no clear image.
Concave mirror: focal length f = R/2 (focus in front of mirror).
Convex mirror: always forms virtual, erect, diminished images — used for wide field of view.
Mirror formula: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f.
Concave mirrors used in headlights (parallel beams), telescopes, solar furnaces.

9|physics|Light_Spherical_mirrors.overview: Spherical mirrors (concave and convex) reflect light to form images. Ray diagrams and the mirror formula relate object distance, image distance, and focal length for both types of mirrors.

key_concepts.definition: Reflecting surface is on the hollow side; converges reflected rays; can form real or virtual images depending on object position.
Reflecting surface is on the bulging side; diverges reflected rays; always forms virtual, erect, diminished images.
Distance from the pole (centre of mirror) to the focus (F). f = R/2 (R = radius of curvature).
1/f = 1/v + 1/u. Using the New Cartesian sign convention.

laws_and_formulas.name: Sign Convention
laws_and_formulas.formula: All distances measured from pole; incident light direction = positive

key_points: Concave mirror applications: torches/headlights (object at F → parallel beam), shaving/makeup mirror (object inside F → enlarged virtual image), solar furnace.
Convex mirror applications: rear-view mirrors (gives wider field of view), security mirrors in shops.
Virtual image: formed behind mirror, cannot be projected on screen, magnification m > 0 (erect).
Real image: formed in front of mirror, can be projected, m < 0 (inverted).
When object is at C (centre of curvature): image is also at C, real, inverted, same size (m = –1).

9|physics|Sound_Nature_of_Sound_waves.overview: Sound is a mechanical longitudinal wave that requires a medium for propagation. It travels through compressions and rarefactions in the medium. Class 9 covers the nature, properties, and characteristics of sound waves.

key_concepts.definition: Wave where particle vibration is along the direction of wave propagation (compressions and rarefactions).
Region where particles are pushed closer together — high pressure zone.
Region where particles are spread farther apart — low pressure zone.
Number of vibrations per second. SI unit: Hertz (Hz).
Maximum displacement of a particle from its equilibrium position; determines loudness.
≈ 343 m/s in air at 20°C; ~1500 m/s in water; faster in solids (steel: ~5000 m/s).

laws_and_formulas.name: Wave speed
laws_and_formulas.formula: v = fλ  (v = speed, f = frequency, λ = wavelength)

key_points: Sound cannot travel through vacuum — it needs a medium (solid, liquid, or gas).
Speed of sound: solids > liquids > gases (particles are closer in denser media for solids/liquids).
Pitch is determined by frequency; loudness by amplitude; quality (timbre) by waveform.
Echo: reflection of sound heard after the original — time gap > 0.1 s (ear's persistence of hearing).
Minimum distance for echo: 17.2 m (time of 0.1 s × speed of 344 m/s ÷ 2 = 17.2 m).

9|physics|Sound_Range_of_hearing_ultraso.overview: The human ear can hear sounds in the range 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz. Sounds below (infrasound) and above (ultrasound) this range have important applications in science, medicine, and industry.

key_concepts.definition: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz) — the frequency range humans can hear.
Frequency below 20 Hz — inaudible to humans; produced by earthquakes, volcanoes; detected by elephants, whales.
Frequency above 20,000 Hz — inaudible to humans; used by bats and dolphins (echolocation); powerful applications in medicine and industry.
Sound Navigation And Ranging — uses ultrasound to detect underwater objects; measures time for echo to return.

key_points: Bats navigate and hunt using echolocation with ultrasound (30 kHz – 100 kHz).
Ultrasound in medicine: sonography (imaging organs and fetuses without radiation), lithotripsy (breaking kidney stones).
Industrial ultrasound: detecting cracks in metals, cleaning delicate parts, welding plastics.
SONAR: depth = (v × t)/2 where t = time for echo, v = speed of sound in water.
Infrasound: elephants communicate over long distances using infrasound (~14–16 Hz).

9|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Stat.overview: Static electricity is the accumulation of electric charge on the surface of objects. It arises due to friction (triboelectric effect) and leads to electric fields, sparks, and lightning.

key_concepts.definition: A property of matter. Two types: positive (+, proton) and negative (–, electron). Measured in Coulombs (C).
When two different materials are rubbed, electrons transfer from one to the other; both become charged.
Force between two point charges: F = kq₁q₂/r². Like charges repel; unlike attract.
Region around a charge where another charge experiences a force. Field lines go from + to –.

laws_and_formulas.name: Conservation of Charge
laws_and_formulas.statement: Total charge in an isolated system is always conserved.

key_points: A glass rod rubbed with silk becomes positively charged (loses electrons to silk).
An ebonite rod rubbed with fur becomes negatively charged (gains electrons from fur).
Lightning: large charge separation in clouds; lightning rod conducts charge safely to Earth.
Grounding (earthing): connecting a charged conductor to Earth to neutralise it.
Static electricity is hazardous near fuel — airplanes are grounded before refuelling.

9|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Curr.overview: Electric current is the flow of charges through a conductor. Ohm's Law, resistance, and circuit analysis (series/parallel) are the core topics of this section.

key_concepts.definition: I = Q/t. Rate of flow of charge. SI unit: Ampere (A). Conventional current flows from + to –.
V = W/Q. Energy per unit charge. SI unit: Volt (V). Drives current in a circuit.
Opposition to current flow. R = V/I. SI unit: Ohm (Ω). Depends on material, length, cross-section, temperature.
V = IR at constant temperature; current is proportional to potential difference.

key_points: In series: same current through all; voltages add; R_total = sum.
In parallel: same voltage across all; currents add; R_total is less than smallest.
Household appliances in parallel → each at full 230V; independent switching.
Fuse (series) breaks circuit at rated current — protects against overload.
1 unit = 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J.

9|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Prop.overview: A bar magnet has two poles (N and S), a magnetic field around it, and exerts forces on other magnets and magnetic materials. These properties are the foundation of electromagnetism.

key_concepts.definition: N (north-seeking) and S (south-seeking) poles of a magnet; like poles repel, unlike poles attract. Cannot be separated.
Region around a magnet where magnetic forces act. Represented by field lines from N to S outside the magnet.
A point where the resultant magnetic field is zero — field of the magnet cancels Earth's field.
The strength of each pole of a magnet. The stronger the magnet, the denser the field lines near the poles.

key_points: Magnetic field lines: originate from N, end at S (outside magnet); continuous closed loops inside.
Field lines never cross — crossing would mean two field directions at one point (impossible).
Closer field lines = stronger field. Near the poles, lines are most dense.
Cutting a magnet in two gives two smaller magnets with N and S poles — can never have a magnetic monopole.
A freely suspended magnet aligns with Earth's magnetic field (N pole points north) — basis of compass.
