9|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Indu.overview: Induced magnetism occurs when a magnetic material is placed near a magnet — it temporarily becomes a magnet itself. Electromagnets use current in a coil to create controllable magnetic fields.

key_concepts.definition: When a piece of soft iron is placed near or in a magnetic field, it becomes a temporary magnet; magnetism disappears when field is removed.
Easily magnetised and demagnetised — ideal for electromagnet cores and transformers.
Difficult to magnetise, but retains magnetism — ideal for permanent magnets.
Coil of wire wound around a soft iron core; behaves as a magnet when current flows; strength controlled by current and turns.

key_points: Electromagnet strength increases with: more current, more turns, smaller air gaps, soft iron core.
Applications of electromagnets: electric bell, relay, crane for lifting iron/steel scrap, MRI machines.
Demagnetisation: heating, hammering, or passing AC through the coil.
The end of a solenoid where current flows anticlockwise (from front view) is the N pole.
Soft iron is used in electric generators and transformers — its rapid magnetisation/demagnetisation allows AC operation.

9|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Line.overview: Magnetic field lines are a visual tool for representing the direction and strength of a magnetic field. They show how the field varies around magnets and current-carrying conductors.

key_concepts.definition: Continuous closed loops showing the path a free N pole would follow in the field. From N to S outside the magnet.
Represented by equally spaced, parallel field lines — found inside a solenoid.
Uniform and strong inside; similar to a bar magnet outside; direction found by right-hand grip rule.
Curl fingers of right hand in the direction of current in the coil; thumb points to N pole.

key_points: Iron filings placed near a magnet align along field lines — experimental proof of field pattern.
The field inside a solenoid is uniform — used for MRI machines and NMR spectrometers.
Two like poles facing: field lines between them push apart — repulsion.
Two unlike poles facing: field lines connect N to S — attraction.
Earth's magnetic field lines run from geographic south (magnetic north) to geographic north (magnetic south).

9|chemistry|The_Language_of_Chemistry_Sy.overview: Chemical symbols are internationally agreed shorthand representations of elements, established primarily by Berzelius in the 19th century. Each element has a unique one- or two-letter symbol derived from its English or Latin name. These symbols form the alphabet of chemistry, enabling scientists worldwide to communicate unambiguously about chemical substances. Understanding symbols is the first step toward reading and writing chemical formulas and equations.

key_concepts.definition: A one- or two-letter representation of an element. The first letter is always capitalised; the second (if present) is lowercase. Symbols may derive from the English name (O for Oxygen) or Latin name (Fe for Ferrum/Iron).
The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry standardises element symbols globally. This ensures a scientist in India and one in Brazil write the same symbol for the same element.

Several elements have symbols from Latin names: Na (Natrium = Sodium), K (Kalium = Potassium), Fe (Ferrum = Iron), Cu (Cuprum = Copper), Au (Aurum = Gold), Ag (Argentum = Silver), Pb (Plumbum = Lead), Sn (Stannum = Tin), Hg (Hydrargyrum = Mercury), Sb (Stibium = Antimony).

Jöns Jacob Berzelius proposed using initial letters of element names as symbols, creating the modern system. His systematic approach replaced earlier alchemical symbols (☿ for mercury, ☉ for gold) with universally legible letters.
A symbol represents: (1) the name of the element, (2) one atom of that element, (3) one mole of atoms (6.022 × 10²³ atoms) of that element. Context determines which meaning applies.
Case matters in chemistry: Co is cobalt, CO is carbon monoxide (a compound). The first letter must be uppercase, the second must be lowercase. Incorrect capitalisation changes the meaning entirely.
Elements 113-118 (Nihonium Nh, Moscovium Mc, etc.) received permanent IUPAC names recently. Before official naming, temporary systematic names using Latin numerals (Uun for element 110) are used.

Ancient alchemists used pictographic symbols. Dalton used his own circular symbols (early 1800s). Berzelius's letter-based system (1813) became universal and remains in use today because it is compact, memorable, and language-independent.

laws_and_formulas.name: Atomic Mass Unit
laws_and_formulas.formula: 1 amu = 1/12 mass of a C-12 atom = 1.66 × 10⁻²⁷ kg
laws_and_formulas.description: The standard unit for expressing atomic masses, used alongside symbols to characterise elements.

key_points: Element symbols are always one or two letters — first letter uppercase, second (if any) lowercase.
Na, K, Fe, Cu, Au, Ag, Pb, Sn, Hg, Sb — all have symbols from Latin names, not English.
Co (cobalt) and CO (carbon monoxide) are completely different — capitalisation matters enormously.
A symbol represents one atom of the element in a structural/molecular context.
Berzelius standardised chemical notation in 1813, ending the confusion of alchemical symbols.
IUPAC ensures global consistency — the same symbol is used in all languages.
Symbols are used in chemical formulas (H₂O) and equations (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O).
Memorise all first-20 elements and their symbols as a foundation for further chemistry.
The symbol for an element is not the same as the formula for its molecular form (e.g., O is the symbol for oxygen atom; O₂ is the formula for oxygen gas).
New elements are named after countries, scientists, or mythology — their symbols follow the same rules.

9|chemistry|The_Language_of_Chemistry_Va.overview: Valency is the combining capacity of an element — the number of electrons an atom gains, loses, or shares when forming chemical bonds. It determines how atoms combine to form compounds and is rooted in the electronic configuration of elements. Understanding valency is essential for writing correct chemical formulas and predicting the products of chemical reactions. The concept bridges atomic structure and macroscopic chemistry.

key_concepts.definition: The combining capacity of an atom, equal to the number of electrons it gains, loses, or shares in forming bonds. For most elements, valency equals the number of electrons in the outermost shell or 8 minus that number.

Many transition metals and some main group elements exhibit more than one valency. Iron shows valency 2 (ferrous) and 3 (ferric). Copper shows valency 1 and 2. The active valency in a compound is indicated by the Roman numeral in the name.

The valency of an element is determined by its valence electrons (electrons in the outermost shell). Metals lose valence electrons (valency = number of valence electrons). Non-metals gain electrons (valency = 8 - valence electrons, for Groups 16-17).
Electrovalency refers to ions formed by electron transfer (ionic bonds). Covalency refers to electron sharing (covalent bonds). An element can show different valency in ionic vs. covalent compounds.
Polyatomic ions (radicals) also have valency. Sulphate SO₄²⁻ has valency 2, hydroxide OH⁻ has valency 1, phosphate PO₄³⁻ has valency 3. These are treated as units in formula writing.
Noble gases (Group 18) have fully filled outermost shells (8 electrons, or 2 for helium). They have zero valency under normal conditions, explaining their chemical inertness.
To write a formula, cross-multiply valencies: element A (valency a) and element B (valency b) gives the formula AₙBₘ where n = b and m = a. Simplify to lowest ratio.
Atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve a stable configuration of 8 electrons in the outermost shell (2 for hydrogen). Valency arises from this tendency toward the octet.

laws_and_formulas.name: Valency Cross Rule
laws_and_formulas.formula: If A has valency m and B has valency n, the formula is AₙBₘ (simplified)
laws_and_formulas.description: The valency of each element becomes the subscript of the other to achieve electrical neutrality.

key_points: Valency = combining capacity = number of electrons gained, lost, or shared in bonding.
Group 1 (alkali metals): valency 1; Group 2: valency 2; Group 17 (halogens): valency 1; Group 18: valency 0.
Variable valency elements include Fe (2,3), Cu (1,2), Mn (2,4,7), Cr (2,3,6).
When writing formulas, valencies are cross-multiplied and the formula is reduced to lowest ratio.
MgO: Mg has valency 2, O has valency 2 → Mg₂O₂ → simplified to MgO.
Al₂O₃: Al valency 3, O valency 2 → cross-multiply → Al₂O₃.
Radicals (polyatomic ions) act as single units with their own valency in formula writing.
The valency of hydrogen is always 1 (a useful reference — 'hydrogen equivalents').
Carbon's valency is 4 — allowing it to form four bonds and the basis of organic chemistry.
Electropositive elements (metals) have low valency equal to their group number; electronegative elements' valency = 8 - group number.

9|chemistry|The_Language_of_Chemistry_Fo.overview: Chemical formulas represent the composition of compounds using element symbols and subscripts. This chapter focuses on the formulas of common radicals (polyatomic ions) and compounds formed from them. A formula tells us the ratio of atoms in a compound and enables stoichiometric calculations. Mastering common radicals and the rules for writing formulas is essential for understanding acids, bases, salts, and the language of chemical reactions.

key_concepts.definition: A shorthand representation of a compound showing the symbols of its constituent elements and the ratio of their atoms using subscripts. For example, H₂SO₄ shows 2 hydrogen atoms, 1 sulphur atom, and 4 oxygen atoms.
A group of atoms that behave as a single unit with a charge in chemical reactions. Examples: hydroxide (OH⁻), sulphate (SO₄²⁻), carbonate (CO₃²⁻), nitrate (NO₃⁻), phosphate (PO₄³⁻), ammonium (NH₄⁺).
When more than one of a polyatomic radical is needed, enclose it in parentheses with the subscript outside. For example, Ca(OH)₂ shows one calcium and two hydroxide units.

Cations are positively charged ions (metals, NH₄⁺); anions are negatively charged ions (non-metals, radicals like SO₄²⁻). In a neutral compound, the total positive charge equals total negative charge.
Hydrochloric acid HCl, sulphuric acid H₂SO₄, nitric acid HNO₃, phosphoric acid H₃PO₄, carbonic acid H₂CO₃, acetic acid CH₃COOH. All contain hydrogen and an acid radical.
Sodium hydroxide NaOH, potassium hydroxide KOH, calcium hydroxide Ca(OH)₂, ammonium hydroxide NH₄OH. Bases contain the hydroxide radical OH⁻.
Sodium chloride NaCl, copper sulphate CuSO₄, calcium carbonate CaCO₃, ammonium nitrate NH₄NO₃, ferric chloride FeCl₃, magnesium sulphate MgSO₄. Salts are formed from acid-base reactions.

Some salts crystallise with water molecules (water of crystallisation). For example, copper sulphate pentahydrate CuSO₄·5H₂O (blue vitriol). The dot notation shows the water molecules are weakly bound.

laws_and_formulas.name: Electrical Neutrality Principle
laws_and_formulas.formula: Sum of positive charges = Sum of negative charges in a compound
laws_and_formulas.description: A compound is electrically neutral; the product of cation valency × number of cations equals the product of anion valency × number of anions.

key_points: Memorise common radicals: OH⁻(1), SO₄²⁻(2), CO₃²⁻(2), NO₃⁻(1), PO₄³⁻(3), NH₄⁺(1), HCO₃⁻(1).
Parentheses are used when a radical appears more than once: Ca(OH)₂ NOT CaOH₂.
Cross-multiplying valencies gives the ratio; always reduce to the simplest ratio.
A compound formula must be electrically neutral overall.
FeCl₂ (iron(II) chloride) vs. FeCl₃ (iron(III) chloride) — the valency must be specified for variable valency elements.
H₂SO₄: 2 H⁺ (total charge +2) + SO₄²⁻ (charge -2) = neutral compound.
Al₂(SO₄)₃: Al³⁺ has valency 3, SO₄²⁻ has valency 2 → cross: 2 Al and 3 sulphate.
Hydrated salt formulas use the · notation (dot/centred dot) for water of crystallisation.
Molecular formulas (like C₂H₆) show actual atom counts; empirical formulas (CH₃) show the simplest ratio.
Learn formulas by practising writing them from names — connect name, radical, and valency systematically.

9|chemistry|The_Language_of_Chemistry_Ba.overview: A chemical equation is a symbolic representation of a chemical reaction showing reactants, products, and their relative quantities. Balancing an equation ensures adherence to the Law of Conservation of Mass — atoms are neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction. Students learn to balance equations by inspection and the hit-and-trial method. Balanced equations are the foundation for all quantitative chemistry (stoichiometry).

key_concepts.definition: A representation of a chemical reaction using symbols and formulas. Reactants are written on the left, products on the right, separated by an arrow (→). Conditions (heat, catalyst) are written above or below the arrow.

Mass is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction. The total mass of reactants equals the total mass of products. This requires that every atom present on the left must appear on the right.

Placing stoichiometric coefficients (whole numbers) in front of formulas to make the number of each type of atom equal on both sides. Coefficients multiply all atoms in the formula. Subscripts in formulas are NEVER changed.

The numbers placed in front of chemical formulas in a balanced equation. They indicate the relative number of moles (or molecules) of each substance. For example, 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O: coefficients are 2, 1, 2.
Letters in parentheses indicating physical states: (s) solid, (l) liquid, (g) gas, (aq) aqueous (dissolved in water). State symbols make equations more informative and complete.

A systematic approach to balancing: start with the most complex molecule, balance metals first, then non-metals (except H and O), then hydrogen, and finally oxygen. Adjust coefficients until all atoms balance.
An unbalanced equation showing only the correct formulas of reactants and products without stoichiometric coefficients. It is the starting point for the balancing process.
A description of a chemical reaction in words before symbols are introduced. For example, 'Hydrogen reacts with oxygen to form water.' The word equation guides writing the skeleton equation.

laws_and_formulas.name: Law of Conservation of Mass
laws_and_formulas.formula: Mass of reactants = Mass of products
laws_and_formulas.description: The total mass is conserved in any chemical reaction — the basis for balancing equations.

laws_and_formulas.name: Atom Balance Check
laws_and_formulas.formula: Atoms of each element on LHS = Atoms of each element on RHS
laws_and_formulas.description: Verification that a balanced equation is correct: each element must have equal atom counts on both sides.

key_points: Never change the subscripts in chemical formulas to balance an equation — only change coefficients.
Atoms must balance for each element — count them carefully on both sides.
Start balancing with the most complex molecule; balance H and O last.
A coefficient multiplies ALL atoms in the formula it precedes.
State symbols (s), (l), (g), (aq) add important physical information to equations.
Example: Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃ balances to 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃.
Example: CH₄ + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O balances to CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O.
Ionic equations show only the species actually involved in the reaction, omitting spectator ions.
A balanced equation conveys mole ratios directly — 2H₂ + O₂ means 2 moles of H₂ react with 1 mole of O₂.
Always verify your balanced equation by counting atoms of each element on both sides.

9|chemistry|Chemical_Changes_and_Reactio.overview: Chemical changes involve the formation of new substances with different properties from the original reactants. Unlike physical changes, chemical changes are generally irreversible and involve breaking and forming chemical bonds. This chapter classifies chemical reactions into combination, decomposition, displacement, and double displacement types, and also covers exothermic and endothermic reactions. Recognising reaction types enables prediction of products and mechanisms.

key_concepts.definition: A change in which new substances are formed with entirely different properties. Indicators: colour change, gas evolution, precipitate formation, temperature change, light emission, irreversibility. Example: burning magnesium, rusting of iron.
Two or more reactants combine to form a single product: A + B → AB. Examples: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ (Haber process); 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O; CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂.

A single compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances: AB → A + B. Energy is usually absorbed (endothermic). Examples: 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂ (electrolysis); 2HgO → 2Hg + O₂ (thermal decomposition).
A more reactive element displaces a less reactive element from its compound: A + BC → AC + B. Example: Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu (zinc is more reactive than copper).
Two compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds: AB + CD → AD + CB. Usually involves precipitate or gas formation. Example: BaCl₂ + Na₂SO₄ → BaSO₄↓ + 2NaCl.

Oxidation is loss of electrons (or gain of oxygen / loss of hydrogen). Reduction is gain of electrons (or loss of oxygen / gain of hydrogen). Oxidation and reduction always occur simultaneously (redox reactions).

Reversible reactions proceed in both directions and establish equilibrium (shown by ⇌). Irreversible reactions go to completion in one direction (→). Most synthesis and decomposition reactions are reversible under suitable conditions.
A double displacement reaction where an insoluble product (precipitate, ↓) forms. The precipitate separates from the solution. Example: Pb(NO₃)₂ + 2KI → PbI₂↓ + 2KNO₃.

laws_and_formulas.name: General Combination
laws_and_formulas.description: Two or more substances combine to form one product.

laws_and_formulas.name: General Decomposition
laws_and_formulas.description: One substance breaks into two or more simpler products.

laws_and_formulas.name: General Displacement
laws_and_formulas.formula: A + BC → AC + B (A more reactive than B)
laws_and_formulas.description: More reactive element A displaces less reactive B from compound BC.

key_points: Chemical change always produces new substances with different properties from the reactants.
Combination: many → one; Decomposition: one → many; Displacement: element replaces element; Double displacement: ion exchange.
Displacement reactions follow the activity series — a metal displaces one below it in the series.
Redox reactions involve simultaneous oxidation and reduction — one species cannot be oxidised without another being reduced.
Precipitate symbol ↓ and gas symbol ↑ in equations indicate state of product.
Thermal decomposition requires heat; photochemical decomposition uses light (e.g., AgCl → Ag + Cl₂).
All combustion reactions are combination reactions AND oxidation reactions.
Double displacement in neutralisation: acid + base → salt + water.
Electrolysis is decomposition using electrical energy: 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂.
The reactivity (activity) series determines which displacement reactions are possible.

9|chemistry|Chemical_Changes_and_Reactio.overview: Chemical reactions involve breaking bonds in reactants and forming new bonds in products — both processes involve energy exchange with the surroundings. Exothermic reactions release energy (heat, light) and are favoured in energy generation; endothermic reactions absorb energy and are driven by heating or light. This chapter explores these energy changes, their measurement using calorimetry, and their applications in everyday life and industry.

key_concepts.definition: A reaction that releases energy (usually heat) to the surroundings. The energy of products is lower than that of reactants. ΔH < 0. Examples: combustion, neutralisation, respiration, rusting, dissolving quicklime in water.

A reaction that absorbs energy from the surroundings. The temperature of the surroundings decreases. ΔH > 0. Examples: decomposition of water, photosynthesis, dissolving ammonium chloride, thermal decomposition of calcium carbonate.
The minimum energy required to initiate a chemical reaction, even for exothermic reactions. Reactant molecules must have sufficient kinetic energy to overcome the energy barrier (transition state).
A thermodynamic quantity representing the heat content of a system. Change in enthalpy ΔH = H(products) - H(reactants). Negative ΔH = exothermic; positive ΔH = endothermic.
The heat released when one mole of a substance completely burns in excess oxygen. Measured in kJ/mol. Higher heat of combustion indicates better fuel. Carbon: 393 kJ/mol; Methane: 890 kJ/mol.
The experimental measurement of heat changes in reactions. A calorimeter measures temperature change of a known mass of water. Q = mcΔT, where m = mass, c = specific heat, ΔT = temperature change.

A graph of energy vs. reaction progress. For exothermic reactions, products are lower than reactants (energy released). For endothermic reactions, products are higher (energy absorbed). The peak represents the transition state and activation energy.

The energy required to break one mole of a particular bond in gaseous molecules. Bond breaking requires energy (endothermic); bond formation releases energy (exothermic). If energy released in bond formation > energy absorbed in bond breaking → exothermic overall.

laws_and_formulas.name: Heat Equation
laws_and_formulas.description: Heat transferred (Q) equals mass (m) × specific heat capacity (c) × temperature change (ΔT). Used in calorimetry.

laws_and_formulas.name: Enthalpy Change
laws_and_formulas.description: Negative ΔH means exothermic (heat released); positive ΔH means endothermic (heat absorbed).

key_points: Exothermic: energy released → surroundings warm up → ΔH < 0.
Endothermic: energy absorbed → surroundings cool down → ΔH > 0.
All combustion reactions are exothermic.
Photosynthesis (6CO₂ + 6H₂O + energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂) is endothermic — it stores solar energy.
Even exothermic reactions need activation energy to begin (e.g., natural gas doesn't burn without a spark).
Catalysts lower the activation energy — speeding up both exothermic and endothermic reactions.
Heat of neutralisation is approximately constant (~57 kJ/mol) for strong acid + strong base in aqueous solution.
Energy profile: peak = transition state; activation energy = peak - reactants level.
Breaking bonds requires energy; forming bonds releases energy — net ΔH depends on the balance.
Refrigerants work by endothermic evaporation; hand warmers use exothermic oxidation reactions.

9|chemistry|Water_Water_as_a_compound_an.overview: Water is the most abundant liquid on Earth and is essential to all known life. Its unique properties — high boiling point, anomalous expansion at 4°C, high specific heat, and remarkable solvent power — arise from its polar covalent structure and hydrogen bonding. This chapter examines water as a chemical compound, its physical and chemical properties, and its role as the universal solvent. Water's chemistry underpins biology, geology, and industrial processes.

key_concepts.definition: Water (H₂O) has a bent molecular shape with O-H bond angle of approximately 104.5°. The oxygen atom is more electronegative, creating partial negative charge on O and partial positive charge on H, making water a polar molecule.

The attraction between the partially positive H of one water molecule and the partially negative O of another. Hydrogen bonds give water its unusually high boiling point (100°C), high surface tension, and high specific heat compared to other similar-sized molecules.

Water dissolves more substances than any other solvent due to its polarity. Polar substances (ionic compounds, polar molecules) dissolve readily in water; non-polar substances do not. 'Like dissolves like' — polarity determines solubility.

Soft water lathers readily with soap. Hard water contains dissolved Ca²⁺ or Mg²⁺ ions (from CaCO₃, CaSO₄, MgCl₂, etc.) which react with soap to form scum. Temporary hardness (from Ca(HCO₃)₂) can be removed by boiling; permanent hardness (from CaSO₄) requires ion exchange or adding Na₂CO₃.

Water reacts with: active metals (Na, K) to release H₂ and form hydroxides; metal oxides to form bases (CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂); non-metal oxides to form acids (CO₂ + H₂O → H₂CO₃); decomposes by electrolysis into H₂ and O₂.

The maximum amount of solute that dissolves in a given amount of solvent at a specified temperature. Solubility of most solids increases with temperature; solubility of gases decreases with temperature.

Water expands as it freezes (density decreases from 4°C to 0°C), unlike most substances. This means ice floats on water, lakes freeze from the top down, and aquatic life survives beneath ice in winter.

Passing electric current through acidified water or water with dilute H₂SO₄ decomposes it: 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂. Hydrogen collects at the cathode (double the volume of oxygen at anode), confirming H₂O's formula.

laws_and_formulas.name: Electrolysis of Water
laws_and_formulas.description: Decomposition of water by electrical energy; hydrogen and oxygen volumes are produced in a 2:1 ratio.

laws_and_formulas.name: Reaction with Sodium
laws_and_formulas.description: Active metals react vigorously with water to produce a metal hydroxide and hydrogen gas.

key_points: Water's formula is H₂O; it is a polar molecule with a bent shape (bond angle ~104.5°).
Hydrogen bonding between water molecules accounts for its anomalously high boiling point.
Water is the universal solvent — more substances dissolve in it than in any other liquid.
Hard water contains dissolved Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ ions; temporary hardness is removed by boiling, permanent by Na₂CO₃.
Water reacts with active metals to produce H₂ and metal hydroxide (a base).
Metal oxides + water → bases; Non-metal oxides + water → acids.
Electrolysis of water: cathode gives H₂, anode gives O₂, in 2:1 volume ratio.
Maximum density of water is at 4°C; above and below this temperature, water expands.
Ice floats because water expands on freezing — this is ecologically vital for aquatic life.
The pH of pure water is 7 (neutral); it can act as both acid and base (amphoteric).

9|chemistry|Water_Water_Pollution.overview: Water pollution occurs when harmful substances contaminate water bodies, making them unsafe for humans and aquatic life. Rapid industrialisation, agricultural practices, and urbanisation have severely degraded freshwater and marine ecosystems. This chapter examines the major types and sources of water pollution, their effects on health and ecology, and the urgent need for prevention. Understanding pollution mechanisms is the first step toward mitigation and environmental stewardship.

key_concepts.definition: The contamination of water bodies (rivers, lakes, groundwater, oceans) with substances that harm living organisms or the environment. Even naturally occurring substances can be pollutants when present in excess concentrations.

Industrial effluents, heavy metals (mercury, lead, arsenic, cadmium), pesticides, fertilisers, and detergents. Heavy metals bioaccumulate in the food chain. Mercury poisoning (Minamata disease) is a classic example.

Disease-causing microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, protozoa) from untreated sewage and animal waste contaminate drinking water. Cause cholera, typhoid, dysentery, hepatitis, and other waterborne diseases.
Discharge of heated water (from power plant cooling systems) into rivers reduces dissolved oxygen, harming fish and aquatic organisms that require cool, oxygen-rich water.

Runoff of fertilisers (rich in nitrates and phosphates) causes eutrophication — excessive algal growth depletes oxygen when algae decompose, creating dead zones. Pesticide runoff also contaminates water.

Excessive nutrient enrichment (nitrates, phosphates) leading to explosive algal bloom growth. When algae die and decompose, oxygen is depleted (BOD increases), killing fish and other aquatic life. A major consequence of agricultural runoff.

The amount of oxygen microorganisms need to decompose organic matter in water. High BOD indicates high organic pollution and low dissolved oxygen — a measure of water quality. Unpolluted water has BOD < 1 mg/L.

Accidental release of petroleum into oceans forms oil slicks that coat seabirds and marine mammals, smother bottom organisms, and block sunlight from reaching aquatic plants. Major ecological disasters with long-lasting effects.

key_points: Water pollution is classified as chemical, biological (pathogenic), radioactive, thermal, and physical (suspended solids, turbidity).
Sewage discharge is the largest source of biological pollution; cholera and typhoid are consequences.
Heavy metals (Hg, Pb, As, Cd) do not degrade; they bioaccumulate and biomagnify up the food chain.
Fertiliser runoff causes eutrophication — excessive algae growth → oxygen depletion → fish kills.
BOD is the key indicator of organic pollution: higher BOD = worse water quality.
Thermal pollution from power plants reduces dissolved oxygen and harms cold-water fish species.
Oil spills coat marine animals, destroying insulating properties of feathers and fur.
Acid mine drainage releases sulphuric acid and heavy metals into nearby water bodies.
Waterborne diseases (cholera, typhoid, dysentery, hepatitis A) spread through contaminated drinking water.
Industrial chemicals including PCBs and DDT persist in the environment and accumulate in fatty tissues.

9|chemistry|Water_Treatment_of_Water_Pol.overview: Treating polluted water to make it safe for drinking and returning it to the environment requires multiple physical and chemical processes. This chapter examines the stages of drinking water treatment — sedimentation, filtration, chlorination — and also wastewater (sewage) treatment. Additionally, it covers methods individuals and industries can use to reduce water pollution. Clean water supply and effective sewage treatment are fundamental to public health.

key_concepts.definition: The first stage of water treatment where large suspended particles are allowed to settle under gravity. Slow stirring, addition of alum (aluminium sulphate), or time allows heavier particles to accumulate at the bottom as sediment.

Addition of alum (Al₂(SO₄)₃) to turbid water causes colloidal particles to clump together (flocculation) into larger aggregates (flocs) that settle more easily. Alum is a common coagulant in water treatment.
Water passes through sand and gravel filter beds that remove remaining suspended particles, bacteria, and fine sediment. Slow sand filters also support microbial action that degrades organic matter.

Addition of chlorine (Cl₂) or chlorine compounds (e.g., sodium hypochlorite NaOCl, bleaching powder) to kill remaining pathogens. Cl₂ dissolves in water to form hypochlorous acid HOCl — the active disinfectant.
Spraying water in air or bubbling air through water removes dissolved gases (CO₂, H₂S), adds oxygen, and oxidises dissolved iron and manganese. Used in both drinking water and wastewater treatment.
Physical removal of large solids via screening and primary sedimentation. Primary sedimentation tanks allow settleable solids to form sludge. The clarified effluent proceeds to secondary treatment.
Biological treatment using microorganisms (bacteria) to decompose organic matter in the wastewater. Methods include activated sludge process and trickling filters. Reduces BOD significantly.

Individual actions: not dumping waste in water bodies, proper solid waste disposal. Industrial: effluent treatment plants before discharge. Agricultural: buffer strips, controlled fertiliser use. Policy: environmental regulations and monitoring.

laws_and_formulas.name: Chlorination Reaction
laws_and_formulas.description: Chlorine dissolves in water to form hypochlorous acid (HOCl), which kills bacteria and other pathogens.

key_points: Drinking water treatment: aeration → coagulation (alum) → sedimentation → filtration → chlorination.
Alum (Al₂(SO₄)₃) is added to cause coagulation and flocculation of suspended colloids.
Chlorination kills pathogens; residual chlorine in treated water continues to protect during distribution.
Slow sand filters are effective for removing fine particles and some microorganisms via biological action.
Sewage treatment: primary (physical) → secondary (biological) → optional tertiary (chemical).
BOD reduction is the main goal of secondary sewage treatment — biologically degrading organic matter.
Activated sludge process uses air + bacteria to aggressively decompose organic matter in sewage.
Treated sludge (biosolids) can be used as agricultural fertiliser after pathogen removal.
Tertiary treatment removes nitrates, phosphates, and residual pathogens — needed for reuse-quality water.
Zero liquid discharge (ZLD) is the industrial ideal where all wastewater is treated and recycled internally.

9|chemistry|Atomic_Structure_and_Chemica.overview: All matter is composed of atoms, and understanding atomic structure is central to all of chemistry. This chapter examines the subatomic particles (protons, neutrons, electrons), electronic configuration, atomic number, and mass number. The Bohr model of the atom — with electrons in discrete shells — explains the periodicity of elements and the basis of chemical bonding. Isotopes show that atoms of the same element can differ in neutron number.

key_concepts.definition: Proton (p⁺): positive charge, relative mass 1, in nucleus. Neutron (n): neutral charge, relative mass 1, in nucleus. Electron (e⁻): negative charge, negligible mass (1/1837 of proton), orbiting nucleus. Atoms are electrically neutral: protons = electrons.

The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom. Defines the element — every atom of the same element has the same atomic number. Z uniquely identifies the element. Hydrogen Z=1, Carbon Z=6, Oxygen Z=8.
The total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus: A = Z + N (where N = number of neutrons). Neutrons = A - Z. The mass number gives the approximate relative atomic mass.

The distribution of electrons in shells (energy levels) around the nucleus. Shell capacities: K(1st)=2, L(2nd)=8, M(3rd)=18 (maximum). For ICSE Class 9, electrons fill shells up to maximum before filling the next: 2,8,8,2 for Calcium.

The outermost occupied shell is the valence shell. Electrons in it are valence electrons. These directly determine chemical properties and bonding behaviour. A full outermost shell (2 or 8 electrons) confers stability.

Atoms of the same element (same atomic number, same protons) but with different numbers of neutrons (different mass numbers). Example: ¹H, ²H (deuterium), ³H (tritium) are isotopes of hydrogen. Isotopes have identical chemical properties but different physical properties.

Electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed circular paths (shells) at specific energy levels. An electron can jump to a higher shell by absorbing energy or fall to a lower shell by emitting energy (as light). This model explains atomic spectra.
The particles in the nucleus — protons and neutrons are collectively called nucleons. The nuclear force holds them together against the electrostatic repulsion between protons.

laws_and_formulas.name: Mass Number
laws_and_formulas.formula: A = Z + N (Z = protons, N = neutrons)
laws_and_formulas.description: The mass number equals the sum of protons and neutrons in the nucleus.

laws_and_formulas.name: Shell Capacity
laws_and_formulas.description: The maximum number of electrons that can occupy the nth energy level.

key_points: Atomic number = number of protons = number of electrons (in neutral atom).
Mass number = protons + neutrons; Neutrons = mass number - atomic number.
Electronic configuration: K shell (max 2), L shell (max 8), M shell (max 18), fill in order.
Isotopes have the same atomic number but different mass numbers — same element, different neutron count.
Valence electrons determine chemical reactivity and bonding behaviour.
Noble gases have completely filled outer shells — chemically inert (valence electrons = 8, or 2 for He).
The Bohr model successfully explains the hydrogen spectrum but is simplified — modern quantum model is more complete.
Carbon-12 (¹²C) is the standard reference for atomic mass: 1 amu = 1/12 mass of C-12.
Radioactive isotopes (radioisotopes) have unstable nuclei and emit radiation — used in medicine (C-14, I-131).
The number of electrons in the outermost shell equals the group number (for main group elements in the modern periodic table).

9|chemistry|Atomic_Structure_and_Chemica.overview: Chemical bonds hold atoms together in compounds. The two primary types are electrovalent (ionic) bonds, formed by electron transfer, and covalent bonds, formed by electron sharing. The type of bond formed depends on the electronegativity difference between atoms. Ionic compounds typically form between metals and non-metals; covalent compounds between non-metals. These bonding types explain the dramatically different physical properties of ionic vs. covalent substances.

key_concepts.definition: A chemical bond formed by the complete transfer of one or more electrons from a metal atom to a non-metal atom, creating oppositely charged ions held together by electrostatic attraction. Example: Na → Na⁺ + e⁻; Cl + e⁻ → Cl⁻; NaCl = Na⁺Cl⁻.

A bond formed by the sharing of one or more pairs of electrons between two non-metal atoms. Each atom contributes one electron to each shared pair. The shared electrons are attracted by both nuclei, holding atoms together.

Atoms form bonds to achieve 8 electrons in their outermost shell (2 for hydrogen). In ionic bonds, ions achieve octets through electron transfer. In covalent bonds, atoms share electrons to complete their octets.
A single bond shares 1 pair of electrons (H₂, HCl). A double bond shares 2 pairs (O₂, CO₂). A triple bond shares 3 pairs (N₂). More shared pairs mean a shorter, stronger bond.
High melting and boiling points (strong ionic lattice). Soluble in water (polar solvent). Conduct electricity when molten or in solution (free ions). Crystalline solid state. Hard but brittle.

Low melting and boiling points (weak intermolecular forces). Generally insoluble or poorly soluble in water (non-polar solvents dissolve them). Poor electrical conductors (no free ions). Can be gases, liquids, or soft solids at room temperature.

If both atoms sharing electrons have the same electronegativity (e.g., H₂, Cl₂), the bond is non-polar. If electronegativities differ (e.g., HCl, H₂O), the bond is polar — electrons are drawn more to the more electronegative atom.
Diagrams showing valence electrons as dots. Bonding pairs appear between atoms; lone pairs appear on individual atoms. Lewis structures help visualise how atoms share electrons in covalent compounds.

laws_and_formulas.name: Electrovalency Rule
laws_and_formulas.formula: Cation charge = electrons lost; Anion charge = electrons gained
laws_and_formulas.description: In ionic bonding, charge of each ion equals the number of electrons transferred.

laws_and_formulas.name: Bond Order
laws_and_formulas.formula: Bond order = number of shared electron pairs
laws_and_formulas.description: Single bond = 1; double bond = 2; triple bond = 3. Higher bond order means shorter and stronger bond.

key_points: Ionic bonds form between metals (lose electrons) and non-metals (gain electrons).
Covalent bonds form between non-metals that share electrons.
Large electronegativity difference (>1.7) → ionic bond; small difference → covalent bond.
Ionic compounds: high MP, conduct electricity when dissolved/molten, crystalline, hard but brittle.
Covalent compounds: low MP, don't conduct electricity, can be gases or liquids at room temperature.
NaCl: ionic; H₂O, CO₂, NH₃: covalent.
A coordinate (dative) bond is formed when both shared electrons come from one atom (e.g., NH₄⁺).
Triple bond in N₂ makes it very stable and unreactive at room temperature.
Polar covalent bonds create polar molecules with partial charges (δ+ and δ−).
Lewis dot structures show bonding pairs as lines (or dots) and lone pairs as dots on individual atoms.

9|chemistry|The_Periodic_Table_Dobereine.overview: The periodic table — the most powerful organising framework in chemistry — was developed through the work of many scientists over the 19th century. Döbereiner's Triads, Newlands' Law of Octaves, and finally Mendeleev's Periodic Law each represented important steps toward understanding the periodic relationship among elements. This historical progression shows how scientific understanding evolves through observation, pattern recognition, and revision.

key_concepts.definition: Döbereiner observed that certain groups of three elements (triads) had related properties, with the middle element's atomic mass being approximately the average of the other two. Example: Li (7), Na (23), K (39) — sodium's mass ≈ (7+39)/2 = 23. Limited to a few triads.

John Newlands arranged elements by increasing atomic mass and noted that every 8th element had properties similar to the first (like the octave in music). This worked for lighter elements but failed for heavier ones and left no room for undiscovered elements.

Dmitri Mendeleev arranged 63 known elements in order of increasing atomic mass, placing elements with similar properties in the same column (vertical group). Crucially, he left gaps for undiscovered elements and predicted their properties accurately.

Mendeleev predicted the existence and properties of several then-unknown elements: eka-aluminium (Gallium, discovered 1875), eka-boron (Scandium, 1879), and eka-silicon (Germanium, 1886). Their actual properties closely matched his predictions, validating the periodic law.
The physical and chemical properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic masses. This was later revised to atomic numbers (Moseley, 1913).

(1) Position of hydrogen (could be in Group 1 with alkali metals OR Group 17 with halogens). (2) Anomalous pairs: Ar before K, Co before Ni (atomic mass order reversed). (3) Isotopes had no clear place. (4) Later elements (0 group for noble gases) had no place initially.

Noble gases (He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn) were discovered after Mendeleev's original table. They were accommodated in a new Group 0 (later Group 18) without disrupting the existing arrangement — a testament to the table's flexibility.

Mendeleev's periodic table was the first truly predictive scientific framework in chemistry. It organised all known matter, revealed patterns in properties, and guided the discovery of new elements — arguably chemistry's greatest intellectual achievement.

laws_and_formulas.name: Döbereiner's Triad Rule
laws_and_formulas.formula: Atomic mass of middle element ≈ (mass of first + mass of third) / 2
laws_and_formulas.description: The middle element of Döbereiner's triad has an atomic mass approximately equal to the arithmetic mean of the outer two.

key_points: Döbereiner (1829): triads of elements with middle mass = average of outer two — limited to a few groups.
Newlands (1866): octave law — every 8th element similar; failed for heavier elements and left no room for new discoveries.
Mendeleev (1869): arranged by atomic mass, grouped similar properties vertically, left gaps, predicted missing elements.
Mendeleev's greatest achievement: predicting properties of undiscovered elements (Ga, Sc, Ge) that were later confirmed.
Mendeleev's periodic law: properties are periodic functions of atomic mass (later corrected to atomic number by Moseley).
Limitations: position of H, anomalous pairs (Ar/K, Co/Ni), isotopes, no place for noble gases initially.
Noble gases (Group 0) discovered later fit neatly into a new column — the table accommodated them.
Moseley (1913) showed atomic number (not mass) is the fundamental basis of periodicity.
The modern periodic table has 118 confirmed elements arranged in 7 periods and 18 groups.
The periodic table remains the single most important reference tool in chemistry.

9|chemistry|The_Periodic_Table_Modern_Pe.overview: The modern periodic table is based on the Modern Periodic Law (Moseley, 1913): properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic numbers. It has 118 elements arranged in 7 horizontal periods and 18 vertical groups. Trends in atomic radius, ionisation energy, electronegativity, metallic character, and valency can be predicted from position in the table. This chapter provides the framework for understanding all element chemistry systematically.

key_concepts.definition: The physical and chemical properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic numbers (not atomic masses). Proposed by Moseley (1913) after discovering X-ray spectra of elements are related to atomic number, resolving anomalies in Mendeleev's table.

Horizontal rows in the periodic table. There are 7 periods. Period number = number of electron shells. Period 1 has 2 elements; Period 2 and 3 have 8 each; Period 4 and 5 have 18 each; Periods 6 and 7 have 32 each.

Vertical columns in the periodic table (18 groups in the modern table). Elements in the same group have the same number of valence electrons and similar chemical properties. Old notation: I-VIII (IUPAC: 1-18).
Atomic radius decreases across a period (left to right) — more protons pull electrons closer. Atomic radius increases down a group — additional shells increase size despite increasing nuclear charge.

Ionisation energy (energy to remove the outermost electron) increases across a period (harder to remove from smaller atoms with more protons). Decreases down a group (outermost electrons further from nucleus and shielded).

Metallic character decreases across a period (left to right). Increases down a group. Thus, the most metallic elements are in the bottom-left of the table (Cs, Fr) and the least metallic in the top-right (F, O).
Electronegativity increases across a period and decreases down a group. Fluorine is the most electronegative element (4.0 on Pauling scale). Electronegativity determines bond polarity.

s-block: Groups 1-2 (valence electrons in s orbital); p-block: Groups 13-18 (in p orbital); d-block: Groups 3-12 (transition metals, d orbital); f-block: Lanthanides and Actinides (f orbital). Block determines the type of valence orbital being filled.

key_points: Modern periodic law: properties are periodic functions of ATOMIC NUMBER (not atomic mass).
Period = horizontal row = number of electron shells (energy levels) being filled.
Group = vertical column = same number of valence electrons = similar chemical properties.
Across a period: atomic radius ↓, ionisation energy ↑, electronegativity ↑, metallic character ↓.
Down a group: atomic radius ↑, ionisation energy ↓, electronegativity ↓, metallic character ↑.
Group 1 (alkali metals) are the most reactive metals; Group 17 (halogens) are the most reactive non-metals.
Group 18 (noble gases) have full outer shells and are the least reactive elements.
The diagonal in the periodic table separates metals (left/below) from non-metals (right/above); metalloids lie along the diagonal.
Transition metals (Groups 3-12) have variable valency and form coloured compounds.
Lanthanides and actinides (f-block) are placed below the main table to keep it compact.

9|chemistry|The_Periodic_Table_Study_of_.overview: Hydrogen is the most abundant element in the universe, yet it stands apart in the periodic table — it resembles both alkali metals (Group 1) and halogens (Group 17) in some properties. This chapter examines the unique position of hydrogen, its isotopes, preparation methods, physical and chemical properties, and uses. As the simplest atom and the lightest element, hydrogen plays a central role in atomic theory, stellar physics, and emerging energy technologies.

key_concepts.definition: Hydrogen has 1 valence electron like alkali metals, but also needs just 1 electron to complete its shell like halogens. It can be placed in Group 1 (loses 1 electron) or Group 17 (gains 1 electron). Most tables place it separately above Group 1.
Three isotopes: Protium ¹H (1p, 0n) — most abundant; Deuterium ²H (D) (1p, 1n) — found in heavy water D₂O; Tritium ³H (T) (1p, 2n) — radioactive, used in nuclear reactors.

Hydrogen is prepared in the lab by the action of dilute sulphuric acid (or dilute HCl) on zinc: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑. The gas is collected by downward displacement of water (water gas jars upside down as H₂ is lighter than water).
Colourless, odourless, tasteless gas. Lightest element (smallest atomic mass). Insoluble in water. Combustible but not a supporter of combustion (unlike oxygen). Burns with a 'pop' sound.

Hydrogen burns in air/oxygen to form water: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. The burning splint test for hydrogen: a burning wooden splint burns with a 'squeaky pop' sound when held at the mouth of a test tube of hydrogen.
Hydrogen reduces metal oxides to the pure metal: CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O. This demonstrates hydrogen's ability to remove oxygen from compounds. Used industrially to extract metals and produce ammonia.
Steam reforming of natural gas: CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (at high temperature, with Ni catalyst). Water-gas shift reaction: CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂. Electrolysis of brine also produces H₂ at the cathode.

Manufacture of ammonia (Haber process: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃); manufacture of methanol; hydrogenation of oils (margarine production); rocket fuel (liquid H₂ + liquid O₂); potential clean fuel (fuel cells produce water as the only product).

laws_and_formulas.name: Lab Preparation
laws_and_formulas.description: Zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid to produce hydrogen gas and zinc sulphate solution.

laws_and_formulas.name: Combustion of Hydrogen
laws_and_formulas.description: Hydrogen burns in oxygen to form water, releasing energy. Products are only water — making it an ideal clean fuel.

key_points: Hydrogen is unique: placed in Group 1 by convention but also resembles halogens (Group 17).
Three isotopes: protium (ordinary H), deuterium (D, heavy hydrogen), tritium (T, radioactive).
Lab preparation: Zn + dilute H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑ (collected by upward displacement of air).
Test for hydrogen: burning splint held at mouth of tube gives 'squeaky pop' sound.
Hydrogen is the lightest gas and is much less dense than air.
Hydrogen is combustible but NOT a supporter of combustion.
Industrial production: steam reforming of methane (CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂).
As a reducing agent, H₂ removes oxygen from metal oxides: CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O.
Haber process uses H₂ to make ammonia: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ (450°C, 200 atm, Fe catalyst).
Hydrogen fuel cells generate electricity from H₂ + O₂ with only water as the product — a clean energy technology.

9|chemistry|The_Periodic_Table_Hydrogen_.overview: Hydrogen can be generated from several sources — water, dilute acids, and alkaline solutions — through reactions with different metals. The reactivity of a metal determines which source can yield hydrogen from it. Very reactive metals react with cold water, moderately reactive metals react with dilute acids, and some metals react with alkalis. These reactions illustrate the activity series and the concept of hydrogen displacement. The reactions are important both as laboratory preparations and industrial processes.

key_concepts.definition: Group 1 metals (Na, K, Li) and Group 2 metals (Ca) react vigorously with cold water to produce hydrogen and metal hydroxide. 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂↑. Potassium reacts even more vigorously, igniting the hydrogen produced.
Moderately reactive metals (Mg, Al, Zn, Fe) react with steam (not cold water) to produce hydrogen and metal oxide. Fe + H₂O (steam) → FeO + H₂. Mg burns in steam: Mg + H₂O → MgO + H₂.

Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series react with dilute H₂SO₄ or HCl to release hydrogen: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑; Fe + 2HCl → FeCl₂ + H₂↑. Metals below hydrogen (Cu, Ag, Au) do not react with dilute acids.

Aluminium and zinc (amphoteric metals) react with strong alkalis (NaOH, KOH) to produce hydrogen: Zn + 2NaOH → Na₂ZnO₂ + H₂↑; 2Al + 2NaOH + 2H₂O → 2NaAlO₂ + 3H₂↑. This is used when acid cannot be employed.

A ranking of metals from most reactive (K, Na, Li) to least reactive (Au, Pt). Metals above hydrogen in the series can displace hydrogen from water or acids; those below cannot. The series: K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Ni > Sn > Pb > H > Cu > Hg > Ag > Au.

Metals like zinc (Zn) and aluminium (Al) that react with both acids and strong alkalis to release hydrogen. They can form both cationic and anionic species, making them uniquely versatile in chemistry.

Hydrogen is collected by upward displacement of air (it is lighter than air) or by downward displacement of water. A gas jar inverted over a beaker of water with the delivery tube feeding into it collects H₂ by water displacement.

K, Na: react violently with cold water; Ca: reacts readily with cold water; Mg: reacts slowly with cold water, faster with steam; Fe: reacts only with steam; Cu, Ag, Au: do not react with water or steam.

laws_and_formulas.name: Na with Water
laws_and_formulas.description: Sodium reacts vigorously with cold water to produce sodium hydroxide and hydrogen gas.

laws_and_formulas.name: Zn with Dilute H₂SO₄
laws_and_formulas.description: Zinc displaces hydrogen from dilute sulphuric acid — the standard lab preparation.

laws_and_formulas.name: Al with NaOH
laws_and_formulas.description: Aluminium (amphoteric) reacts with sodium hydroxide solution to release hydrogen.

key_points: Very reactive metals (K, Na, Ca): react with cold water → H₂ + metal hydroxide.
Moderately reactive metals (Mg, Al, Zn, Fe): react with steam → H₂ + metal oxide.
Reactive metals above H in activity series + dilute acids → H₂ + salt.
Metals below hydrogen (Cu, Ag, Au) do not displace hydrogen from dilute acids.
Amphoteric metals (Zn, Al) react with strong alkalis to release H₂.
Activity series: K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Pb > H > Cu > Ag > Au.
Potassium reacts so vigorously with water that the H₂ produced ignites spontaneously.
Hydrogen is lighter than air and is collected by upward displacement of air (or water displacement).
The squeaky pop test confirms hydrogen: a burning splint at the mouth of the tube ignites the gas.
Industrial hydrogen production uses steam reforming of natural gas (much cheaper than lab methods).

9|chemistry|Study_of_Gas_Laws_The_behavi.overview: Gases have unique properties — they are highly compressible and their volume changes significantly with temperature and pressure. The gas laws (Boyle's, Charles', Gay-Lussac's) describe these relationships quantitatively. Together they combine into the Combined Gas Law, and with the addition of Avogadro's Law, the Ideal Gas Law. These laws are fundamental to atmospheric science, engineering (engines, compressors), and understanding the behaviour of all gaseous substances.

key_concepts.definition: At constant temperature, the pressure of a given mass of gas is inversely proportional to its volume: P ∝ 1/V, or PV = constant. Doubling pressure halves volume. Demonstrated by compressing a gas in a closed cylinder.

At constant pressure, the volume of a given mass of gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature (Kelvin): V ∝ T, or V/T = constant. Heating a gas at constant pressure expands it proportionally.

At constant volume, the pressure of a given mass of gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature: P ∝ T, or P/T = constant. Heating a gas in a rigid container increases pressure proportionally.

The theoretical temperature at which the volume and pressure of an ideal gas become zero: 0 K = -273.15°C. At absolute zero, all molecular motion would cease. It is the zero point of the Kelvin temperature scale.

The absolute temperature scale where 0 K = -273.15°C and 273 K = 0°C. Always used in gas law calculations. Conversion: T(K) = T(°C) + 273. Gas laws require Kelvin because negative Kelvin temperatures are impossible.
Combines Boyle's and Charles' laws for a fixed mass of gas: P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂. Allows calculation of the new state of a gas when pressure, volume, and temperature all change simultaneously.
A reference condition for comparing gases: 0°C (273 K) and 1 atm (101.325 kPa) pressure. At STP, one mole of an ideal gas occupies 22.4 litres (the molar volume).

Gas particles have negligible volume; no intermolecular forces between particles; all collisions are elastic; average kinetic energy is proportional to absolute temperature. Real gases deviate from ideal behaviour at high pressures and low temperatures.

laws_and_formulas.name: Boyle's Law
laws_and_formulas.description: The product of pressure and volume is constant at constant temperature for a fixed mass of gas.

laws_and_formulas.name: Charles' Law
laws_and_formulas.description: The ratio of volume to absolute temperature is constant at constant pressure for a fixed mass of gas.

laws_and_formulas.name: Combined Gas Law
laws_and_formulas.description: Combines Boyle's and Charles' laws, valid for a fixed mass of gas undergoing any change in P, V, and T.

key_points: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant T → P₁V₁ = P₂V₂.
Charles' Law: V ∝ T (Kelvin) at constant P → V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂.
Always convert Celsius to Kelvin before using gas laws: K = °C + 273.
Combined gas law: P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂ — use when two of the three variables change.
At STP (0°C, 1 atm), 1 mole of ideal gas occupies 22.4 L.
Absolute zero (0 K = -273°C): theoretically minimum temperature where gas has zero volume and pressure.
Gay-Lussac's Law: P ∝ T at constant V → P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂ (car tyres get harder in summer).
Real gases deviate from ideal behaviour at high pressure (particle volume matters) and low temperature (intermolecular forces matter).
Divers' equipment, syringes, and weather balloons all involve gas law applications.
When using combined gas law, if one variable is constant, the other two ratios simplify to the individual gas law.

9|chemistry|Study_of_Gas_Laws_Relationsh.overview: Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles. Two common temperature scales — Celsius (°C) and Kelvin (K) — are related by a simple linear equation. The Kelvin scale is absolute: 0 K is the theoretical minimum temperature, making it essential for gas law calculations and thermodynamics. This chapter ensures students can confidently convert between scales and understand why Kelvin must be used in all quantitative gas law problems.

key_concepts.definition: The everyday temperature scale where 0°C = freezing point of water and 100°C = boiling point of water at 1 atm. Named after Anders Celsius (1742). Negative Celsius temperatures are common in cold climates.

The absolute temperature scale. 0 K = absolute zero (-273.15°C); 273.15 K = 0°C; 373.15 K = 100°C. Named after Lord Kelvin. Kelvin temperatures are always positive (or zero). The Kelvin is the SI unit of temperature.
T(K) = T(°C) + 273 (using the approximation -273.15°C ≈ -273°C). To convert K to °C: T(°C) = T(K) - 273. This simple relationship is critical for all gas law calculations.

0 K = -273.15°C. The theoretical temperature at which molecules have minimum kinetic energy. Cannot be achieved in practice but has been approached experimentally (within billionths of a degree). All molecular motion effectively ceases at 0 K.

Gas laws require proportional relationships (V ∝ T, P ∝ T). At -273°C, volume and pressure would be zero. If Celsius were used, negative temperatures would give nonsensical negative volumes or pressures. Kelvin avoids this by starting at the physical zero point.

Ice point: 0°C = 273 K. Steam point: 100°C = 373 K. Absolute zero: -273°C = 0 K. Body temperature: 37°C = 310 K. Room temperature: ~25°C = 298 K. These reference points help build intuition for both scales.
Used in the USA: 32°F = 0°C; 212°F = 100°C. Conversion: °F = (°C × 9/5) + 32. Not used in scientific work for chemistry or physics — Celsius and Kelvin are the scientific standards.

Mercury thermometers: liquid mercury expands with temperature. Range: -39°C to 357°C. Clinical thermometers measure body temperature (35-42°C). Absolute temperatures are measured using gas thermometers and other physical methods.

laws_and_formulas.name: Celsius to Kelvin
laws_and_formulas.description: Converts a Celsius temperature to Kelvin by adding 273 (or 273.15 for precision).

laws_and_formulas.name: Kelvin to Celsius
laws_and_formulas.description: Converts a Kelvin temperature to Celsius by subtracting 273.

key_points: K = °C + 273 (or +273.15 for precision); the scales differ only by the zero point.
0 K = -273°C = absolute zero (theoretical minimum temperature).
Always use Kelvin in gas law calculations — never use Celsius directly.
Ice point: 273 K = 0°C; Steam point: 373 K = 100°C.
The size of one degree is the same on both scales — only the zero point differs.
Negative Kelvin temperatures do not exist — Kelvin is always ≥ 0.
Body temperature (37°C) = 310 K; room temperature (25°C) = 298 K.
The Kelvin is the SI base unit for thermodynamic temperature.
In Charles' Law, doubling temperature in Kelvin doubles volume — this makes intuitive sense only in Kelvin.
Gas law problems always begin with a unit check: 'Is my temperature in Kelvin?'

9|chemistry|Atmospheric_Pollution_Acid_r.overview: Acid rain is precipitation (rain, snow, fog) with a pH lower than 5.6, caused by the dissolution of sulphur dioxide and nitrogen oxides in atmospheric water. These pollutants originate primarily from burning fossil fuels and industrial processes. Acid rain has devastating effects on ecosystems, buildings, soil, and human health. Understanding its chemistry and causes is essential for developing effective pollution control strategies.

key_concepts.definition: Normal rain has pH ~5.6 due to dissolved CO₂ forming carbonic acid (H₂CO₃). Acid rain has pH below 5.6 — sometimes as low as 4 or even less — due to dissolved SO₂ and NOₓ forming stronger acids.

Burning coal and petroleum (which contain sulphur impurities) releases SO₂: S + O₂ → SO₂. SO₂ is further oxidised: 2SO₂ + O₂ → 2SO₃. SO₃ dissolves in rainwater: SO₃ + H₂O → H₂SO₄ (sulphuric acid) — the primary cause of acid rain.

High-temperature combustion (car engines, power stations) converts N₂ + O₂ → 2NO. NO reacts with O₂: 2NO + O₂ → 2NO₂. NO₂ + H₂O → HNO₃ + HNO₂ (nitric acid). NOₓ contributes to acid rain and photochemical smog.

Acid rain lowers the pH of lakes and rivers (acidification). Fish and amphibians cannot survive below pH 5. Acidic water also leaches toxic aluminium ions from soil into water, killing fish even further. Lakes can become completely lifeless (dead lakes).

Acid rain damages plant leaves, slowing photosynthesis. It leaches nutrients (Ca, Mg) from soil, weakening trees. Acid soil releases toxic Al ions harmful to roots. Forests in Germany, Scandinavia, and North America have suffered severe damage (Waldsterben — forest death).

Marble and limestone buildings are attacked by acid rain: CaCO₃ + H₂SO₄ → CaSO₄ + H₂O + CO₂. CaSO₄ (gypsum) is soluble and washes away, eroding sculptures and historic monuments (Taj Mahal, Parthenon).

Acid rain itself causes skin and eye irritation. More importantly, SO₂ and NO₂ (the precursor gases) cause respiratory problems: asthma, bronchitis, and reduced lung function. Fine particulate matter (associated with these gases) penetrates deep into lungs.

Acid rain is a transboundary problem — pollutants from one country can travel thousands of kilometres in the atmosphere before being deposited. Prevailing winds carry pollution from industrial areas to pristine wilderness areas and other countries.

laws_and_formulas.name: Formation of H₂SO₄ in Atmosphere
laws_and_formulas.description: SO₂ from fossil fuel combustion oxidises to SO₃ in the atmosphere, then reacts with water to form sulphuric acid — a major component of acid rain.

laws_and_formulas.name: Limestone Attack by Acid Rain
laws_and_formulas.description: Acid rain (containing H₂SO₄) dissolves limestone and marble, causing irreversible damage to buildings and monuments.

key_points: Normal rain pH = 5.6 (dissolved CO₂); acid rain pH < 5.6, sometimes as low as 4.
Primary causes: SO₂ from coal burning + NOₓ from high-temperature combustion (vehicles, power plants).
SO₂ → SO₃ (catalysed by O₂) → H₂SO₄; NO₂ + H₂O → HNO₃.
Effects: acidification of lakes (fish die), leaching of soil nutrients, forest damage, corrosion of buildings.
Marble/limestone + H₂SO₄ → CaSO₄ (gypsum) + CO₂ — Taj Mahal suffers from this.
pH scale is logarithmic: pH 4 is 10× more acidic than pH 5.
Acid rain causes transboundary pollution — crosses national borders via wind.
Forest death (Waldsterben) in Europe was linked to acid rain in the 1970s-80s.
Control measures: scrubbers in power plant chimneys, catalytic converters in vehicles, using low-sulphur fuels.
Liming of acidified lakes (adding CaCO₃) temporarily neutralises acidity — a short-term remediation measure.

9|chemistry|Atmospheric_Pollution_Global.overview: Global warming refers to the long-term rise in Earth's average surface temperature primarily due to the enhanced greenhouse effect caused by increased concentrations of greenhouse gases from human activities. CO₂, methane, nitrous oxide, and water vapour trap heat radiated from Earth's surface. The consequences — rising sea levels, extreme weather events, biodiversity loss — make climate change the defining environmental challenge of our time. Chemistry explains both the mechanism and potential solutions.

key_concepts.definition: The natural process by which certain atmospheric gases (CO₂, H₂O vapour, CH₄, N₂O) absorb infrared radiation re-emitted by Earth's surface and re-radiate it back, warming the surface. Without this effect, Earth's average temperature would be -18°C instead of +15°C.

Human activities have increased concentrations of greenhouse gases, amplifying the natural greenhouse effect and causing additional warming beyond natural levels. CO₂ levels have risen from ~280 ppm (pre-industrial) to over 420 ppm today.

CO₂ (from fossil fuel combustion, deforestation), CH₄ (from livestock, paddy fields, natural gas leaks), N₂O (from fertilisers, combustion), HFCs (refrigerants), O₃ (in lower atmosphere), H₂O vapour (amplifies warming). CO₂ is the most significant anthropogenic greenhouse gas.

Burning coal, oil, and natural gas releases CO₂ stored over millions of years in a matter of decades: C + O₂ → CO₂; CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. The rate of release far exceeds natural absorption by oceans and forests.

Rising sea levels (thermal expansion of oceans + melting ice caps), more frequent and intense extreme weather events (hurricanes, droughts, floods), shifts in agricultural zones, coral reef bleaching, species extinction, and disruption of ocean circulation patterns.

Carbon continuously cycles between atmosphere, biosphere (plants, animals), oceans, and lithosphere (fossil fuels, rocks). Human combustion of fossil fuels and deforestation have disrupted the balance — inputs to atmosphere exceed removals.
A measure of how much heat a greenhouse gas traps relative to CO₂ over a specified period. CO₂ GWP = 1 (reference). CH₄ GWP = 25 (over 100 years). N₂O GWP = 298. Some HFCs have GWP > 10,000.

Transition to renewable energy (solar, wind); improving energy efficiency; carbon capture and storage (CCS); reforestation; reducing methane from agriculture; international agreements (Paris Agreement, IPCC recommendations).

key_points: Greenhouse gases absorb and re-emit infrared (heat) radiation, warming Earth's surface.
Natural greenhouse effect makes Earth habitable (+33°C warming); enhanced effect causes additional warming.
CO₂ is the most significant anthropogenic greenhouse gas; CH₄ is more potent per molecule (GWP = 25).
Burning fossil fuels releases CO₂ stored for millions of years — the rate far exceeds natural absorption.
Consequences: sea level rise, extreme weather, ecosystem disruption, food security threats.
CO₂ concentration: ~280 ppm (1750) → >420 ppm (2024) — a 50% increase.
Deforestation reduces CO₂ absorption by trees and adds CO₂ from burning or decomposition.
Paris Agreement (2015): international commitment to limit warming to 1.5-2°C above pre-industrial levels.
Carbon footprint: total CO₂ (and equivalent) emissions from an activity, product, or person.
Renewable energy, electric vehicles, and energy efficiency are key technological solutions.

9|chemistry|Atmospheric_Pollution_Ozone_.overview: The ozone layer in the stratosphere (15-35 km altitude) absorbs harmful ultraviolet (UV-B and UV-C) radiation from the Sun, protecting all life on Earth. Industrial chemicals — particularly chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) — catalytically destroy ozone molecules. The discovery of the Antarctic ozone hole in the 1980s galvanised international action, leading to the Montreal Protocol (1987), widely considered the most successful global environmental agreement. This chapter examines the chemistry of ozone depletion and its implications.

key_concepts.definition: An allotrope of oxygen with three oxygen atoms. In the stratosphere (ozone layer), O₃ absorbs UV-B (280-315 nm) and UV-C (100-280 nm) radiation. At ground level, ozone is a pollutant causing respiratory problems and smog.
UV radiation splits O₂ molecules: O₂ + UV → 2O•. Oxygen radicals combine with O₂: O• + O₂ → O₃. Ozone also absorbs UV: O₃ + UV → O₂ + O•. This dynamic equilibrium maintains the ozone layer.

Synthetic organic compounds containing carbon, chlorine, and fluorine (e.g., CCl₂F₂ — Freon). Used as refrigerants, propellants, and foam-blowing agents. Chemically very stable in the lower atmosphere but break down in the stratosphere under UV light, releasing chlorine radicals.

UV breaks CFCs in stratosphere: CCl₂F₂ → CClF₂• + Cl•. Chlorine radical attacks ozone: Cl• + O₃ → ClO• + O₂. ClO• + O → Cl• + O₂. The Cl• is regenerated — one Cl atom can destroy 100,000 ozone molecules catalytically.

A region of the stratosphere with severely depleted ozone concentration. First detected over Antarctica in the 1980s. Severe depletion occurs in polar spring due to unique atmospheric conditions (polar vortex, ice cloud surfaces that accelerate CFC chemistry).

Without ozone protection: increased UV-B causes skin cancer, cataracts, immune system suppression, coral bleaching, reduced plant productivity, and damage to phytoplankton (base of ocean food chain). Even a 1% decrease in ozone increases UV by ~2%.

An international treaty to phase out ozone-depleting substances (CFCs, HCFCs, halons). Ratified by all 197 UN member states — the only universally ratified UN treaty. CFCs are being replaced by HFCs (which don't deplete ozone but have high GWP for climate) and now by HFOs.

Due to the Montreal Protocol, the ozone layer is slowly recovering — projected to return to pre-1980 levels by ~2065. This is one of the greatest successes of international environmental cooperation and demonstrates that global action can solve global problems.

laws_and_formulas.name: Ozone Formation
laws_and_formulas.description: Ultraviolet radiation splits oxygen molecules into atoms that combine with other oxygen molecules to form ozone.

laws_and_formulas.name: Catalytic Ozone Destruction by Cl
laws_and_formulas.formula: Cl• + O₃ → ClO• + O₂; ClO• + O → Cl• + O₂ (net: O₃ + O → 2O₂)
laws_and_formulas.description: Chlorine radicals catalytically destroy ozone — each Cl atom can destroy up to 100,000 O₃ molecules before being removed.

key_points: The ozone layer in the stratosphere absorbs harmful UV-B and UV-C radiation.
CFCs are the primary cause of ozone depletion — they release Cl radicals in the stratosphere.
One Cl radical can destroy up to 100,000 ozone molecules catalytically (not consumed in the reaction).
The Antarctic ozone hole forms each polar spring due to polar vortex conditions.
Increased UV-B causes: skin cancer (melanoma), cataracts, immune suppression, phytoplankton reduction.
Montreal Protocol (1987) — internationally agreed phase-out of CFCs — universally ratified.
The ozone layer is recovering due to Montreal Protocol; projected full recovery by ~2065.
Ground-level ozone (O₃) is a pollutant (smog); stratospheric ozone is a protective shield — same molecule, different context.
HFCs replaced CFCs as refrigerants (no ozone depletion) but have high GWP — now being replaced by HFOs.
Ozone recovery is a demonstration that international environmental agreements can successfully address global problems.

9|biology|Basic_Biology_The_cell.overview: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms. Class 9 covers the detailed structure of plant and animal cells, organelles and their functions, and the differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.

key_concepts.definition: Selectively permeable phospholipid bilayer surrounding all cells; controls entry and exit of substances.
Rigid outer layer of plant cells made of cellulose; provides shape, support, and protection.
Control centre of the cell; contains DNA (chromosomes) that carries genetic information; surrounded by nuclear membrane.
Site of aerobic respiration (ATP production); called the 'powerhouse of the cell'; has its own DNA.
Found in plant cells; site of photosynthesis; contains green pigment chlorophyll; has its own DNA.
Network of membrane-bound tubes; Rough ER (with ribosomes) makes proteins; Smooth ER makes lipids.
Modifies, packages, and distributes proteins from ER to their destinations (secretion or cell membrane).

key_points: Plant cells have: cell wall (cellulose), chloroplasts, large central vacuole, plasmodesmata — absent in animal cells.
Animal cells have: centrioles (for cell division), smaller/no vacuoles — absent in plant cells.
Prokaryotes (bacteria): no nucleus, no membrane-bound organelles, smaller, circular DNA.
Eukaryotes (plants, animals, fungi, protists): true nucleus, membrane-bound organelles.
Ribosomes: tiny organelles where protein synthesis occurs; present in ALL living cells (prokaryote and eukaryote).

9|biology|Basic_Biology_Tissues.overview: Tissues are groups of similar cells performing the same function. Plants have meristematic and permanent tissues; animals have four tissue types. Understanding tissues bridges cells and organs.

key_concepts.definition: Plant tissue with actively dividing cells; found at growing tips (apical meristem), sides of stems (lateral — vascular cambium, cork cambium).
Simple permanent plant tissue; thin-walled, loosely packed; functions: photosynthesis, storage, packing.
Plant tissue with thickened corners; provides flexible support (found in leaf stalks, young stems).
Dead cells with thick, lignified walls; provides rigid support (fibres, sclereids).
Vascular plant tissue conducting water and minerals upward; dead cells (tracheids, vessel elements).
Vascular plant tissue conducting food (sucrose) in both directions; living cells (sieve tubes, companion cells).
Animal tissue covering body surfaces and lining organs; protection, absorption, secretion.

key_points: Animal tissue types: Epithelial, Connective, Muscular, Nervous.
Connective tissue: blood (liquid), bone, cartilage, adipose — all have cells in a matrix.
Muscular: skeletal (voluntary, striated), smooth (involuntary, unstriated), cardiac (involuntary, striated).
Neurons: functional units of nervous tissue; dendrites receive, axon transmits electrical signals.
Blood is a connective tissue: plasma + red blood cells (haemoglobin) + white blood cells + platelets.

9|biology|Basic_Biology_Flowering_Plants.overview: Flowering plants (angiosperms) are the dominant plant group. Their vegetative organs (root, stem, leaf) and reproductive organs (flower, fruit, seed) are studied in detail for structure-function relationships.

key_concepts.definition: Below-ground organ system; absorbs water and minerals, anchors plant, stores food (tap roots in carrots).
Above-ground system: stem (support, transport), leaves (photosynthesis, gas exchange, transpiration).
Epidermis (upper + lower), mesophyll (palisade + spongy), veins (xylem + phloem), stomata (on lower epidermis).
Small pores on leaf surface controlled by guard cells; allow gas exchange (CO₂/O₂) and water loss (transpiration).
Loss of water vapour through stomata; creates tension that pulls water up xylem (transpiration pull).

key_points: Palisade mesophyll: tightly packed, many chloroplasts — maximum photosynthesis.
Spongy mesophyll: loosely packed with air spaces — gas exchange.
Guard cells: turgid (swollen) → stomata open; flaccid → stomata closed. Opens in light, closes in dark.
Monocot vs Dicot: 1 vs 2 cotyledons; parallel vs reticulate veins; fibrous vs tap roots; 3s vs 4-5s floral parts.
Leaf modifications: spines (protection — cactus), tendrils (climbing — pea), pitcher (carnivorous — Nepenthes).

9|biology|Basic_Biology_Vegetative_Propa.overview: Vegetative propagation is asexual reproduction in plants — new plants grow from vegetative parts (stem, root, leaf) of the parent. It produces genetically identical offspring (clones) and is widely used in horticulture.

key_concepts.definition: Occurs naturally: stolons (runners) in strawberry, rhizomes in ginger/turmeric, tubers in potato, bulbs in onion/garlic, corms in taro.
Human-assisted: cuttings (rose, sugarcane), layering (jasmine), grafting (mango, apple), budding.
A stem section with nodes placed in soil; roots develop from nodes; quick and inexpensive.
Joining the stem of one plant (scion — desired variety) to the rootstock of another; combines benefits of both.
Growing plants from tiny tissue pieces in sterile nutrient medium; produces thousands of identical plants quickly.

key_points: Advantages of vegetative propagation: faster than seed propagation, offspring identical to parent (uniform crop), can propagate plants that produce few/no seeds.
Disadvantage: no genetic variation — susceptible to same diseases.
Potato tubers have 'eyes' (nodes) from which new plants grow.
Budding: a bud from one plant is grafted onto rootstock; used in roses.
Tissue culture is used to produce virus-free plants and preserve rare species.

9|biology|Basic_Biology_Flower_Pollinati.overview: A flower is the reproductive organ of angiosperms. Pollination transfers pollen to the stigma; fertilisation fuses gametes to form a zygote. Double fertilisation is unique to flowering plants.

key_concepts.definition: Sepals (K), Petals (C), Stamens = anther + filament (A), Carpel/Pistil = stigma + style + ovary (G).
Transfer of pollen from anther to stigma — self-pollination (same plant) or cross-pollination (different plant of same species).
One sperm fuses with egg cell → zygote (embryo); another fuses with two polar nuclei → triploid endosperm (food for embryo).
After fertilisation: ovary → fruit (pericarp); ovule → seed; zygote → embryo; endosperm → food store.

key_points: Wind-pollinated flowers: small, dull, no scent, large pollen, feathery stigma (grass, maize, rice).
Insect-pollinated flowers: large, colourful, scented, sticky/rough pollen, honey guides (sunflower, rose).
After fertilisation: ovule becomes seed; ovary becomes fruit.
The seed contains: embryo (radicle + plumule + cotyledons) + endosperm (food) + seed coat (testa).
Parthenocarpy: fruit development without fertilisation → seedless fruits (banana, seedless grapes).

9|biology|Plant_Physiology_Structure_of_.overview: Seeds are mature ovules containing an embryo, food stores, and a protective coat. Dicot seeds (two cotyledons) and monocot seeds (one cotyledon) have distinct structures important for germination and agriculture.

key_concepts.definition: Outer protective covering; may be hard (bean) or papery (pea); protects embryo from damage and desiccation.
