Key properties include: adding or subtracting the same quantity from both sides preserves the inequality; multiplying or dividing by a positive number preserves the inequality; multiplying or dividing by a negative number reverses the inequality. These mirror equation properties except for the sign-reversal rule.

When the replacement set is integers (Z), the solution to an inequation is listed as discrete values. For -2 < x <= 4 in integers, the solution is {-1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4}. Careful reading of strict vs. non-strict boundaries determines inclusion of endpoints.

laws_and_formulas.name: Transposition Rule
laws_and_formulas.formula: If a < b, then a + c < b + c and a - c < b - c
laws_and_formulas.description: Adding or subtracting the same real number from both sides preserves the inequality

laws_and_formulas.name: Multiplication by Positive
laws_and_formulas.formula: If a < b and c > 0, then ac < bc
laws_and_formulas.description: Multiplying both sides by a positive number preserves the inequality direction

laws_and_formulas.name: Multiplication by Negative (Sign Reversal)
laws_and_formulas.formula: If a < b and c < 0, then ac > bc
laws_and_formulas.description: Multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative number reverses the inequality

laws_and_formulas.name: Compound Inequation Solution
laws_and_formulas.formula: a < f(x) < b => solve a < f(x) AND f(x) < b separately, then intersect
laws_and_formulas.description: Used to find the solution set of a combined double-sided inequality

key_points: Always reverse the inequality sign when multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative number
An open circle on the number line represents a strict inequality (< or >); a filled circle represents a non-strict inequality (<= or >=)
The solution set over real numbers is an interval; over integers or natural numbers it is a finite or infinite list of discrete values
When solving compound inequations joined by 'and', the solution is the intersection of the two individual solution sets
Transposing a positive term from one side changes its sign but does not reverse the inequality
To compare two inequation results, express both solution sets on a number line and identify common regions
Replacement set determines the nature of the solution: natural numbers give only positive integer solutions, integers include zero and negatives
ICSE problems frequently ask for the greatest or smallest integer satisfying the inequation; identify boundary endpoints carefully
Dividing by zero is undefined and must never appear in a solution step
When the solution set is empty (no values satisfy both conditions), state explicitly that the solution set is the null set (empty set)

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_1_Quadratic_.overview: Quadratic Equations in One Variable deals with polynomial equations of degree 2 of the form ax^2 + bx + c = 0, where a is not zero. Students learn three solution methods: factorisation, completing the square, and the quadratic formula. The nature of roots is determined by the discriminant D = b^2 - 4ac, which reveals whether roots are real and distinct, real and equal, or complex (non-real). The chapter also covers forming quadratic equations from given roots using the sum and product relationships.

key_concepts.definition: A polynomial equation of degree 2 in one variable, written in standard form as ax^2 + bx + c = 0 where a, b, c are real numbers and a != 0. Examples include x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0 and 2x^2 + 3x - 2 = 0. Every quadratic equation has exactly two roots (counting multiplicity).

The expression D = b^2 - 4ac computed from the coefficients of ax^2 + bx + c = 0. It determines the nature of roots: if D > 0, roots are real and unequal; if D = 0, roots are real and equal; if D < 0, roots are non-real (complex conjugates). For x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0, D = 25 - 24 = 1 > 0, so roots are real and distinct.

Solving a quadratic by expressing it as a product of two linear factors and applying the zero-product property. For x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0, factorise as (x - 2)(x - 3) = 0, giving x = 2 or x = 3. This method works cleanly when the discriminant is a perfect square.

A method of rewriting ax^2 + bx + c = 0 by adding and subtracting (b/2a)^2 to form a perfect square trinomial. For x^2 + 4x - 5 = 0: (x + 2)^2 - 4 - 5 = 0, so (x + 2)^2 = 9, giving x + 2 = +/-3, hence x = 1 or x = -5. This method derives the quadratic formula.

The universal formula for solving ax^2 + bx + c = 0: x = (-b +/- sqrt(b^2 - 4ac)) / (2a). For 2x^2 - 3x - 2 = 0, x = (3 +/- sqrt(9 + 16)) / 4 = (3 +/- 5) / 4, giving x = 2 or x = -1/2. This formula works for all quadratics.

For ax^2 + bx + c = 0 with roots alpha and beta: sum of roots = alpha + beta = -b/a and product of roots = alpha x beta = c/a. For x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0 (a=1, b=-5, c=6), sum = 5 and product = 6, verified since 2 + 3 = 5 and 2 x 3 = 6.

If alpha and beta are given roots, the quadratic equation is x^2 - (alpha + beta)x + (alpha x beta) = 0. For roots 3 and -4: x^2 - (3 + (-4))x + (3 x (-4)) = 0, i.e., x^2 + x - 12 = 0. This uses the converse of Vieta formulas.
If the product of two factors is zero, at least one factor must be zero. That is, if A x B = 0, then A = 0 or B = 0. This property is the logical foundation for solving quadratics by factorisation.

The values of x that satisfy ax^2 + bx + c = 0, also called solutions or zeros. A quadratic always has exactly two roots (which may be equal or complex). Real roots correspond to x-intercepts of the parabola y = ax^2 + bx + c.

laws_and_formulas.name: Standard Form
laws_and_formulas.description: The general form of a quadratic equation; all solving methods begin by rearranging into this form

laws_and_formulas.name: Quadratic Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Universal formula to find both roots of any quadratic equation

laws_and_formulas.name: Discriminant
laws_and_formulas.description: Determines nature of roots: D > 0 (real, distinct), D = 0 (real, equal), D < 0 (non-real)

laws_and_formulas.name: Sum of Roots
laws_and_formulas.description: Relationship between roots and coefficients; used to verify solutions or form new equations

laws_and_formulas.name: Product of Roots
laws_and_formulas.description: Relationship between roots and coefficients; used in conjunction with sum of roots

laws_and_formulas.name: Quadratic from Roots
laws_and_formulas.description: Forms a quadratic equation when both roots are known

key_points: Always rearrange the equation to standard form ax^2 + bx + c = 0 before applying any solving method
Factorisation is the fastest method when the discriminant is a perfect square and roots are rational
The quadratic formula works universally but can be slower for simple equations; use it when factorisation is not obvious
Completing the square is the algebraic basis from which the quadratic formula is derived
When D = 0, the equation has two equal real roots (a repeated root): x = -b/(2a)
When D < 0, roots are complex conjugates; at ICSE level this means the equation has no real solutions
Sum of roots = -b/a and product of roots = c/a are powerful shortcuts for verification and for forming new equations
To form a quadratic with given roots, write x^2 minus (sum of roots) times x plus (product of roots) = 0
Problems involving word problems reduce to setting up a quadratic; always check that both roots make physical sense in context
A quadratic can have at most two distinct roots; if three values seem to satisfy it, an error has been made

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_1_Ratio_and_.overview: Ratio and Proportion builds on foundational arithmetic to explore relationships between quantities using advanced algebraic properties. Students learn componendo, dividendo, alternendo, and invertendo as tools to manipulate ratios without solving for individual variables. The concept of continued proportion (a:b = b:c) introduces the geometric mean and is applied to find missing quantities. Cross-multiplication, the k-method, and the properties of equal ratios enable elegant solutions to problems that would otherwise require simultaneous equations.

key_concepts.definition: A ratio a:b compares two quantities a and b of the same kind, expressed as the fraction a/b where b != 0. Ratios are always simplified to lowest terms. For example, 15:25 simplifies to 3:5 by dividing both terms by their HCF of 5.

A proportion states that two ratios are equal: a:b = c:d, equivalently written as a/b = c/d. The product of means equals the product of extremes: b x c = a x d (cross-multiplication). For example, if x/4 = 6/8, then 8x = 24, so x = 3.

Three quantities a, b, c are in continued proportion if a:b = b:c, meaning b^2 = ac. Here b is the geometric mean of a and c. For example, if 3, x, 12 are in continued proportion, then x^2 = 36, so x = 6.

The mean proportion between a and c is the value b such that a:b = b:c, giving b = sqrt(ac). For the mean proportion between 4 and 16: b = sqrt(64) = 8. Mean proportion always equals the geometric mean.
If a/b = c/d, then (a + b)/b = (c + d)/d. This allows the numerator sum to be related directly. If x/y = 3/5, then (x + y)/y = 8/5.
If a/b = c/d, then (a - b)/b = (c - d)/d. This gives a subtracted relationship. For a/b = 7/4, (a - b)/b = 3/4.

A combined operation: if a/b = c/d, then (a + b)/(a - b) = (c + d)/(c - d). This is among the most frequently used tools in ICSE proportion problems and eliminates the need to find a and b individually.
If a/b = c/d, then a/c = b/d (swapping the means). This rearrangement is valid because the cross-product remains equal.
If a/b = c/d, then b/a = d/c (inverting both ratios). Combined with other operations, invertendo enables systematic manipulation of complex proportion expressions.

Setting each equal ratio to a constant k simplifies problems involving equal ratios. If a/b = c/d = e/f = k, then a = bk, c = dk, e = fk. Substituting and summing: (a + c + e)/(b + d + f) = k. Useful when multiple ratios are equal.

laws_and_formulas.name: Cross-Multiplication
laws_and_formulas.description: Fundamental property of proportion used to solve for unknowns

laws_and_formulas.name: Continued Proportion
laws_and_formulas.description: Defines the geometric mean relationship; b is the mean proportional between a and c

laws_and_formulas.name: Componendo and Dividendo
laws_and_formulas.description: Most powerful ratio manipulation tool; eliminates the need to find individual values

laws_and_formulas.name: Componendo
laws_and_formulas.description: Adds 1 to both sides of the ratio

laws_and_formulas.name: Dividendo
laws_and_formulas.description: Subtracts 1 from both sides of the ratio

key_points: In a proportion a:b = c:d, the extremes are a and d; the means are b and c; product of means = product of extremes
Componendo and dividendo is the single most important tool for ICSE proportion problems; learn to recognise when to apply it
For continued proportion a, b, c: b is the geometric mean and b^2 = ac; always write this relationship first
The k-method assumes each ratio equals k; substitute and simplify using algebraic identities to prove the required result
Alternendo swaps the inner terms of the proportion, useful when the required ratio has the same denominator style as the alternate form
Invertendo simply inverts both fractions; often used as a first step before applying componendo or dividendo
When comparing two ratios, convert both to equivalent fractions with a common denominator or cross-multiply to compare
Ratio problems often involve finding a third quantity given two ratios sharing a common term; adjust terms so the common part is equal
ICSE questions frequently ask to prove a result using componendo-dividendo; start from the given ratio and apply operations step by step
If a:b:c is a continued ratio (a/b = b/c = c/d), it is an extended form; use the k-method to express all terms in terms of k

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_1_Factorisat.overview: Factorisation of Polynomials focuses on the Factor Theorem and Remainder Theorem as tools for factorising cubic and higher-degree polynomials. Students learn that if p(a) = 0, then (x - a) is a factor of p(x). By finding one root through trial substitution, they reduce a cubic to a product of a linear and quadratic factor, then factorise further if possible. The Remainder Theorem states that dividing p(x) by (x - a) gives a remainder of p(a), which enables quick evaluation without long division.

key_concepts.definition: An algebraic expression consisting of terms with non-negative integer exponents, such as p(x) = 2x^3 - 3x^2 + x - 5. The degree of a polynomial is the highest power of x. At Class 10 ICSE level, polynomials up to degree 3 (cubic) are factorised.

When a polynomial p(x) is divided by (x - a), the remainder is p(a). No actual division is needed; substitute x = a into p(x). For p(x) = x^3 - 2x + 1 divided by (x - 1): remainder = p(1) = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0, confirming (x - 1) is a factor.
A special case of the Remainder Theorem: (x - a) is a factor of p(x) if and only if p(a) = 0. To check if (x - 2) is a factor of x^3 - 3x^2 + 4: p(2) = 8 - 12 + 4 = 0, so (x - 2) is a factor.

A shorthand method for dividing a polynomial by a linear factor (x - a), using only the coefficients. It reduces the computation of long division significantly. At ICSE level, students more commonly use long division or inspection after confirming a factor via the Factor Theorem.

The standard algorithm for dividing one polynomial by another, analogous to numerical long division. Used after finding one factor to reduce the degree of the polynomial. Dividing x^3 - 7x + 6 by (x - 1) gives x^2 + x - 6, which further factors as (x + 3)(x - 2).

To factorise a cubic p(x): (1) find a value a such that p(a) = 0 by trial; (2) write (x - a) as a factor; (3) divide p(x) by (x - a) to get a quadratic; (4) factorise the quadratic. For p(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6, p(1) = 0 so (x - 1) is a factor, leading to (x-1)(x-2)(x-3).

For a polynomial with integer coefficients, any rational root p/q (in lowest terms) has p as a factor of the constant term and q as a factor of the leading coefficient. For 2x^3 - 3x^2 - 2x + 3, rational root candidates are +/-1, +/-3, +/-1/2, +/-3/2.

Standard identities such as a^3 + b^3 = (a + b)(a^2 - ab + b^2) and a^3 - b^3 = (a - b)(a^2 + ab + b^2) are used directly to factorise sum and difference of cubes without division. Recognising the pattern is the key skill.

laws_and_formulas.name: Remainder Theorem
laws_and_formulas.formula: Remainder when p(x) is divided by (x - a) = p(a)
laws_and_formulas.description: Quickly finds the remainder without performing polynomial division

laws_and_formulas.name: Factor Theorem
laws_and_formulas.formula: (x - a) is a factor of p(x) if and only if p(a) = 0
laws_and_formulas.description: Used to identify factors by substituting trial values

laws_and_formulas.name: Sum of Cubes
laws_and_formulas.description: Direct factorisation of expressions of the form x^3 + k^3

laws_and_formulas.name: Difference of Cubes
laws_and_formulas.description: Direct factorisation of expressions of the form x^3 - k^3

key_points: The Remainder Theorem states that the remainder of p(x) / (x - a) equals p(a); no actual division is required to find the remainder
The Factor Theorem is a corollary: if p(a) = 0 then (x - a) is a factor; check using small integers first (0, 1, -1, 2, -2)
Once one linear factor is found, perform polynomial long division to reduce the cubic to a quadratic, then factorise the quadratic
Try integer values of a that are factors of the constant term divided by the leading coefficient when searching for rational roots
If p(a) = 0 and p(-a) = 0, both (x - a) and (x + a) are factors; their product (x^2 - a^2) may be extracted directly
Sum and difference of cubes identities should be memorised for direct application without needing the Factor Theorem
After factorising a cubic, verify by expanding the three linear factors to confirm you recover the original polynomial
For problems that ask to find k given that (x - a) is a factor, set p(a) = 0 and solve for k
A cubic polynomial has exactly three roots (counting multiplicity); all three can be real or one real and two complex conjugates
The leading coefficient of the quotient after division equals the leading coefficient of the original polynomial

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_1_Matrices.overview: Matrices introduces students to rectangular arrays of numbers and the operations defined on them. The chapter covers matrix addition, subtraction, scalar multiplication, and matrix multiplication, with an emphasis on when these operations are defined. Students learn that matrix multiplication is not commutative (AB is generally not equal to BA) and explore special matrices including the identity matrix, zero matrix, and the transpose. ICSE problems frequently involve 2x2 matrix computations and setting up matrix equations to find unknown elements.

key_concepts.definition: A rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns, enclosed in brackets. A matrix with m rows and n columns has order m x n. For example, A = [[1, 2], [3, 4]] is a 2x2 matrix. The element in row i and column j is denoted a_ij.

Described as m x n (rows x columns). A matrix [[2, -1, 3], [0, 5, 1]] has order 2x3, meaning 2 rows and 3 columns. Two matrices are equal only if they have the same order and corresponding elements are equal.
Defined only for matrices of the same order, performed element-wise. If A = [[1, 2], [3, 4]] and B = [[5, 6], [7, 8]], then A + B = [[6, 8], [10, 12]]. Matrices of different orders cannot be added.

Multiplying every element of a matrix by a scalar (constant). If k = 3 and A = [[1, -2], [0, 4]], then kA = [[3, -6], [0, 12]]. Scalar multiplication changes the magnitude of elements but preserves the matrix order.

The product AB is defined only when the number of columns of A equals the number of rows of B. For A (m x n) and B (n x p), the product AB has order m x p. Each element of AB is computed as the dot product of a row of A with a column of B. Matrix multiplication is not commutative: AB is not necessarily equal to BA.
A square matrix with 1s on the main diagonal and 0s elsewhere, denoted I. For 2x2: I = [[1, 0], [0, 1]]. It acts as the multiplicative identity: AI = IA = A for any square matrix A of the same order.
A matrix with all elements equal to zero, denoted O. For any matrix A, A + O = A and A x O = O (when multiplication is defined). The zero matrix is the additive identity for matrix addition.
The transpose of A, denoted A^T, is obtained by interchanging rows and columns. If A = [[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]], then A^T = [[1, 4], [2, 5], [3, 6]]. A square matrix is symmetric if A = A^T.

Two matrices are equal if and only if they have the same order and every corresponding pair of elements is equal. Used in ICSE problems to set up equations: if [[x+1, 2], [3, y-4]] = [[5, 2], [3, 2]], then x + 1 = 5 and y - 4 = 2, giving x = 4 and y = 6.

laws_and_formulas.name: Matrix Multiplication Condition
laws_and_formulas.formula: A(m x n) x B(n x p) = C(m x p)
laws_and_formulas.description: Multiplication is defined only when inner dimensions match; the product has the outer dimensions

laws_and_formulas.name: Element of Product Matrix
laws_and_formulas.formula: c_ij = sum over k of a_ik x b_kj
laws_and_formulas.description: Each element of the product matrix is the dot product of row i of A and column j of B

laws_and_formulas.name: Non-commutativity
laws_and_formulas.formula: AB is not equal to BA in general
laws_and_formulas.description: Matrix multiplication order matters; always compute products in the specified order

laws_and_formulas.name: Transpose of Product
laws_and_formulas.description: The transpose of a product reverses the order of the matrices

laws_and_formulas.name: Scalar Multiple
laws_and_formulas.description: Every element of the matrix is multiplied by the scalar k

key_points: Matrix addition is defined only for matrices of the same order; always verify orders before adding or subtracting
Matrix multiplication AB is defined only when the number of columns of A equals the number of rows of B
The result of multiplying an m x n matrix by an n x p matrix is an m x p matrix
Matrix multiplication is not commutative: AB is generally not equal to BA even when both products are defined
The identity matrix I acts as the multiplicative identity: AI = IA = A for any compatible square matrix A
Scalar multiplication distributes over matrix addition: k(A + B) = kA + kB
To find unknown elements in a matrix equation, equate corresponding positions and solve the resulting system of linear equations
The transpose of a matrix swaps rows and columns; the order changes from m x n to n x m
A symmetric matrix satisfies A = A^T; all elements are reflected across the main diagonal
ICSE problems often require computing products like A^2 = A x A or expressions like 2A + 3B; compute step-by-step to avoid sign errors

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_1_Arithmetic.overview: Arithmetic Progression (AP) studies sequences where consecutive terms differ by a constant value called the common difference. Students identify whether a given sequence is an AP, find the nth term using the general term formula, compute sums of the first n terms, and solve problems involving finding specific terms or the number of terms. APs are foundational in understanding linear growth patterns and appear in problems about savings, salary increments, and series of numbers in practical contexts.

key_concepts.definition: A sequence in which each term after the first is obtained by adding a fixed constant (the common difference d) to the preceding term. For example, 3, 7, 11, 15, ... is an AP with first term a = 3 and d = 4. Formally, a_n - a_(n-1) = d for all n > 1.
The first term a (also written a_1) is the starting value of the sequence. The common difference d = a_2 - a_1 = a_3 - a_2, etc. For the AP 10, 7, 4, 1, ..., a = 10 and d = -3 (decreasing AP).
The formula for the nth term is a_n = a + (n - 1)d. For the AP 2, 5, 8, 11, ..., the 10th term = 2 + 9 x 3 = 29. This formula converts a position (n) to the value of the term at that position.

The sum S_n = n/2 x (2a + (n-1)d) or equivalently S_n = n/2 x (a + l), where l is the last term. For the AP 1, 3, 5, ..., the sum of first 10 odd numbers: S_10 = 10/2 x (2 + 9 x 2) = 5 x 20 = 100 = 10^2.

The arithmetic mean (AM) of two numbers a and b is (a + b)/2, which is the middle term when a, AM, b form an AP. Three terms are in AP if and only if the middle term equals the average of the other two. For 3, x, 15 in AP: x = (3 + 15)/2 = 9.
If the first term a, common difference d, and last term l are known, the number of terms n = (l - a)/d + 1. For the AP 5, 9, 13, ..., 49: n = (49 - 5)/4 + 1 = 44/4 + 1 = 12.
The final term of a finite AP, denoted l = a + (n-1)d. Used in the simplified sum formula S_n = n(a + l)/2. For a = 3, d = 2, n = 8: l = 3 + 14 = 17, and S_8 = 8 x (3 + 17)/2 = 80.

key_concepts.term: Condition for Three Terms in AP
key_concepts.definition: Three quantities p, q, r are in AP if and only if q - p = r - q, i.e., 2q = p + r. For example, are 2, 5, 8 in AP? 2 x 5 = 10 = 2 + 8 = 10, yes. This condition simplifies problem-solving when the middle term is the unknown.

laws_and_formulas.name: nth Term of AP
laws_and_formulas.description: Finds the value of the term at position n in an arithmetic progression with first term a and common difference d

laws_and_formulas.name: Sum of First n Terms (Form 1)
laws_and_formulas.description: Computes the sum when first term and common difference are known

laws_and_formulas.name: Sum of First n Terms (Form 2)
laws_and_formulas.description: Computes the sum when first and last terms are known; simpler when both are available

laws_and_formulas.name: Number of Terms
laws_and_formulas.description: Finds how many terms are in a finite AP given first term, last term, and common difference

laws_and_formulas.name: Condition for AP
laws_and_formulas.formula: 2b = a + c (for three terms a, b, c in AP)
laws_and_formulas.description: Tests or proves that three given quantities form an arithmetic progression

key_points: In an AP, the common difference d is constant; verify by checking that consecutive differences are equal
The general term formula a_n = a + (n-1)d converts a position n into the corresponding value
To find how many terms an AP has, use n = (l - a)/d + 1 where l is the last term
Use S_n = n/2(a + l) when both first and last terms are given; use S_n = n/2(2a + (n-1)d) when only a and d are known
The nth term can also be found as a_n = S_n - S_(n-1) for n >= 2; this link between sum and terms is frequently tested
Three numbers a, b, c are in AP if and only if 2b = a + c; the middle term equals the arithmetic mean of the outer two
In word problems, assign the unknown as the first term a and set up equations using the nth term or sum formulas
ICSE problems often present APs in context: salary sequences, rows of seats, patterns of numbers; translate carefully
If the sum of n terms is given as a quadratic in n (e.g., S_n = 3n^2 + 2n), the nth term is S_n - S_(n-1) = 6n - 1, an AP
The sum of the first n natural numbers 1 + 2 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2 is a special case of the AP sum formula with a = 1, d = 1

10|mathematics|Geometry_Similarity.overview: Similarity in Geometry deals with figures that have the same shape but not necessarily the same size. Two triangles are similar if their corresponding angles are equal and corresponding sides are proportional. Students learn three criteria for triangle similarity: AA (Angle-Angle), SSS (Side-Side-Side), and SAS (Side-Angle-Side). The chapter introduces the Basic Proportionality Theorem (Thales theorem), its converse, and angle bisector properties. A key result is that the ratio of areas of similar triangles equals the square of the ratio of corresponding sides.

key_concepts.definition: Two figures are similar (~) if they have the same shape with corresponding angles equal and corresponding sides proportional (in the same ratio). All squares are similar to each other; all circles are similar; equilateral triangles are all similar.

If two angles of one triangle are respectively equal to two angles of another triangle, the triangles are similar. Since angle sums equal 180 degrees, equality of any two angles implies equality of all three. This is the most commonly used similarity criterion.

If the three sides of one triangle are proportional to the three sides of another triangle, the triangles are similar. For triangles ABC and PQR: if AB/PQ = BC/QR = CA/RP, then triangle ABC ~ triangle PQR.

If one angle of a triangle is equal to one angle of another, and the sides including these angles are proportional, the triangles are similar. If angle A = angle P and AB/PQ = AC/PR, then triangle ABC ~ triangle PQR.

If a line is drawn parallel to one side of a triangle, it divides the other two sides in the same ratio. If DE is parallel to BC in triangle ABC with D on AB and E on AC, then AD/DB = AE/EC. Also known as Thales theorem.
If a line divides two sides of a triangle in the same ratio, then it is parallel to the third side. If AD/DB = AE/EC in triangle ABC, then DE is parallel to BC.

key_concepts.term: Ratio of Areas of Similar Triangles
key_concepts.definition: The ratio of areas of two similar triangles equals the square of the ratio of their corresponding sides. If triangle ABC ~ triangle PQR with AB/PQ = k, then Area(ABC)/Area(PQR) = k^2. For similar triangles with sides in ratio 3:5, areas are in ratio 9:25.

When triangles are similar, all linear measurements (sides, perimeters, altitudes, medians) are in the same ratio (the scale factor), while areas are in the square of this ratio. Always identify corresponding vertices carefully from the similarity statement.
The angle bisector of an angle in a triangle divides the opposite side in the ratio of the adjacent sides. In triangle ABC, if AD bisects angle A and meets BC at D, then BD/DC = AB/AC.

laws_and_formulas.name: Basic Proportionality Theorem
laws_and_formulas.description: A line parallel to one side divides the other two sides in equal ratios

laws_and_formulas.name: Ratio of Areas
laws_and_formulas.description: For similar triangles, areas are proportional to the square of corresponding sides

laws_and_formulas.name: Angle Bisector Theorem
laws_and_formulas.formula: BD/DC = AB/AC (where AD bisects angle A)
laws_and_formulas.description: Divides opposite side in ratio of adjacent sides

laws_and_formulas.name: Scale Factor Relationship
laws_and_formulas.formula: Perimeter ratio = k; Area ratio = k^2
laws_and_formulas.description: Linear measures scale by k, areas by k^2 between similar figures

key_points: Similarity is denoted by ~ (tilde); always state corresponding vertices in the correct order in a similarity statement
AA is the most efficient criterion: if two pairs of angles are equal, the third pair is automatically equal by angle sum property
The ratio of corresponding sides in similar triangles is called the scale factor; all linear elements (sides, medians, altitudes) share this ratio
Areas of similar triangles are in the ratio of squares of corresponding sides; perimeters are in the same ratio as sides
BPT requires the line to be parallel to one side; its converse uses the equal-ratio condition to prove parallelism
When solving BPT problems, set up the proportion AD/DB = AE/EC and cross-multiply or use componendo to find unknowns
In proofs involving similarity, mark equal angles with the same symbol and establish the proportion before writing the similarity statement
The angle bisector theorem BD/DC = AB/AC is often tested in combination with similarity proofs
In problems asking for the area ratio, always square the linear ratio; a common error is to use the linear ratio directly
Map coordinates or measurements onto a diagram before writing proportions; a labelled diagram prevents correspondence errors

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_2_Coordinate.overview: Coordinate Geometry establishes the link between algebraic equations and geometric figures plotted on the Cartesian plane. Students study the distance between two points, the midpoint of a segment, the section formula for internal and external division, and the area of a triangle using coordinates. The slope (gradient) of a line determines its inclination, and conditions for parallel and perpendicular lines are derived from slope relationships. Students also write and interpret equations of straight lines in various forms, making this chapter central to linking algebra with spatial reasoning.

key_concepts.definition: A two-dimensional coordinate system formed by two perpendicular number lines (x-axis and y-axis) intersecting at the origin (0,0). Every point P is uniquely identified by an ordered pair (x, y) called its coordinates. The four quadrants are determined by the signs of x and y.
The distance between two points A(x1, y1) and B(x2, y2) is d = sqrt((x2 - x1)^2 + (y2 - y1)^2). For A(1, 2) and B(4, 6): d = sqrt(9 + 16) = sqrt(25) = 5. Derived from the Pythagorean theorem.
The midpoint M of segment AB where A(x1, y1) and B(x2, y2) is M = ((x1 + x2)/2, (y1 + y2)/2). For A(2, 4) and B(6, 8): M = (4, 6). Used to find centres of line segments or centroids.

Point P dividing AB joining A(x1, y1) and B(x2, y2) in ratio m:n internally is P = ((mx2 + nx1)/(m+n), (my2 + ny1)/(m+n)). For external division, replace n with -n. The midpoint is the special case m = n = 1.

The slope m of a line measures its steepness: m = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1) for two points on the line. It equals tan(theta) where theta is the angle of inclination. A horizontal line has slope 0; a vertical line has undefined slope.

key_concepts.term: Conditions for Parallel and Perpendicular Lines
key_concepts.definition: Two lines are parallel if their slopes are equal: m1 = m2. Two lines are perpendicular if the product of their slopes is -1: m1 x m2 = -1. For example, lines with slopes 2 and -1/2 are perpendicular since 2 x (-1/2) = -1.

Several forms: slope-intercept y = mx + c (m = slope, c = y-intercept); point-slope y - y1 = m(x - x1); two-point form using slope between two points. All represent the same line but are used in different problem contexts.
Three points A, B, C are collinear (lie on the same straight line) if the slope of AB equals the slope of BC (or AC). Equivalently, the area of triangle ABC = 0 using the coordinate area formula.
For vertices A(x1,y1), B(x2,y2), C(x3,y3): Area = (1/2)|x1(y2 - y3) + x2(y3 - y1) + x3(y1 - y2)|. A non-zero area confirms the three points form a triangle; zero area indicates collinearity.

laws_and_formulas.name: Distance Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Computes the straight-line distance between two points on the Cartesian plane

laws_and_formulas.name: Midpoint Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Finds the midpoint of a line segment; also used to locate the centroid-related median points

laws_and_formulas.name: Section Formula (Internal)
laws_and_formulas.description: Coordinates of point dividing segment AB in ratio m:n internally

laws_and_formulas.name: Slope Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Gradient of the line through two given points; equals tan of inclination angle

laws_and_formulas.name: Perpendicularity Condition
laws_and_formulas.description: Two lines are perpendicular if the product of their slopes equals negative one

laws_and_formulas.name: Area of Triangle
laws_and_formulas.description: Calculates area of a triangle from its three vertex coordinates

key_points: The distance formula is derived from the Pythagorean theorem applied to the right triangle formed by horizontal and vertical displacements
The midpoint formula gives the average of x-coordinates and the average of y-coordinates of the two endpoints
In the section formula, m:n is the ratio in which the dividing point P lies closer to B (m) than to A (n) for internal division
Parallel lines have equal slopes; perpendicular lines have slopes whose product is -1 (negative reciprocals)
A horizontal line y = k has slope 0; a vertical line x = h has undefined (infinite) slope
The y-intercept c in y = mx + c is where the line crosses the y-axis; set x = 0 to find it
To find where two lines intersect, solve their equations simultaneously as a system of two linear equations
Three points are collinear if the area of the triangle they form is zero, or if slope AB = slope BC
External division uses a negative ratio; if P divides AB externally in ratio m:n, it lies beyond B on the extension
Centroid of a triangle with vertices (x1,y1), (x2,y2), (x3,y3) is ((x1+x2+x3)/3, (y1+y2+y3)/3) — a direct application of the midpoint idea

10|mathematics|Geometry_Part_2_Circles.overview: Circle theorems form one of the most theorem-rich topics in Class 10 ICSE geometry. Students study angle subtended by arcs, the relationship between inscribed and central angles, cyclic quadrilaterals, tangent properties, and the alternate segment theorem. Key theorems establish that the angle in a semicircle is 90 degrees, opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary, and a tangent at any point is perpendicular to the radius at that point. Proofs, constructions, and numerical problems require systematic application of multiple theorems simultaneously.

key_concepts.definition: A chord is a line segment with both endpoints on the circle. An arc is the portion of the circle between two points. The chord divides the circle into a minor segment and a major segment. Equal chords of a circle subtend equal angles at the centre.

The central angle is formed at the centre; the inscribed angle (angle in a segment) is formed at any point on the circle. The inscribed angle is exactly half the central angle subtending the same arc. If the central angle subtending arc BC is 80 degrees, the inscribed angle from any point on the major arc is 40 degrees.

The angle subtended by a diameter at any point on the circle is a right angle (90 degrees). This is a special case of the inscribed angle theorem where the central angle is 180 degrees. Angle in semicircle = 180/2 = 90 degrees.

All inscribed angles subtending the same arc (from the same side) are equal. If angles ABC and ADC both subtend arc AC from the same segment, then angle ABC = angle ADC. This is a core theorem used in nearly every circle proof.

A quadrilateral whose all four vertices lie on a circle. In a cyclic quadrilateral ABCD, opposite angles are supplementary: angle A + angle C = 180 degrees and angle B + angle D = 180 degrees. The converse is also true: if opposite angles are supplementary, the quadrilateral is cyclic.

A tangent touches the circle at exactly one point (the point of tangency). At the point of tangency, the tangent is perpendicular to the radius: angle OTA = 90 degrees where O is the centre and T is the point of tangency. Two tangents from an external point are equal in length.

The angle between a tangent to a circle and a chord drawn from the point of tangency equals the inscribed angle subtending the same chord from the opposite segment. If TA is a tangent at A and AB is a chord, then angle TAB = angle ACB where C is any point on the major arc AB.

The radius to the point of contact of a tangent is always perpendicular to the tangent. This creates a right angle that enables Pythagoras theorem applications: if OT is radius and PA is a tangent from external point P, then OT is perpendicular to PA.
An exterior angle of a cyclic quadrilateral equals the interior opposite angle. If ABCD is cyclic and angle DCE is the exterior angle at C (with E on BC extended), then angle DCE = angle DAB.

laws_and_formulas.name: Inscribed Angle Theorem
laws_and_formulas.formula: Inscribed Angle = (1/2) x Central Angle (subtending same arc)
laws_and_formulas.description: The angle at the circumference is half the angle at the centre for the same arc

laws_and_formulas.name: Angle in Semicircle
laws_and_formulas.description: Any angle inscribed in a semicircle (subtended by diameter) is always 90 degrees

laws_and_formulas.name: Cyclic Quadrilateral
laws_and_formulas.formula: Opposite angles sum to 180 degrees: angle A + angle C = 180, angle B + angle D = 180
laws_and_formulas.description: Fundamental property of a cyclic quadrilateral; used to find unknown angles

laws_and_formulas.name: Tangent Lengths
laws_and_formulas.formula: From external point P: PA = PB (tangents to circle from same external point)
laws_and_formulas.description: Two tangents drawn from the same external point are always equal in length

laws_and_formulas.name: Alternate Segment Theorem
laws_and_formulas.formula: Angle between tangent and chord = inscribed angle in alternate segment
laws_and_formulas.description: Links tangent-chord angle to the inscribed angle on the opposite side of the chord

key_points: The inscribed angle is always half the central angle subtending the same arc; memorise this as the base theorem from which others derive
All inscribed angles subtending the same arc from the same side are equal to each other
The angle in a semicircle is 90 degrees; use this to identify right angles in circle diagrams where a diameter is drawn
In a cyclic quadrilateral, opposite angles add up to 180 degrees; and the exterior angle equals the interior opposite angle
A tangent is perpendicular to the radius at the point of contact; use this with Pythagoras to find lengths
Two tangents from the same external point are equal in length; combined with right angles, this often creates an isosceles triangle
The alternate segment theorem relates the tangent-chord angle to the inscribed angle in the other segment; it is frequently tested in proofs
To prove a quadrilateral is cyclic, show that opposite angles sum to 180 degrees, or that it lies on a circle through given conditions
Circle theorem problems often require chaining multiple theorems; label all known angles at each step before applying the next theorem
The perpendicular from the centre to a chord bisects the chord; this property helps find chord lengths given the distance from centre

10|mathematics|Mensuration_Complete_Unit.overview: Mensuration deals with the measurement of areas and volumes of geometric solids, including cylinders, cones, spheres, hemispheres, and combinations thereof. Students apply formulas for curved surface area (CSA), total surface area (TSA), and volume to solve real-world problems involving containers, molds, and conversion of solids. A recurring type involves recasting one solid into another, requiring the use of volume conservation. The chapter demands careful selection of the correct formula and unit consistency throughout calculations.

key_concepts.definition: A solid with two parallel circular bases connected by a curved surface. For radius r and height h: Volume = pi r^2 h, Curved Surface Area = 2 pi r h, Total Surface Area = 2 pi r (h + r). A hollow cylinder also accounts for inner radius in its area calculations.

A solid with a circular base tapering to a point (apex). For radius r, height h, and slant height l = sqrt(r^2 + h^2): Volume = (1/3) pi r^2 h, Curved Surface Area = pi r l, Total Surface Area = pi r (l + r). The slant height l is always greater than h.

A perfectly round solid where every point on the surface is equidistant from the centre. For radius r: Volume = (4/3) pi r^3, Surface Area = 4 pi r^2. A sphere has no flat base; its total surface area equals its curved surface area.

Half of a sphere. For radius r: Volume = (2/3) pi r^3, Curved Surface Area = 2 pi r^2, Total Surface Area = 3 pi r^2 (curved surface + circular flat base = 2 pi r^2 + pi r^2). Often used in combination with cylinders in ICSE problems.

The distance from the apex to any point on the base circumference, l = sqrt(r^2 + h^2). Used in the formula for curved surface area of the cone. Given l and r, height h = sqrt(l^2 - r^2). Always compute l before applying the curved surface area formula.

When a solid is melted and recast into another shape, the volume remains constant. If a sphere of radius 6 cm is recast into a cylinder of radius 4 cm, the height h satisfies pi x 16 x h = (4/3) pi x 216, giving h = 18 cm.

Composite solids are formed by joining two or more standard solids. For example, a solid consisting of a cone on top of a cylinder requires: Volume = V(cone) + V(cylinder); TSA = CSA(cone) + CSA(cylinder) + one circular base (top of cylinder = base of cone, excluded). Careful identification of shared surfaces is essential.

The portion of a cone left when a smaller cone is cut from the top by a plane parallel to the base. For frustum with radii R (bottom) and r (top) and slant height l = sqrt(h^2 + (R-r)^2): Volume = (pi h/3)(R^2 + r^2 + Rr), CSA = pi(R + r)l.

laws_and_formulas.name: Cylinder
laws_and_formulas.formula: V = pi r^2 h; CSA = 2 pi r h; TSA = 2 pi r(h + r)
laws_and_formulas.description: Volume and surface areas of a right circular cylinder

laws_and_formulas.name: Cone
laws_and_formulas.formula: V = (1/3) pi r^2 h; CSA = pi r l; TSA = pi r(l + r); l = sqrt(r^2 + h^2)
laws_and_formulas.description: Volume and surface areas of a right circular cone

laws_and_formulas.name: Sphere
laws_and_formulas.formula: V = (4/3) pi r^3; SA = 4 pi r^2
laws_and_formulas.description: Volume and total surface area of a sphere

laws_and_formulas.name: Hemisphere
laws_and_formulas.formula: V = (2/3) pi r^3; CSA = 2 pi r^2; TSA = 3 pi r^2
laws_and_formulas.description: Volume and surface areas of a solid hemisphere

laws_and_formulas.name: Frustum
laws_and_formulas.formula: V = (pi h/3)(R^2 + r^2 + Rr); CSA = pi(R+r)l; l = sqrt(h^2 + (R-r)^2)
laws_and_formulas.description: Volume and curved surface area of a frustum (truncated cone)

key_points: Always identify which formula to use (CSA, TSA, or Volume) based on what the problem asks for
The slant height l of a cone must be computed from l = sqrt(r^2 + h^2) before using the CSA formula
For a hemisphere, TSA = 3 pi r^2 = CSA (2 pi r^2) + flat circular base (pi r^2); do not confuse with sphere
When two solids are joined, the shared surface area is not counted in the total; identify which faces are internal
Volume is conserved when a solid is melted and recast; set V1 = V2 and solve for the unknown dimension
All dimensions must be in the same units before substituting into formulas; convert cm to m if required
Use pi = 22/7 or 3.14 as specified in the problem; do not mix both in a single calculation
A frustum is obtained by slicing a cone parallel to its base; its slant height uses (R - r), not R or r alone
For a hollow cylinder (pipe), TSA includes outer curved surface + inner curved surface + two annular rings at the ends
Volume of water that fills a container equals the volume of the container; this connects mensuration to capacity problems

10|mathematics|Trigonometry_Complete_Uni.overview: Trigonometry at Class 10 ICSE level focuses on trigonometric ratios of acute angles, standard values for 0, 30, 45, 60, and 90 degrees, fundamental identities, and complementary angle relationships. Students apply these concepts to prove trigonometric identities and solve heights and distances problems involving angles of elevation and depression. The chapter demands fluency with the Pythagorean identities and the ability to manipulate trigonometric expressions by converting all terms to sines and cosines or using reciprocal and quotient identities systematically.

key_concepts.definition: Six ratios defined for a right-angled triangle: sin A = opposite/hypotenuse, cos A = adjacent/hypotenuse, tan A = opposite/adjacent, cosec A = 1/sin A, sec A = 1/cos A, cot A = 1/tan A. For a right triangle with angle A, opposite side 3, adjacent 4, hypotenuse 5: sin A = 3/5, cos A = 4/5, tan A = 3/4.

Key values to memorise: sin 0 = 0, sin 30 = 1/2, sin 45 = 1/sqrt(2), sin 60 = sqrt(3)/2, sin 90 = 1. Cosine values are the reverse: cos 0 = 1, cos 30 = sqrt(3)/2, cos 45 = 1/sqrt(2), cos 60 = 1/2, cos 90 = 0. Tangent: tan 30 = 1/sqrt(3), tan 45 = 1, tan 60 = sqrt(3).

Three fundamental identities derived from the Pythagorean theorem: sin^2 A + cos^2 A = 1; 1 + tan^2 A = sec^2 A; 1 + cot^2 A = cosec^2 A. These are the workhorses of identity proofs. From sin^2 A + cos^2 A = 1, all other Pythagorean identities can be derived by dividing by cos^2 A or sin^2 A.

cosec A = 1/sin A, sec A = 1/cos A, cot A = 1/tan A. These are exact reciprocals, not inverses. Also, sin A x cosec A = 1, cos A x sec A = 1, tan A x cot A = 1. Using these simplifies expressions containing sums or products of a ratio and its reciprocal.
tan A = sin A / cos A and cot A = cos A / sin A. These allow tan and cot to be expressed purely in terms of sin and cos, which is often the first step in proving identities.

For complementary angles (summing to 90 degrees): sin(90 - A) = cos A, cos(90 - A) = sin A, tan(90 - A) = cot A, sec(90 - A) = cosec A, and vice versa. For example, sin 70 = cos 20; these simplify expressions involving angle pairs adding to 90.

The angle measured upward from the horizontal to the line of sight toward an object above the observer. Used in problems like: from a point 30 m from a tree, the angle of elevation of the top is 60 degrees, so height = 30 tan 60 = 30 sqrt(3) m.

The angle measured downward from the horizontal to the line of sight toward an object below the observer. The angle of depression from the top of a cliff to a boat equals the angle of elevation from the boat to the top of the cliff (alternate interior angles).

An identity is a trigonometric equation true for all valid values of the angle. To prove LHS = RHS: start from the more complex side, convert all ratios to sin and cos using reciprocal/quotient identities, apply Pythagorean identities, and simplify until it equals the other side. Never move terms across the equals sign.

laws_and_formulas.name: Pythagorean Identity 1
laws_and_formulas.description: Fundamental identity; rearranged as sin^2 A = 1 - cos^2 A or cos^2 A = 1 - sin^2 A

laws_and_formulas.name: Pythagorean Identity 2
laws_and_formulas.description: Derived by dividing sin^2 + cos^2 = 1 by cos^2 A; use when sec or tan appears

laws_and_formulas.name: Pythagorean Identity 3
laws_and_formulas.description: Derived by dividing sin^2 + cos^2 = 1 by sin^2 A; use when cosec or cot appears

laws_and_formulas.name: Complementary Angles
laws_and_formulas.formula: sin(90 - A) = cos A; cos(90 - A) = sin A; tan(90 - A) = cot A
laws_and_formulas.description: Relates trigonometric ratios of complementary angles

laws_and_formulas.name: Height and Distance
laws_and_formulas.formula: Height = Distance x tan(angle of elevation)
laws_and_formulas.description: Basic formula for heights and distances problems using the tangent ratio

key_points: Memorise the trigonometric ratios for 0, 30, 45, 60, and 90 degrees; all standard value problems depend on these
The three Pythagorean identities are derived from sin^2 A + cos^2 A = 1 by dividing by cos^2 A or sin^2 A
In identity proofs, start from the more complex side and simplify; never cross-multiply or move terms across the equals sign
Convert all trigonometric ratios to sin and cos as the first step in any identity proof when unsure of the approach
Complementary angle identities state that sin(90 - A) = cos A and cos(90 - A) = sin A; use these to simplify expressions where angles add to 90 degrees
Angle of depression from a higher point equals angle of elevation from the lower point (alternate interior angles with the horizontal)
For heights and distances: draw a diagram, identify the right triangle, label the known angle and one known side, then apply the appropriate ratio
tan 45 = 1 is the most useful standard value; it simplifies heights-and-distances problems significantly
The reciprocal identities (cosec, sec, cot) are not inverses (which are arcsin, arccos, arctan); distinguish them carefully
Factor expressions before cancelling; a common error is to cancel individual terms within an addition rather than factoring first

10|mathematics|Statistics_Complete_Unit.overview: Statistics at Class 10 ICSE level covers measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode) for grouped and ungrouped data, cumulative frequency distributions, and graphical methods including the ogive (cumulative frequency curve). Students calculate the mean using the direct method, assumed mean method, and step deviation method. Median is found from the cumulative frequency table using the formula, and the ogive is used graphically to read off the median, quartiles, and other percentiles. The chapter builds data literacy essential for interpreting real-world statistical information.

key_concepts.definition: The mean of grouped data is calculated as mean = sum(f x x) / sum(f), where f is frequency and x is the class midpoint. For ungrouped data, mean = sum of values / number of values. The assumed mean method (mean = A + sum(fd)/sum(f)) reduces computation when midpoints are large.

The representative value of a class interval, calculated as (lower boundary + upper boundary) / 2. For the class 20-30, midpoint = 25. All mean calculations for grouped data use midpoints to represent the class, since individual values are unknown.

A computational shortcut for finding the mean: choose an assumed mean A (usually the central class midpoint), compute deviations d = x - A for each class, then mean = A + sum(fd)/sum(f). Reduces the size of numbers handled, minimising arithmetic errors.

A further simplification: define u = (x - A)/h where h is the class width, then mean = A + h x sum(fu)/sum(f). Used when class widths are uniform. For a dataset with class width 10 and sum(fu)/sum(f) = 0.6 and A = 35: mean = 35 + 10 x 0.6 = 41.

The middle value of a dataset when arranged in order. For grouped data, median = L + ((n/2 - cf) / f) x h, where L is the lower boundary of the median class, cf is the cumulative frequency before the median class, f is its frequency, and h is the class width. Locate the median class by finding where n/2 falls in cumulative frequency.

The value (or class) with the highest frequency. For grouped data, modal class is the class with highest frequency. Mode = L + ((f1 - f0) / (2f1 - f0 - f2)) x h, where f1 is the modal class frequency, f0 is the previous class frequency, and f2 is the next class frequency.

The running total of frequencies up to a given class boundary. Used to construct ogives and find the median. For classes with frequencies 5, 8, 12, 7, cumulative frequencies are 5, 13, 25, 32. The median class contains the (n/2)th value.

A graph of cumulative frequency against the upper class boundary, giving an S-shaped curve. Used to read off the median (at n/2 on the y-axis), lower quartile (at n/4), upper quartile (at 3n/4), and other percentiles. Draw the ogive by plotting (upper boundary, cf) points and connecting with a smooth curve.

Values dividing the distribution into four equal parts. Q1 (lower quartile) is the (n/4)th value, Q2 is the median, Q3 (upper quartile) is the (3n/4)th value. From the ogive, read the corresponding x-values. The interquartile range IQR = Q3 - Q1 measures the spread of the middle 50% of data.

laws_and_formulas.name: Mean (Direct Method)
laws_and_formulas.description: Computes the arithmetic mean of grouped data using class midpoints

laws_and_formulas.name: Mean (Assumed Mean)
laws_and_formulas.formula: Mean = A + sum(f x d) / sum(f), where d = x - A
laws_and_formulas.description: Reduces computation by working with deviations from an assumed mean A

laws_and_formulas.name: Mean (Step Deviation)
laws_and_formulas.formula: Mean = A + h x sum(f x u) / sum(f), where u = (x - A)/h
laws_and_formulas.description: Most efficient for uniform class widths; scale deviations by class width h

laws_and_formulas.name: Median (Grouped)
laws_and_formulas.formula: Median = L + ((n/2 - cf) / f) x h
laws_and_formulas.description: Calculates median from grouped frequency table; L = lower boundary of median class

laws_and_formulas.name: Mode (Grouped)
laws_and_formulas.formula: Mode = L + ((f1 - f0) / (2f1 - f0 - f2)) x h
laws_and_formulas.description: Calculates mode from grouped frequency table; f1 = modal class frequency

key_points: Always use class midpoints (not boundaries) as the x-value when computing mean from grouped data
The assumed mean method and step deviation method give the same result as the direct method; choose based on the size of numbers
To find the median class, compute n/2 and locate where it first appears (or is exceeded) in the cumulative frequency column
The median formula requires L (lower boundary), cf (cumulative frequency BEFORE the class), f (frequency OF the class), and h (class width)
For the ogive, plot points using UPPER class boundaries against cumulative frequency; for a less-than ogive the curve rises from left to right
Quartiles Q1, Q2, Q3 correspond to n/4, n/2, 3n/4 on the cumulative frequency axis of the ogive
The mode is the class with the highest frequency; the formula for grouped data uses the frequencies of the neighbouring classes
Mean is affected by extreme values; median is resistant to outliers; mode may not be unique
For the ogive to read the median correctly, draw a horizontal line at y = n/2, find the x-value where it meets the curve
In ICSE problems, presenting the ogive construction clearly (table, scale, plotted points, smooth curve) earns method marks

10|mathematics|Probability_Complete_Unit.overview: Probability quantifies the likelihood of events using a scale from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). Class 10 ICSE covers classical (theoretical) probability based on equally likely outcomes, complementary events, mutually exclusive events, and simple combined events. Students calculate probabilities for experiments involving cards, dice, coins, and coloured balls. The addition rule for mutually exclusive events and the complement rule (P(not A) = 1 - P(A)) are the main tools. Sample spaces are enumerated explicitly for problems involving two dice or two-card draws.

key_concepts.definition: The measure of how likely an event is to occur, defined as P(A) = number of favourable outcomes / total number of equally likely outcomes. Probability always satisfies 0 <= P(A) <= 1. P(A) = 0 means the event is impossible; P(A) = 1 means it is certain.

The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment, denoted S. For a single die, S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}, so |S| = 6. For two coins, S = {HH, HT, TH, TT}, so |S| = 4. Correctly listing the sample space is the first step in any probability problem.

A subset of the sample space. A simple event contains one outcome; a compound event contains two or more. For a die, the event 'even number' = {2, 4, 6}, so P(even) = 3/6 = 1/2. Events are described in words and then expressed as subsets of S.

The complement of event A, written A or A', is the set of outcomes in S not in A. P(A') = 1 - P(A). For example, P(at least one head) = 1 - P(no heads). Using complementary counting is faster when the complement is simpler to count.

Two events A and B are mutually exclusive if they cannot both occur simultaneously (A and B have no common outcomes). For a die, 'rolling a 2' and 'rolling a 5' are mutually exclusive. P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) for mutually exclusive events.

key_concepts.formula: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)
key_concepts.definition: The general addition rule for any two events. When A and B are mutually exclusive, P(A and B) = 0, simplifying to P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). For events with common outcomes, the overlap must be subtracted to avoid double counting.

Applicable when all outcomes in the sample space are equally likely. P(A) = |A| / |S|, where |A| is the number of outcomes in event A and |S| is the total number of outcomes. Requires careful enumeration of both the event and the sample space.

A standard deck has 52 cards: 4 suits (hearts, diamonds, clubs, spades) of 13 cards each (Ace, 2-10, Jack, Queen, King). Hearts and diamonds are red; clubs and spades are black. There are 4 aces, 12 face cards (J, Q, K in each suit), and 26 red cards. These counts form the basis of all card probability problems.

An impossible event has probability 0 (e.g., rolling a 7 on a standard die). A sure event has probability 1 (e.g., rolling a number less than 7). These boundary cases test the understanding that probability lies between 0 and 1 inclusive.

laws_and_formulas.name: Classical Probability
laws_and_formulas.formula: P(A) = Number of favourable outcomes / Total number of equally likely outcomes
laws_and_formulas.description: The fundamental formula; assumes all outcomes in the sample space are equally likely

laws_and_formulas.name: Complement Rule
laws_and_formulas.description: Probability of an event NOT occurring; useful when the complement is easier to count

laws_and_formulas.name: Addition Rule (Mutually Exclusive)
laws_and_formulas.description: For events with no common outcomes; probability of either event is the sum of individual probabilities

laws_and_formulas.name: Addition Rule (General)
laws_and_formulas.formula: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)
laws_and_formulas.description: General rule; subtracts the probability of both events occurring to avoid double counting

laws_and_formulas.name: Probability Bounds
laws_and_formulas.formula: 0 <= P(A) <= 1 for any event A; P(S) = 1; P(empty set) = 0
laws_and_formulas.description: Fundamental axioms ensuring probability is always a number between 0 and 1 inclusive

key_points: Probability is always between 0 and 1 inclusive; any answer outside this range indicates an error
The sample space must be listed completely and correctly before computing probabilities; omitting outcomes is the most common mistake
P(A') = 1 - P(A); use the complement when computing the probability of 'at least one' or 'not all' scenarios
Mutually exclusive events cannot occur together; their union probability is simply the sum of individual probabilities
A standard deck has 52 cards, 4 suits, 13 cards per suit, 26 red and 26 black, 4 of each denomination
For two dice, the sample space has 36 equally likely outcomes; list them systematically as ordered pairs (a, b)
For problems involving 'at least one' outcome, use complement: P(at least one) = 1 - P(none)
Equal likelihood is the key assumption of classical probability; if outcomes are not equally likely, classical probability does not apply
Express final answers as fractions in lowest terms unless decimals are requested
Verify that probabilities of all mutually exclusive and exhaustive events sum to exactly 1

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_U.overview: An object moving at constant speed in a circle is continuously accelerating because its direction changes. This centripetal acceleration is always directed toward the centre of the circle and is provided by a centripetal force — not a new type of force, but whichever existing force (gravity, tension, friction) is directed inward. At Class 10, this builds on the Class 9 introduction with more mathematical depth and applications to satellites, road banking, and centrifuges.

key_concepts.definition: a_c = v²/r = ω²r directed toward the centre. 'Centre-seeking' acceleration arising from continuously changing direction. Even at constant speed, the changing velocity direction means the body accelerates inward constantly.

F_c = mv²/r = mω²r. The net force directed toward the centre that keeps the body in circular motion. Provided by: tension (stone on string), gravity (satellite), friction (car turning), normal reaction (wall of death). It is not a separate 'new' force.

ω = angle turned per unit time = 2π/T = 2πf. Unit: rad/s. Linear speed v = ωr. Period T = 2πr/v. Angular velocity connects rotational motion to linear speed: faster rotation or larger radius means higher linear speed.

A road banked at angle θ: the inward component of normal reaction (N sinθ) provides centripetal force. tan θ = v²/(rg). At the design speed, no friction is needed. Above design speed: friction required inward; below: friction required outward.

For a satellite at altitude h, radius r = R+h (R = Earth radius): gravitational force provides centripetal force. mg' = mv²/r → v = √(gr). Geostationary satellite: T = 24 h at specific altitude. Lower orbit → higher speed.

In the rotating frame, occupants feel a 'centrifugal force' pushing them outward. This is a pseudo-force — not real in the ground frame. In the ground frame, the inward centripetal force keeps them in the circle; remove it and they fly off tangentially.

key_concepts.term: Minimum Speed at Top of Loop
key_concepts.definition: For a body completing a vertical circle: minimum speed at the top v_min = √(gr), where the centripetal force is provided entirely by gravity (normal reaction = 0). Below this speed, the string goes slack / the vehicle leaves the track.

When turning, a cyclist leans inward so that the resultant of weight and normal reaction is directed toward the centre (providing centripetal force). The lean angle θ satisfies tan θ = v²/(rg) — the same as banking.

laws_and_formulas.name: Centripetal Force
laws_and_formulas.description: Net inward force required for circular motion at speed v and radius r.

laws_and_formulas.name: Centripetal Acceleration
laws_and_formulas.description: Acceleration of body in uniform circular motion.

laws_and_formulas.name: Banking Angle
laws_and_formulas.description: Angle of road banking for a vehicle to navigate a circular curve of radius r at speed v without friction.

key_points: Constant speed in a circle means non-zero acceleration (direction changes).
Centripetal force: F = mv²/r, always toward centre — provided by an existing force.
Sources: gravity (satellites), tension (string), friction (car turning), normal (banking).
Banking: tan θ = v²/(rg) — no friction needed at design speed.
Satellite orbit: gravitational force = centripetal force → v = √(gr).
ω = 2π/T = 2πf; v = ωr.
Remove centripetal force: body moves tangentially (not outward — tangentially).
Vertical circle minimum speed at top: v_min = √(gr).
Cyclist leans inward: tan (lean angle) = v²/(rg).
Centrifugal force is a pseudo-force (appears only in the rotating frame).

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_T.overview: A force can not only push or pull a body — it can also cause it to rotate. The turning effect of a force is called the moment of force (or torque). The principle of moments states that for a body in rotational equilibrium, the sum of clockwise moments equals the sum of anticlockwise moments about any pivot. This underlies the operation of levers, seesaws, see-saws, spanners, and many mechanical devices.

key_concepts.definition: Torque = Force × perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line of action of the force. τ = F × d. Unit: N·m. The perpendicular distance is called the moment arm or lever arm. Torque is a measure of the turning tendency of a force.

A force causing clockwise rotation produces a clockwise moment; counterclockwise rotation produces an anticlockwise moment. Convention: anticlockwise moments are positive, clockwise negative (or vice versa — consistent choice matters). Sign depends on the reference convention.

For a body in rotational equilibrium: Sum of clockwise moments = Sum of anticlockwise moments. This applies about ANY pivot point. It is the condition for no net rotation, used to solve problems involving beams, seesaws, and levers.

For complete static equilibrium: (1) Net force = 0 (translational equilibrium); (2) Net torque = 0 (rotational equilibrium). Both must be satisfied simultaneously. A seesaw balanced at the pivot has zero net force and zero net torque.

The point where the entire weight of the body can be considered to act. For symmetric uniform bodies: at the geometric centre. For irregular bodies: found by suspending from two different points and finding the intersection of vertical lines. Body in equilibrium: centre of gravity must be above the base of support.

Stable equilibrium: disturbed object returns to original position (centre of gravity rises on displacement). Unstable: centre of gravity falls on displacement (object topples). Neutral: centre of gravity stays at same height. Low centre of gravity and wide base → greater stability.

Two equal and opposite parallel forces acting at different points. The net force of a couple is zero (no translation) but the net torque is non-zero. Torque of a couple = F × d (where d is perpendicular distance between the forces). Steering wheel and opening a lid use couples.

Levers are classified by the position of the fulcrum (F), effort (E), and load (L). Class 1: F between E and L (scissors, see-saw). Class 2: L between F and E (wheelbarrow, nutcracker). Class 3: E between F and L (tongs, forearm). Mechanical advantage = Load/Effort = effort arm/load arm.

laws_and_formulas.name: Moment of Force
laws_and_formulas.description: Torque = force × perpendicular distance from pivot to line of action.

laws_and_formulas.name: Principle of Moments
laws_and_formulas.description: Condition for rotational equilibrium about any point.

key_points: Moment of force = Force × perpendicular distance from pivot.
Principle of moments: sum of CW moments = sum of ACW moments (for equilibrium).
Both force equilibrium (ΣF=0) and moment equilibrium (Στ=0) required for static equilibrium.
Centre of gravity: point where weight effectively acts; at geometric centre for uniform symmetric bodies.
Low CG + wide base = stable; high CG + narrow base = unstable.
Couple: two equal, opposite, non-collinear forces — zero net force, non-zero torque.
Class 1 lever: fulcrum in middle (scissors). Class 2: load in middle (wheelbarrow). Class 3: effort in middle (tongs).
MA = Load/Effort = effort arm/load arm.
Taking moment about any point works — choose wisely to eliminate unknowns.
If an object does not rotate, net torque about ANY point = 0.

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_G.overview: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. This single law explains the fall of an apple, the orbit of the Moon, the tides, and the structure of the solar system. The gravitational constant G connects mass, distance, and force, while g (surface acceleration) is derived from G, Earth's mass, and radius.

key_concepts.definition: F = GMm/r². Every particle in the universe attracts every other particle with a force F directly proportional to the product of their masses (M, m) and inversely proportional to the square of the distance r between their centres. G = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².

G = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg². A universal constant of nature — the same everywhere in the universe. Extremely small value: gravity is by far the weakest of the four fundamental forces but acts over infinite range and is always attractive.
On Earth's surface: F = mg (weight) = GMm/R² → g = GM/R². g depends on Earth's mass M and radius R. Using M ≈ 6×10²⁴ kg, R ≈ 6.4×10⁶ m, G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹: g ≈ 9.8 m/s².

At altitude h above the surface: g_h = GM/(R+h)². As h increases, g decreases. At height R (one Earth radius above surface): g_R = GM/(2R)² = g/4. In orbit (continuously falling): effective g = 0 (weightlessness).

g decreases linearly with depth inside a uniform Earth: g_d = g(1 - d/R). At Earth's centre (d = R): g = 0. The mass of the sphere below you decreases while the mass of the shell above pulls outward (cancelling). So g decreases going inward.

For a satellite orbiting at radius r: gravity provides centripetal force: GMm/r² = mv²/r → v = √(GM/r). For low Earth orbit (r ≈ R): v ≈ 7.9 km/s. Higher orbits have lower orbital speed but longer period.

The minimum speed to escape Earth's gravity: v_escape = √(2gR) = √(2GM/R) ≈ 11.2 km/s. At this speed, the kinetic energy equals the magnitude of the gravitational potential energy. The Moon's lower escape velocity (2.4 km/s) explains why it has no atmosphere.

1st Law: Planets orbit the Sun in ellipses with the Sun at one focus. 2nd Law: Equal areas swept in equal times (faster when closer to Sun). 3rd Law: T² ∝ r³ (square of period proportional to cube of average radius). Newton derived these from his law of gravitation.

laws_and_formulas.name: Newton's Law of Gravitation
laws_and_formulas.description: Gravitational force between masses M and m separated by distance r; G = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².

laws_and_formulas.name: g from G
laws_and_formulas.description: Gravitational acceleration at Earth's surface derived from universal gravitation.

laws_and_formulas.name: Escape Velocity
laws_and_formulas.description: Minimum speed to escape Earth's gravitational field from the surface.

key_points: F = GMm/r²; G = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².
Gravity: always attractive, infinite range, weakest fundamental force.
g = GM/R² at surface; derives g from G, M, R.
g decreases with altitude: g_h = GM/(R+h)².
g decreases with depth: g_d = g(1 - d/R); zero at centre.
Orbital speed: v = √(GM/r) — higher orbit, lower speed.
Escape velocity: v = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s from Earth's surface.
Weightlessness in orbit: not zero gravity — both satellite and occupant are in free fall.
Kepler's 3rd Law: T² ∝ r³ — derived from Newton's law of gravitation.
Tides caused by differential gravitational pull of Moon (and Sun) on different sides of Earth.

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_W.overview: Work, energy, and power are the quantitative measures of mechanical interactions. Work is done when a force causes displacement; energy is the capacity to do work; power is the rate of doing work. The work-energy theorem directly links net work to the change in kinetic energy. At Class 10 level, these concepts are applied to more complex systems including machines, inclined planes, and problems where multiple forces act at angles to the displacement.

key_concepts.definition: W = F·d·cosθ, where θ is the angle between force and displacement. When θ = 0°: W = Fd (maximum). θ = 90°: W = 0 (no work — normal force, centripetal force). θ = 180°: W = -Fd (negative work — friction opposing motion).

W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mu². The net work done on a body equals the change in its kinetic energy. This is a direct consequence of Newton's 2nd Law integrated over displacement. If the net work is positive, KE increases; if negative, KE decreases.

P = W/t = Fv. Average power = work done / time taken. Instantaneous power = force × velocity at that instant. Unit: watt (W) = J/s. 1 horsepower (hp) = 746 W. Power rating of an engine determines how quickly it can do work.

η = useful output / total input = (work output / work input) × 100%. Always < 100% due to friction. For a motor lifting a load: useful output = mgh; input = Pt (electrical power × time). η = mgh/(Pt).

Gravitational PE = mgh (height above reference). Elastic PE = ½kx² (spring stretched/compressed by x, spring constant k). Chemical PE (stored in bonds — released in reactions). All are forms of stored energy that can do work when released.

In the absence of non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance): KE + PE = constant. A ball thrown upward: KE decreases, PE increases by exactly the same amount. At any point: ½mv² + mgh = ½mv₀² + mgh₀ (initial conditions).

Forces like friction and air resistance convert mechanical energy to heat. Work done by friction = -f × d (negative work; removes energy from the system). Total energy is still conserved (heat generated + remaining mechanical energy = initial mechanical energy).

A vehicle moving at constant velocity v against a resistive force F_resistance requires power P = F_resistance × v. Maximum speed is reached when driving force equals resistance: F_engine = F_resistance → P = F_resistance × v_max.

laws_and_formulas.name: Work
laws_and_formulas.description: Work done by force F over displacement d at angle θ to the displacement.

laws_and_formulas.name: Work-Energy Theorem
laws_and_formulas.description: Net work equals change in kinetic energy.

laws_and_formulas.name: Conservation of Mechanical Energy
laws_and_formulas.description: Total mechanical energy is conserved when only conservative forces act.

laws_and_formulas.name: Power
laws_and_formulas.description: Rate of doing work; also equals force times velocity for motion at constant speed.

key_points: W = Fd cosθ; zero work when θ = 90° (centripetal, normal forces).
Negative work: force opposes displacement (friction, braking).
Work-Energy Theorem: W_net = ΔKE — directly links dynamics to kinematics.
KE = ½mv²; PE = mgh; elastic PE = ½kx².
Conservation: KE + PE = constant (no friction) — energy converts between forms.
With friction: KE + PE decreases; mechanical energy lost = heat generated = friction force × distance.
P = W/t = Fv; unit: watt (W) = J/s.
Efficiency = (useful output / total input) × 100%; always < 100% with friction.
1 horsepower = 746 W.
Maximum speed: driving force = resistance force → P = F_resistance × v_max.

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_M.overview: Simple machines — levers, pulleys, inclined planes, gears, and screws — allow a smaller effort to overcome a larger load by trading force for distance. The mechanical advantage (MA) and velocity ratio (VR) characterise a machine's force multiplication and geometry. Efficiency = MA/VR. Due to friction, a real machine always has MA < VR. These principles underpin the design of all mechanical systems, from bicycles to cranes.

key_concepts.definition: MA = Load / Effort. The factor by which a machine multiplies the applied force. MA > 1: machine amplifies force (wheelbarrow). MA < 1: machine increases speed/distance at the cost of force (rowing oar). MA = 1: neither advantage nor disadvantage (simple pulley).

VR = distance moved by effort / distance moved by load (in the same time). Also called the ideal mechanical advantage. It is determined purely by the geometry of the machine, not by friction. For an ideal (frictionless) machine: MA = VR.
η = MA/VR = (useful work output / work input) × 100%. Due to friction, η < 1 (or < 100%) for all real machines. A machine with MA = 3 and VR = 5 has efficiency = 60%.

VR = 1, MA ≈ 1 (ideal). Only changes the direction of effort — no force advantage. Useful for redirecting effort from downward pull to lifting a load upward. Friction in the axle makes MA slightly less than 1 (effort > load).

VR = 2 (effort rope moves twice as far as the load). Ideal MA = 2. One end of the rope is fixed; load is attached to the moving pulley. A combination of fixed and movable pulleys forms a block and tackle.

A system of multiple pulleys. VR = number of rope segments supporting the load. If 4 segments support the load: VR = 4, ideal MA = 4, effort = load/4 (in ideal case). More segments: greater force advantage but more friction.

VR = length of incline / height (= 1/sinθ). Effort = load × sinθ (ideal). A ramp with gentle angle has high VR — small force to push load up slowly. Used in wheelchair ramps, road switchbacks, screw threads.

Class 1 (fulcrum between E and L): scissors, crowbar, see-saw — can have MA > or < 1. Class 2 (load between fulcrum and effort): wheelbarrow, nutcracker — always MA > 1. Class 3 (effort between fulcrum and load): forearm, tongs — always MA < 1 but gives larger movement of load.

laws_and_formulas.name: Mechanical Advantage
laws_and_formulas.description: Force multiplication factor of a machine.

laws_and_formulas.name: Velocity Ratio
laws_and_formulas.description: Geometric ratio determined by the design of the machine, independent of friction.

laws_and_formulas.name: Efficiency
laws_and_formulas.formula: η = MA / VR = (Work output / Work input) × 100%
laws_and_formulas.description: Fraction of input work converted to useful output work.

key_points: MA = Load/Effort; VR = effort distance / load distance; η = MA/VR.
Ideal machine (no friction): MA = VR; efficiency = 100%.
Real machine: MA < VR due to friction; efficiency < 100%.
Fixed pulley: VR = 1 (only changes direction). Movable pulley: VR = 2.
Block and tackle: VR = number of rope segments supporting the load.
Inclined plane: VR = L/h = 1/sinθ; gentler slope → higher VR.
Class 1 lever: MA can be > or < 1. Class 2: always MA > 1. Class 3: always MA < 1.
MA > 1: force advantage; VR > MA means friction losses.
Principle of conservation of energy: work input = useful work output + work against friction.
A machine cannot create energy: the advantage in force is paid for in distance.

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_C.overview: The law of conservation of energy — energy can neither be created nor destroyed, only transformed from one form to another — is one of the most fundamental principles in all of science. At Class 10 level, this is applied quantitatively to mechanical systems, energy transformations in machines, and the relationship between different energy forms. Understanding energy conservation is essential for analysing all physical, chemical, and biological processes.

key_concepts.definition: The total energy of an isolated system is constant. Energy can be transformed (mechanical → heat, chemical → electrical, nuclear → heat) but the total never increases or decreases. This is a universal law with no known exceptions in classical or quantum physics.

Examples: Burning fuel: chemical → thermal → kinetic (engine); Hydroelectric dam: gravitational PE → KE → electrical; Solar panel: radiant → electrical; Human body: chemical (food) → mechanical + thermal; Loudspeaker: electrical → sound.
For a ball falling freely: KE + PE = constant = E_total. At height h: KE = 0, PE = mgh. At ground: KE = mgh, PE = 0. At any intermediate height h': ½mv'² + mgh' = mgh → v' = √(2g(h-h')).
