At the highest point of swing: KE = 0, PE = max. At the lowest point: KE = max, PE = 0. At any intermediate point: KE + PE = total energy = mgh (h = max height). Friction gradually reduces the total mechanical energy (converted to heat in the string/air).

All energy transformations involve some energy converted to less useful forms (typically heat). This is a statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics: energy becomes less organised (degraded) with each transformation. The total energy is conserved but useful energy decreases.

Einstein's E = mc²: mass can be converted to energy. In nuclear fission or fusion, a tiny mass deficit results in enormous energy release. This is the principle behind nuclear reactors and nuclear weapons, and the energy source of stars.

Non-renewable: fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas), nuclear (fission) — finite supply, release CO₂ (fossil fuels). Renewable: solar, wind, hydroelectric, tidal, geothermal — replenished naturally, lower environmental impact. Energy conservation includes reducing unnecessary energy use.

A power station: chemical (fuel) → thermal → mechanical → electrical. Each step has losses. Overall efficiency of a coal power station: ~35–40%. LED light bulb: ~50% efficient (more efficient than 5% for incandescent). Efficiency = useful output / total input.

laws_and_formulas.name: Conservation of Mechanical Energy
laws_and_formulas.formula: KE + PE = constant; ½mv² + mgh = ½mu² + mgh₀
laws_and_formulas.description: In the absence of friction, total mechanical energy is conserved.

laws_and_formulas.name: Mass-Energy Equivalence
laws_and_formulas.description: Energy equivalent of mass m (c = speed of light = 3×10⁸ m/s); basis of nuclear energy.

key_points: Energy is conserved in all processes — it transforms, never disappears.
Mechanical energy: KE + PE = constant (no friction).
With friction: mechanical energy decreases (converted to heat); total energy still conserved.
Free fall: KE gained = PE lost; v = √(2gh) at ground (started from rest at height h).
Pendulum: KE↔PE exchange; friction slowly reduces mechanical energy total.
E = mc²: mass-energy equivalence; nuclear reactions convert tiny mass to enormous energy.
Energy degradation: useful energy decreases in each transformation (2nd Law).
Fossil fuels: chemical PE → thermal → mechanical → electrical (each step has losses).
Renewable energy: solar, wind, hydro — replenished naturally.
Efficiency = useful output / total input; always < 100% in real systems.

10|physics|Light_Refraction_of_Light.overview: Refraction is the bending of light at the interface between two transparent media due to a change in speed. Snell's Law quantitatively relates the angles of incidence and refraction to the refractive indices of the media. At Class 10, refraction is studied more mathematically — including solving problems using Snell's Law, computing refractive indices, and understanding real and apparent depth. These concepts are the gateway to understanding lenses, cameras, and optical instruments.

key_concepts.definition: n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂. The product of refractive index and sine of angle (from normal) is conserved across a refracting interface. For light going from air (n=1) into glass (n=1.5): sinθ_glass = sinθ_air / 1.5 → light bends toward the normal (slows down).

n = c/v where c = speed of light in vacuum (3×10⁸ m/s) and v = speed in the medium. Also n = sinθ₁/sinθ₂ (from Snell's Law, with θ₁ in vacuum/air). Glass n ≈ 1.5 → light travels at 2×10⁸ m/s in glass.

When light passes through a glass slab, it refracts twice: entering and exiting. The emergent ray is parallel to the incident ray but laterally displaced (shifted sideways). More displacement with thicker slab or larger incident angle.

Apparent depth = Real depth / n. A fish in water at 3 m real depth appears to be at 3/1.33 ≈ 2.26 m. Conversely, a bird looking at a fish from above: apparent depth (to the fish) of the bird = real height / n_water... actually this is: for viewing from air into water, apparent depth = real depth/n.

sin C = n₂/n₁ (1/n if going from medium into air). For glass (n=1.5): sin C = 1/1.5 = 0.667 → C ≈ 42°. For water (n=1.33): C ≈ 48.8°. Diamond (n=2.42): C ≈ 24° — hence brilliant sparkle via total internal reflection.

Different wavelengths of white light travel at slightly different speeds in glass → different refractive indices → different angles of refraction. Violet light has the highest n (refracted most); red has the lowest n (refracted least). A prism disperses white light into a spectrum.

Earth's atmosphere has increasing density toward the surface → refractive index increases downward → light rays curve toward the surface. Effects: stars appear higher than actual; Sun visible slightly before sunrise and after sunset; stars twinkle (variable refraction due to atmospheric turbulence).

In hot weather, air near the ground is less dense (lower n). Light from the sky undergoes total internal reflection in the hot air layer near the ground and reaches the observer from below — appearing as water. In deserts (inferior mirage) or at sea (superior mirage).

laws_and_formulas.name: Snell's Law
laws_and_formulas.description: Quantitative law of refraction relating angles and refractive indices.

laws_and_formulas.name: Refractive Index
laws_and_formulas.description: Ratio of speed of light in vacuum to speed in medium; also ratio of sines of angles.

laws_and_formulas.name: Apparent Depth
laws_and_formulas.description: Object in a medium of refractive index n appears at reduced depth when viewed from a less dense medium.

key_points: Snell's Law: n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂.
n = c/v; glass n ≈ 1.5 → light at 2×10⁸ m/s in glass.
Entering denser medium: bends toward normal (speed decreases).
Lateral displacement through glass slab: emergent ray parallel to incident ray.
Apparent depth = real depth / n (pool looks shallower from above).
Critical angle: sin C = 1/n (for light going from medium to air).
Dispersion: violet (n highest, bends most); red (n lowest, bends least).
Mirage: hot air near ground has lower n → total internal reflection of sky light.
Atmospheric refraction: stars appear higher than they are; Sun visible past horizon.
Diamond brilliance: very small critical angle (≈24°) → light trapped by TIR inside.

10|physics|Light_Refraction_through_Glas.overview: A glass slab causes lateral displacement of light without change in direction; a glass prism disperses white light into its spectrum. In both cases, refraction occurs at two surfaces. Prisms are used in spectroscopes, periscopes (using TIR), and optical instruments. Dispersion by a prism reveals the component wavelengths of white light and is the basis of spectroscopy — the analysis of light from atoms, molecules, and stars.

key_concepts.definition: Light is refracted twice: at the air-glass interface (bends toward normal) and at the glass-air interface (bends away from normal). The emergent ray is parallel to the incident ray but laterally displaced. Displacement increases with slab thickness and incident angle.

The perpendicular distance between the incident ray and the emergent ray after passing through the slab. d = t sinθ₁(1 - cosθ₁/n) approximately, or d = t/cosθ₂ × sin(θ₁-θ₂) exactly. Greater thickness or larger angle → more displacement.

Light refracts at both faces of the prism. The prism bends light toward its base. Deviation δ = angle between incident ray direction and emergent ray direction. For a symmetric path (minimum deviation): δ_min = 2θ - A (A = apex angle).

Different wavelengths of white light have different refractive indices in glass. Violet has the highest n (most bending toward base); red has the lowest n (least bending). White light is spread into a spectrum: VIBGYOR (from most to least deviated).

The difference in deviation between extreme wavelengths (violet and red): δ_V - δ_R. The ratio (δ_V - δ_R)/δ_mean = dispersive power ω. A high-dispersion prism (like flint glass) spreads colours more than a low-dispersion prism (crown glass).

Newton demonstrated that white light consists of all visible wavelengths (colours). A prism separates them by differential refraction. Combining two prisms apex-to-base recombines the spectrum into white light, confirming white light is a mixture.

At minimum deviation, the light passes symmetrically through the prism (the path through the prism is parallel to the base). At this angle, the relationship n = sin((A + δ_min)/2) / sin(A/2) can be used to find n from measured A and δ_min.

A 45°-45°-90° prism (with TIR at the hypotenuse) is used in: periscopes (bends light through 90°), binoculars (twice, to shorten and reinvert the image), and retroreflectors. It gives sharper, brighter images than flat mirrors (no partial transmission).

laws_and_formulas.name: Refractive Index from Prism
laws_and_formulas.description: Measures n from the apex angle A and minimum deviation δ_min.

laws_and_formulas.name: Deviation by Prism
laws_and_formulas.description: Approximate deviation for a thin prism.

key_points: Glass slab: two refractions → emergent ray parallel to incident ray but laterally displaced.
Lateral displacement increases with slab thickness and incident angle.
Prism: bends light toward base; greater deviation for smaller wavelength (violet).
VIBGYOR: violet deviated most, red deviated least through a prism.
Dispersion: different n for different wavelengths → different bending angles.
Minimum deviation: light passes symmetrically; n = sin((A+δ_min)/2)/sin(A/2).
White light = all visible wavelengths; prism separates them (Newton's demonstration).
TIR prism: 45°-45°-90°; used in periscopes, binoculars (more efficient than flat mirrors).
Recombining prisms apex-to-base: reverses dispersion, produces white light again.
Spectroscopy: different elements emit characteristic spectral lines — used to identify substances.

10|physics|Light_Total_Internal_Reflecti.overview: When light travels from a denser to a less dense medium and the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle, all light is reflected back into the denser medium — total internal reflection. This phenomenon is the basis for optical fibres (internet, medical endoscopes), the brilliance of diamonds, and prism-based periscopes. Understanding TIR and the critical angle is a key application of Snell's Law and is central to modern photonics.

key_concepts.definition: Two conditions must both be met: (1) Light must travel from a denser medium (higher n) to a less dense medium (lower n). (2) The angle of incidence must be greater than the critical angle (C). When both conditions are met, no light is transmitted — all is reflected.

The angle of incidence (in the denser medium) at which the angle of refraction = 90° (refracted ray travels along the interface). sin C = n₂/n₁ = 1/n (for n₁ > n₂, with n₂ = 1 for air). Glass (n=1.5): C = 42°; water (n=1.33): C ≈ 49°; diamond (n=2.42): C ≈ 24°.

key_concepts.term: Total Internal Reflection vs. Partial Reflection
key_concepts.definition: Below C: light is partially refracted (transmitted) and partially reflected. At C: refracted ray grazes the surface. Above C: 100% of light is reflected (total internal reflection). TIR is perfect — no energy is lost in the reflection.

A thin glass or plastic strand; light enters at one end and is repeatedly totally internally reflected along the length, even around curves. Core has higher n than cladding. Used in telecommunications (internet, telephone) at high speed; endoscopes transmit light and images inside the body.

A 45°-45°-90° glass prism reflects light internally at 90° — used in periscopes (replacing flat mirrors), binoculars, and cameras. Since 45° > critical angle of glass (42°), total internal reflection occurs at the hypotenuse face.

Diamond has n ≈ 2.42 → C ≈ 24°. Light enters the top face, strikes the angled facets at angles > 24° → total internal reflection. Light bounces around inside and exits from the top, producing intense sparkle. Facets are cut to maximise TIR.

On hot roads, the air near the ground has lower density (lower n). Light from the sky hits this less-dense layer at angles > critical angle → total internal reflection. The reflected sky appears as a shimmering 'water' pool on the road ahead.

A medical instrument using optical fibres to view inside body cavities. Light travels down one bundle of fibres to illuminate the site; reflected light travels back up another bundle to form an image. No incision needed; revolutionised non-invasive medicine.

laws_and_formulas.name: Critical Angle
laws_and_formulas.formula: sin C = 1/n (for medium with index n, adjacent to air)
laws_and_formulas.description: Angle of incidence at which refracted ray travels along the interface; TIR occurs above this angle.

key_points: TIR requires: (1) denser to less dense medium; (2) angle of incidence > critical angle.
sin C = 1/n (for glass-air: C ≈ 42° for n=1.5).
At the critical angle: refracted ray along the boundary (90° refraction).
Above C: 100% reflection, no refraction — TIR is perfect.
Optical fibre: repeated TIR along the fibre; used in internet cables and endoscopes.
45°-45°-90° prism: reflects at 90° via TIR; used in periscopes and binoculars.
Diamond: C ≈ 24° (n ≈ 2.42); facets cut for maximum internal reflection → brilliance.
Mirage: TIR in hot air layer near the ground.
Higher n → smaller critical angle → more easily achieves TIR.
Cladding of optical fibre has lower n than core to ensure TIR at the boundary.

10|physics|Light_Lenses_Convex_and_Conca.overview: Lenses refract light at two curved surfaces to converge (convex lens) or diverge (concave lens) rays. Convex (converging) lenses form real, inverted images when the object is beyond the focal point, and virtual, erect, magnified images when inside the focal length. The lens formula (1/v - 1/u = 1/f) and magnification (m = v/u) enable image location and size calculation. Lenses are the basis of eyes, cameras, microscopes, telescopes, and spectacles.

key_concepts.definition: Thicker at the centre, thinner at the edges. Converges parallel rays to the principal focus F. Focal length f > 0. Can form both real and virtual images depending on object position. Used in: magnifying glass, camera, projector, eye (crystalline lens), telescope objective.

Thinner at the centre, thicker at the edges. Diverges parallel rays — the extensions of the refracted rays appear to come from the principal focus (on the same side as the incident light). Focal length f < 0. Always forms virtual, erect, diminished images. Used in: spectacles for short-sightedness.

1/v - 1/u = 1/f (using the real-is-positive sign convention: distances in the direction of incident light are positive; against it are negative). For a convex lens, f is positive; concave lens, f is negative.
m = v/u = height of image / height of object. If m > 0: virtual, erect image. If m < 0: real, inverted image. If |m| > 1: image is magnified; |m| < 1: image is diminished.

P = 1/f (f in metres). Unit: dioptre (D). A convex lens of f = 0.5 m has P = +2 D. Concave: P is negative. Power of combination of thin lenses in contact: P_total = P₁ + P₂ + ... This is why optometrists specify lens strength in dioptres.

Object at infinity: image at F, real, inverted, point-sized. Beyond 2F: real, inverted, diminished (camera). At 2F: real, inverted, same size. Between F and 2F: real, inverted, magnified (projector). At F: image at infinity (parallel beams — searchlight). Inside F: virtual, erect, magnified (magnifying glass).

The eye's crystalline lens is a flexible convex lens that accommodates (changes focal length) to focus objects at different distances. Near point: 25 cm. Far point: infinity (for normal vision). Short-sighted (myopia): images form in front of retina — corrected with concave lens. Long-sighted (hyperopia): images form behind retina — corrected with convex lens.

Spherical aberration: rays through the edge focus at different points than paraxial rays → blurry image. Chromatic aberration: different wavelengths focus at different points due to dispersion. Corrected by using compound lenses (achromatic doublets).

laws_and_formulas.name: Lens Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Relates object distance u, image distance v, and focal length f of a lens.

laws_and_formulas.name: Magnification
laws_and_formulas.formula: m = v/u = image height / object height
laws_and_formulas.description: Lateral magnification; positive = virtual/erect; negative = real/inverted.

laws_and_formulas.name: Power of Lens
laws_and_formulas.description: Lens strength; convex: positive P; concave: negative P.

key_points: Convex (converging): f > 0, thicker centre. Concave (diverging): f < 0, thinner centre.
Lens formula: 1/v - 1/u = 1/f (real-is-positive convention).
m = v/u; negative m → real, inverted; positive m → virtual, erect.
Convex lens: object inside F → virtual, erect, magnified (magnifying glass).
Convex lens: object beyond 2F → real, inverted, diminished (camera).
Concave lens: always virtual, erect, diminished.
Power P = 1/f(metres); unit dioptre; P_combination = P₁ + P₂.
Short-sightedness: concave lens (diverges to move image back to retina).
Long-sightedness: convex lens (converges to move image forward onto retina).
Eye's ciliary muscles change the curvature of the crystalline lens for accommodation.

10|physics|Light_The_Electromagnetic_Spe.overview: Visible light is just a tiny portion of the electromagnetic spectrum — a family of transverse waves that includes radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays. All travel at the speed of light (3×10⁸ m/s) in a vacuum. They differ only in frequency (and wavelength). The electromagnetic spectrum has revolutionised communications, medicine, astronomy, and energy production.

key_concepts.definition: Transverse waves consisting of oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation. Require no medium — can travel through a vacuum at c = 3×10⁸ m/s. All EM waves obey: c = fλ.

Radio waves → Microwaves → Infrared → Visible light (ROYGBIV) → Ultraviolet → X-rays → Gamma rays. As frequency increases: wavelength decreases, energy per photon increases (E = hf). Gamma rays carry the most energy per photon; radio waves the least.
Frequency: 3 Hz – 3 GHz; wavelength: 100 km – 0.1 m. Used in radio and television broadcasting, radar, mobile communications. Low energy per photon — not ionising. Longest wavelength in the spectrum.

Frequency: 3 GHz – 300 GHz; wavelength: 0.1 m – 1 mm. Used in microwave ovens (absorbed by water molecules → heating food), mobile phone networks (4G/5G), satellite communications, radar. Microwave ovens heat from inside the food.

Frequency: 300 GHz – 430 THz; wavelength: 1 mm – 700 nm. Felt as heat. Emitted by all warm objects. Used in: TV remote controls, night vision cameras, heat sensors, thermal imaging, optical fibre communications (near-infrared).

Frequency: 430 THz – 750 THz; wavelength: 700 nm (red) – 400 nm (violet). The only part of the EM spectrum detectable by the human eye. Used in optical instruments, photography, fibre optics, photosynthesis.

Frequency: 750 THz – 30 PHz; wavelength: 400 nm – 10 nm. Ionising at higher frequencies. Sources: Sun, UV lamps. Applications: sterilisation (kills bacteria), fluorescence (UV paints, security markers), sun-tanning/sunburn, vitamin D production. Absorbed by ozone layer.

X-rays (10 PHz – 30 EHz; 10–0.01 nm): penetrate soft tissue, absorbed by bone → medical imaging, airport security, crystallography. Gamma rays (>30 EHz; <0.01 nm): from nuclear processes; highest energy; used in cancer radiotherapy (kill tumour cells), food irradiation, sterilisation of medical equipment.

laws_and_formulas.name: EM Wave Equation
laws_and_formulas.description: All EM waves travel at c in vacuum; frequency × wavelength = speed of light.

laws_and_formulas.name: Photon Energy
laws_and_formulas.description: Energy of a single photon; higher frequency = more energetic (more ionising).

key_points: All EM waves travel at c = 3×10⁸ m/s in vacuum; c = fλ.
Order (increasing frequency/energy): Radio → Microwave → IR → Visible → UV → X-ray → Gamma.
Radio: broadcasting. Microwave: ovens, mobile phones. IR: heat sensors, remote controls.
Visible: 400 nm (violet) to 700 nm (red).
UV: sterilisation, vitamin D, fluorescence; blocked by ozone layer.
X-rays: medical imaging; penetrate soft tissue, absorbed by dense materials.
Gamma rays: highest energy; cancer therapy, nuclear medicine, sterilisation.
Ionising radiation: UV (high), X-rays, gamma rays — can damage DNA.
All EM waves are transverse; no medium needed; all polarisable.
Photon energy E = hf — gamma photons are millions of times more energetic than radio photons.

10|physics|Sound_Reflection_of_Sound_Wav.overview: Sound waves reflect from surfaces following the same laws as light — the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. Echoes are heard when reflected sound arrives at least 0.1 s after the original. Reverberation is the persistence of sound in an enclosed space due to multiple reflections. Acoustics of concert halls, sonar mapping, echolocation, and ultrasonic distance measurement all exploit sound reflection. This chapter also covers the speed of sound in different media and how distance is calculated from echo time.

key_concepts.definition: 1. Angle of incidence = angle of reflection (both measured from the normal to the reflecting surface). 2. Incident wave, reflected wave, and normal are in the same plane. Identical to the laws of reflection for light, but sound is a mechanical wave requiring a medium.

A reflected sound heard as a distinct repetition. Human ear cannot distinguish two sounds less than 0.1 s apart. So for an echo to be heard: reflected sound must travel an extra distance = v × 0.1 = 340 × 0.1 = 34 m → minimum distance to reflector = 17 m. Distance = v × t/2.

The persistence of sound in a room due to multiple reflections. Reverberation time: time for sound to decay to 10⁻⁶ of original intensity (60 dB drop). Concert halls: designed for 1.5–2.5 s reverberation. Too much: speech becomes muddy. Too little: sound seems dead.

Concert halls use curved walls to reflect sound uniformly. Sound-absorbing materials (thick curtains, padded seats, carpet) reduce reverberation. Hard surfaces (bare concrete) increase reverberation. Optimal reverberation depends on the type of sound (speech vs. orchestral music).

Emits ultrasonic pulses and detects the echo. Distance = v × t/2. Used to: map ocean floors, locate submarines, detect fish, measure water depth (echo sounding). Speed of sound in seawater ≈ 1500 m/s. Used since World War I to detect submarines.

Bats emit ultrasonic pulses (20–200 kHz) and interpret the echo arrival time and direction to locate prey with millimetre precision even in complete darkness. Dolphins use sonar at 150 kHz. Barn swallows, oilbirds, and cave swiftlets also use echolocation to navigate in dark caves.

A megaphone is a cone that directs and amplifies sound in one direction by preventing spreading — the cross-sectional area increases so energy concentration increases at the wide end for input or narrows for projection. An ear trumpet converges diffuse sound waves into the ear canal.

Sound from the patient's body is reflected multiple times along the tubing to the ear. Sound is a mechanical wave and can be guided along a tube, similar to how light travels along an optical fibre. Total reflection of sound in the tube prevents energy loss.

laws_and_formulas.name: Echo Distance
laws_and_formulas.formula: d = vt/2 (v = speed of sound, t = time for echo to return)
laws_and_formulas.description: Distance to reflecting surface; total path = 2d; so d = vt/2.

laws_and_formulas.name: Minimum Echo Distance
laws_and_formulas.description: Minimum distance from which a distinct echo can be heard (0.1 s threshold for human ear).

key_points: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection (from normal) — same as light.
Echo heard distinctly if reflected sound arrives ≥ 0.1 s later.
Minimum reflecting distance for echo = 17 m (in air at 340 m/s).
d = vt/2 — sonar, echolocation, and rangefinding formula.
Reverberation: persistence of sound from multiple reflections in enclosed spaces.
Too much reverberation: speech unintelligible. Too little: music sounds dead.
Sonar: depth/distance measurement using ultrasonic echoes underwater.
Echolocation: bats (20–200 kHz), dolphins (up to 150 kHz).
Acoustic design: curved walls distribute sound; soft furnishings absorb excess.
Speed of sound in air ≈ 340 m/s; water ≈ 1500 m/s; steel ≈ 5000 m/s.

10|physics|Sound_Natural_Damped_and_Forc.overview: All vibrating systems — strings, air columns, bridges — have a natural frequency determined by their physical properties. When a system vibrates after an initial disturbance without any continuing force, it undergoes natural (free) vibration. When the driving force is removed, friction gradually reduces the amplitude — damped vibration. When an external force drives the system at a specific frequency, the response can be enormously amplified — resonance. These concepts explain musical instruments, structural failures, and medical imaging.

key_concepts.definition: Vibration of a system at its natural frequency f₀ after an initial disturbance, with no continuing applied force. Frequency depends only on the system's properties (mass, stiffness). A tuning fork, guitar string, and pendulum all vibrate at their characteristic natural frequencies.

The frequency at which a system naturally oscillates. For a simple pendulum: f = 1/(2π)√(g/L). For a mass on a spring: f = 1/(2π)√(k/m). The natural frequency is an intrinsic property — it does not depend on amplitude (for small oscillations).

Vibration in which amplitude decreases over time due to energy loss to friction, air resistance, or other dissipative forces. The frequency remains approximately the same but the amplitude decays. Heavily damped: system returns to equilibrium without oscillation (shock absorbers).

Underdamped: oscillates with decreasing amplitude. Critically damped: returns to equilibrium as quickly as possible without oscillating (ideal for door closers, galvanometers). Overdamped: returns to equilibrium slowly without oscillating (too much friction).

When an external periodic force drives a system at a frequency determined by the source (not the natural frequency). The system is forced to vibrate at the driving frequency. The amplitude depends on how close the driving frequency is to the natural frequency.

When the driving frequency equals the natural frequency, the system absorbs energy most efficiently → amplitude builds up dramatically. Resonance is the condition f_driving = f_natural. The larger the damping, the broader but lower the resonance peak.

The Tacoma Narrows Bridge (1940) collapsed because wind-driven oscillations were close to the bridge's natural frequency → resonance → amplitude built up → structural failure. A dramatic example of destructive resonance in an engineering structure.

A guitar's hollow body resonates at the frequencies of the strings, amplifying the sound. A wine glass sings when rubbed (natural frequency); a powerful singer can shatter it with the same frequency. Tuning forks: two identical forks — one vibrated near the other causes the second to vibrate (resonance).

laws_and_formulas.name: Natural Frequency (Pendulum)
laws_and_formulas.description: Natural frequency of a simple pendulum of length L in gravitational field g.

laws_and_formulas.name: Natural Frequency (Spring)
laws_and_formulas.description: Natural frequency of mass m on spring of spring constant k.

key_points: Natural vibration: system oscillates at f₀ after disturbance; no continuing force.
f₀ determined by physical properties (length of pendulum, mass and spring constant).
Damped vibration: amplitude decreases due to energy loss; frequency approximately unchanged.
Forced vibration: system driven at the source's frequency, not its own.
Resonance: driving frequency = natural frequency → maximum amplitude.
Resonance: potentially destructive (Tacoma Bridge); potentially useful (MRI, tuning).
More damping: resonance peak is lower and broader.
Critically damped: fastest return to equilibrium without oscillation (shock absorbers).
Two identical tuning forks: striking one causes the other to sound (resonance).
Musicians tune instruments by matching frequencies to reference (resonance criterion).

10|physics|Sound_Resonance.overview: Resonance is the dramatic amplification of vibration that occurs when a driving frequency matches a system's natural frequency. It is central to the operation of musical instruments — the air column inside a flute or pipe organ resonates at specific frequencies determined by the column's length. The chapter also covers stationary waves, harmonics, and overtones. Resonance has profound applications in MRI scanners, radio tuning, bridge engineering, and acoustic design.

key_concepts.definition: When sound waves travel down a pipe and reflect from the closed or open end, they interfere with incoming waves. At specific frequencies, constructive interference creates a standing wave with maximum amplitude — resonance. Organ pipes, flutes, and the resonance tube experiment all exploit this.

An open pipe (both ends open) has antinodes at both ends. Fundamental: L = λ₁/2 → f₁ = v/2L. Second harmonic: L = λ₂ → f₂ = v/L = 2f₁. Third harmonic: f₃ = 3f₁. All harmonics (integer multiples of f₁) are present.

A closed pipe (one end closed, one open) has a node at the closed end and antinode at the open end. Fundamental: L = λ₁/4 → f₁ = v/4L. Third harmonic: f₃ = 3v/4L = 3f₁. Only odd harmonics are present.

Formed by superposition of two identical waves travelling in opposite directions. Points of zero amplitude: nodes. Points of maximum amplitude: antinodes. Between adjacent node and antinode: λ/4. Between adjacent nodes: λ/2. Energy does not travel in stationary waves.

A tube partially immersed in water with a tuning fork above the open end. As the water level is lowered, the air column length increases. Resonance (loud sound) is heard when the column length = λ/4, 3λ/4, 5λ/4... From these lengths, the speed of sound v = f × λ = f × 4 × first resonant length.

Harmonics: integer multiples of the fundamental frequency (f₁, 2f₁, 3f₁, ...). Overtones: harmonics above the fundamental. 1st overtone = 2nd harmonic (for open pipe) or 3rd harmonic (for closed pipe). The mixture of harmonics determines the quality (timbre) of a musical sound.

String instruments: string vibrates, resonates in the hollow body (amplified). Wind instruments: air column resonates. The length determines the pitch: longer column → lower frequency. Holes or valves change the effective length → different pitches.

Marching soldiers must break step on bridges — their cadence could match the bridge's natural frequency → resonance → dangerous oscillations. Radio tuning: a resonant circuit selected to match the transmitter's frequency. MRI: radio waves at the resonant frequency of hydrogen nuclei.

laws_and_formulas.name: Open Pipe Fundamental
laws_and_formulas.description: Fundamental frequency of an open pipe of length L with sound speed v.

laws_and_formulas.name: Closed Pipe Fundamental
laws_and_formulas.description: Fundamental frequency of a closed pipe (closed at one end) of length L.

laws_and_formulas.name: Speed of Sound from Resonance Tube
laws_and_formulas.description: Calculate speed of sound from first resonance length and tuning fork frequency.

key_points: Resonance: driving frequency = natural frequency → maximum amplitude.
Open pipe: f_n = nv/2L (n = 1,2,3,...) — all harmonics present.
Closed pipe: f_n = nv/4L (n = 1,3,5,...) — only odd harmonics.
Closed pipe: fundamental = half the frequency of equivalent open pipe.
Standing wave: nodes (zero displacement), antinodes (maximum displacement).
Node-to-node distance = λ/2; node-to-adjacent-antinode = λ/4.
Resonance tube: first resonance at L = λ/4 → v = 4fL₁.
Timbre: mixture of harmonics determines quality of musical sound.
Radio tuning: resonant circuit matches transmitter frequency — selectivity.
MRI: resonance of hydrogen nuclei at radio frequencies in a magnetic field.

10|physics|Sound_Loudness_Pitch_and_Qual.overview: Every sound can be characterised by three subjective qualities — loudness, pitch, and quality (timbre) — each directly linked to an objective physical property of the sound wave. Loudness depends on intensity (amplitude); pitch depends on frequency; quality depends on the waveform (mixture of harmonics). Understanding these relationships is essential for acoustics, music production, medical audiology, and noise pollution control.

key_concepts.definition: Subjective perception of sound intensity. Loudness increases with amplitude (and intensity). Measured on the decibel (dB) scale: dB = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀), where I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² (threshold of hearing). Whisper ≈ 30 dB; normal conversation ≈ 60 dB; jet engine ≈ 140 dB.

The power per unit area of the wavefront: I = P/(4πr²) for a point source (inverse square law). Intensity decreases with the square of distance. Intensity is an objective measurable quantity; loudness is its subjective perception.

A logarithmic scale for sound intensity. Every 10 dB increase represents a tenfold increase in intensity; every 6 dB increase roughly doubles the perceived loudness. The scale is logarithmic because human perception of loudness is approximately logarithmic (Weber-Fechner Law).

Subjective perception of frequency — how high or low a sound seems. Higher frequency → higher pitch. Fundamental frequency determines the perceived pitch. Range of human hearing: 20 Hz (very low, thunder-like) to 20,000 Hz (very high, high-pitched whistle). The range narrows with age.

Musical notes correspond to specific frequencies: A4 = 440 Hz (standard concert pitch). An octave corresponds to doubling of frequency (e.g., A3 = 220 Hz, A4 = 440 Hz, A5 = 880 Hz). The 12 semitones of an octave are equally spaced on a logarithmic scale.

The characteristic that distinguishes two sounds of the same pitch and loudness from different instruments or sources. Determined by the waveform shape — specifically the number and relative amplitude of harmonics (overtones) present. A flute produces nearly pure tones; a violin has many harmonics → richer timbre.

Amplitude: larger amplitude → more energy → louder. Distance from source: inverse square law (I ∝ 1/r²). Medium: sound travels differently in different media. Frequency: human ear is most sensitive at 1000–4000 Hz (equal loudness contours — Fletcher-Munson curves).

Excessive or unwanted sound that is harmful to health. Prolonged exposure to > 85 dB damages hearing. Sources: traffic, industrial machinery, aircraft. Control: engineering (quieter engines), barriers, distance, personal protection (ear muffs). Noise ordinances limit environmental sound levels.

laws_and_formulas.name: Sound Intensity Level
laws_and_formulas.description: Sound level in decibels relative to the threshold of hearing.

laws_and_formulas.name: Intensity Inverse Square Law
laws_and_formulas.description: Intensity decreases with the square of distance from a point source.

key_points: Loudness ↔ amplitude; pitch ↔ frequency; quality ↔ waveform/harmonics.
Intensity: I = P/(4πr²); inverse square law — double distance → quarter intensity.
Decibel scale: β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀); 10 dB increase = 10× intensity.
Threshold of hearing: I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m²; threshold of pain: ~130 dB.
Pitch = frequency perception; higher f → higher pitch.
Octave: frequency doubles (e.g., 440 Hz → 880 Hz).
Timbre: mixture of harmonics; flute (pure), violin (rich overtones).
Human hearing most sensitive: 1000–4000 Hz.
Hearing range: 20 Hz – 20,000 Hz (decreases with age).
> 85 dB: risk of hearing damage with prolonged exposure.

10|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Ohm.overview: Ohm's Law — voltage is proportional to current at constant temperature — is the foundation of circuit analysis. Resistance is the property that limits current flow and converts electrical energy to heat. This chapter deepens the understanding of resistance: its dependence on length, area, and resistivity; the distinction between ohmic and non-ohmic conductors; and the use of V-I characteristics to identify device behaviour. These concepts underpin the analysis of all electrical circuits.

key_concepts.definition: V = IR. The current through a conductor at constant temperature is directly proportional to the voltage across it. An ohmic conductor shows a linear V-I graph passing through the origin. Non-ohmic conductors (diodes, bulbs, thermistors) show non-linear V-I graphs.

R = V/I. The opposition to current flow. Unit: ohm (Ω). 1 Ω = 1 V/A. Resistance arises from collisions between electrons (charge carriers) and the lattice atoms of the conductor. These collisions convert electrical energy to heat.

R = ρL/A. Resistance of a conductor depends on: resistivity ρ (material property, Ω·m), length L (R ∝ L — longer wire has more resistance), cross-sectional area A (R ∝ 1/A — thicker wire has less resistance). Resistivity of copper ≈ 1.7×10⁻⁸ Ω·m.

For metals: resistance increases with temperature (more lattice vibration → more collisions). For semiconductors (thermistors, NTC): resistance decreases with temperature (more charge carriers released). This is why incandescent bulbs have different resistance when hot vs. cold.

Ohmic: V-I graph is a straight line through origin (R = constant). Examples: metal wire (at constant temperature), resistors. Non-ohmic: V-I graph is curved. Examples: filament bulb (R increases as it heats), diode (conducts in one direction only), thermistor.

A graph of voltage (x-axis) vs. current (y-axis) for a component. Slope = 1/R (conductance). For an ohmic conductor: straight line (constant slope). For a bulb: slope decreases (R increases) at high voltage. For a diode: almost no current for negative V; steep rise for positive V above 0.7 V.

A real battery has internal resistance r: terminal voltage V = EMF - Ir. When current I flows, some voltage is 'lost' internally: V_lost = Ir. Short circuit current = EMF/r. Good batteries have low internal resistance → terminal voltage close to EMF under load.

Four resistors arranged in a diamond; a galvanometer across the middle. When P/Q = R/S (balanced bridge): galvanometer reads zero. Used to find an unknown resistance precisely. The principle of the Wheatstone bridge is used in strain gauges and sensor circuits.

laws_and_formulas.name: Ohm's Law
laws_and_formulas.description: Voltage = current × resistance (at constant temperature).

laws_and_formulas.name: Resistivity
laws_and_formulas.description: Resistance depends on material (ρ), length (L), and cross-sectional area (A).

laws_and_formulas.name: Terminal Voltage
laws_and_formulas.description: Terminal voltage of a battery with internal resistance r carrying current I.

key_points: Ohm's Law: V = IR — linear V-I relationship at constant temperature.
Ohmic: straight V-I line through origin. Non-ohmic: curved (bulb, diode, thermistor).
R = ρL/A: resistance ∝ length, ∝ 1/area, depends on material (ρ).
Metals: R increases with temperature. Semiconductors/NTC thermistors: R decreases.
Internal resistance: terminal voltage = EMF - Ir.
High internal resistance: large voltage drop under load; battery 'flattens' quickly.
Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + ... (add directly).
Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + ... (add reciprocals).
Slope of V-I graph = R; slope of I-V graph = 1/R = conductance (G).
Wheatstone bridge: measures unknown resistance when galvanometer reads zero.

10|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Ele.overview: Electrical power is the rate at which energy is converted in a circuit. The relationships P = VI = I²R = V²/R connect power, voltage, current, and resistance. The unit of electrical energy — the kilowatt-hour — is what appears on electricity bills. This chapter develops the skills to calculate energy consumption and cost, and to understand why power is transmitted at high voltages. These are critical practical concepts for energy management and engineering design.

key_concepts.definition: P = VI = I²R = V²/R. The rate of energy conversion from electrical to other forms (heat, light, mechanical). Unit: watt (W) = joule/second. An appliance rated at 2 kW converts 2000 J of electrical energy every second.
E = Pt = VIt = I²Rt. Total energy converted in time t. Unit: joule (J) for scientific use; kilowatt-hour (kWh) for commercial billing. 1 kWh = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3.6 MJ.
The commercial unit of electrical energy. 1 kWh = energy consumed by a 1 kW device in 1 hour. Units consumed = power (kW) × time (h). Cost = units consumed × price per unit (tariff).

H = I²Rt. Heat produced in a resistance R carrying current I for time t. For a given power, high-resistance components dissipate more heat. Thick low-resistance cables: less heat loss. This is why transmission lines use low current (high voltage).

A 60W bulb consumes 60 J per second. A 1000W kettle consumes 1000 J per second. The power rating is stamped at the rated voltage (e.g., 220V). At lower voltages: actual power = V²/R = less than rated power (since R is fixed).

Cost = power (kW) × time (h) × tariff (cost per kWh). Example: 3 kW heater used for 4 hours at ₹6/unit: cost = 3 × 4 × 6 = ₹72. Reducing energy use: using efficient appliances, turning off standby, insulating buildings.

P_loss = I²R for transmission lines. For fixed power P = IV: higher V → lower I → less I²R loss. A transformer steps up voltage at the power station (low I, low loss) and steps it down at the substation for safe household use. This makes electricity generation economically viable.

Fuse rating = power/voltage = P/V. A 3 kW appliance at 230 V: I = P/V = 3000/230 ≈ 13 A. Choose a 13 A fuse. The fuse must handle normal operating current but melt if current is significantly exceeded.

laws_and_formulas.name: Electrical Power
laws_and_formulas.description: Power dissipated/consumed in a component; three equivalent forms using Ohm's Law.

laws_and_formulas.name: Electrical Energy
laws_and_formulas.description: Energy = power × time; commercial unit is the kilowatt-hour.

laws_and_formulas.name: Transmission Loss
laws_and_formulas.description: Power lost as heat in transmission line resistance; minimised by using high voltage (low I).

key_points: P = VI = I²R = V²/R.
E = Pt; joule for science; kWh for billing. 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ.
Cost = P(kW) × t(h) × tariff(₹/unit).
Transmission: P_loss = I²R; use high V → low I → minimal line loss.
Transformer: steps voltage up at generation (low I, low loss); down at consumer end.
Fuse current rating: I = P/V for the appliance.
Joule heating: H = I²Rt — heating is proportional to I², so high current is costly.
Thick cables: low resistance → lower heat loss.
Appliance rating (W) at a given voltage — actual power changes if supply voltage changes.
Energy-saving: LED (5–10 W) vs. incandescent (60 W) for same luminous output.

10|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Hou.overview: Modern household electrical systems are carefully designed for safety and efficiency using parallel wiring, dedicated circuits, fuses, circuit breakers, RCDs, and earthing. At Class 10 level, this chapter extends the understanding of household wiring to include how parallel circuits maintain constant voltage, how safety devices work, power consumption calculations, and the economics of electricity bills. This knowledge is directly applicable in everyday life.

key_concepts.definition: All appliances in a house are connected in parallel across the mains supply. This ensures: (1) each appliance receives the full supply voltage (220–240 V); (2) failure of one appliance doesn't affect others; (3) appliances can be switched independently; (4) total current drawn = sum of individual appliance currents.

The standard wiring for power sockets in UK/India. Both ends of the live (and neutral) wire connect to the consumer unit (fuse box), forming a ring. Each socket connects to two points on the ring in parallel. Effectively reduces the resistance of the circuit — allows higher current for kettles, heaters, etc.

A radial circuit branches from the consumer unit. Each light is connected in parallel. Lights are controlled by switches in series (interrupting the live wire). Parallel connection: each bulb gets full voltage, one failed bulb doesn't affect others.

The central distribution board: contains the main switch, MCBs or fuses for each circuit (ring main, lighting, cooker, shower), and an RCD. Each circuit is rated for a specific maximum current. Separate circuits for high-power appliances (cooker, shower) prevent overloading.

Detects any imbalance between current in the live and neutral wires (which should be equal if no earth fault exists). Trips in < 30 ms when imbalance > 30 mA. Provides protection against electric shock (even from a faulty appliance where a person becomes part of the circuit to earth).

Some appliances (hairdryers, hand drills) have two layers of insulation between live components and outer casing. No metal casing → no earth connection needed. Marked with a double-square symbol (⧠). Safer in damp environments. No earth wire in the plug.

Never: use water near electrical appliances; overload sockets; use damaged plugs or cables; touch live wires; use mains appliances in the bathroom (except specifically rated). Always: use correctly rated fuses; turn off at the switch before changing a fuse; earth metal-cased appliances.

Bill = energy used (kWh) × tariff (cost per unit). Units used = Σ(power_i × time_i) for each appliance. Example: 5 × 60W bulbs for 6 hours + 1 × 1000W heater for 3 hours = 5×0.06×6 + 1×3 = 1.8 + 3 = 4.8 kWh. At ₹5/unit: bill = ₹24.

laws_and_formulas.name: Household Energy Cost
laws_and_formulas.description: Calculate electricity bill from power rating, time of use, and unit cost.

key_points: All household appliances are connected in parallel — each gets full voltage.
Parallel wiring: independent switching; one failure doesn't affect others.
Ring main: both ends connect to consumer unit — effectively parallel, lower resistance.
Lighting: parallel bulbs on a radial circuit; switch is in series with each bulb.
RCD: trips in < 30 ms on 30 mA earth leakage — most sensitive safety device.
MCB replaces fuses: electromagnetically tripped, can be reset.
Double-insulated appliances: no earth wire needed (double-square symbol).
Fuse in live wire: melts on overcurrent, breaks circuit.
Earth wire: connects metal casing to ground — diverts fault current safely.
Bill = kWh used × tariff; always calculate in kW (not W) × hours.

10|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Mag.overview: Moving charges create magnetic fields, and magnetic fields exert forces on moving charges — these two facts are the twin pillars of electromagnetism. This chapter covers the magnetic fields of solenoids and bar magnets, the force on a current-carrying conductor (the motor effect), electromagnetic induction (Faraday's Law), and the AC generator and DC motor. Electromagnetism is the basis of all electrical machines — motors, generators, transformers, and MRI scanners.

key_concepts.definition: A solenoid is a helical coil. Inside: uniform field parallel to axis (like a bar magnet). Strength proportional to: number of turns per unit length (n), current (I), and permeability of core. B = μ₀nI (inside an air-core solenoid). With a soft iron core: B increases dramatically.

A changing magnetic flux through a coil induces an EMF. EMF = -N(ΔΦ/Δt). Flux changes by: moving a magnet into/out of a coil, changing current in a nearby coil, rotating a coil in a field. Lenz's Law: induced current direction opposes the change causing it (conservation of energy).

Left-Hand Rule (motor): index = B field; middle = current I; thumb = force/motion. Used for DC motors. Right-Hand Rule (generator): index = B field; thumb = motion; middle = induced current. Used for generators.

Converts electrical energy → mechanical energy. A current-carrying coil in a magnetic field experiences a torque (F = BIL × moment arm). The commutator reverses current every half turn, maintaining continuous rotation in the same direction. Uses: fans, drills, electric vehicles.

Converts mechanical energy → electrical energy. A coil rotates in a magnetic field; flux changes sinusoidally → EMF = NBAω sin(ωt). Slip rings connect the rotating coil to the external circuit. The output is sinusoidal (AC). Uses: power stations, bicycle dynamos.

Changes AC voltage using electromagnetic induction. Two coils wound on a soft iron core. V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p = I_p/I_s (ideal transformer). Step-up: N_s > N_p → V increases. Step-down: N_s < N_p → V decreases. Works only with AC (changing flux). Power transmission uses step-up, then step-down at substations.

The induced current opposes the change in flux that caused it. If a north pole approaches a coil, the induced current creates a north pole at the near end of the coil (repulsion). This is a manifestation of conservation of energy — you must do work against the opposing force to maintain the flux change.

When the current in a coil changes, it induces an EMF in the same coil that opposes the change. This self-induced EMF is proportional to the rate of change of current: EMF = -L(dI/dt), where L is the inductance (unit: henry, H). Inductors resist changes in current — used in filters, transformers.

laws_and_formulas.name: Faraday's Law
laws_and_formulas.description: Induced EMF is proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkage (N = number of turns).

laws_and_formulas.name: Transformer Equation
laws_and_formulas.formula: V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p; V_p I_p = V_s I_s (ideal)
laws_and_formulas.description: Voltage ratio equals turns ratio; power is conserved (ideal transformer).

laws_and_formulas.name: Motor Force
laws_and_formulas.formula: F = BIL (force on a conductor of length L carrying current I in field B)
laws_and_formulas.description: Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field — the motor effect.

key_points: Solenoid field strength ∝ nI (turns per length × current); soft iron core amplifies greatly.
Faraday's Law: EMF = -N ΔΦ/Δt — changing flux induces EMF.
Lenz's Law: induced current opposes the change causing it (energy conservation).
Motor effect: F = BIL; Fleming's Left-Hand Rule for direction.
DC motor: electrical → mechanical; commutator reverses current each half turn.
AC generator: mechanical → electrical; slip rings maintain connection.
Transformer: V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p; step-up increases voltage; step-down decreases.
Transformer works only with AC (requires changing flux).
High voltage transmission: lower current → less I²R loss in cables.
Right-Hand Rule for generator; Left-Hand Rule for motor.

10|physics|Heat_Latent_Heat.overview: Latent heat is the energy absorbed or released during a phase change at constant temperature. Class 10 extends Class 9's introduction to include applications in refrigeration, steam heating, and mixed-phase calorimetry problems. The specific latent heat of fusion (melting/freezing) and vaporisation (boiling/condensing) are large quantities that dominate energy calculations wherever phase changes occur. These concepts explain why steam is such an effective heating agent and how refrigerators and air conditioners work.

key_concepts.definition: Energy to convert 1 kg of solid to liquid (or vice versa) at the melting point: Q = mLf. For ice: Lf = 336 kJ/kg = 3.36×10⁵ J/kg. No temperature change occurs during fusion. All energy goes into breaking intermolecular bonds rather than increasing kinetic energy.

Energy to convert 1 kg of liquid to vapour (or vice versa) at the boiling point: Q = mLv. For water: Lv = 2260 kJ/kg = 2.26×10⁶ J/kg. Much larger than Lf — water molecules must be completely separated from the liquid's surface tension and pressure forces.

Heat for phase change = mass × specific latent heat. For melting: Q = mLf. For vaporisation: Q = mLv. Substitute the phase change heat in the energy balance for calorimetry problems: Q_latent + Q_sensible = 0 (energy conservation).

A refrigerant (e.g., Freon, R-134a) evaporates inside the refrigerator's coils at low pressure — absorbing Lv from the food. The vapour is pumped to the condenser coils at the back/outside — where it condenses, releasing Lv to the surroundings. The refrigerant is recycled continuously.

Steam at 100°C condenses on cold surfaces, releasing Lv = 2260 kJ/kg — much more energy than cooling water by the same ΔT. This makes steam an extremely effective heating agent in industrial processes, central heating systems, and autoclaves (sterilisation).

Sweating: water on skin evaporates, absorbing Lv from the body → cools the body. Petrol evaporates from your hand → cooling sensation. Desert coolers (evaporative coolers): water evaporates from wet pads → cooled air blown into room. Effective in hot, dry climates.

During melting: energy breaks the regular lattice structure of solid; molecules gain potential energy (not kinetic). During vaporisation: energy completely separates molecules from the liquid's intermolecular attractions; huge increase in potential energy. Lv >> Lf because much more bond energy must be overcome.

Boiling point increases with pressure. A pressure cooker boils water at ~120°C (higher pressure), cooking food faster. At high altitudes (lower atmospheric pressure), water boils below 100°C — food takes longer to cook. This is why cooking instructions vary with altitude.

laws_and_formulas.name: Latent Heat Equation
laws_and_formulas.formula: Q = mL (L = specific latent heat, J/kg)
laws_and_formulas.description: Heat for phase change; Lf (fusion) = 336 kJ/kg for water; Lv (vaporisation) = 2260 kJ/kg for water.

key_points: Q = mL: no temperature change during phase change.
Lf (ice) = 336 kJ/kg; Lv (steam) = 2260 kJ/kg — Lv >> Lf.
Phase change: constant temperature, energy changes bonding (potential energy).
Refrigerator: refrigerant evaporates inside (absorbs Lv) → condenses outside (releases Lv).
Steam at 100°C more effective than hot water: extra Lv = 2260 kJ/kg released on condensation.
Sweating: Lv absorbed from body → cooling (2260 kJ per kg evaporated).
Pressure cooker: higher pressure → higher boiling point → faster cooking.
High altitude: lower pressure → lower boiling point (< 100°C).
In mixed-phase problems: include Q=mLf or Q=mLv along with Q=mcΔθ.
Heating curve flat portions (at 0°C and 100°C) represent latent heat absorption.

10|physics|Heat_Specific_Heat_Capacity_a.overview: Different materials require different amounts of heat to change temperature by the same amount — this is specific heat capacity. Calorimetry is the science of measuring these heat transfers precisely. At Class 10 level, problems involve mixtures with both temperature changes and phase changes, and require careful application of conservation of energy. The method of mixtures, cooling correction, and the heat capacity of the calorimeter itself are all examined.

key_concepts.definition: c = Q/(mΔθ). Heat required to raise 1 kg by 1°C. Unit: J/(kg·°C) or J/(kg·K). Water: 4200 J/kg°C (highest among common substances). Copper: 390 J/kg°C. Aluminium: 900 J/kg°C. Iron: 450 J/kg°C. High c: substance resists temperature change (thermal reservoir).

Heat energy Q = mass m × specific heat capacity c × temperature change Δθ. Used for both heating and cooling. Q is positive when heat is absorbed (temperature rises); negative when released (temperature falls).

Principle: heat lost by hot body = heat gained by cold body (assuming an isolated system). m₁c₁(T₁-T_f) = m₂c₂(T_f-T₂). Solve for unknown (T_f, c₂, etc.). Always verify T₂ < T_f < T₁ as a sanity check.

The calorimeter absorbs some heat: Q_cal = m_cal × c_cal × ΔT. Water equivalent W = m_cal × c_cal / c_water — the mass of water with the same heat capacity. Include in: heat gained by (calorimeter + water + contents). This correction is significant with light liquids.

Rate of heat loss from a body is proportional to the excess of its temperature over the surroundings: dQ/dt = k(T - T₀). This is an approximation for moderate temperature differences. Used to apply a cooling correction in calorimetry experiments.

During a calorimetry experiment, the hot body cools to the surroundings while mixing. The actual final temperature without losses would be slightly higher. Correction = estimated heat loss during experiment. Practically: plot temperature-time graph and extrapolate.

Land has lower specific heat capacity than water — it heats and cools faster. During the day: land heats faster → lower pressure → sea breeze (from sea to land). At night: land cools faster → sea is warmer → land breeze (from land to sea). Explains coastal weather patterns.

If steam is added to cold water, or ice is added to hot water, both a phase change (latent heat) and a temperature change occur. Energy balance: all heat released = all heat absorbed. Identify each contribution: Q_latent (state change) + Q_sensible (temperature change) for each body.

laws_and_formulas.name: Heat Equation
laws_and_formulas.description: Heat absorbed/released by mass m with SHC c over temperature change Δθ.

laws_and_formulas.name: Method of Mixtures
laws_and_formulas.description: Heat lost by hot body = heat gained by cold body in an insulated system.

key_points: c = Q/(mΔθ); water c = 4200 J/kg°C (highest common value).
Q = mcΔθ: use consistently with positive Δθ (magnitude of temperature change).
Method of mixtures: m₁c₁(T₁-T_f) = m₂c₂(T_f-T₂); solve for T_f or unknown c.
Calorimeter water equivalent: W = m_cal × c_cal / c_water; add to the 'cold side'.
Newton's Law of Cooling: rate of heat loss ∝ temperature excess over surroundings.
Cooling correction: correct final temperature for heat lost to surroundings.
Land has lower c than water: heats and cools faster → land/sea breezes.
Mixed phase problems: include latent heat + sensible heat in energy balance.
Always verify: final temperature lies between initial temperatures of the two bodies.
High specific heat capacity → good heat store; low c → responds quickly to heating.

10|physics|Heat_Loss_and_Gain_of_Heat.overview: This chapter consolidates the quantitative treatment of heat transfer: the method of mixtures, cooling corrections, and mixed-phase problems. It also addresses practical heat loss — conduction through walls, convection in buildings, and radiation — and connects these to real-world energy efficiency in buildings, thermal insulation, and climate control. Understanding heat gain and loss is fundamental to architecture, engineering, and climate science.

key_concepts.definition: In an isolated system: total heat lost = total heat gained. Q_hot = Q_cold. This is the energy balance applied in the method of mixtures. For each body: Q = mcΔθ for temperature changes; Q = mL for phase changes. All contributions are summed and set equal.

When a phase change occurs during mixing: energy balance must include latent heat contributions. Example: 0.1 kg ice at 0°C added to 0.5 kg water at 80°C. Heat to melt ice = 0.1 × 336000 = 33600 J. Heat available from water = 0.5 × 4200 × 80 = 168000 J. All ice melts; final T found from remaining balance.

Heat flow rate Q/t = kA(T₁-T₂)/d. Where k = thermal conductivity, A = area, ΔT = temperature difference, d = thickness. Good insulators (low k): wool, foam, glass wool, air. Poor insulators (high k = good conductors): metals. Double-glazing uses the low k of trapped air.

Heat loss in buildings occurs through: conduction (walls, windows, roof, floor), convection (draughts), and radiation. Measures to reduce: cavity wall insulation (trapped air), double glazing, loft insulation, draught excluders, low-emissivity window coatings (reduce radiation), reflective roof coatings.

The rate of heat transfer per unit area per degree temperature difference: U = Q/(AΔTt). Unit: W/(m²·K). Lower U-value = better insulation. Building regulations specify maximum U-values for walls, roofs, and windows. Triple-glazed windows achieve U ≈ 0.5 W/m²K vs. single glazing U ≈ 5.8 W/m²K.

The atmosphere (CO₂, H₂O, CH₄) is transparent to incoming visible radiation but absorbs re-emitted infrared from Earth's surface. Enhanced greenhouse effect from excess CO₂ (fossil fuels): traps more heat → global warming. IPCC: current warming primarily anthropogenic.

Solar collectors (black surfaces) absorb solar radiation, heating water. Photovoltaic cells convert light directly to electrical energy. Passive solar building design: south-facing windows maximise solar gain; thermal mass stores daytime heat for night release.

When two bodies are in thermal contact, heat flows from hotter to cooler until they reach the same temperature — thermal equilibrium. The final temperature can be calculated using the method of mixtures if masses and specific heat capacities are known.

laws_and_formulas.name: Heat Conduction Rate
laws_and_formulas.description: Rate of conductive heat flow through a slab: k = thermal conductivity, A = area, d = thickness.

laws_and_formulas.name: Energy Balance
laws_and_formulas.description: Total heat released (negative terms) = total heat absorbed (positive terms).

key_points: Heat balance: heat released by hot bodies = heat absorbed by cold bodies.
Include latent heat terms (mL) alongside sensible heat terms (mcΔθ) in mixed-phase problems.
Conduction rate: Q/t = kAΔT/d — higher k, larger area, larger ΔT, thinner wall → more loss.
Good insulators: low k — air, foam, wool, glass wool.
U-value: lower = better insulation. Building regulations set maximum U-values.
Cavity wall insulation, double glazing, loft insulation — reduce building heat loss.
Greenhouse effect: CO₂ traps infrared reradiation → global warming.
Thermal equilibrium: heat flows until temperatures equalise.
Always check final temperature is between the two initial temperatures in mixtures.
Passive solar design: maximise solar gain, minimise heat loss through insulation.

10|physics|Modern_Physics_Radioactivity_.overview: Radioactivity is the spontaneous emission of radiation from unstable atomic nuclei. There are three types of nuclear radiation — alpha (α), beta (β), and gamma (γ) — each with distinct properties, penetrating power, and ionising ability. Radioactive decay follows an exponential law characterised by the half-life. These concepts underlie nuclear medicine, power generation, radiometric dating, and radiation safety — one of the most important applications of modern physics.

key_concepts.definition: The spontaneous emission of particles or electromagnetic radiation from unstable atomic nuclei. Radioactivity is a nuclear process (unaffected by chemical state, temperature, or pressure). It occurs naturally in heavy elements (uranium, thorium, radium) and artificially in produced radioisotopes.

Emission of an alpha particle: a helium nucleus (2 protons + 2 neutrons, charge +2). Z decreases by 2, A decreases by 4. Least penetrating (stopped by paper or 5 cm of air). Most ionising (highly charged, heavy). Dangerous if ingested/inhaled (internal exposure). Example: U-238 → Th-234 + α.

Emission of a fast electron (β⁻) from the nucleus when a neutron converts to a proton + electron. Z increases by 1; A unchanged. Penetrating power: stopped by a few mm of aluminium. Less ionising than alpha. Example: C-14 → N-14 + β⁻. Also β⁺ emission (positron) in artificial radioisotopes.

High-energy electromagnetic radiation emitted from a nucleus after alpha or beta decay (transition from excited to ground state). Z and A unchanged. Most penetrating (stopped only by thick lead or concrete). Least ionising. Travels at the speed of light. Same as X-rays but from the nucleus.

Ionising power: α >> β >> γ. Penetrating power: γ >> β >> α. Deflection in electric/magnetic fields: α and β deflected (charged); γ not deflected (uncharged). α: +2 deflection; β: -1 deflection (opposite direction, lighter).

The time for half of the radioactive nuclei in a sample to decay. It is constant and characteristic of each isotope: C-14: 5730 years; U-238: 4.5 billion years; I-131: 8 days. After n half-lives: N = N₀/2ⁿ; A = A₀/2ⁿ (activity halves each half-life).
N(t) = N₀ e^(-λt), where λ = decay constant (λ = 0.693/T₁/₂). Activity A = λN (decays per second). Unit of activity: becquerel (Bq) = 1 decay/s; curie (Ci) = 3.7×10¹⁰ Bq.

Dose unit: sievert (Sv) = absorbed dose × quality factor (α: Q=20, β: Q=1, γ: Q=1). Limiting exposure: distance (I ∝ 1/r²), shielding (appropriate for type of radiation), time. ALARA principle: As Low As Reasonably Achievable. Monitoring with Geiger-Müller counter.

laws_and_formulas.name: Radioactive Decay
laws_and_formulas.description: Number of remaining nuclei after time t; T½ = half-life; λ = 0.693/T½.

laws_and_formulas.name: Activity
laws_and_formulas.description: Activity (rate of decay) = decay constant × number of radioactive nuclei.

key_points: α: helium nucleus (2p+2n, charge +2); Z-2, A-4; stopped by paper; most ionising.
β: fast electron (charge -1); Z+1, A unchanged; stopped by aluminium.
γ: high-energy EM wave; Z and A unchanged; stopped by thick lead; least ionising.
Ionising: α >> β >> γ. Penetrating: γ >> β >> α.
α and β deflected by electric/magnetic fields; γ is not (uncharged).
Half-life: time for N to halve; T½ is constant and isotope-specific.
After n half-lives: N = N₀/2ⁿ.
Activity A = λN; decreases exponentially with time.
C-14 (T½=5730 yr) used for archaeological dating; U-238 for geological age.
Safety: distance, shielding (appropriate type), time minimised (ALARA).

10|physics|Modern_Physics_Nuclear_Energy.overview: Nuclear reactions release energy millions of times greater than chemical reactions because they involve the strong nuclear force and mass-energy equivalence (E = mc²). Fission splits heavy nuclei (uranium, plutonium) releasing enormous energy; fusion combines light nuclei (hydrogen) releasing even more energy per kg of fuel. Fission powers current nuclear reactors and weapons; fusion promises a virtually limitless clean energy source. Understanding these processes is essential for energy policy and international security.

key_concepts.definition: E = mc². A small mass m is equivalent to an enormous energy E (c = 3×10⁸ m/s). In nuclear reactions, the products have slightly less mass than the reactants (mass defect). This mass defect Δm → energy released: ΔE = Δmc². Even tiny Δm gives huge ΔE.

The energy needed to completely separate a nucleus into its constituent protons and neutrons. Equivalently, the energy released when the nucleus is assembled from free nucleons. Higher binding energy per nucleon = more stable nucleus. Iron (Fe-56) has the highest binding energy per nucleon.

Splitting a heavy nucleus (U-235 or Pu-239) into two medium-mass nuclei (fission fragments) + 2–3 neutrons + energy (≈200 MeV per fission). Triggered by neutron absorption. Chain reaction: each fission releases neutrons that trigger more fissions. U-235 + n → Ba-141 + Kr-92 + 3n + energy.

Each fission releases ~2.5 neutrons that can trigger further fissions. Uncontrolled: exponential growth → nuclear explosion. Controlled: each fission triggers exactly 1 further fission (k=1, critical). Critical mass: minimum mass for a sustained chain reaction. Sub-critical: reaction dies out.

Controlled fission to generate heat → steam → turbine → electricity. Components: fuel rods (enriched U-235), moderator (slows neutrons — graphite or water), control rods (absorb neutrons — cadmium or boron), coolant (water, CO₂), pressure vessel, biological shield (concrete).

Combining light nuclei (typically deuterium + tritium → helium-4 + neutron + 17.6 MeV). More energy per kg than fission. Fuels abundant (hydrogen isotopes from seawater). No long-lived radioactive waste. Requires enormous temperature (>10⁷ K) to overcome electrostatic repulsion — achieved in stars and hydrogen bombs; not yet sustained in reactors (ITER project).

Fission: heavy → medium nuclei; commercial power available; produces radioactive waste; fuel is limited uranium. Fusion: light → heavier nuclei; not yet commercially viable; virtually no radioactive waste; fuel from seawater (limitless). Both release energy via mass defect (E=mc²).

Uncontrolled fission: atomic bomb (implosion or gun-type — two sub-critical masses brought together rapidly). Uncontrolled fusion: hydrogen bomb (uses fission to create the temperature needed for fusion). International Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) limits spread of nuclear weapons.

laws_and_formulas.name: Mass-Energy Equivalence
laws_and_formulas.description: Energy released equals mass defect × speed of light squared. Explains nuclear energy.

laws_and_formulas.name: Energy per Fission
laws_and_formulas.formula: U-235 fission releases ≈ 200 MeV = 3.2×10⁻¹¹ J per fission
laws_and_formulas.description: Typical energy released per fission of uranium-235 nucleus.

key_points: E = mc²: tiny mass defect → enormous energy release in nuclear reactions.
Binding energy: energy to disassemble nucleus; Fe-56 = most stable.
Fission: heavy nucleus splits → two fragments + neutrons + ~200 MeV.
Chain reaction: each fission triggers more; controlled (reactor) vs. uncontrolled (bomb).
Nuclear reactor: fuel rods, moderator, control rods, coolant, shield.
Control rods (cadmium/boron): absorb neutrons; adjust rate of chain reaction.
Fusion: light nuclei combine; D + T → He + n + 17.6 MeV.
Fusion needs >10⁷ K: achieved in stars, H-bombs, not yet in commercial reactors.
Fission: radioactive waste problem. Fusion: minimal waste, abundant fuel.
1 kg U-235: releases ~8×10¹³ J — equivalent to burning ~2700 tonnes of coal.

10|physics|Modern_Physics_Photocells_and.overview: Modern physics spans the photoelectric effect, semiconductor devices, photocells, and the practical applications of nuclear physics in medicine and industry. The photoelectric effect — explained by Einstein using photons — was a cornerstone of quantum physics. Photovoltaic cells, photodiodes, and LDRs (light-dependent resistors) translate light into electrical signals. Nuclear medicine uses radioisotopes for diagnosis (PET, gamma cameras) and treatment (radiotherapy). These technologies are transforming energy, medicine, and communications.

key_concepts.definition: Emission of electrons from a metal surface when light of sufficient frequency strikes it. Key features: (1) below a threshold frequency, no emission regardless of intensity; (2) above threshold, emission is instantaneous; (3) the kinetic energy of emitted electrons depends on frequency, not intensity. Explained by Einstein using photons (E = hf).

A quantum (packet) of electromagnetic radiation. Energy E = hf = hc/λ (h = Planck's constant = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J·s). Photons have zero rest mass but carry energy and momentum. Einstein's explanation of the photoelectric effect using photons won him the 1921 Nobel Prize.

A semiconductor device that converts light (photons) directly to electrical energy. When photons strike the semiconductor (silicon), electron-hole pairs are created — the built-in electric field at the p-n junction separates them, driving a current. Used in solar panels, calculators, satellites.

Photodiode: reverse-biased p-n junction; photons generate electron-hole pairs → current proportional to light intensity. Used in optical fibre receivers, smoke detectors, CD players. LDR (Light-Dependent Resistor): resistance decreases as light intensity increases (photoconductor). Used in automatic street lights, burglar alarms, camera exposure control.

Radioactive tracers: radioisotopes injected into the body accumulate in target organs. Gamma emission detected externally (gamma camera) → image. Tc-99m (6 hour half-life): most widely used; bone scans, thyroid imaging. PET scan: F-18 FDG (glucose analog) → positron emission → annihilation photons detected.

High-energy gamma rays (from Co-60 or linear accelerators) directed at tumours to kill cancer cells. The radiation destroys DNA. Aimed from multiple angles to converge at the tumour, minimising damage to surrounding tissue. Brachytherapy: radioactive seeds placed directly in the tumour (prostate cancer treatment).

Gamma rays from Co-60 irradiate food, killing bacteria, insects, and moulds without the food becoming radioactive. Extends shelf life without preservatives. Approved by WHO for spices, dried fruits, grains. Controversial among consumers despite scientific evidence of safety.

Ionisation type: contains Am-241 (alpha emitter). Alpha particles ionise air → current flows. Smoke particles absorb alpha → current drops → alarm triggers. Am-241 T½ = 432 years — provides decades of reliable operation from a tiny quantity. Photoelectric type uses a photodiode.

laws_and_formulas.name: Photon Energy
laws_and_formulas.description: Energy of a photon with frequency f or wavelength λ.

laws_and_formulas.name: Photoelectric Equation
laws_and_formulas.description: Maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons; φ = minimum energy to release electron.

key_points: Photoelectric effect: light ejects electrons from metal surface when f > threshold.
Threshold frequency f₀ = φ/h; below this: no emission regardless of intensity.
Einstein: photons have E = hf; this explains the frequency dependence.
Solar cell: photons → electron-hole pairs → electrical current (p-n junction).
LDR: resistance decreases with increasing light intensity.
Photodiode: reverse-biased; current ∝ light intensity; used in optical receivers.
Tc-99m (T½ = 6 h): ideal diagnostic radioisotope — short half-life, gamma emitter.
PET scan: F-18 FDG → positron emission → pair of 511 keV gamma photons detected.
Radiotherapy: gamma rays from multiple angles converge on tumour → DNA damage.
Smoke detector: Am-241 alpha source; smoke absorbs ions → alarm.

10|chemistry|1_Periodic_Properties_and_V.overview: The Modern Periodic Law, proposed by Henry Moseley in 1913, states that the physical and chemical properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic numbers. The periodic table arranges all known elements in order of increasing atomic number into horizontal rows called periods and vertical columns called groups. Elements in the same group share similar valence electron configurations and therefore exhibit similar chemical properties. Key periodic trends — atomic radius, ionisation energy, electron affinity, electronegativity, and metallic character — show predictable patterns across periods and down groups, making the periodic table an indispensable tool for predicting and explaining chemical behaviour. The table is divided into four blocks (s, p, d, and f) based on the subshell into which the differentiating electron enters, and noble gases at the far right achieve particularly stable filled-shell configurations.

key_concepts.definition: Moseley's Modern Periodic Law states that the properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic numbers (number of protons), not atomic masses as Mendeleev proposed. This resolved anomalies such as the positions of argon and potassium, and cobalt and nickel, in Mendeleev's table. Atomic number is an absolute, invariable property of an element, making it the correct basis for classification.

A period is a horizontal row in the periodic table; elements in a period have the same number of electron shells. A group (or family) is a vertical column; elements in the same group have the same number of valence electrons and similar chemical properties. There are 7 periods and 18 groups in the modern periodic table. Period number equals the number of occupied electron shells of an element.

Atomic radius is a measure of the size of an atom, usually taken as half the distance between two bonded nuclei of the same element. Across a period (left to right), atomic radius decreases because increasing nuclear charge pulls electrons closer to the nucleus while the number of shells remains constant. Down a group, atomic radius increases because each successive element has an additional electron shell, placing valence electrons farther from the nucleus.

Ionisation energy (IE) is the minimum energy required to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom in its ground state. Across a period, IE increases because the nuclear charge increases while shielding remains roughly constant, making it harder to remove an electron. Down a group, IE decreases because the valence electrons are farther from the nucleus and more effectively shielded by inner shells, reducing the nuclear attraction.

Electron affinity is the energy released when a neutral gaseous atom accepts an electron to form a negatively charged ion. Elements with high electron affinity (such as halogens like chlorine) readily accept electrons and form stable anions. Electron affinity generally increases across a period (more nuclear charge attracts the extra electron) and decreases down a group (extra electron enters a shell farther from the nucleus with more shielding).

Electronegativity is the ability of a bonded atom to attract the shared pair of electrons towards itself. It is measured on the Pauling scale, where fluorine has the highest value (4.0). Electronegativity increases across a period (greater nuclear charge) and decreases down a group (greater atomic radius and shielding). The difference in electronegativity between two bonded atoms determines the polarity of the bond.

Metallic character refers to the tendency of an element to lose electrons and form positive ions (cations). Metals have low ionisation energies and low electronegativities. Metallic character decreases across a period as ionisation energy increases (it becomes harder to lose electrons). Down a group, metallic character increases as ionisation energy decreases (valence electrons are farther from the nucleus and easier to remove). The most metallic element is francium (bottom-left of the table).

Elements are classified into four blocks based on the subshell receiving the last (differentiating) electron. The s-block (Groups 1–2 plus helium) includes alkali and alkaline earth metals. The p-block (Groups 13–18) includes non-metals, metalloids, and noble gases. The d-block (Groups 3–12) comprises transition metals. The f-block (lanthanides and actinides) involves filling the 4f and 5f orbitals respectively.

Noble gases (Group 18: He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn) possess completely filled outermost electron shells — helium has 2 electrons (1s²) and the rest have 8 valence electrons (ns²np⁶). This stable, fully filled configuration (called the octet) gives noble gases extremely low reactivity. Other elements tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve a similar noble gas configuration, which is the driving force behind chemical bonding.

The shielding (or screening) effect refers to the reduction in effective nuclear charge experienced by outer electrons due to the repulsion from inner shell electrons. Inner electrons partially 'screen' the outer electrons from the full attractive force of the nucleus. Greater shielding (more inner shells) means outer electrons are held less tightly, leading to larger atomic radii, lower ionisation energies, and reduced electronegativity as you go down a group.

laws_and_formulas.name: Modern Periodic Law
laws_and_formulas.description: The physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers. As atomic number increases, properties recur at regular intervals (periods), reflecting the repetition of valence electron configurations.

laws_and_formulas.name: Effective Nuclear Charge (Slater's Rule concept)
