laws_and_formulas.description: The effective nuclear charge (Z_eff) experienced by a valence electron equals the actual nuclear charge (Z) minus the shielding constant (σ) from inner electrons. Higher Z_eff means a stronger pull on valence electrons, resulting in smaller atomic radii and higher ionisation energies.

key_points: Moseley's Modern Periodic Law (1913) is based on atomic number, correcting Mendeleev's atomic mass-based arrangement.
Atomic radius decreases across a period (increasing nuclear charge, same number of shells) and increases down a group (additional electron shells).
Ionisation energy increases across a period and decreases down a group; noble gases have the highest IE in each period.
Electronegativity increases across a period and decreases down a group; fluorine (F) is the most electronegative element.
Metallic character decreases across a period and increases down a group; metals are on the left, non-metals on the right of the periodic table.
Elements in the same group have the same number of valence electrons and similar chemical properties (e.g., all Group 1 elements react with water to form hydroxides and hydrogen gas).
The s-block contains Groups 1 and 2; p-block contains Groups 13–18; d-block contains Groups 3–12 (transition metals); f-block contains lanthanides and actinides.
Noble gases (Group 18) have completely filled outermost shells (2 or 8 electrons) — their stability explains why other elements form bonds to achieve a similar configuration.
Electron affinity is the energy released on adding an electron to a neutral atom; halogens (Group 17) have the highest electron affinities in their respective periods.
Shielding by inner electrons reduces the effective nuclear charge felt by outer electrons, explaining why properties change more gradually going down a group than across a period.

10|chemistry|2_Chemical_Bonding.overview: Chemical bonding describes the forces of attraction that hold atoms together in compounds, and the type of bond formed determines the physical and chemical properties of the resulting substance. An ionic (electrovalent) bond forms by the complete transfer of one or more electrons from a metal to a non-metal, producing oppositely charged ions held together by electrostatic attraction. A covalent bond forms when two non-metal atoms share one or more pairs of electrons to achieve a stable noble-gas configuration; covalent bonds may be single, double, or triple depending on the number of shared pairs. A coordinate (dative) bond is a special type of covalent bond in which both electrons of the shared pair are donated by the same atom. Metallic bonding involves a lattice of positive ions (cations) surrounded by a sea of delocalised electrons, explaining conductivity, malleability, and ductility of metals. Understanding the distinction between ionic and covalent bonding is essential for predicting solubility, melting points, conductivity, and reactivity.

key_concepts.definition: An ionic bond forms when a metal atom transfers one or more electrons to a non-metal atom, producing a positively charged cation and a negatively charged anion; the strong electrostatic attraction between these ions constitutes the ionic bond. For example, sodium (Na, [Ne]3s¹) loses one electron to chlorine (Cl, [Ne]3s²3p⁵) to form Na⁺ and Cl⁻, which together make up sodium chloride (NaCl). The number of electrons lost or gained is called the electrovalency of the element.

Electrovalency is the number of electrons an atom loses or gains in forming an ionic compound. Elements in Group 1 (e.g., Na, K) have electrovalency 1 (lose 1e⁻); Group 2 elements (e.g., Mg, Ca) have electrovalency 2 (lose 2e⁻); Group 17 halogens have electrovalency 1 (gain 1e⁻); Group 16 elements (e.g., O, S) have electrovalency 2 (gain 2e⁻). Electrovalency equals the number of electrons transferred per atom.

Ionic compounds form rigid crystalline lattice structures held together by strong electrostatic forces, giving them high melting and boiling points (e.g., NaCl melts at 801°C). They are generally soluble in polar solvents like water and insoluble in non-polar solvents. In the solid state, ions are fixed in position and cannot conduct electricity, but in the molten state or when dissolved in water, the free-moving ions allow conduction of electricity. Ionic compounds are typically brittle because displacing one layer of ions brings like charges alongside each other, causing repulsion.

A covalent bond forms when two non-metal atoms share one or more pairs of electrons so that each atom achieves a stable (usually octet) electron configuration. A single bond involves one shared pair (bond order 1), a double bond involves two shared pairs (bond order 2), and a triple bond involves three shared pairs (bond order 3). Examples: H₂ (single bond H–H), O₂ (double bond O=O), N₂ (triple bond N≡N).

A coordinate or dative bond is a special type of covalent bond in which both electrons of the shared pair are provided by one atom (the donor) and accepted by the other atom (the acceptor, which has an empty orbital). The bond is identical to a normal covalent bond once formed, but its origin differs. Examples include the ammonium ion (NH₄⁺), where the nitrogen atom donates its lone pair to H⁺, and the ozone molecule (O₃).

Covalent compounds generally have low melting and boiling points because only weak intermolecular van der Waals forces (not the covalent bonds themselves) must be overcome during melting or boiling. Most covalent compounds are poor conductors of electricity because they contain no free ions or electrons. They are typically soluble in non-polar solvents (like-dissolves-like). Notable exceptions: network covalent solids like diamond and silicon dioxide have very high melting points due to an extended lattice of strong covalent bonds.

Metallic bonding consists of a regular lattice of metal cations immersed in a 'sea' of delocalised (free-moving) electrons derived from the valence electrons of all metal atoms. The electrostatic attraction between the positive cations and the negative electron sea holds the metal together. This model explains why metals are good conductors of heat and electricity (free electrons carry charge and kinetic energy), malleable and ductile (layers of cations can slide without breaking the bond), and have characteristic metallic lustre.

A covalent bond is non-polar when both atoms have the same electronegativity and share electrons equally (e.g., Cl₂, H₂). A polar covalent bond forms when two atoms of different electronegativity share electrons unequally; the more electronegative atom acquires a partial negative charge (δ−) and the less electronegative atom acquires a partial positive charge (δ+). For example, in HCl, Cl is more electronegative and pulls the shared pair toward itself: H(δ+)–Cl(δ−).

The octet rule states that atoms tend to bond in ways that give each atom eight electrons in its outermost shell (resembling a noble gas configuration), thereby achieving maximum stability. Hydrogen is an exception, seeking a duplet (2 electrons, like helium). The rule successfully predicts the formulas of many ionic and covalent compounds, though exceptions exist — for example, BF₃ (boron has 6 electrons, an incomplete octet) and PCl₅ (phosphorus has 10 electrons, an expanded octet).

Electrovalency involves complete electron transfer between atoms, forming ions (found in ionic compounds like NaCl, MgO). Covalency involves electron sharing between atoms, forming molecules (found in covalent compounds like H₂O, CO₂). Ionic compounds have high melting points, conduct electricity when dissolved or molten, and dissolve in water. Covalent compounds generally have low melting points, do not conduct electricity, and often dissolve in organic solvents. The bonding type is primarily determined by the electronegativity difference between the bonding atoms.

laws_and_formulas.name: Electrovalency Rule
laws_and_formulas.formula: Electrovalency = electrons lost or gained per atom
laws_and_formulas.description: An element's electrovalency equals the number of electrons it must transfer to achieve a noble gas configuration. Metals lose electrons (positive electrovalency) and non-metals gain electrons (negative electrovalency). The formula of an ionic compound is determined by the principle of electrical neutrality: total positive charge = total negative charge (e.g., Mg²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → MgCl₂).

laws_and_formulas.name: Bond Order
laws_and_formulas.formula: Bond Order = (number of shared electron pairs between two atoms)
laws_and_formulas.description: A single bond has bond order 1 (e.g., C–C in ethane), a double bond has bond order 2 (e.g., C=C in ethene), and a triple bond has bond order 3 (e.g., C≡C in ethyne or N≡N in nitrogen gas). Higher bond order means a shorter, stronger bond and lower bond energy required for breaking.

key_points: Ionic bonds form by electron transfer from metal to non-metal; covalent bonds form by electron sharing between non-metals.
Ionic compounds have high melting points, are brittle, and conduct electricity only when dissolved in water or in the molten state.
Covalent compounds generally have low melting points and are poor conductors of electricity (most are non-electrolytes).
A coordinate (dative) bond is a covalent bond where both electrons of the shared pair come from the same atom (the donor); seen in NH₄⁺ and H₃O⁺.
Electrovalency is the number of electrons lost or gained; covalency is the number of electron pairs shared by an atom in forming bonds.
In metallic bonding, delocalised electrons move freely through the metal lattice, explaining electrical/thermal conductivity, malleability, and ductility.
Polar covalent bonds arise from unequal electronegativity; the more electronegative atom carries a partial negative charge (δ−).
The octet rule (8 electrons in outer shell) drives bond formation; hydrogen follows the duplet rule (2 electrons).
Double bonds (O=O, C=C) and triple bonds (N≡N, C≡C) involve 2 and 3 shared pairs respectively and are progressively shorter and stronger.
The type of chemical bond formed determines key physical properties: ionic compounds dissolve in water and conduct; covalent compounds often dissolve in organic solvents and do not conduct.

10|chemistry|3_Study_of_Acids_Bases_and_.overview: Acids, bases, and salts constitute the backbone of inorganic chemistry, and understanding their definitions, properties, and reactions is fundamental to chemistry at the secondary level. Acids are substances that produce hydrogen ions (H⁺) in solution, have a sour taste, turn blue litmus red, and react with metals, bases, and carbonates. Bases are substances that neutralise acids, have a bitter taste, feel slippery, and turn red litmus blue; a base that is soluble in water is called an alkali. Three major theoretical frameworks define acids and bases — Arrhenius (ionisation in water), Brønsted-Lowry (proton donor/acceptor), and Lewis (electron pair acceptor/donor). The pH scale (0–14) quantifies acidity and alkalinity, with neutralisation reactions forming salts and water. Indicators such as litmus, phenolphthalein, and methyl orange signal pH changes through colour changes, and salts are classified as acidic, basic, or neutral based on the strength of the parent acid and base.

key_concepts.term: Arrhenius Definition of Acids and Bases
key_concepts.definition: Svante Arrhenius defined an acid as a substance that dissociates in water to produce hydrogen ions (H⁺), e.g., HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻, and a base as a substance that dissociates in water to produce hydroxide ions (OH⁻), e.g., NaOH → Na⁺ + OH⁻. This definition is limited to aqueous solutions and cannot explain the basicity of substances like NH₃ (which contains no OH⁻ group) or acid-base reactions in non-aqueous media.

Brønsted and Lowry (1923) defined an acid as a proton (H⁺) donor and a base as a proton acceptor. This broader definition covers reactions in non-aqueous solvents and explains why NH₃ acts as a base (it accepts a proton to form NH₄⁺). Every acid-base reaction involves conjugate acid-base pairs: HCl + H₂O → H₃O⁺ + Cl⁻; here HCl is the acid, H₂O is the base, H₃O⁺ is the conjugate acid of H₂O, and Cl⁻ is the conjugate base of HCl.

Gilbert Lewis defined an acid as an electron-pair acceptor and a base as an electron-pair donor. This is the most general definition, encompassing reactions with no proton transfer. For example, BF₃ acts as a Lewis acid (accepts an electron pair from NH₃, a Lewis base) to form F₃B←NH₃. Metal ions acting as central atoms in coordination complexes are Lewis acids, while ligands (with lone pairs) are Lewis bases.

Acids in aqueous solution have a sour taste, turn blue litmus red, and have pH less than 7. They react with active metals (e.g., Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑) to produce hydrogen gas, react with bases to form salt and water (neutralisation), react with metal carbonates to produce salt, water, and CO₂ (e.g., Na₂CO₃ + H₂SO₄ → Na₂SO₄ + H₂O + CO₂↑), and react with metal oxides to form salt and water. Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) dissociate completely; weak acids (CH₃COOH, H₂CO₃) dissociate partially.

Bases have a bitter taste, feel slippery, turn red litmus blue, and have pH greater than 7. They react with acids to form salt and water (neutralisation), react with ammonium salts on heating to produce ammonia gas (e.g., Ca(OH)₂ + 2NH₄Cl → CaCl₂ + 2H₂O + 2NH₃), and precipitate metal hydroxides from solutions of metal salts. A base that is soluble in water is an alkali (e.g., NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂). Strong bases dissociate completely; weak bases (e.g., NH₄OH) dissociate partially.

The pH scale runs from 0 to 14 and measures the concentration of hydrogen ions in a solution. pH = −log[H⁺]. A pH of 7 is neutral (pure water at 25°C); pH < 7 is acidic (the lower the pH, the stronger the acid); pH > 7 is basic/alkaline (the higher the pH, the stronger the base). Each unit change in pH represents a tenfold change in H⁺ concentration — a solution of pH 3 is 100 times more acidic than one of pH 5.

Indicators are substances that change colour at different pH values, signalling the acidity or alkalinity of a solution. Litmus turns red in acid and blue in alkali (range pH ~5–8). Phenolphthalein is colourless in acid and neutral solution and turns pink/magenta in alkali (range pH 8.2–10). Methyl orange is red in acid and yellow in alkali (range pH 3.1–4.4), making it useful for titrations of strong acid with strong or weak base. Universal indicator gives a continuous colour range across the full pH scale.

Neutralisation is the reaction between an acid and a base to form a salt and water only, with no change in pH if stoichiometric amounts are used: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. For example, HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O. At the ionic level, the net ionic equation is simply H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O, showing that the reaction is between H⁺ ions and OH⁻ ions. The process is exothermic — heat is released.

Salts are classified based on the strength of the parent acid and base. Normal (neutral) salts are formed from strong acid and strong base and have pH ≈ 7 (e.g., NaCl, KNO₃). Acidic salts are formed from strong acid and weak base and have pH < 7 (e.g., NH₄Cl, formed from HCl and NH₃). Basic (alkaline) salts are formed from weak acid and strong base and have pH > 7 (e.g., CH₃COONa, formed from acetic acid and NaOH). Double salts contain two different cations or anions (e.g., alum: KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O).

A strong acid or base is one that completely dissociates into ions in aqueous solution; strong acids include HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, and strong bases include NaOH and KOH. A weak acid or base only partially dissociates; examples of weak acids are acetic acid (CH₃COOH) and carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), and the common weak base is ammonium hydroxide (NH₃·H₂O). The degree of dissociation is expressed by the dissociation constant (Kₐ for acids, K_b for bases); a higher constant means stronger dissociation.

laws_and_formulas.name: pH Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: pH is the negative logarithm (base 10) of the hydrogen ion concentration [H⁺] in mol/L. For a neutral solution at 25°C, [H⁺] = 10⁻⁷ mol/L, giving pH = 7. For 0.1 mol/L HCl: [H⁺] = 0.1 = 10⁻¹, so pH = 1.

laws_and_formulas.name: Neutralisation (General Equation)
laws_and_formulas.description: This is the general equation for a neutralisation reaction. The H⁺ from the acid combines with the OH⁻ from the base to form water, while the remaining ions form the salt. Net ionic equation: H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l). The reaction is exothermic (ΔH is negative).

laws_and_formulas.name: Acid Reaction with Metal Carbonate
laws_and_formulas.formula: Acid + Metal Carbonate → Salt + H₂O + CO₂↑
laws_and_formulas.description: Acids react with carbonates and bicarbonates to release carbon dioxide gas. Example: 2HCl + Na₂CO₃ → 2NaCl + H₂O + CO₂↑. The effervescence (fizzing) and the ability of the gas to turn lime water milky is used as a confirmatory test for carbonates and for CO₂.

laws_and_formulas.name: Acid Reaction with Active Metals
laws_and_formulas.description: Active metals (Zn, Fe, Mg, Al) above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace H⁺ from dilute acids. Example: Zn + H₂SO₄(dil) → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑. The hydrogen gas produced relights a glowing splint (the 'pop' test). Noble metals like copper, silver, and gold do not react with dilute acids.

laws_and_formulas.name: Water Ionic Product
laws_and_formulas.description: Water autoionises slightly: H₂O ⇌ H⁺ + OH⁻. The product of [H⁺] and [OH⁻] is always 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C. In neutral water, [H⁺] = [OH⁻] = 10⁻⁷ mol/L. Knowing one concentration allows calculation of the other, and from that, the pH or pOH.

key_points: Arrhenius acids produce H⁺ in water; Brønsted-Lowry acids are proton donors; Lewis acids are electron-pair acceptors — each definition is broader than the previous.
Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) fully dissociate; weak acids (CH₃COOH, H₂CO₃) partially dissociate. Strength is NOT the same as concentration.
pH < 7 is acidic, pH = 7 is neutral, pH > 7 is alkaline; each unit represents a 10-fold change in H⁺ concentration.
Litmus: red in acid, blue in alkali. Phenolphthalein: colourless in acid, pink in alkali. Methyl orange: red in acid, yellow in alkali.
Neutralisation produces a salt and water only: Acid + Base → Salt + Water (net ionic: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O).
Acids react with active metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series to produce hydrogen gas (H₂), confirmed by the 'pop' test with a lit splint.
Acids react with metal carbonates to produce CO₂ gas, confirmed by the lime water test (Ca(OH)₂ turns milky).
Acidic salt (e.g., NH₄Cl): strong acid + weak base, pH < 7. Basic salt (e.g., Na₂CO₃): weak acid + strong base, pH > 7. Neutral salt (e.g., NaCl): strong acid + strong base, pH ≈ 7.
A base soluble in water is called an alkali (e.g., NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂). All alkalis are bases but not all bases are alkalis.
Universal indicator gives a continuous colour range from red (very acidic) through green (neutral) to violet (very alkaline), allowing estimation of pH.

10|chemistry|4_Analytical_Chemistry.overview: Analytical chemistry provides systematic tests to identify the constituents of unknown chemical substances, forming the basis of qualitative analysis. Flame tests exploit the characteristic colours produced when metal ions are heated in a flame, while reactions with sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ammonia solution (NH₃(aq)) allow identification of cations through the colour and solubility of the precipitates formed. Anion identification relies on specific tests — silver nitrate for halides, barium chloride for sulphates, dilute acid for carbonates, and the brown ring test for nitrates. Gas identification tests (for H₂, O₂, CO₂, Cl₂, NH₃, SO₂, H₂S) are a core practical skill. Together, these tests form a methodical scheme of qualitative analysis that underpins laboratory chemistry and industrial quality control.

key_concepts.definition: When a metal salt is heated in a non-luminous Bunsen flame, the metal ions emit characteristic coloured light as excited electrons fall back to lower energy levels. Lithium (Li): crimson/scarlet red. Sodium (Na): persistent bright yellow. Potassium (K): lilac (pale violet). Calcium (Ca): brick red (orange-red). Copper (Cu): blue-green. Barium (Ba): apple green. The platinum or nichrome wire is cleaned with concentrated HCl before each test to prevent contamination.

Adding NaOH solution to a solution of a metal salt causes precipitation of the metal hydroxide. NH₄⁺ ions: NaOH (warm) releases NH₃ gas (pungent smell, turns moist red litmus blue). Fe²⁺: dirty green precipitate of Fe(OH)₂. Fe³⁺: reddish-brown precipitate of Fe(OH)₃. Cu²⁺: pale blue precipitate of Cu(OH)₂. Zn²⁺: white precipitate of Zn(OH)₂, soluble in excess NaOH (amphoteric). Pb²⁺: white precipitate of Pb(OH)₂, soluble in excess NaOH. Al³⁺: white precipitate of Al(OH)₃, soluble in excess NaOH (amphoteric).

Adding dilute ammonia solution to metal salt solutions also precipitates metal hydroxides, but some dissolve in excess ammonia forming complex ions. Fe²⁺: dirty green precipitate of Fe(OH)₂, insoluble in excess NH₃. Fe³⁺: reddish-brown precipitate of Fe(OH)₃, insoluble in excess NH₃. Cu²⁺: pale blue precipitate of Cu(OH)₂, dissolves in excess NH₃ to give deep blue [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺ (tetraamminecopper(II)) solution. Zn²⁺: white precipitate of Zn(OH)₂, dissolves in excess NH₃ to give a colourless solution. Al³⁺: white precipitate of Al(OH)₃, insoluble in excess NH₃ (distinguishes Al³⁺ from Zn²⁺).

key_concepts.term: Tests for Halide Anions (Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻)
key_concepts.definition: Halides are identified by adding dilute nitric acid followed by silver nitrate solution (AgNO₃). Chloride (Cl⁻): white precipitate of AgCl, soluble in dilute ammonia. Bromide (Br⁻): pale yellow/cream precipitate of AgBr, sparingly soluble in concentrated ammonia. Iodide (I⁻): yellow precipitate of AgI, insoluble in ammonia. The solubility in ammonia is the key distinguishing criterion among the three silver halide precipitates.

Sulphates are identified by acidifying the test solution with dilute hydrochloric acid (to remove carbonate/sulphite interference) and then adding barium chloride solution (BaCl₂). A dense white precipitate of barium sulphate (BaSO₄) confirms the presence of sulphate ions: Ba²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → BaSO₄↓ (white). BaSO₄ is insoluble in dilute acids, which distinguishes it from barium sulphite or carbonate precipitates.

Carbonates react with dilute acids to produce carbon dioxide gas, which turns lime water (Ca(OH)₂ solution) milky due to the formation of insoluble calcium carbonate: CO₃²⁻ + 2H⁺ → H₂O + CO₂↑; Ca(OH)₂ + CO₂ → CaCO₃↓ + H₂O. If excess CO₂ is passed through lime water, the precipitate re-dissolves as soluble Ca(HCO₃)₂: CaCO₃ + H₂O + CO₂ → Ca(HCO₃)₂.

key_concepts.term: Test for Nitrate Anion (NO₃⁻ — Brown Ring Test)
key_concepts.definition: The brown ring test identifies nitrate ions: freshly prepared iron(II) sulphate (FeSO₄) solution is mixed with the suspected nitrate solution, and concentrated sulphuric acid is carefully added down the side of the test tube. A brown ring at the interface between the two layers confirms nitrates: NO₃⁻ + Fe²⁺ → [Fe(NO)]²⁺ (brown complex). The concentrated H₂SO₄ provides an acidic medium and reduces the nitrate to nitric oxide (NO), which then coordinates with Fe²⁺.

H₂ (hydrogen): colourless, odourless; 'pop' sound when a lit splint is brought near. O₂ (oxygen): colourless; relights a glowing splint. CO₂ (carbon dioxide): colourless; turns lime water milky. Cl₂ (chlorine): greenish-yellow, pungent; bleaches damp litmus paper; turns starch-iodide paper blue-black then decolourises it. NH₃ (ammonia): colourless, pungent; turns moist red litmus paper blue; dense white fumes with HCl. SO₂ (sulphur dioxide): colourless, pungent acidic; turns potassium permanganate/dichromate paper green (reducing agent); turns lime water milky then clear. H₂S (hydrogen sulphide): rotten-egg smell; turns lead acetate paper black.

key_concepts.term: Tests for Iron Ions (Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺)
key_concepts.definition: Fe²⁺ (ferrous): with NaOH → dirty/pale green precipitate (Fe(OH)₂); with NH₃ → pale green precipitate; turns reddish-brown on exposure to air (oxidised to Fe³⁺); with potassium thiocyanate (KSCN) → no red colour (negative); with potassium ferricyanide K₃[Fe(CN)₆] → deep blue precipitate (Turnbull's blue). Fe³⁺ (ferric): with NaOH → reddish-brown precipitate (Fe(OH)₃); with KSCN → deep blood-red colour ([FeSCN]²⁺); with potassium ferrocyanide K₄[Fe(CN)₆] → deep blue precipitate (Prussian blue).

Systematic qualitative analysis follows a logical sequence: preliminary observations (colour, smell, physical state), solubility tests, flame test, group reactions with NaOH and NH₃ to identify cation group, followed by specific confirmatory tests. Anion identification uses acid tests (carbonate → CO₂; sulphite → SO₂) and precipitation reactions (halides with AgNO₃; sulphates with BaCl₂). Working methodically avoids ambiguity and ensures that the presence of multiple ions does not mask results.

laws_and_formulas.name: Silver Halide Solubility in Ammonia
laws_and_formulas.formula: AgCl (white) > AgBr (cream) > AgI (yellow) — solubility in NH₃ decreases
laws_and_formulas.description: AgCl dissolves readily in dilute ammonia, AgBr dissolves only in concentrated ammonia, and AgI is insoluble even in concentrated ammonia. This differential solubility in ammonia solution is the key test to distinguish Cl⁻, Br⁻, and I⁻ after precipitation with AgNO₃.

laws_and_formulas.name: Barium Sulphate Precipitate Test
laws_and_formulas.formula: Ba²⁺(aq) + SO₄²⁻(aq) → BaSO₄↓ (white, insoluble in dilute acid)
laws_and_formulas.description: Addition of BaCl₂ to an acidified solution containing sulphate ions produces a dense white precipitate of BaSO₄ insoluble in HCl. Acidification before adding BaCl₂ rules out carbonate and sulphite, which also precipitate with Ba²⁺ but dissolve in acid.

laws_and_formulas.name: Lime Water Test for CO₂
laws_and_formulas.description: Passing CO₂ through lime water produces a white precipitate of calcium carbonate, turning the lime water milky. If excess CO₂ is bubbled through, the precipitate dissolves: CaCO₃ + H₂O + CO₂ → Ca(HCO₃)₂ (soluble), and the solution clears again.

key_points: Flame test colours: Li = crimson, Na = yellow (persistent), K = lilac, Ca = brick red, Cu = blue-green, Ba = apple green.
With NaOH: NH₄⁺ → pungent NH₃ gas (warm); Fe²⁺ → dirty green precipitate; Fe³⁺ → reddish-brown precipitate; Cu²⁺ → pale blue precipitate.
With NH₃ solution: Cu²⁺ precipitate dissolves in excess to give deep blue solution [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺; Zn²⁺ dissolves in excess NH₃ (colourless); Al³⁺ does not dissolve in excess NH₃.
Both Zn²⁺ and Al³⁺ give white precipitates with NaOH that dissolve in excess NaOH (amphoteric hydroxides). Distinction: Zn(OH)₂ dissolves in excess NH₃; Al(OH)₃ does not.
Halide test: add dilute HNO₃ then AgNO₃ — white AgCl (soluble in dil. NH₃), cream AgBr (soluble in conc. NH₃), yellow AgI (insoluble in NH₃).
Sulphate test: acidify with HCl, then add BaCl₂ → dense white BaSO₄ precipitate insoluble in acid.
Brown ring test for NO₃⁻: fresh FeSO₄ + suspected solution + conc. H₂SO₄ (carefully) → brown ring at interface.
CO₂ gas turns lime water milky; excess CO₂ makes it clear again. SO₂ turns lime water milky but also decolourises acidified KMnO₄.
H₂ gas produces a 'pop' with a lit splint; O₂ relights a glowing splint; Cl₂ bleaches damp litmus paper; NH₃ turns moist red litmus blue.
H₂S (rotten-egg smell) turns lead acetate paper black (PbS) — this is the confirmatory test for H₂S and sulphide ions.

10|chemistry|5_Mole_Concept_and_Stoichio.overview: The mole concept provides the fundamental bridge between the microscopic world of atoms and molecules and the macroscopic world of grams and litres that chemists work with in the laboratory. One mole of any substance contains 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number, Nₐ), and the molar mass of an element numerically equals its relative atomic mass in grams per mole. Stoichiometry uses balanced chemical equations to determine the quantitative relationships between reactants and products in terms of moles, masses, and volumes. Key applications include calculating percentage composition, determining empirical and molecular formulas, applying the concept of molar volume (22.4 L/mol at STP for gases), and identifying the limiting reagent that controls the amount of product formed in a reaction. These calculations form the quantitative foundation of all chemistry.

key_concepts.definition: A mole is the SI unit for the amount of substance, defined as the amount of a substance that contains as many elementary entities (atoms, molecules, ions, or other particles) as there are atoms in exactly 12 grams of carbon-12. This number, Avogadro's number (Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³), is the same for all substances. Thus, 1 mole of carbon atoms = 6.022 × 10²³ atoms; 1 mole of water molecules = 6.022 × 10²³ molecules of H₂O; 1 mole of Na⁺ ions = 6.022 × 10²³ Na⁺ ions.

Avogadro's number is 6.022 × 10²³ per mole, representing the number of specified particles in one mole of any substance. It relates the microscopic scale (individual atoms or molecules) to the macroscopic scale (grams and litres). For example, 1 mole of hydrogen gas (H₂, molar mass = 2 g/mol) contains 6.022 × 10²³ molecules and has a mass of 2 g; 1 mole of water (H₂O, molar mass = 18 g/mol) contains 6.022 × 10²³ molecules and has a mass of 18 g.

Molar mass is the mass of one mole of a substance, expressed in grams per mole (g/mol). Numerically, the molar mass of an element equals its relative atomic mass (Ar) and the molar mass of a compound equals its relative molecular mass (Mr). For example: Molar mass of O = 16 g/mol; Molar mass of H₂O = 2(1) + 16 = 18 g/mol; Molar mass of H₂SO₄ = 2 + 32 + 64 = 98 g/mol. Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol).

key_concepts.term: Molar Volume of Gases at STP
key_concepts.definition: At Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP: 0°C and 1 atm), one mole of any gas occupies 22.4 litres (22.4 dm³), regardless of the identity of the gas — this is the molar volume. This follows from Avogadro's Law, which states that equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain equal numbers of molecules. For example, 1 mol (2 g) of H₂ occupies 22.4 L at STP; 1 mol (32 g) of O₂ also occupies 22.4 L at STP.

Stoichiometry is the calculation of the quantities of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on balanced chemical equations. The coefficients in a balanced equation give the molar ratios of reactants to products. For example, in N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃, 1 mol of N₂ reacts with 3 mol of H₂ to produce 2 mol of NH₃. Stoichiometric calculations allow chemists to predict how much product is obtained from a given mass of reactant, or how much reactant is needed to obtain a desired amount of product.

Percentage composition gives the mass percentage of each element in a compound. It is calculated from the formula: % by mass of element = (mass of element in 1 mol of compound ÷ molar mass of compound) × 100. For example, in H₂O: % H = (2/18) × 100 = 11.1%; % O = (16/18) × 100 = 88.9%. Percentage composition is used to verify the purity of a compound or to determine its empirical formula.

The empirical formula of a compound gives the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element present in the compound. It is determined from percentage composition data by: (1) dividing the percentage of each element by its atomic mass to get the mole ratio, (2) dividing all ratios by the smallest value, and (3) multiplying to get whole numbers if necessary. For example, a compound with 40% C, 6.7% H, and 53.3% O has the empirical formula CH₂O (the simplest ratio 1:2:1).

The molecular formula gives the actual number of atoms of each element in one molecule of the compound. It is a whole-number multiple of the empirical formula: molecular formula = (empirical formula)ₙ, where n = molar mass of compound ÷ empirical formula mass. For example, if the empirical formula of glucose is CH₂O (mass = 30 g/mol) and the molecular mass is 180 g/mol, then n = 180/30 = 6, so the molecular formula is C₆H₁₂O₆.

In a reaction where one reactant is in excess and another is insufficient, the limiting reagent (or limiting reactant) is the one that is completely consumed first, thereby limiting the amount of product that can form. The other reactant is in excess and remains partially unconsumed. To identify the limiting reagent, convert all reactant masses to moles, divide by their stoichiometric coefficients, and the smallest value corresponds to the limiting reagent. The theoretical yield of product is calculated from the amount of the limiting reagent.

key_concepts.term: Gay-Lussac's Law of Gaseous Volumes and Avogadro's Law
key_concepts.definition: Gay-Lussac's Law states that volumes of gases taking part in a reaction (at constant temperature and pressure) are in simple whole-number ratios, equal to the stoichiometric coefficients. Avogadro's Law states that equal volumes of all gases, at the same temperature and pressure, contain equal numbers of molecules. Together, these laws justify using volume ratios directly from balanced equations for gaseous reactions. For example, H₂(g) + Cl₂(g) → 2HCl(g): 1 volume H₂ reacts with 1 volume Cl₂ to give 2 volumes HCl.

laws_and_formulas.name: Mole Definition
laws_and_formulas.description: Number of moles (n) equals mass of sample (m, in grams) divided by molar mass (M, in g/mol). Also: n = N / Nₐ, where N is the number of particles and Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹. For gases at STP: n = V / 22.4, where V is the volume in litres.

laws_and_formulas.name: Percentage Composition
laws_and_formulas.formula: % element = (mass of element in 1 mol / molar mass of compound) × 100
laws_and_formulas.description: Used to find the proportion by mass of each element in a compound. This is the starting point for determining empirical formulas from experimental data. Also used to check whether a synthesised product has the correct composition.

laws_and_formulas.name: Empirical to Molecular Formula
laws_and_formulas.formula: n = Molecular Mass / Empirical Formula Mass; Molecular Formula = (Empirical Formula)ₙ
laws_and_formulas.description: The molecular formula is an integer multiple (n) of the empirical formula. The value of n is found by dividing the experimentally determined molecular mass (from mass spectrometry or other methods) by the mass of the empirical formula unit.

laws_and_formulas.name: Avogadro's Number
laws_and_formulas.description: The number of elementary entities (atoms, molecules, ions) in one mole of any substance. This constant links the atomic mass unit (amu) to the gram: 1 amu = 1/Nₐ grams ≈ 1.66 × 10⁻²⁴ g.

laws_and_formulas.name: Molar Volume at STP
laws_and_formulas.description: One mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 litres at STP. This is used to interconvert between volume and moles for gaseous reactants and products in stoichiometric calculations.

key_points: One mole of any substance contains 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number); this links microscopic atoms to measurable grams.
Molar mass (in g/mol) numerically equals the relative atomic or molecular mass (Ar or Mr).
n (moles) = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol); n = volume (L) ÷ 22.4 (for gases at STP); n = number of particles ÷ 6.022 × 10²³.
At STP (0°C, 1 atm), 1 mole of any gas occupies 22.4 L — the molar volume. This applies to all ideal gases regardless of their identity.
Stoichiometric coefficients in a balanced equation give the mole ratios (and for gases at constant T and P, the volume ratios) of reactants and products.
Percentage composition: % element = (mass of element per mole of compound ÷ molar mass of compound) × 100.
Empirical formula: simplest whole-number ratio of atoms — found by dividing mole ratios by the smallest value.
Molecular formula = n × empirical formula, where n = molecular mass ÷ empirical formula mass.
The limiting reagent is the reactant that is completely consumed first and determines the maximum theoretical yield of product.
Gay-Lussac's Law: gas volumes in reactions are in simple whole-number ratios (equal to molar ratios at constant T and P).

10|chemistry|6_Electrolysis.overview: Electrolysis is the process of using electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous chemical reaction, causing decomposition of an electrolyte. In an electrolytic cell, direct current (DC) is passed through a molten or dissolved ionic compound (the electrolyte) via two electrodes — the anode (connected to the positive terminal, where oxidation occurs) and the cathode (connected to the negative terminal, where reduction occurs). The specific products of electrolysis depend on the nature of the electrolyte and the electrode material. Faraday's laws of electrolysis quantitatively relate the mass of substance deposited or liberated at an electrode to the quantity of charge passed. Electrolysis has widespread industrial applications, including electroplating for corrosion protection and aesthetics, extraction of reactive metals like aluminium and sodium, and purification of copper.

key_concepts.definition: An electrolytic cell consists of: (1) a DC power source (battery), (2) two electrodes — the cathode (negative electrode, where reduction/cation discharge occurs) and the anode (positive electrode, where oxidation/anion discharge occurs), and (3) the electrolyte — a molten ionic compound or an aqueous ionic solution that contains free-moving ions to carry the current. The electrolyte must be in liquid form (molten or dissolved) for the ions to migrate; solid ionic compounds cannot be electrolysed because ions are fixed.

Pure water is a very poor conductor, so dilute sulphuric acid is added to facilitate electrolysis. At the cathode (−): 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ → 2H₂ (hydrogen gas is produced by reduction of H⁺ ions). At the anode (+): 2H₂O → O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ (oxygen gas is produced by oxidation of water). The volume ratio of H₂:O₂ collected is 2:1 (consistent with the formula of water H₂O). This demonstrates electrolytic decomposition of water.

key_concepts.term: Electrolysis of Molten NaCl vs Aqueous NaCl
key_concepts.definition: Molten NaCl contains only Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions. At the cathode: Na⁺ + e⁻ → Na (sodium metal). At the anode: 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂ + 2e⁻ (chlorine gas). In aqueous NaCl solution, competing species exist — at the cathode, H⁺ (from water) is discharged in preference to Na⁺ (since Na⁺ requires more energy to discharge), producing H₂. At the anode, Cl⁻ is discharged in preference to OH⁻ (when solution is concentrated), producing Cl₂. Products of aqueous NaCl electrolysis: H₂ at cathode, Cl₂ at anode, NaOH remains in solution.

key_concepts.term: Electrolysis of CuSO₄ with Copper Electrodes
key_concepts.definition: When copper electrodes are used with CuSO₄ solution: at the cathode, Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (copper is deposited). At the anode, the copper electrode dissolves: Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻. The net effect is that copper is transferred from the anode to the cathode, with the concentration of CuSO₄ remaining constant. This is the principle of electrorefining (purification) of copper, where impure copper is the anode and pure copper is the cathode.

key_concepts.term: Electrolysis of CuSO₄ with Platinum/Carbon Electrodes
key_concepts.definition: With inert electrodes (platinum or carbon) and CuSO₄ solution: at the cathode, Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (copper deposited). At the anode, 2H₂O → O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ (oxygen gas released since SO₄²⁻ is not discharged — it is too stable). As the reaction proceeds, the concentration of CuSO₄ decreases and the solution becomes more acidic (due to H⁺ formation).

Faraday's First Law: The mass of substance deposited or liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity (charge) passed through the electrolyte; m ∝ Q, where Q = I × t (current × time in coulombs). Faraday's Second Law: For the same quantity of electricity passed through different electrolytes, the masses of substances deposited are proportional to their equivalent masses (molar mass ÷ number of electrons transferred). One Faraday (F = 96,500 C) deposits one mole of a monovalent ion or half a mole of a divalent ion.

When a mixture of ions is present in an electrolyte, the ion that requires the least energy to be discharged is preferentially discharged. At the cathode, cations lower in the electrochemical series (activity series) are preferentially discharged (e.g., Cu²⁺ before H⁺ before Na⁺). At the anode, anions that require less energy to be oxidised are preferentially discharged (e.g., Cl⁻ before OH⁻ before SO₄²⁻ in concentrated solutions; OH⁻ before SO₄²⁻ in dilute solutions).

Electroplating uses electrolysis to coat a thin layer of one metal onto the surface of another object to improve appearance, prevent corrosion, or increase hardness. In electroplating: the object to be plated is the cathode; the plating metal is the anode; and the electrolyte is a solution of a salt of the plating metal. Common applications: gold plating of jewellery (using gold(III) chloride or gold cyanide solution), silver plating of cutlery, chromium plating of car parts (using chromic acid solution). The deposit thickness is controlled by the current, time, and concentration.

Aluminium is extracted from purified aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃, bauxite ore → alumina) by electrolysis in the Hall-Héroult process. Pure Al₂O₃ has a very high melting point (2050°C), so it is dissolved in molten cryolite (Na₃AlF₆) at about 950°C, which lowers the melting point and increases conductivity. At the cathode (carbon lining): Al³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Al (molten aluminium collects at the bottom). At the anode (carbon): 2O²⁻ → O₂ + 4e⁻ (oxygen gas burns the carbon anode, which must be replaced periodically).

key_concepts.term: Electrolysis of Dilute H₂SO₄ with Platinum Electrodes
key_concepts.definition: When dilute sulphuric acid is electrolysed with platinum (inert) electrodes, the effective species are H⁺, OH⁻, and SO₄²⁻. At the cathode: 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ → 2H₂ (hydrogen gas). At the anode: 4OH⁻ → 2H₂O + O₂ + 4e⁻ (oxygen gas; OH⁻ is discharged in preference to SO₄²⁻). The net result is decomposition of water into H₂ and O₂ in a 2:1 volume ratio. The concentration of H₂SO₄ increases as water is consumed.

laws_and_formulas.name: Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis
laws_and_formulas.formula: m = Z × Q = Z × I × t
laws_and_formulas.description: The mass (m) of substance deposited or liberated at an electrode is proportional to the quantity of charge (Q = current I in amperes × time t in seconds). Z is the electrochemical equivalent (mass deposited per coulomb). In practice: m = (M × I × t) / (n × F), where M = molar mass, n = number of electrons transferred, F = 96,500 C/mol.

laws_and_formulas.name: Faraday's Second Law of Electrolysis
laws_and_formulas.formula: m₁/m₂ = E₁/E₂ (where E = M/n is the equivalent mass)
laws_and_formulas.description: For the same charge passed through different electrolytes, masses deposited are proportional to their equivalent masses. Equivalent mass = molar mass ÷ number of electrons transferred per ion (e.g., for Cu²⁺: E = 64/2 = 32 g/equiv; for Ag⁺: E = 108/1 = 108 g/equiv).

laws_and_formulas.name: Faraday's Constant
laws_and_formulas.formula: F = 96,500 C/mol ≈ 96,500 C per mole of electrons
laws_and_formulas.description: One Faraday is the charge carried by one mole of electrons (Nₐ × e = 6.022×10²³ × 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C ≈ 96,500 C). Passing 1 F of charge deposits 1 mole of a monovalent cation (e.g., 108 g of Ag), 0.5 mol of a divalent cation (e.g., 32 g of Cu²⁺ from CuSO₄), or 1/3 mol of Al³⁺ (9 g).

laws_and_formulas.name: Charge Equation
laws_and_formulas.description: Charge (Q) in coulombs equals current (I) in amperes multiplied by time (t) in seconds. This is the fundamental relationship used in all electrolysis calculations. For example, 2 A for 30 minutes = 2 × 1800 = 3600 C.

key_points: Electrolysis uses DC electricity to decompose an electrolyte; reduction occurs at the cathode (negative), oxidation at the anode (positive).
The electrolyte must be molten or in aqueous solution for ions to be free to migrate; solid ionic compounds do not conduct electricity.
Electrolysis of water (acidulated): H₂ at cathode, O₂ at anode, in a 2:1 volume ratio.
Electrolysis of molten NaCl: Na metal at cathode, Cl₂ gas at anode. Electrolysis of aqueous NaCl: H₂ at cathode, Cl₂ at anode (concentrated), NaOH remains in solution.
Electrolysis of CuSO₄ with copper electrodes: cathode gains Cu, anode loses Cu — the basis of copper electrorefining/purification.
Preferential discharge: at cathode, ions lower in activity series discharged first (Cu²⁺ before H⁺ before Na⁺); at anode, in concentrated Cl⁻ solution, Cl⁻ is discharged before OH⁻.
Faraday's First Law: mass deposited ∝ charge passed (Q = I × t).
Faraday's Second Law: for same charge, masses deposited ∝ equivalent masses (M/n).
1 Faraday (96,500 C) deposits 1 mole of a monovalent ion (e.g., 108 g Ag), 0.5 mol of a divalent ion (e.g., 32 g Cu), or 1/3 mol of a trivalent ion.
Aluminium extraction (Hall-Héroult process): Al₂O₃ dissolved in molten cryolite at 950°C; Al deposited at carbon cathode; O₂ produced at carbon anode burns the electrodes.
Electroplating: object to plate = cathode; plating metal = anode; electrolyte = salt solution of plating metal. Used for decoration, corrosion protection (Ag, Au, Cr plating).

10|chemistry|7_Metallurgy.overview: Metallurgy is the branch of chemistry concerned with the extraction and purification of metals from their naturally occurring ores and the study of their properties and uses. Most metals occur in nature as ores — naturally occurring mineral compounds from which metals can be economically extracted. The extraction process typically involves ore dressing (concentration of the ore), reduction to the free metal, and refining (purification). The method of extraction depends on the position of the metal in the electrochemical (activity) series — metals high in the series (like aluminium and sodium) require electrolysis, while those in the middle (like iron and zinc) can be reduced by carbon or carbon monoxide. The iron blast furnace and the Hall-Héroult electrolytic process for aluminium are the two most economically significant metal extraction processes. Prevention of corrosion through protective coatings, alloying, and galvanisation is also a critical aspect of metallurgy.

key_concepts.definition: A mineral is any naturally occurring inorganic substance with a definite chemical composition; rocks contain many minerals. An ore is a mineral from which a particular metal can be extracted profitably and economically. For example, haematite (Fe₂O₃) is an ore of iron, and bauxite (Al₂O₃·2H₂O) is an ore of aluminium. Gangue (matrix) refers to the unwanted rocky impurities (sand, clay, limestone) associated with the ore mineral; it must be removed before smelting to reduce energy costs.

Ore dressing involves physical methods used to remove gangue from the ore before chemical extraction. Methods include: (1) Crushing and grinding — mechanical reduction to increase surface area. (2) Gravity separation (hydraulic washing) — denser ore particles sink, lighter gangue is washed away by water current; used for heavy metal ores like tin. (3) Froth flotation — ore particles cling to oil froth and float while gangue sinks; used for sulphide ores (e.g., copper pyrite). (4) Magnetic separation — magnetic minerals (e.g., magnetite, Fe₃O₄) are separated by an electromagnet from non-magnetic gangue.

key_concepts.term: Reduction by Carbon and Carbon Monoxide
key_concepts.definition: Metals in the middle of the activity series (e.g., iron, zinc, lead) can be extracted by reducing their oxides with carbon (coke) or carbon monoxide at high temperatures. The reducing agent (carbon or CO) removes oxygen from the metal oxide. Examples: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂ (in the blast furnace); ZnO + C → Zn + CO (zinc smelting). Carbon can only reduce the oxides of metals below it in the electrochemical series — it cannot reduce Al₂O₃ or Na₂O economically.

The thermite reaction uses aluminium powder as a reducing agent to extract iron from iron(III) oxide: 2Al + Fe₂O₃ → Al₂O₃ + 2Fe + heat. This highly exothermic reaction produces molten iron and is used to weld railway tracks and in incendiary devices. Aluminium is a stronger reducing agent than iron and displaces iron from its oxide. The thermite reaction demonstrates the relative positions of Al and Fe in the activity series.

Iron is extracted in a blast furnace from iron ore (haematite, Fe₂O₃), coke (carbon), and limestone (CaCO₃). Reactions: (1) Coke burns: C + O₂ → CO₂, then CO₂ + C → 2CO (reducing agent). (2) Iron oxide is reduced: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂. (3) Limestone decomposes: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂; CaO acts as a flux, reacting with silica gangue: CaO + SiO₂ → CaSiO₃ (slag). The molten iron (pig iron) sinks to the bottom; slag floats above it. Pig iron is brittle due to dissolved carbon (~4%); it is refined to wrought iron or steel by removing impurities.

Aluminium cannot be extracted by carbon reduction (Al₂O₃ is too stable). Bauxite ore is first purified to give alumina (Al₂O₃) by the Bayer process (treatment with NaOH). Alumina is dissolved in molten cryolite (Na₃AlF₆) at about 950°C (much lower than the melting point of Al₂O₃ alone, 2050°C). Electrolysis then takes place in a carbon-lined steel tank: cathode reaction: Al³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Al (molten aluminium sinks to bottom and is tapped off); anode reaction (carbon rods): 2O²⁻ → O₂ + 4e⁻ (oxygen burns the carbon anodes, which need regular replacement).

Corrosion is the gradual deterioration of a metal due to chemical or electrochemical reaction with its environment. The most familiar example is rusting of iron: in the presence of water and oxygen, iron forms hydrated iron(III) oxide (Fe₂O₃·xH₂O, rust). Electrochemical corrosion involves the formation of tiny galvanic cells on the metal surface, where anodic regions (iron) dissolve and cathodic regions (areas with impurities or at grain boundaries) are protected. Corrosion causes enormous economic losses globally and is accelerated by acidic conditions, salt solutions, and high humidity.

Methods to prevent corrosion include: (1) Painting or oiling — barrier protection. (2) Galvanisation — coating iron with zinc; even if the zinc layer is scratched, zinc (being higher in the activity series) acts as a sacrificial anode and corrodes instead of iron (cathodic protection). (3) Tinning (tin plating) — tin coat protects as long as it is intact, but if scratched, iron corrodes faster (tin is lower than iron in activity series). (4) Alloying — stainless steel (Fe + Cr + Ni) resists corrosion. (5) Electroplating with chromium or nickel. (6) Sacrificial anode — attaching zinc/magnesium blocks to iron pipes or ship hulls.

The electrochemical (reactivity) series ranks metals from most reactive (K at the top) to least reactive (Au at the bottom). The method of extraction is determined by the metal's position: highly reactive metals (Na, K, Ca, Mg, Al) can only be extracted by electrolysis because carbon cannot reduce their very stable oxides. Moderately reactive metals (Zn, Fe, Pb, Sn) are extracted by reduction with carbon or CO in a furnace. Unreactive metals (Cu, Hg, Ag, Au) occur as native metals or are easily reduced by heating alone (e.g., 2HgO → 2Hg + O₂).

laws_and_formulas.name: Reduction by CO in Blast Furnace
laws_and_formulas.description: Carbon monoxide (produced from coke) reduces iron(III) oxide to molten iron at temperatures above 700°C in the blast furnace. This is the principal ore-reduction reaction in the extraction of iron.

laws_and_formulas.name: Thermite Reaction
laws_and_formulas.description: Aluminium, being a stronger reducing agent than iron, reduces iron oxide to molten iron in this highly exothermic reaction. It illustrates displacement reactions in the activity series and is used for field welding of rail tracks.

laws_and_formulas.name: Formation of Slag (Blast Furnace)
laws_and_formulas.description: Limestone added to the blast furnace decomposes to quicklime (CaO) which reacts with silica (gangue) to form calcium silicate slag. The slag is less dense than iron and floats on top of the molten iron, making separation easy.

laws_and_formulas.name: Rusting of Iron
laws_and_formulas.description: Rusting requires both oxygen and water (moisture). The process is electrochemical: iron at anodic areas is oxidised (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻) while oxygen is reduced at cathodic areas (O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻). The final product is rust — hydrated iron(III) oxide.

key_points: An ore is a naturally occurring mineral from which a metal can be extracted profitably; gangue is the useless rocky material removed during ore dressing.
Ore dressing methods: gravity separation (heavy ores), froth flotation (sulphide ores), magnetic separation (magnetic ores like magnetite), crushing/grinding.

Metals high in the activity series (Na, K, Al, Mg) require electrolysis for extraction; metals in the middle (Fe, Zn, Pb) are extracted by reduction with carbon/CO; unreactive metals (Cu, Ag, Au) need little or no reduction.
Blast furnace extracts iron from haematite (Fe₂O₃) using coke (reducing agent) and limestone (flux to remove silica gangue as slag).
Key blast furnace reactions: C + O₂ → CO₂; CO₂ + C → 2CO; Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂; CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂; CaO + SiO₂ → CaSiO₃ (slag).
Aluminium is extracted by the Hall-Héroult electrolytic process: Al₂O₃ dissolved in molten cryolite at 950°C; Al deposited at cathode; O₂ burns carbon anodes at anode.
Thermite process: 2Al + Fe₂O₃ → Al₂O₃ + 2Fe (exothermic); aluminium displaces iron from its oxide; used for rail welding.
Corrosion (rusting) requires both water AND oxygen; salt water and acids accelerate rusting.
Galvanisation (zinc coating) provides cathodic protection — if zinc layer is scratched, Zn corrodes preferentially (sacrificial anode) and iron is protected.
Tinning protects only while the tin layer is intact; a scratch exposes iron which then corrodes faster than plain iron (tin is less reactive, iron becomes anode in the galvanic cell).

10|chemistry|8_Study_of_Compounds.overview: This chapter provides a detailed study of four industrially vital inorganic compounds — ammonia, nitric acid, hydrogen chloride, and sulphuric acid — covering their laboratory preparation, industrial manufacture, physical and chemical properties, and major uses. Ammonia is manufactured by the Haber process and is crucial for fertiliser production. Nitric acid is manufactured by the Ostwald process from ammonia and is a powerful oxidising acid. Hydrogen chloride is a covalent compound at room temperature but forms a strong acid (hydrochloric acid) in aqueous solution. Sulphuric acid, produced by the Contact process, is the world's most produced industrial chemical and has unique properties both in dilute and concentrated form. Understanding the conditions, catalysts, and equations for each industrial process, as well as the diagnostic tests for these acids and their salts, is central to the ICSE syllabus.

key_concepts.definition: Ammonia (NH₃) is manufactured industrially by the Haber process: N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g); ΔH = −92 kJ/mol. Conditions: temperature 450–500°C (compromise between yield and reaction rate — lower temperatures favour equilibrium yield but too slow), pressure 150–200 atm (high pressure favours the side with fewer gas molecules — 2 mol NH₃ vs 4 mol reactant gases), iron catalyst (with promoters K₂O and Al₂O₃). The nitrogen comes from fractional distillation of liquid air; hydrogen comes from steam reforming of methane (CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂) or from the water-gas process. Yield per pass is typically 10–15%; unreacted gases are recycled.

Ammonia (NH₃) is a colourless gas with a pungent smell, lighter than air (Molar mass = 17 g/mol), highly soluble in water (fountain experiment) forming alkaline ammonium hydroxide solution. It turns moist red litmus paper blue. Chemical properties: acts as a reducing agent (reduces CuO to Cu: 3CuO + 2NH₃ → 3Cu + N₂ + 3H₂O); reacts with acids to form ammonium salts (NH₃ + HCl → NH₄Cl — dense white fumes); when mixed with oxygen with a Pt catalyst (Ostwald process), it is oxidised to NO. Uses: manufacture of fertilisers (urea CO(NH₂)₂, ammonium sulphate, ammonium nitrate), manufacture of nitric acid, refrigerant (liquid ammonia), production of explosives.

Nitric acid is manufactured by the Ostwald process in three stages: (1) Catalytic oxidation of ammonia: 4NH₃ + 5O₂ → 4NO + 6H₂O (Pt/Rh catalyst, 850–950°C, 1 atm). (2) Oxidation of NO to NO₂: 2NO + O₂ → 2NO₂ (cooled, no catalyst). (3) Absorption of NO₂ in water: 4NO₂ + O₂ + 2H₂O → 4HNO₃. Dilute HNO₃ (~60%) is obtained; concentrated HNO₃ is made by distillation with concentrated H₂SO₄. Nitric acid is a strong, highly oxidising acid.

Dilute HNO₃ is a strong monobasic acid that fully dissociates in water. It does not liberate hydrogen with metals (it is an oxidising acid); instead it oxidises metals: Zn + 4HNO₃(conc.) → Zn(NO₃)₂ + 2NO₂ + 2H₂O; 8HNO₃(dil.) + 3Cu → 3Cu(NO₃)₂ + 2NO + 4H₂O. Concentrated HNO₃ turns protein yellow (xanthoproteic test); it is stored in dark brown bottles because it decomposes in light: 4HNO₃ → 4NO₂ + O₂ + 2H₂O. A 3:1 mixture of conc. HCl and conc. HNO₃ is aqua regia, which dissolves noble metals (Au, Pt). Main use: manufacture of fertilisers (ammonium nitrate), explosives (TNT, nitroglycerine), and dyes.

HCl gas can be prepared by: (1) Action of concentrated H₂SO₄ on common salt (NaCl): NaCl + H₂SO₄(conc.) → NaHSO₄ + HCl↑ (cold) or Na₂SO₄ + 2HCl↑ (hot). (2) Direct synthesis: H₂ + Cl₂ → 2HCl (explosive when sparked). HCl is a colourless gas with a pungent acidic smell, denser than air. It fumes in moist air, is extremely soluble in water (1:500), and is the fountain experiment gas. When dissolved in water, it forms hydrochloric acid — a strong monobasic acid that fully dissociates: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻. It reacts with NH₃ to form dense white fumes of NH₄Cl.

key_concepts.term: Properties and Tests for Hydrochloric Acid
key_concepts.definition: Hydrochloric acid (HCl(aq)) is a strong acid that: reacts with metals above H in the activity series to give metal chloride + H₂; reacts with bases/alkalis to form chloride salts + water; reacts with metal carbonates to give CO₂; precipitates white AgCl (insoluble in dilute HNO₃) when AgNO₃ is added — this is the confirmatory test for Cl⁻ ions. It does not oxidise metals to higher-than-normal oxidation states (unlike HNO₃ and H₂SO₄). Concentrated HCl releases HCl fumes; dilute HCl is used in labs for salt preparation and as a laboratory reagent.

Sulphuric acid is manufactured industrially by the Contact process: (1) Burning sulphur or roasting pyrites: S + O₂ → SO₂ or 4FeS₂ + 11O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃ + 8SO₂. (2) Catalytic oxidation: 2SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2SO₃ (V₂O₅ catalyst, 450°C, 1–2 atm — these are compromise conditions). (3) Absorption: SO₃ is absorbed in concentrated H₂SO₄ to form oleum (H₂S₂O₇), which is diluted with water to give concentrated H₂SO₄: H₂S₂O₇ + H₂O → 2H₂SO₄. Note: SO₃ cannot be absorbed directly in water (forms a stable mist).

key_concepts.term: Properties of Sulphuric Acid (Dilute and Concentrated)
key_concepts.definition: Dilute H₂SO₄ is a strong dibasic acid: it reacts with metals above H in activity series (producing H₂), with bases (neutralisation), and with carbonates (producing CO₂). Concentrated H₂SO₄ is an oily, dense (1.84 g/cm³), colourless liquid with very high boiling point (337°C); it is a powerful dehydrating agent (removes H₂O from CuSO₄·5H₂O to give white anhydrous CuSO₄; chars sugar: C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ → 12C + 11H₂O); it is a strong oxidising agent (reacts with Cu: Cu + 2H₂SO₄(conc.) → CuSO₄ + SO₂ + 2H₂O; with C: C + 2H₂SO₄(conc.) → CO₂ + 2SO₂ + 2H₂O). Concentrated H₂SO₄ reacts violently with water; always add acid to water, not water to acid.

Sulphuric acid is the most produced industrial chemical in the world and is often called 'the king of chemicals'. Its uses include: manufacture of fertilisers (ammonium sulphate, superphosphate), manufacture of other acids (HCl, HNO₃, H₃PO₄), as electrolyte in lead-acid batteries, manufacture of detergents and soaps, petroleum refining, dyes and pharmaceuticals, and as a laboratory reagent. The volume of H₂SO₄ produced by a nation is a traditional indicator of its industrial development.

key_concepts.term: Tests for Sulphuric Acid and Sulphates
key_concepts.definition: For H₂SO₄ (dilute): (1) Blue litmus turns red. (2) Addition of Ba²⁺ (BaCl₂ + HCl) gives white BaSO₄ precipitate insoluble in acid. (3) Reaction with zinc gives H₂ gas (pop test). For concentrated H₂SO₄: (1) Chars sugar black. (2) Turns white anhydrous CuSO₄ blue (dehydrating action reversed). (3) Reacts with copper metal to give CuSO₄ + SO₂ (pungent gas turning acidified K₂Cr₂O₇ green). Sulphate anion test: add dilute HCl (to acidify) then BaCl₂ — white precipitate of BaSO₄ confirms SO₄²⁻.

laws_and_formulas.name: Haber Process (Ammonia Synthesis)
laws_and_formulas.description: An exothermic, reversible reaction. Conditions: 450–500°C, 150–200 atm, iron catalyst. Low temperature favours yield but slows rate; high pressure favours yield (fewer mol gas on right) but increases cost. The compromise conditions (450°C, 200 atm) maximise economic yield.

laws_and_formulas.name: Ostwald Process — Key Steps
laws_and_formulas.description: Stage 1: Ammonia is catalytically oxidised over platinum/rhodium gauze at 850–950°C to form NO. Stage 2: NO is oxidised to NO₂ by cooling with excess air. Stage 3: NO₂ is absorbed in water with oxygen to form dilute HNO₃ (~60%).

laws_and_formulas.name: Contact Process — Key Step
laws_and_formulas.description: Catalytic oxidation of SO₂ to SO₃ over vanadium pentoxide (V₂O₅) at 450°C and 1–2 atm. This step gives ~99.5% conversion. SO₃ is absorbed in conc. H₂SO₄ to form oleum: SO₃ + H₂SO₄ → H₂S₂O₇, then diluted to produce concentrated H₂SO₄.

laws_and_formulas.name: Laboratory Preparation of HCl
laws_and_formulas.description: Concentrated sulphuric acid reacts with sodium chloride to produce hydrogen chloride gas. At room temperature, acid bisulphate (NaHSO₄) is the primary product; on further heating, the bisulphate also reacts to release a second mole of HCl. The gas is dried with concentrated H₂SO₄ (not CaCl₂ which reacts with HCl).

laws_and_formulas.name: Dehydrating Action of Conc. H₂SO₄ on Sugar
laws_and_formulas.formula: C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ → 12C + 11H₂O (H₂SO₄ removes water, leaving black carbon sponge)
laws_and_formulas.description: Concentrated sulphuric acid removes the elements of water (H and O in 2:1 ratio) from organic compounds without breaking C–C bonds. This dramatic demonstration (black carbon column rises from white sugar) is used to illustrate the powerful dehydrating property of concentrated H₂SO₄.

key_points: Haber process (NH₃): N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃; conditions — 450°C, 200 atm, Fe catalyst; yield ~15% per pass; gases recycled.
Ostwald process (HNO₃): NH₃ → NO (Pt catalyst, 850°C) → NO₂ (cool, air) → HNO₃ (absorb NO₂ in water + O₂).
Contact process (H₂SO₄): S → SO₂ → SO₃ (V₂O₅ catalyst, 450°C) → oleum (H₂S₂O₇) → H₂SO₄ (dilute with water).
HCl gas: colourless, pungent, denser than air, extremely water-soluble, turns damp blue litmus red, reacts with NH₃ to form white NH₄Cl fumes.
Laboratory preparation of HCl: NaCl + conc. H₂SO₄ (heat) → NaHSO₄/Na₂SO₄ + HCl↑.
Confirmatory test for Cl⁻: add dilute HNO₃ + AgNO₃ → white AgCl precipitate soluble in dilute NH₃.
Dilute HNO₃ is an oxidising acid — does NOT produce H₂ with metals; instead produces NO (with dilute) or NO₂ (with concentrated) and metal nitrate.
Concentrated H₂SO₄ is a dehydrating agent (chars sugar, converts hydrated salts to anhydrous form) and an oxidising agent (reacts with Cu to give SO₂).
Rule: ALWAYS add concentrated H₂SO₄ (acid) to water slowly; NEVER add water to concentrated acid (violent, exothermic, spattering).
Sulphuric acid is the most produced industrial chemical; primary uses are fertilisers, batteries, and synthesis of other acids.
Ammonia turns moist red litmus paper blue; HCl turns moist blue litmus red — opposite effect, used to identify each gas.

10|chemistry|9_Organic_Chemistry.overview: Organic chemistry is the study of carbon compounds, and carbon's unique properties — tetravalency (forming 4 bonds), catenation (forming long chains and rings with other carbon atoms), and the ability to form stable single, double, and triple bonds — give rise to the vast diversity of organic molecules. A homologous series is a family of organic compounds with the same functional group, same general formula, similar chemical properties, and properties that change gradually with increasing molecular size. The IUPAC system provides a systematic method of naming all organic compounds. The key classes studied at this level are alkanes (saturated hydrocarbons undergoing substitution), alkenes (unsaturated hydrocarbons undergoing addition), alkynes, alcohols (including the industrial importance of ethanol), carboxylic acids (particularly ethanoic acid), and polymers (macromolecules formed by polymerisation). Understanding these compound families — their formulae, reactions, preparation, and uses — is fundamental to modern chemistry and biochemistry.

key_concepts.definition: Carbon (Group 14, atomic number 6) has four unique properties that explain the diversity of organic compounds: (1) Tetravalency: carbon has 4 valence electrons and forms exactly 4 covalent bonds, allowing it to bond to other carbons and to H, O, N, S, halogens. (2) Catenation: carbon atoms bond to each other in long chains, branched chains, and rings, forming the backbone of all organic molecules. (3) Multiple bonding: carbon forms stable single (C–C), double (C=C), and triple (C≡C) bonds. (4) Allotropy: carbon exists in several allotropic forms — diamond (each C bonded to 4 C in a tetrahedral network, making it the hardest natural substance), graphite (layered structure, each C bonded to 3 C with delocalised electrons, making it a conductor), and fullerenes.

A homologous series is a group of organic compounds that: (1) share the same general formula (e.g., alkanes: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂), (2) each successive member differs by one CH₂ group (methylene group), (3) have the same functional group and similar chemical properties, (4) show a gradual change in physical properties (boiling point, melting point, viscosity, density increase with chain length — because van der Waals forces increase with molecular mass). Examples of series: alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, alcohols, carboxylic acids. Studying one member of a series allows prediction of properties of others.

The IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) system names organic compounds systematically. Steps: (1) Identify the longest carbon chain — this gives the parent name (1C=meth, 2C=eth, 3C=prop, 4C=but, 5C=pent, 6C=hex). (2) Identify the principal functional group and add the suffix (alkane -ane, alkene -ene, alkyne -yne, alcohol -ol, carboxylic acid -oic acid). (3) Number the chain from the end that gives the principal functional group the lowest locant. (4) Name substituents as prefixes (methyl-, ethyl-, chloro-, bromo-) with locant numbers. Example: CH₃-CH(CH₃)-CH₂-CH₃ is 2-methylbutane.

Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ and contain only single C–C and C–H bonds (saturated). They are relatively unreactive due to the strength of these bonds. Key members: methane (CH₄), ethane (C₂H₆), propane (C₃H₈), butane (C₄H₁₀). Typical reactions: (1) Combustion: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O (complete); CH₄ + O₂ → CO + 2H₂O (incomplete, produces toxic CO). (2) Substitution (halogenation in UV light): CH₄ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + HCl (chloromethane); this is a free radical chain reaction. Alkanes do not decolourise bromine water or KMnO₄ solution.

Alkenes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ and contain at least one C=C double bond (unsaturated). Key members: ethene (C₂H₄, ethylene), propene (C₃H₆). The C=C double bond makes alkenes much more reactive than alkanes. Typical reactions (all addition across the double bond): (1) Addition of H₂ (hydrogenation): C₂H₄ + H₂ → C₂H₆ (Ni catalyst, heat). (2) Addition of Br₂ (halogenation): C₂H₄ + Br₂ → C₂H₄Br₂ (1,2-dibromoethane) — decolourises bromine water (diagnostic test for unsaturation). (3) Addition of HCl: C₂H₄ + HCl → C₂H₅Cl. (4) Addition of H₂O (hydration): C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH (ethanol; H₃PO₄ catalyst). (5) Polymerisation: nCH₂=CH₂ → [–CH₂–CH₂–]ₙ (polyethylene).

Alkynes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₋₂ and contain at least one C≡C triple bond. The simplest alkyne is ethyne (C₂H₂, acetylene: HC≡CH). Ethyne is prepared by the action of water on calcium carbide: CaC₂ + 2H₂O → Ca(OH)₂ + C₂H₂. Like alkenes, alkynes undergo addition reactions (e.g., C₂H₂ + 2H₂ → C₂H₆; C₂H₂ + Br₂ → C₂H₂Br₂, further to C₂H₂Br₄); they also decolourise bromine water. Ethyne burns with a bright luminous flame and, when mixed with oxygen, produces the oxyacetylene flame (3500°C) used in welding and cutting metal.

Alcohols contain the hydroxyl functional group (–OH). Ethanol (C₂H₅OH, CH₃CH₂OH) is the most important alcohol. Preparation: (1) Fermentation: glucose (from starch/sugars) + yeast (enzymes) → ethanol + CO₂; C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂ (anaerobic process, 25–37°C); alcohol concentration limited to ~15% as higher concentrations kill yeast. (2) Industrial hydration of ethene: C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH (H₃PO₄ catalyst, 300°C). Properties: miscible with water, flammable (burns with clean blue flame), neutral pH. Reactions: (1) Dehydration with conc. H₂SO₄ at 170°C: C₂H₅OH → C₂H₄ + H₂O (gives ethene). (2) Oxidation (by K₂Cr₂O₇/H⁺): C₂H₅OH → CH₃CHO → CH₃COOH (ethanoic acid). Uses: solvent, antiseptic, fuel (bioethanol), beverages.

Carboxylic acids contain the carboxyl group (–COOH). Ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH, acetic acid) is the most familiar carboxylic acid — it is the acid in vinegar (5% solution). General formula for carboxylic acids: CₙH₂ₙ₊₁COOH. Ethanoic acid is a weak acid (partially dissociates: CH₃COOH ⇌ CH₃COO⁻ + H⁺). It reacts with metals (Mg + 2CH₃COOH → Mg(CH₃COO)₂ + H₂), with bases (neutralisation), with carbonates (releasing CO₂), and with alcohols (esterification): CH₃COOH + C₂H₅OH ⇌ CH₃COOC₂H₅ + H₂O (ethyl ethanoate, a fruity-smelling ester; conc. H₂SO₄ catalyst). Esterification is a reversible reaction.

Polymers are large molecules (macromolecules) made by joining many small repeating units (monomers) through polymerisation. Addition polymers form from alkenes by opening the double bond: (1) Polyethylene (polythene): nCH₂=CH₂ → [–CH₂–CH₂–]ₙ; uses — plastic bags, films. (2) Polyvinyl chloride (PVC): nCH₂=CHCl → [–CH₂–CHCl–]ₙ; uses — pipes, insulation, flooring. Condensation polymers form by eliminating small molecules (H₂O or HCl): Nylon-6,6 (condensation of hexanedioic acid and hexane-1,6-diamine — amide bonds form with loss of H₂O); Terylene/polyester (from benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid and ethane-1,2-diol). Natural polymers: starch (glucose monomers), proteins (amino acid monomers), rubber (isoprene monomers).

Isomers are compounds with the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements, giving them different physical or chemical properties. Structural isomers differ in connectivity of atoms. For example, butane (C₄H₁₀) has two structural isomers: n-butane (CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₃, straight chain) and isobutane/2-methylpropane (CH₃CH(CH₃)CH₃, branched chain). Isomerism is possible only from carbon number 4 onwards for alkanes. Branched-chain isomers have lower boiling points than their straight-chain counterparts because branching reduces surface contact area and hence van der Waals forces.

laws_and_formulas.name: General Formulae of Homologous Series
laws_and_formulas.formula: Alkanes: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ | Alkenes: CₙH₂ₙ | Alkynes: CₙH₂ₙ₋₂ | Alcohols: CₙH₂ₙ₊₁OH | Carboxylic acids: CₙH₂ₙ₊₁COOH
laws_and_formulas.description: These general formulae allow the molecular formula of any member of each homologous series to be determined if the value of n (number of carbon atoms) is known. Each successive member differs by CH₂ (14 mass units).

laws_and_formulas.name: Combustion of Hydrocarbons
laws_and_formulas.formula: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ + (3n+1)/2 O₂ → nCO₂ + (n+1)H₂O (complete combustion of alkanes)
laws_and_formulas.description: Complete combustion of hydrocarbons in excess oxygen produces only CO₂ and H₂O. Incomplete combustion (insufficient O₂) produces CO (toxic) and carbon (soot). Example: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O; C₂H₆ + 7/2 O₂ → 2CO₂ + 3H₂O.

laws_and_formulas.name: Fermentation of Glucose
laws_and_formulas.description: Glucose (from starch via amylase) is anaerobically converted to ethanol and carbon dioxide by zymase enzymes in yeast. Conditions: ~25–37°C, absence of oxygen, yeast. The process is limited to ~15% ethanol concentration (yeast dies above this level). Industrial production of ethanol for beverages and biofuel uses this reaction.

laws_and_formulas.name: Dehydration of Ethanol
laws_and_formulas.description: Concentrated sulphuric acid catalyses the removal of water from ethanol (intramolecular dehydration) at 170°C to give ethene. At 140°C with excess alcohol, the product is diethyl ether (intermolecular dehydration). This is the reverse of the hydration of ethene.

laws_and_formulas.name: Esterification
laws_and_formulas.formula: CH₃COOH + C₂H₅OH ⇌ CH₃COOC₂H₅ + H₂O (conc. H₂SO₄ catalyst, reversible)
laws_and_formulas.description: An acid and an alcohol react in the presence of concentrated sulphuric acid (catalyst) to form an ester (with fruity smell) and water. The reaction is reversible (equilibrium), and yield is improved by removing water or using excess of one reactant. Esters are used as flavouring agents, solvents, and perfumes.

key_points: Carbon's uniqueness: tetravalency (4 bonds), catenation (C–C chains), ability to form C=C and C≡C bonds, and allotropy (diamond, graphite, fullerenes).
Homologous series: same general formula, each member differs by CH₂, similar chemical properties, gradual change in physical properties.
Alkanes (CₙH₂ₙ₊₂): saturated, undergo substitution with halogens in UV light (free radical mechanism), do NOT decolourise bromine water.
Alkenes (CₙH₂ₙ): unsaturated (C=C), undergo addition reactions (hydrogenation, halogenation, hydration, polymerisation), DECOLOURISE bromine water — key test for unsaturation.
Alkynes (CₙH₂ₙ₋₂): unsaturated (C≡C), also undergo addition reactions and decolourise bromine water; ethyne is prepared from CaC₂ + H₂O.
Ethanol production: fermentation (C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂; yeast, ~30°C) or industrial hydration of ethene (C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH; H₃PO₄ catalyst).
Dehydration of ethanol: C₂H₅OH → C₂H₄ + H₂O (conc. H₂SO₄, 170°C) — reversal of ethene hydration.
Ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH): weak acid present in vinegar; reacts with alcohols to form esters (esterification, conc. H₂SO₄ catalyst, reversible).
Addition polymer formation: monomers (alkenes) open their double bonds and join in a chain; e.g., polyethylene from ethene, PVC from vinyl chloride.
Condensation polymers (nylon, terylene) form with elimination of small molecules (H₂O); natural polymers include starch, proteins, and rubber.
Structural isomers: same molecular formula, different structural connectivity; e.g., n-butane and 2-methylpropane (both C₄H₁₀); branched isomers have lower boiling points.

10|biology|Basic_Biology_Chromosomes_Str.overview: Chromosomes are thread-like structures found in the nucleus of eukaryotic cells, composed of DNA tightly coiled around histone proteins. They carry the genetic information that determines the traits and characteristics of an organism. In humans, the diploid number is 46 chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs, with 22 pairs of autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes. Chromosomes are best observed during the metaphase stage of cell division when they are maximally condensed. Different organisms have characteristic chromosome numbers, and abnormalities in chromosome number or structure can lead to genetic disorders.

key_concepts.definition: A chromosome is a thread-like structure composed of DNA and histone proteins found in the nucleus of a cell. Each chromosome carries genes that code for specific traits. Humans have 46 chromosomes in their somatic cells arranged in 23 homologous pairs.

Chromatin is the complex of DNA and proteins (primarily histones) that forms chromosomes within the nucleus of eukaryotic cells. During interphase, chromatin exists in a loosely packed form called euchromatin, while tightly packed regions are called heterochromatin. During cell division, chromatin condenses into visible chromosomes.

The centromere is a specialized region of the chromosome where the two sister chromatids are joined together and where the spindle fibers attach during cell division. The position of the centromere determines the shape of the chromosome during metaphase and classifies chromosomes into metacentric, submetacentric, acrocentric, and telocentric types.

The diploid number (2n) refers to the full set of chromosomes found in somatic (body) cells, while the haploid number (n) is half this number found in gametes (sex cells). In humans, 2n = 46 and n = 23. Fertilization restores the diploid number when egg and sperm fuse.

Autosomes are chromosomes that are identical in both males and females and do not determine the sex of an individual. Humans possess 22 pairs of autosomes. These chromosomes carry genes for general body characteristics and non-sex-linked traits.

Sex chromosomes, also called allosomes, are chromosomes that determine the biological sex of an organism. In humans, females have two X chromosomes (XX) while males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY). The Y chromosome carries the SRY gene that triggers male development.

A chromatid is one of the two identical copies of a duplicated chromosome that are joined at the centromere. Before cell division, each chromosome replicates to form two sister chromatids. During anaphase, the chromatids separate and move to opposite poles of the cell.

A karyotype is the complete set of chromosomes of an organism arranged in homologous pairs in order of decreasing size. Karyotyping is a diagnostic technique used to detect chromosomal abnormalities such as Down syndrome (trisomy 21), Turner syndrome (45,X), and Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY).

Telomeres are repetitive DNA sequences found at the ends of chromosomes that protect them from degradation and fusion with other chromosomes. They function like the plastic tips on shoelaces. Telomeres shorten with each cell division and their progressive shortening is associated with cellular aging.

key_points: Human somatic cells contain 46 chromosomes (2n = 46) arranged in 23 homologous pairs
Chromosomes are best seen during metaphase when they are maximally condensed
The centromere divides the chromosome into two arms called the p (short) arm and q (long) arm
Metacentric chromosomes have the centromere in the middle, submetacentric chromosomes have it off-center
Acrocentric chromosomes have the centromere near one end, telocentric chromosomes have it at the tip
Sex is determined by sex chromosomes: XX = female, XY = male in humans
Chromosomes consist of DNA double helix wound around histone octamers to form nucleosomes
Abnormalities in chromosome number are called aneuploidy (e.g., trisomy, monosomy)
Down syndrome results from trisomy 21 (three copies of chromosome 21)
Each chromosome is composed of a single continuous DNA molecule that may be several centimeters long when uncoiled
