10|biology|Basic_Biology_Genetics_Mendel.overview: Genetics is the branch of biology that studies heredity and variation in living organisms. Gregor Mendel, an Austrian monk, conducted pioneering experiments on garden peas (Pisum sativum) in the 1860s and formulated the fundamental laws of inheritance. His work established that traits are controlled by discrete units called genes, which exist in alternative forms called alleles. Mendel proposed two laws: the Law of Segregation and the Law of Independent Assortment. These laws explain how traits are passed from parents to offspring and form the basis of modern genetics. Mendels work was rediscovered in 1900 and has been foundational to our understanding of heredity.

key_concepts.definition: A gene is the basic unit of heredity, consisting of a specific sequence of DNA that codes for a particular protein or trait. Genes are located at specific positions (loci) on chromosomes. Each gene may exist in different forms called alleles, which can produce different versions of a trait.

An allele is one of two or more alternative forms of a gene that occupy the same locus on homologous chromosomes. For example, the gene for seed color in peas has two alleles: yellow (dominant) and green (recessive). An organism inherits one allele from each parent for each gene.

A dominant allele is one that expresses its trait even when only one copy is present (heterozygous condition). A recessive allele only expresses its trait when two copies are present (homozygous condition). Mendel denoted dominant alleles with uppercase letters and recessive alleles with lowercase letters.

An organism is homozygous for a gene if it carries two identical alleles (e.g., TT or tt), and heterozygous if it carries two different alleles (e.g., Tt). Homozygous dominant (TT) and heterozygous (Tt) individuals show the dominant phenotype, while only homozygous recessive (tt) individuals show the recessive phenotype.

Genotype refers to the genetic constitution of an organism, i.e., the specific alleles it carries for a trait (e.g., Tt for height). Phenotype refers to the observable physical characteristics of an organism that result from the interaction of genotype with the environment (e.g., tall plant). Two organisms can have different genotypes but the same phenotype.

Mendels First Law states that during the formation of gametes, the two alleles of a gene separate so that each gamete receives only one allele. This ensures that offspring receive one allele from each parent. The ratio of dominant to recessive phenotypes in an F2 cross of monohybrids is 3:1.

Mendels Second Law states that genes for different traits located on different chromosomes are distributed independently into gametes. This means the inheritance of one trait does not affect the inheritance of another. A dihybrid cross (RrYy x RrYy) produces offspring in a 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio.

A monohybrid cross involves the study of inheritance of a single pair of contrasting traits. When two heterozygous parents (Tt x Tt) are crossed, the offspring show a 3:1 phenotypic ratio and a 1:2:1 genotypic ratio (TT:Tt:tt). This cross demonstrates the Law of Segregation.

A Punnett square is a diagram used to predict the probability of possible genotypes and phenotypes of offspring from a genetic cross. It was developed by Reginald Crundall Punnett. Alleles from each parent are placed along the top and side of the square, and all possible combinations are filled in the boxes.

A test cross is performed to determine the unknown genotype of an organism showing the dominant phenotype by crossing it with a homozygous recessive individual (tt). If all offspring show the dominant trait, the parent is homozygous dominant (TT); if half show dominant and half recessive, the parent is heterozygous (Tt).

key_points: Mendel used garden peas (Pisum sativum) for his experiments because of their distinct contrasting traits and short generation time
Mendel studied 7 pairs of contrasting traits including seed shape, seed color, pod shape, pod color, flower color, flower position, and stem height
The F1 generation (first filial generation) from a cross between pure breeding parents shows only the dominant trait
Self-fertilization of F1 plants produces F2 generation with a 3:1 ratio of dominant to recessive phenotypes
The Law of Segregation is also called the Law of Purity of Gametes
Dihybrid cross ratio in F2 generation is 9:3:3:1 (dominant both : dominant first recessive second : recessive first dominant second : recessive both)
Mendels laws apply to genes on different chromosomes; linked genes on the same chromosome do not follow independent assortment
Incomplete dominance occurs when neither allele is completely dominant, producing a blended intermediate phenotype in heterozygotes
Codominance occurs when both alleles are equally expressed in the heterozygote (e.g., AB blood group)
Multiple alleles exist for some genes, such as the ABO blood group system with alleles IA, IB, and i

10|biology|Basic_Biology_Cell_Division_M.overview: Mitosis is a type of cell division in which a parent cell divides to produce two daughter cells that are genetically identical to the parent cell, each with the same number of chromosomes. This process is essential for growth, repair, and replacement of cells in multicellular organisms. Mitosis consists of four distinct phases: prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase, collectively called karyokinesis, followed by cytoplasmic division called cytokinesis. The entire process ensures accurate distribution of genetic material to daughter cells. Mitosis occurs in somatic (body) cells and in asexual reproduction, maintaining the diploid chromosome number.

key_concepts.definition: Mitosis is a form of eukaryotic cell division that produces two genetically identical daughter cells, each containing the same number and types of chromosomes as the parent cell. It is the primary mechanism for growth, tissue repair, and asexual reproduction. Mitosis is preceded by DNA replication during the S phase of interphase.

Prophase is the first and longest stage of mitosis during which chromatin condenses into visible chromosomes, each consisting of two sister chromatids joined at the centromere. The nuclear envelope begins to dissolve, the nucleolus disappears, and the mitotic spindle begins to form from the centrioles in animal cells.

Metaphase is the stage of mitosis during which chromosomes are maximally condensed and align along the equatorial plate (metaphase plate) at the center of the cell. Spindle fibers from opposite poles attach to the centromeres of each chromosome. This is the best stage to observe and count chromosomes for karyotyping.

Anaphase is the stage of mitosis during which the centromeres split and sister chromatids are pulled to opposite poles of the cell by shortening spindle fibers. Each pole receives an identical set of chromosomes. The cell elongates as the poles move apart. At the end of anaphase, each pole has the same chromosome number as the original parent cell.

Telophase is the final stage of mitosis during which chromosomes arrive at the poles and begin to decondense back into chromatin. The nuclear envelope reforms around each set of chromosomes, and the nucleolus reappears. The mitotic spindle disassembles, resulting in two nuclei within one cell.

Cytokinesis is the division of the cytoplasm that follows karyokinesis (nuclear division) to produce two separate daughter cells. In animal cells, cytokinesis occurs by cleavage furrow formation that pinches the cell in two. In plant cells, a cell plate forms in the middle of the cell and grows outward to divide the cell.

The spindle apparatus is a structure composed of microtubules that forms during cell division and is responsible for separating chromosomes to opposite poles. Spindle fibers attach to chromosomes at the kinetochore region of the centromere. In animal cells, the spindle forms between two centrioles; plant cells lack centrioles but still form spindle fibers.

Mitosis maintains genetic continuity by producing daughter cells identical to the parent cell. It is responsible for growth from a single fertilized egg to a multicellular organism. It repairs and replaces damaged or worn-out cells (e.g., skin cells, blood cells). It is also used in asexual reproduction and regeneration in organisms like Hydra and Planaria.

key_points: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells from one parent cell, maintaining the diploid chromosome number (2n)
The stages of mitosis in order are: Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase (PMAT)
DNA replication occurs during S phase of interphase before mitosis begins
Chromosomes are most visible and distinct during metaphase when they align at the equatorial plate
Sister chromatids separate during anaphase when centromeres split and spindle fibers shorten
Animal cells divide by cleavage furrow; plant cells form a cell plate during cytokinesis
Centrioles are present in animal cells and help organize the spindle; plant cells lack centrioles
Each daughter cell receives an exact copy of all chromosomes from the parent cell
Mitosis is responsible for growth, repair, and replacement of cells in multicellular organisms
Uncontrolled mitosis leads to tumor formation and cancer

10|biology|Basic_Biology_Cell_Division_M.overview: Meiosis is a specialized type of cell division that produces four haploid daughter cells from a single diploid parent cell, each containing half the chromosome number of the parent. This process is essential for sexual reproduction and occurs in the gonads (testes and ovaries in animals, and reproductive organs in plants). Meiosis consists of two successive divisions: Meiosis I and Meiosis II, each with prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase stages. Meiosis I is the reductional division that separates homologous chromosomes, while Meiosis II is equational and separates sister chromatids. Crossing over during Prophase I and independent assortment generate genetic variation in offspring.

key_concepts.definition: Meiosis is a type of cell division that reduces the chromosome number by half, producing four haploid (n) gametes from one diploid (2n) parent cell. It involves two sequential divisions (Meiosis I and II) without an intervening DNA replication. Meiosis is the basis of sexual reproduction and contributes to genetic variation through crossing over and independent assortment.

Meiosis I is the first division of meiosis, called the reductional division, because homologous chromosome pairs (bivalents) separate, reducing the chromosome number from diploid to haploid. The key event of Meiosis I is the pairing of homologous chromosomes (synapsis) and crossing over during Prophase I. At the end of Meiosis I, two haploid cells are produced, each with duplicated chromosomes.

Meiosis II is the second division of meiosis, similar to mitosis, in which sister chromatids separate. It is called the equational division because the chromosome number does not change. Meiosis II produces four haploid cells from the two cells produced by Meiosis I. Each resulting cell contains one chromosome from each homologous pair.

Prophase I is the longest and most complex phase of meiosis, during which homologous chromosomes pair up (synapsis) to form bivalents. Crossing over occurs between non-sister chromatids at points called chiasmata, exchanging segments of DNA and creating new combinations of alleles. Prophase I has five sub-stages: Leptotene, Zygotene, Pachytene, Diplotene, and Diakinesis.

Synapsis is the pairing of homologous chromosomes during Prophase I of meiosis, held together by a protein structure called the synaptonemal complex. Each pair of synapsed homologous chromosomes (one from each parent) forms a structure called a bivalent or tetrad, consisting of four chromatids total. Synapsis enables crossing over to occur between homologous chromosomes.

Crossing over is the exchange of segments of DNA between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes during Prophase I of meiosis. The points of exchange are called chiasmata (singular: chiasma). Crossing over creates new combinations of alleles (recombinant chromosomes), increasing genetic diversity in offspring.

Meiosis maintains the chromosome number constant across generations in sexually reproducing organisms by producing haploid gametes that fuse during fertilization. It is the primary source of genetic variation through crossing over and random assortment of homologous chromosomes. Genetic variation generated by meiosis is the raw material for evolution and natural selection.

Meiosis differs from mitosis in several key ways: meiosis involves two divisions while mitosis involves one; meiosis produces four haploid cells while mitosis produces two diploid cells; meiosis results in genetic variation through crossing over while mitosis produces genetically identical cells; meiosis occurs only in reproductive organs while mitosis occurs in somatic cells throughout the body.

key_points: Meiosis reduces the chromosome number from diploid (2n) to haploid (n), producing four genetically different cells
Meiosis I is the reductional division; Meiosis II is the equational division similar to mitosis
Crossing over occurs during Pachytene of Prophase I at points called chiasmata
The five sub-stages of Prophase I are Leptotene, Zygotene, Pachytene, Diplotene, and Diakinesis
Bivalents (tetrads) formed by paired homologous chromosomes align at the metaphase plate during Metaphase I
In Meiosis I, homologous chromosomes separate; in Meiosis II, sister chromatids separate
Meiosis occurs in gonads: testes produce sperm, ovaries produce eggs in animals
Independent assortment of non-homologous chromosomes during Metaphase I creates 2^23 (over 8 million) possible chromosome combinations in human gametes
Errors in meiosis can lead to aneuploidy (wrong chromosome number) in gametes, causing conditions like Down syndrome when such gametes are fertilized
Spermatogenesis produces 4 functional sperm from one primary spermatocyte; oogenesis produces 1 egg and 3 polar bodies

10|biology|Basic_Biology_Cell_Cycle.overview: The cell cycle is the ordered series of events that a cell undergoes from its formation to its division into two daughter cells. It is divided into two main phases: interphase and the mitotic phase (M phase). Interphase is the longest phase, comprising G1 (first gap), S (synthesis), and G2 (second gap) phases, during which the cell grows and replicates its DNA. The M phase includes mitosis (nuclear division) and cytokinesis (cytoplasmic division). The cell cycle is tightly regulated by checkpoints that ensure accurate DNA replication and chromosome segregation. Disruption of cell cycle control can lead to uncontrolled cell division and cancer.

key_concepts.definition: The cell cycle is the sequence of events by which a cell grows, replicates its DNA, and divides into two daughter cells. It consists of interphase (G1, S, G2 phases) and the M phase (mitosis and cytokinesis). The complete cell cycle duration varies by cell type, from about 10 hours in rapidly dividing cells to years in neurons that rarely divide.

Interphase is the longest phase of the cell cycle, during which the cell is not dividing but is actively growing and preparing for division. It consists of three sub-phases: G1 (cell growth and protein synthesis), S phase (DNA replication), and G2 (further growth and preparation for division). During interphase, the chromosomes are not visible as they exist as loosely packed chromatin.

The G1 (Gap 1) phase is the first phase of interphase during which the cell grows in size, synthesizes proteins and RNA, and carries out its normal functions. The cell increases its cytoplasmic contents, including organelles and ribosomes, to prepare for DNA replication. A critical checkpoint called the restriction point (G1 checkpoint) determines whether the cell will proceed with division.

The S (Synthesis) phase is the phase of interphase during which DNA replication occurs. Each chromosome is duplicated to produce two identical sister chromatids joined at the centromere. The amount of DNA in the cell doubles during this phase. Histone proteins are also synthesized during S phase to package the newly replicated DNA.

The G2 (Gap 2) phase follows DNA replication and is the period during which the cell continues to grow and prepares for mitosis. The cell synthesizes proteins required for cell division, such as tubulin for spindle formation. A G2 checkpoint verifies that DNA replication is complete and that no DNA damage is present before the cell enters mitosis.

Cell cycle checkpoints are regulatory mechanisms that monitor the accuracy of cell cycle events before the cell proceeds to the next phase. The three major checkpoints are: G1 checkpoint (checks DNA integrity and cell size), G2 checkpoint (checks DNA replication completion), and M checkpoint or spindle assembly checkpoint (checks chromosome attachment to spindle fibers). Checkpoint failure can lead to mutations and cancer.

The G0 phase is a resting state in which a cell exits the cell cycle temporarily or permanently and ceases to divide. Cells in G0 are metabolically active but not preparing for division. Some cells like mature neurons and muscle cells remain in G0 permanently, while others like liver cells can re-enter the cycle when stimulated.

The cell cycle is regulated by proteins called cyclins and cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs). Cyclins are regulatory proteins whose concentration rises and falls at specific stages of the cell cycle. CDKs are enzymes that become active when bound to cyclins and drive cell cycle progression by phosphorylating target proteins. Different cyclin-CDK complexes regulate different phases of the cell cycle.

key_points: The cell cycle consists of interphase (G1, S, G2) and M phase (mitosis + cytokinesis)
DNA replication occurs during the S phase of interphase
During interphase, chromosomes are not visible as they exist as diffuse chromatin
The cell cycle duration varies: HeLa cells divide in about 24 hours; some liver cells can take months
Three checkpoints control the cell cycle: G1/S checkpoint, G2/M checkpoint, and spindle assembly checkpoint
Cells that are not dividing enter a resting state called G0 phase
Mature neurons and skeletal muscle cells are permanently in G0 and do not divide
Cyclins and CDKs are the key molecular regulators of cell cycle progression
Mutations in cell cycle regulator genes (proto-oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes) can cause cancer
p53 is a crucial tumor suppressor protein that halts the cell cycle when DNA damage is detected
The spindle assembly checkpoint ensures all chromosomes are properly attached before anaphase begins

10|biology|Plant_Physiology_Absorption_b.overview: Water and mineral absorption by plant roots is a fundamental process that sustains all plant life. Roots absorb water and dissolved minerals from the soil through osmosis and active transport mechanisms. The root hair cells are specialized for absorption, having a large surface area and thin walls for efficient uptake. Osmosis drives water movement from soil (high water potential) into root cells (lower water potential), while minerals are absorbed against their concentration gradient by active transport requiring energy. Water then moves through the root cortex via the apoplast (through cell walls) and symplast (through cytoplasm) pathways before entering the xylem vessels for upward transport.

key_concepts.definition: Osmosis is the movement of water molecules from a region of higher water potential (or lower solute concentration) to a region of lower water potential (or higher solute concentration) through a selectively permeable membrane. In plants, water moves from soil into root hair cells by osmosis because the cell sap has a higher solute concentration than the dilute soil solution. Osmosis is a passive process requiring no energy.

Diffusion is the movement of molecules or ions from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration along a concentration gradient. In plants, gases like carbon dioxide and oxygen diffuse through stomata. Dissolved minerals may diffuse into root cells when present in higher concentration outside than inside, though most mineral uptake requires active transport.

Osmotic pressure is the pressure that must be applied to a solution to prevent the inward flow of water by osmosis through a semipermeable membrane. It is directly proportional to the concentration of solutes in the solution. Cells with higher solute concentration have higher osmotic pressure and tend to absorb water from surrounding cells.

Turgor pressure is the pressure exerted by the fluid (cell sap) inside a plant cell against the cell wall. When a plant cell absorbs water, it swells and the cell wall exerts an inward pressure (wall pressure) that counteracts further water entry. Turgor pressure keeps plant cells rigid and maintains the structural support of non-woody plant parts.

Root hair cells are elongated extensions of epidermal cells near the root tip that dramatically increase the surface area available for water and mineral absorption. They are thin-walled, have a large vacuole with concentrated cell sap, and are in close contact with soil particles and water. A single plant may have billions of root hairs collectively providing enormous absorptive surface area.

Water moves through the root cortex by two pathways: the apoplast pathway (through cell walls and intercellular spaces, bypassing cell membranes) and the symplast pathway (through the cytoplasm and plasmodesmata connections between cells). At the endodermis, the Casparian strip (waterproof suberin layer) blocks apoplast movement, forcing all water and solutes through the symplast and membrane.

Active transport is the movement of ions and molecules against their concentration gradient, requiring energy (ATP) and carrier proteins. Plant roots absorb essential mineral ions (like nitrates, phosphates, potassium) from the soil by active transport even when their concentration in the soil is lower than inside the cell. This process is carried out by the root hair cells using metabolic energy.

Plasmolysis is the shrinkage of the cytoplasm away from the cell wall of a plant cell when placed in a hypertonic solution. Water leaves the cell by osmosis, causing the cell membrane and cytoplasm to pull away from the rigid cell wall. The cell becomes flaccid and loses turgor. Plasmolysis is reversible when the cell is returned to a hypotonic solution, a process called deplasmolysis.

key_points: Water moves into root hair cells by osmosis from soil (high water potential) to cell sap (low water potential)
Minerals are absorbed by active transport, requiring ATP energy, against the concentration gradient
Root hair cells increase the surface area of roots for efficient absorption
The Casparian strip in the endodermis is a waterproof band of suberin that forces water through cell membranes
Osmosis is the diffusion of water through a selectively (semi-) permeable membrane
A turgid cell has high turgor pressure; a flaccid cell has lost water and turgor
Plasmolysis occurs when plant cells are placed in concentrated salt or sugar solutions (hypertonic solutions)
The apoplast pathway moves water through cell walls; the symplast pathway moves water through cytoplasm
Wilting occurs when plants lose water faster than they can absorb it, reducing turgor pressure
Soil water concentration must be greater than that of root cell sap for osmotic absorption to occur

10|biology|Plant_Physiology_Photosynthes.overview: Photosynthesis is the fundamental biological process by which green plants, algae, and some bacteria use light energy to convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen. This process takes place in the chloroplasts, primarily in the palisade mesophyll cells of leaves. Photosynthesis has two main stages: the light-dependent reactions (occurring in the thylakoid membranes) and the light-independent reactions or Calvin cycle (occurring in the stroma). The overall equation is: 6CO2 + 6H2O + light energy yields C6H12O6 + 6O2. Photosynthesis is the primary source of energy for virtually all life on Earth and is responsible for the oxygen in our atmosphere.

key_concepts.definition: Chlorophyll is the primary photosynthetic pigment found in the chloroplasts of plant cells that absorbs light energy for photosynthesis. It strongly absorbs red and blue-violet light but reflects green light, which is why plants appear green. Two main types exist: chlorophyll a (primary pigment) and chlorophyll b (accessory pigment). Chlorophyll is located in the thylakoid membranes of chloroplasts.

The chloroplast is the organelle in plant cells where photosynthesis occurs. It has a double membrane envelope and contains an internal system of flattened membrane sacs called thylakoids, which are stacked into grana. The fluid-filled space surrounding the thylakoids is called the stroma. Light reactions occur in the thylakoid membranes; the Calvin cycle occurs in the stroma.

The light-dependent reactions occur in the thylakoid membranes and require light energy to proceed. Light energy is absorbed by chlorophyll and used to split water molecules (photolysis), releasing oxygen as a byproduct. The energy captured is used to produce ATP and NADPH, which power the Calvin cycle. These reactions occur in two photosystems: Photosystem I and Photosystem II.

The Calvin cycle (light-independent reactions or dark reactions) occurs in the stroma of chloroplasts and uses ATP and NADPH produced by light reactions to fix atmospheric CO2 into organic molecules. Carbon dioxide is attached to a 5-carbon compound (RuBP) by the enzyme RuBisCO, and through a series of reactions, glucose is ultimately produced. Three turns of the cycle produce one molecule of glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (G3P), a precursor to glucose.

Photolysis is the light-driven splitting of water molecules in the thylakoids during the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis. The equation is: 2H2O + light energy yields 4H+ + 4e- + O2. The oxygen produced is released as a byproduct through stomata. The hydrogen ions and electrons are used to reduce NADP+ to NADPH, which powers the Calvin cycle.

A limiting factor is any environmental condition that, when in short supply, limits the rate of photosynthesis even if other conditions are favorable. The three main limiting factors are light intensity, carbon dioxide concentration, and temperature. At low light intensity, increasing light increases the rate of photosynthesis; at high light intensity, CO2 concentration becomes the limiting factor.

Stomata are tiny pores found mainly on the lower epidermis of leaves, each surrounded by two guard cells that control the opening and closing of the pore. Carbon dioxide enters and oxygen exits through stomata during photosynthesis. Stomata open during the day when guard cells become turgid due to potassium ion uptake, and close at night to prevent water loss.

The primary products of photosynthesis are glucose and oxygen. Glucose serves as the main energy source for plants and, through food chains, for most living organisms. It is also the raw material for synthesizing other organic molecules like starch, cellulose, proteins, and fats. Photosynthesis replenishes atmospheric oxygen and removes CO2, making it essential for life on Earth.

key_points: Overall equation: 6CO2 + 6H2O + light energy yields C6H12O6 + 6O2
Photosynthesis occurs in two stages: light reactions (in thylakoids) and Calvin cycle (in stroma)
Chlorophyll absorbs red and blue-violet light; reflects green light
Water is the source of oxygen released during photosynthesis (proved by isotope labeling experiments)
Light-dependent reactions produce ATP, NADPH, and O2; Calvin cycle uses ATP and NADPH to fix CO2 into glucose
The three limiting factors of photosynthesis are light intensity, CO2 concentration, and temperature
Stomata open in light and close in dark to regulate gas exchange and water loss
Chlorophyll a is the primary photosynthetic pigment; chlorophyll b, carotenes, and xanthophylls are accessory pigments
Leaf adaptations for photosynthesis: large flat surface, thin lamina, chlorophyll in palisade cells, stomata for gas exchange
Variegated leaves experiment demonstrates that only green parts (with chlorophyll) can photosynthesize

10|biology|Plant_Physiology_Transpiratio.overview: Transpiration is the process by which water vapor is lost from the aerial parts of a plant, particularly through the stomata of leaves. It is often described as a necessary evil because while it drives the absorption of water and minerals from the soil, excessive transpiration can cause wilting. Transpiration creates a transpiration pull that is a major force in drawing water upward through the xylem vessels from roots to leaves. The rate of transpiration is influenced by environmental factors including light, temperature, humidity, wind speed, and soil water availability. Stomata are the primary sites of transpiration and are regulated by guard cells.

key_concepts.definition: Transpiration is the evaporation of water from the surface of plant cells followed by diffusion of water vapor through the stomata into the atmosphere. It occurs mainly through stomata (stomatal transpiration), accounting for about 90% of total water loss, with smaller amounts lost through the cuticle (cuticular transpiration) and lenticels (lenticular transpiration).

Transpiration pull is the main force that draws water upward through the xylem from roots to leaves. As water evaporates from mesophyll cells into air spaces and exits through stomata, it creates tension in the water column. This tension is transmitted down through continuous water columns in xylem vessels, pulling water upward. This is part of the cohesion-tension theory of water ascent.

Guard cells are specialized kidney-shaped cells that flank each stoma and regulate its opening and closing. When guard cells take up potassium ions (K+) in the presence of light, water follows by osmosis, making the cells turgid and bowing them outward to open the stoma. When potassium ions leave in darkness or drought, guard cells lose water, become flaccid, and the stoma closes.

Transpiration rate is influenced by several environmental factors: light (promotes stomatal opening), temperature (increases water vapor pressure), humidity (high humidity reduces the gradient for water vapor loss), wind speed (removes water vapor from leaf surface, increasing gradient), and soil water content (drought causes stomatal closure). Plants in dry environments have adaptations to reduce transpiration.

Wilting is the loss of rigidity in plant stems and leaves due to insufficient water in cells, reducing turgor pressure. It occurs when the rate of transpiration exceeds the rate of water absorption by roots. Temporary wilting during hot afternoons is reversed at night; permanent wilting occurs when soil water is unavailable and causes irreversible cell damage if prolonged.

A potometer is an experimental apparatus used to measure the rate of water uptake by a cut shoot, which approximates the rate of transpiration. A capillary tube with an air bubble allows measurement of water uptake over time. The potometer measures water uptake, not transpiration directly, but the two are closely related under most conditions.

Transpiration plays several important roles: it creates the transpiration pull that draws water and dissolved minerals up through the plant; evaporation of water from leaves cools the leaf surface (like sweating in animals); the continuous flow of water transports minerals absorbed by roots throughout the plant. However, excessive transpiration causes water stress and wilting.

Xerophytes are plants adapted to survive in arid conditions by reducing water loss through transpiration. Adaptations include thick, waxy cuticles; sunken stomata; reduced leaf area or leaf modification into spines (as in cacti); water storage in stems or leaves (succulents); CAM metabolism allowing stomata to open only at night; and extensive root systems to maximize water uptake.

key_points: About 90% of transpiration occurs through stomata (stomatal transpiration)
The three types of transpiration are stomatal, cuticular, and lenticular
Transpiration creates a continuous transpiration pull that draws water up through xylem vessels
Guard cells control stomatal opening: turgid guard cells open stomata, flaccid guard cells close them
Stomata open in light (due to K+ uptake by guard cells) and close in darkness
High temperature, low humidity, bright light, and high wind speed increase transpiration rate
A potometer measures water uptake by a cut shoot as an indirect measure of transpiration
Transpiration helps cool leaves through evaporative cooling
Wilting occurs when transpiration rate exceeds absorption rate, reducing turgor pressure
Xerophytes have adaptations like thick cuticles, sunken stomata, and reduced leaves to minimize transpiration
The cohesion-tension theory explains how water moves up tall trees: water molecules stick together (cohesion) and transpiration pull creates tension

10|biology|Plant_Physiology_Chemical_Coo.overview: Plants coordinate their growth and responses to environmental stimuli through chemical messengers called plant hormones or phytohormones. Unlike animals, plants lack a nervous system, so they rely entirely on chemical coordination. The major plant hormones include auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins, abscisic acid, and ethylene, each produced in specific tissues and transported to target areas. These hormones regulate diverse processes including cell elongation, seed germination, flowering, fruit development, and responses to light and gravity. The concept of plant tropisms, directed growth responses to environmental stimuli, is closely linked to hormone action.

key_concepts.definition: Plant hormones are naturally occurring organic compounds produced in small quantities in one part of a plant that are transported to another part where they regulate growth and development. They work by altering gene expression or enzyme activity in target cells. The major plant hormones are auxins (IAA), gibberellins (GAs), cytokinins, abscisic acid (ABA), and ethylene.

Auxins, primarily indole-3-acetic acid (IAA), are plant hormones produced in the apical meristems (shoot tips) that promote cell elongation by causing cell walls to loosen and expand. They control phototropism (bending toward light) and gravitropism. At high concentrations, auxins inhibit root growth while promoting shoot growth. Commercially, synthetic auxins are used as rooting hormones and herbicides.

Gibberellins are plant hormones produced in young leaves, seeds, and roots that promote stem elongation, seed germination, and flowering. They stimulate the production of amylase enzyme in germinating seeds to break down starch reserves. Gibberellin-deficient dwarf plants can be restored to normal height by applying gibberellins. They also break seed and bud dormancy.

Cytokinins are plant hormones produced mainly in roots and young fruits that promote cell division (cytokinesis) and differentiation. They work synergistically with auxins: high cytokinin to auxin ratio promotes shoot formation, while high auxin to cytokinin ratio promotes root formation. Cytokinins delay leaf senescence (aging) and promote lateral bud growth.

Abscisic acid is an inhibitory plant hormone produced in leaves, fruits, and seeds that promotes dormancy, causes stomatal closure during drought, and inhibits growth. Called the stress hormone, ABA accumulates under conditions of water stress and cold temperatures. It promotes seed dormancy and delays germination until conditions are favorable for growth.

Ethylene is a gaseous plant hormone produced in ripening fruits, damaged tissues, and flowers that promotes fruit ripening, leaf and fruit abscission, and flower senescence. It is the only gaseous plant hormone. Ethylene stimulates the production of enzymes that break down cell walls in fruits during ripening. Commercially, ethylene is used to ripen fruits during transport and storage.

Phototropism is the directional growth response of a plant to light stimulus. Shoots show positive phototropism (grow toward light) while roots show negative phototropism (grow away from light). Phototropism is caused by unequal distribution of auxin: in response to unilateral light, auxin moves to the shaded side of the shoot, causing faster elongation there and bending toward light.

Gravitropism (formerly geotropism) is the directional growth response of a plant to gravity. Roots are positively gravitropic (grow toward gravity/downward) and shoots are negatively gravitropic (grow against gravity/upward). The response is mediated by the redistribution of auxin and by statoliths (starch-containing plastids) in root cap cells that detect gravity direction.

key_points: The five major plant hormones are auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins, abscisic acid, and ethylene
Auxin (IAA) is produced in shoot tips and causes cell elongation; it is responsible for phototropism and gravitropism
Gibberellins promote stem elongation and seed germination; used commercially to increase fruit size
Cytokinins promote cell division and delay leaf senescence; produced mainly in roots
Abscisic acid is the stress hormone that causes stomatal closure during drought and promotes dormancy
Ethylene is the only gaseous hormone; promotes fruit ripening and leaf/fruit abscission
Phototropism: shoots grow toward light due to higher auxin concentration on the shaded side
Shoots are negatively gravitropic; roots are positively gravitropic
Auxin promotes shoot growth at low concentrations but inhibits root growth at those same concentrations
Apical dominance is caused by auxin from the apical bud inhibiting lateral bud growth; removal of apical bud promotes lateral branching

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: The human circulatory system is a closed, double circulation system that transports blood, nutrients, oxygen, carbon dioxide, and waste products throughout the body. It consists of the heart, blood vessels (arteries, veins, capillaries), and blood. The heart is a four-chambered muscular pump that maintains two separate circuits: pulmonary circulation (heart to lungs and back) and systemic circulation (heart to body and back). Blood carries oxygen from the lungs to all body cells and returns carbon dioxide to the lungs for elimination. The system also transports hormones, nutrients, immune cells, and helps regulate body temperature.

key_concepts.definition: The human heart is a four-chambered muscular organ located in the thoracic cavity between the lungs, slightly tilted to the left. It consists of two atria (upper chambers) and two ventricles (lower chambers). The right side handles deoxygenated blood while the left side handles oxygenated blood. The heart wall consists of three layers: epicardium (outer), myocardium (muscular middle), and endocardium (inner lining).

Double circulation means blood passes through the heart twice in one complete circuit of the body. In pulmonary circulation, deoxygenated blood flows from the right ventricle to the lungs via the pulmonary artery, picks up oxygen, and returns to the left atrium via the pulmonary vein. In systemic circulation, oxygenated blood flows from the left ventricle to the body via the aorta and returns deoxygenated blood via the vena cava.

The cardiac cycle is the sequence of events in one complete heartbeat, consisting of systole (contraction) and diastole (relaxation). During atrial systole, both atria contract simultaneously, pushing blood into the ventricles. During ventricular systole, both ventricles contract, pumping blood into the pulmonary artery and aorta. The entire cycle lasts about 0.8 seconds at a normal resting heart rate of 72 beats per minute.

The heart contains four valves that ensure one-way blood flow. The bicuspid (mitral) valve is between the left atrium and left ventricle; the tricuspid valve is between the right atrium and right ventricle. The pulmonary semilunar valve controls blood flow into the pulmonary artery; the aortic semilunar valve controls flow into the aorta. Heart sounds (lub-dup) are produced by valve closure.

Arteries are thick-walled vessels that carry blood away from the heart under high pressure. Veins are thin-walled vessels with valves that carry blood toward the heart under low pressure. Capillaries are microscopic vessels with walls one cell thick where exchange of nutrients, gases, and wastes between blood and tissue cells occurs. The portal vein is unique in connecting two capillary beds.

Blood is a fluid connective tissue consisting of plasma (55%) and formed elements (45%). Plasma is a pale yellow fluid containing water, proteins, hormones, glucose, and waste products. Formed elements include red blood cells (erythrocytes) that carry oxygen via hemoglobin, white blood cells (leucocytes) that fight infection, and platelets (thrombocytes) that aid in blood clotting.

The ABO blood group system classifies blood into four types based on the presence of antigens (agglutinogens) on red blood cell surfaces and antibodies (agglutinins) in the plasma. Group A has antigen A and anti-B antibodies; Group B has antigen B and anti-A antibodies; Group AB has both antigens and no antibodies (universal recipient); Group O has no antigens and both antibodies (universal donor).

The lymphatic system is a network of vessels, nodes, and organs that drains excess tissue fluid (lymph) back into the blood circulation. Lymph capillaries collect interstitial fluid and carry it through lymph vessels to lymph nodes, where pathogens and foreign particles are filtered out. The system eventually drains into the subclavian veins. It plays a crucial role in immunity and fat absorption.

key_points: The human heart has four chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle
Oxygenated blood: left side of heart; deoxygenated blood: right side of heart
The right ventricle pumps blood to the lungs via the pulmonary artery (deoxygenated blood)
The left ventricle pumps blood to the body via the aorta (oxygenated blood)
Normal adult heart rate is 60-80 beats per minute; cardiac output is about 5 liters per minute
Arteries carry blood away from the heart; veins carry blood toward the heart
The bicuspid (mitral) valve is between the left atrium and ventricle; tricuspid is on the right
RBC lifespan is about 120 days; they lack a nucleus and are produced in red bone marrow
Blood group O is the universal donor; blood group AB is the universal recipient
The Rh factor (Rh positive or negative) is another important blood grouping system
Pulse is the rhythmic expansion of artery walls caused by ventricular systole; normal pulse rate is 72 per minute

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: Excretion is the process by which metabolic waste products are removed from the body to maintain homeostasis. The human excretory system primarily consists of the kidneys, which filter blood and produce urine containing nitrogenous wastes (mainly urea), excess salts, and water. Each kidney contains about one million functional units called nephrons that perform filtration, reabsorption, and secretion. Other excretory organs include the lungs (excreting CO2 and water vapor), skin (secreting water, salts, and small amounts of urea through sweat), and liver (converting toxic ammonia to urea). The urinary system includes kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder, and urethra.

key_concepts.definition: The kidneys are paired, bean-shaped organs located in the abdominal cavity on either side of the vertebral column. Each kidney has an outer cortex, an inner medulla with pyramid-shaped structures, and a central renal pelvis that collects urine. The renal artery brings blood to the kidney for filtration, and the renal vein carries filtered blood away. The ureter carries urine from each kidney to the bladder.

The nephron is the structural and functional unit of the kidney. Each kidney contains approximately one million nephrons. A nephron consists of the Bowmans capsule (surrounding the glomerulus), proximal convoluted tubule, loop of Henle (descending and ascending limbs), distal convoluted tubule, and collecting duct. The nephron performs ultrafiltration, selective reabsorption, and tubular secretion.

Ultrafiltration is the first step of urine formation occurring in the glomerulus and Bowmans capsule. High blood pressure in the glomerular capillaries forces water, glucose, amino acids, urea, salts, and other small molecules from the blood into the Bowmans capsule, forming the glomerular filtrate. Large molecules like proteins and blood cells cannot pass through and remain in the blood.

Selective reabsorption is the process by which useful substances from the glomerular filtrate are reabsorbed back into the blood. All glucose and amino acids, most water, and much of the salt are reabsorbed in the proximal convoluted tubule, loop of Henle, and distal convoluted tubule. Urea and excess salts are not fully reabsorbed and are excreted in urine. Glucose reabsorption requires active transport and carrier proteins.

Osmoregulation is the regulation of water and salt balance in body fluids to maintain constant blood osmolarity. The kidneys, under hormonal control by ADH (antidiuretic hormone from the pituitary gland), adjust water reabsorption in the collecting duct. ADH increases water permeability of collecting duct, causing more water reabsorption and concentrated urine. Low water intake increases ADH secretion.

Normal urine is a pale yellow liquid composed of about 95% water, 2% urea, and small amounts of uric acid, creatinine, various salts (primarily sodium chloride), and sometimes small amounts of amino acids. Normal urine does not contain glucose, proteins, red blood cells, or bacteria. Presence of these substances indicates disease conditions (glycosuria indicates diabetes; proteinuria indicates kidney damage).

Dialysis is a medical procedure that artificially removes waste products and excess fluid from the blood when the kidneys fail to function adequately. In hemodialysis, blood is passed through a dialysis machine where it is filtered across a selectively permeable membrane, removing urea and other wastes while retaining blood cells and proteins. Peritoneal dialysis uses the peritoneal membrane of the abdomen as the filter.

The skin excretes water, small amounts of urea, and salts through sweat produced by sweat glands. Sweating is primarily a thermoregulatory mechanism but also contributes to excretion. During heavy exercise or hot weather, significant amounts of water and electrolytes can be lost through sweat. The liver also plays an excretory role by converting toxic ammonia to less toxic urea for kidney excretion.

key_points: Excretion removes metabolic waste products: urea (from protein metabolism), CO2 (from respiration), water
Each kidney contains about one million nephrons as functional units
Ultrafiltration occurs in the glomerulus; the filtrate contains glucose, amino acids, urea, salts, and water
All glucose and amino acids are completely reabsorbed in the proximal convoluted tubule
Urea is the main nitrogenous waste in humans; formed in the liver from amino acid metabolism
ADH (antidiuretic hormone) increases water reabsorption in the collecting duct, producing concentrated urine
Normal urine does not contain glucose, protein, or RBCs; their presence indicates disease
The renal cortex contains Bowmans capsules and convoluted tubules; the medulla contains loops of Henle
About 180 liters of filtrate is produced daily by both kidneys, but only 1-1.5 liters is excreted as urine
The lungs excrete CO2 and water vapor; skin excretes water, salts, and trace amounts of urea

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: The human nervous system is a complex network of neurons and supporting cells that coordinates voluntary and involuntary body functions and enables the organism to detect and respond to stimuli. It is divided into the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) and the peripheral nervous system (cranial and spinal nerves). The basic structural and functional unit is the neuron, which transmits electrical impulses. The nervous system works with the endocrine system to maintain homeostasis. Reflexes are involuntary, rapid responses to stimuli that protect the body and are coordinated by the spinal cord without involving the brain.

key_concepts.definition: A neuron is the structural and functional unit of the nervous system, specialized to receive, integrate, and transmit electrical signals (nerve impulses). It consists of a cell body (soma) containing the nucleus, dendrites that receive signals from other neurons, and a long axon that transmits signals away from the cell body. Axons may be covered by a myelin sheath that insulates and speeds up impulse conduction.

Neurons are classified by function into three types: sensory (afferent) neurons that carry impulses from sense organs to the CNS; motor (efferent) neurons that carry impulses from the CNS to effectors (muscles and glands); and relay (interneurons/connector) neurons that connect sensory and motor neurons within the CNS. The majority of neurons in the brain are interneurons.

A nerve impulse is an electrochemical signal that travels along a neuron. At rest, a neuron has a resting potential (inside is negative relative to outside due to K+ ions). When stimulated, sodium ions rush in, causing depolarization (action potential). This depolarization wave travels along the axon. After the impulse, the neuron repolarizes through K+ efflux and returns to resting potential.

A synapse is a junction between two neurons or between a neuron and an effector where nerve impulses are transmitted. The presynaptic neuron releases chemical messengers called neurotransmitters (e.g., acetylcholine) into the synaptic cleft. Neurotransmitters bind to receptors on the postsynaptic membrane, generating a new impulse. Synapses ensure one-way transmission of impulses.

A reflex action is a rapid, automatic, involuntary response to a stimulus that occurs without conscious brain involvement. The pathway taken is called a reflex arc: Receptor (detects stimulus) > Sensory neuron > Relay neuron in spinal cord > Motor neuron > Effector (muscle or gland). Examples include the knee-jerk reflex, pupillary light reflex, and withdrawal reflex.

The human brain consists of three major parts: the cerebrum (largest part, responsible for thought, memory, voluntary movement, sensory processing, and consciousness), the cerebellum (coordinates muscle movement and maintains balance and posture), and the brain stem (medulla oblongata, pons, and midbrain — controls involuntary functions like breathing, heart rate, and digestion). The brain is protected by the skull and three meningeal membranes.

The spinal cord is a cylindrical bundle of nerve fibers enclosed within the vertebral column, connecting the brain to the peripheral nervous system. It has a central H-shaped grey matter (containing cell bodies) surrounded by white matter (containing myelinated axons). The spinal cord serves as the center for spinal reflexes and a pathway for impulses between the body and brain.

The autonomic nervous system (ANS) controls involuntary functions of internal organs including heart rate, digestion, respiratory rate, and glandular secretion. It has two divisions: the sympathetic system (fight or flight response — increases heart rate, dilates pupils, redirects blood to muscles) and the parasympathetic system (rest and digest — decreases heart rate, promotes digestion). These two divisions have antagonistic effects.

key_points: The nervous system consists of the CNS (brain and spinal cord) and PNS (peripheral nerves)
A neuron has a cell body, dendrites (receive impulses), and an axon (transmits impulses away from cell body)
Three types of neurons: sensory (receptor to CNS), relay/interneurons (within CNS), motor (CNS to effector)
Myelin sheath is a fatty insulating layer around axons that speeds up nerve impulse conduction
Impulse transmission at a synapse uses chemical neurotransmitters like acetylcholine
Synapses ensure one-way transmission of nerve impulses
Reflex arc pathway: Receptor > Sensory neuron > Relay neuron (spinal cord) > Motor neuron > Effector
The cerebrum controls voluntary actions, thinking, memory, and consciousness
The cerebellum controls balance, posture, and coordination of movement
The medulla oblongata controls involuntary vital functions: breathing, heart rate, blood pressure
The sympathetic system prepares for emergency (fight or flight); parasympathetic for rest and digestion

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: The endocrine system is a network of ductless glands that secrete chemical messengers called hormones directly into the bloodstream, which carries them to target organs where they produce specific effects. Unlike the nervous system that provides rapid, short-lived responses, the endocrine system produces slower but longer-lasting effects. Major endocrine glands include the pituitary (master gland), thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal glands, pancreas, gonads (testes and ovaries), and pineal gland. Hormones regulate metabolism, growth, reproduction, and homeostasis. Hormonal imbalances can cause diseases like diabetes, goiter, and dwarfism.

key_concepts.definition: A hormone is a chemical messenger secreted in small amounts by endocrine glands directly into the blood, which transports it to specific target organs where it produces a physiological response. Hormones are either proteins/peptides (e.g., insulin, ADH) or steroids (e.g., estrogen, cortisol). They work by binding to specific receptor molecules in or on target cells.

The pituitary gland (hypophysis) is a pea-sized gland at the base of the brain that is called the master gland because it controls other endocrine glands. The anterior pituitary secretes growth hormone (GH), thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), luteinizing hormone (LH), and prolactin. The posterior pituitary releases ADH (antidiuretic hormone) and oxytocin, which are actually made in the hypothalamus.

The thyroid gland is a butterfly-shaped gland in the neck that secretes thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3), which regulate metabolic rate, growth, and development. Thyroxine requires iodine for its synthesis. Hypothyroidism (underactivity) causes cretinism in children and myxedema in adults. Hyperthyroidism (overactivity) causes Graves disease. Iodine deficiency causes goiter (enlargement of the thyroid gland).

The pancreas is a dual-function organ with both exocrine (digestive enzymes) and endocrine functions. The endocrine portion consists of clusters of cells called Islets of Langerhans containing alpha cells (secrete glucagon, which raises blood glucose) and beta cells (secrete insulin, which lowers blood glucose). Diabetes mellitus results from insufficient insulin production or target cell resistance to insulin.

The adrenal glands are paired glands located on top of each kidney consisting of two regions. The adrenal cortex secretes steroid hormones including cortisol (regulates metabolism and stress response) and aldosterone (regulates salt and water balance). The adrenal medulla secretes adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline, which prepare the body for emergency (fight or flight response) by increasing heart rate, blood pressure, and blood glucose.

Hormone secretion is largely controlled by negative feedback mechanisms. When blood hormone levels rise above normal, this signals the gland to reduce secretion; when levels fall below normal, secretion increases. For example, high blood glucose stimulates insulin secretion; insulin lowers blood glucose; low blood glucose inhibits insulin secretion. This maintains hormone levels within narrow homeostatic ranges.

Growth hormone is secreted by the anterior pituitary gland and stimulates protein synthesis and cell division, promoting growth of bones and muscles. Excess GH in childhood causes gigantism; in adults, it causes acromegaly (abnormal growth of hands, feet, and face). Deficiency of GH in childhood causes pituitary dwarfism. GH secretion is highest during sleep.

Insulin and glucagon are antagonistic hormones secreted by beta and alpha cells of the Islets of Langerhans in the pancreas. Insulin (secreted when blood glucose is high after a meal) promotes glucose uptake by cells and conversion to glycogen in the liver, lowering blood glucose. Glucagon (secreted when blood glucose is low) promotes glycogen breakdown in the liver to glucose, raising blood glucose.

key_points: Endocrine glands are ductless and secrete hormones directly into the blood (unlike exocrine glands with ducts)
The pituitary gland is the master gland; it controls other endocrine glands via tropic hormones
Thyroxine regulates metabolic rate; requires iodine for synthesis; goiter results from iodine deficiency
Insulin (from beta cells) lowers blood glucose; glucagon (from alpha cells) raises blood glucose
Diabetes mellitus: Type 1 (no insulin production); Type 2 (insulin resistance) — both cause hyperglycemia
Adrenaline from adrenal medulla prepares body for fight or flight: increases heart rate, blood pressure, blood glucose
Negative feedback controls hormone levels: high hormone levels inhibit further secretion
GH deficiency in children causes pituitary dwarfism; excess causes gigantism
ADH (from posterior pituitary) promotes water reabsorption in kidneys; deficiency causes diabetes insipidus
Parathyroid hormone (PTH) regulates calcium levels in blood by increasing calcium release from bones
The hypothalamus links the nervous and endocrine systems, controlling pituitary hormone secretion

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: Reproduction is the biological process by which new individuals of the same species are produced to ensure survival of the species. The human reproductive system involves specialized organs and hormones that enable sexual reproduction through the production of gametes (eggs and sperm) and their union during fertilization. The male reproductive system produces sperm (spermatogenesis) in the testes and delivers them through a series of ducts. The female reproductive system produces eggs (oogenesis) in the ovaries and provides an environment for fertilization, implantation, and development of the embryo. Hormonal regulation by FSH, LH, estrogen, and progesterone controls the menstrual cycle and reproductive processes.

key_concepts.definition: The male reproductive system consists of the testes (produce sperm and testosterone), epididymis (stores and matures sperm), vas deferens (carries sperm to urethra), seminal vesicles, prostate gland, and Cowpers glands (add secretions to form semen), and the urethra and penis for delivery of sperm. Testes are located in the scrotum outside the body cavity to maintain temperature (34 degree C) required for sperm production.

Spermatogenesis is the process of sperm production in the seminiferous tubules of the testes. Diploid spermatogonia undergo mitosis, then meiosis to produce haploid spermatids, which mature into spermatozoa (sperm cells) in a process called spermiogenesis. Testosterone from Leydig cells and FSH from the pituitary are required for spermatogenesis. The process takes about 70 days in humans.

The female reproductive system consists of the ovaries (produce eggs and hormones), fallopian tubes (carry eggs from ovary to uterus and site of fertilization), uterus (womb, site of implantation and fetal development), cervix (lower part of uterus), and vagina (birth canal). The uterine wall (endometrium) thickens each month to receive a fertilized egg and sheds during menstruation if fertilization does not occur.

Oogenesis is the process of egg (ovum) production in the ovaries. Unlike spermatogenesis, oogenesis begins before birth, when primary oocytes develop from oogonia and are arrested in prophase I of meiosis. After puberty, one primary oocyte completes meiosis I each month (during ovulation), producing a secondary oocyte and a polar body. Meiosis II is completed only upon fertilization.

The menstrual cycle is the monthly hormonal cycle in females of reproductive age, typically lasting 28 days, that prepares the uterus for potential pregnancy. Phases include: menstruation (days 1-5, uterine lining sheds), follicular phase (days 1-13, follicle grows under FSH), ovulation (day 14, egg released triggered by LH surge), and luteal phase (days 15-28, corpus luteum forms and secretes progesterone).

Fertilization is the fusion of a haploid sperm cell with a haploid egg cell (ovum) to form a diploid zygote. It typically occurs in the fallopian tube within hours of ovulation. Only one sperm penetrates the egg; this triggers a cortical reaction that prevents additional sperm entry. The resulting zygote contains 46 chromosomes (23 from each parent) and begins mitotic divisions to form an embryo.

After fertilization, the zygote undergoes several mitotic divisions as it travels down the fallopian tube, forming a morula and then a blastocyst. The blastocyst implants into the thickened endometrium of the uterus about 6-7 days after fertilization. The placenta develops from embryonic and maternal tissues and serves as the organ of exchange (nutrients, oxygen, antibodies in; CO2, wastes out) between mother and fetus.

Sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) or sexually transmitted infections (STIs) are infections spread primarily through sexual contact. Common STDs include HIV/AIDS (caused by Human Immunodeficiency Virus, attacks immune cells), gonorrhea (caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae bacteria), syphilis (caused by Treponema pallidum bacteria), and herpes (caused by Herpes Simplex Virus). Prevention includes safe sex practices, vaccination (for some), and abstinence.

key_points: Testes produce sperm and testosterone; ovaries produce eggs and estrogen/progesterone
Testes are located in the scrotum to maintain lower temperature (34 degree C) needed for spermatogenesis
Spermatogenesis occurs in seminiferous tubules; produces 4 functional sperm from each primary spermatocyte
Oogenesis produces only 1 egg and 3 polar bodies from each primary oocyte
Ovulation (release of egg) occurs around day 14 of a 28-day menstrual cycle, triggered by LH surge
Fertilization normally occurs in the fallopian tube (oviduct); produces a diploid zygote (2n = 46)
The blastocyst implants in the uterine endometrium about 6-7 days after fertilization
The placenta allows exchange of materials between mother and fetus without blood mixing
FSH stimulates follicle growth; LH triggers ovulation; estrogen builds endometrium; progesterone maintains it
The corpus luteum forms from the follicle after ovulation and secretes progesterone
If fertilization does not occur, the corpus luteum degenerates, progesterone levels fall, and menstruation occurs

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: Population refers to a group of individuals of the same species living in a defined geographic area at a given time. The study of population dynamics examines factors that influence population size, density, and growth rates. Human population has grown exponentially, reaching over 8 billion people, driven by declining death rates due to advances in medicine, sanitation, and food production while birth rates remained high. Overpopulation leads to resource depletion, environmental degradation, poverty, and declining quality of life. Population control measures, family planning, and education, particularly of women, are essential strategies for achieving sustainable population growth.

key_concepts.definition: Population growth refers to the change in the number of individuals in a population over time. It is determined by birth rate (natality), death rate (mortality), immigration (movement into the area), and emigration (movement out of the area). Natural growth rate = birth rate - death rate. Populations can show exponential growth (J-curve) when resources are unlimited or logistic growth (S-curve) when resources are limited.

Birth rate (natality) is the number of births per 1000 people per year in a population. Death rate (mortality) is the number of deaths per 1000 people per year. When birth rate exceeds death rate, the population grows. Improvements in healthcare, sanitation, and nutrition have dramatically reduced death rates globally, contributing to population explosion, particularly in developing countries.

Population explosion refers to the rapid and dramatic increase in human population that began in the 18th century with the Industrial Revolution and has accelerated since the 20th century. The global human population took thousands of years to reach 1 billion (1804), but reached 8 billion by 2022. Population explosion is driven by falling death rates due to medical advances while birth rates remained high.

Overpopulation leads to multiple negative consequences: depletion of natural resources (water, forests, minerals); food insecurity and malnutrition; increased pollution and environmental degradation; unemployment and poverty; inadequate housing, sanitation, and healthcare; reduced biodiversity; and global climate change. These consequences disproportionately affect developing nations and marginalized communities.

Family planning refers to practices that enable individuals and couples to decide the number, spacing, and timing of their children. Methods include natural methods (abstinence, rhythm method), barrier methods (condoms, diaphragms), hormonal contraceptives (pills, injections), intrauterine devices (IUDs), and surgical sterilization (vasectomy, tubal ligation). Family planning programs are key to controlling population growth.

The demographic transition model describes the historical shift in populations from high birth and death rates to low birth and death rates as countries develop economically. Stage 1: both rates high; Stage 2: death rate falls, birth rate high (population explosion); Stage 3: birth rate starts to fall; Stage 4: both rates low (stable population). Most developed nations are in Stage 4; many developing nations are in Stage 2-3.

Population density is the number of individuals per unit area (or volume). It is calculated as total population divided by total area. High population density in urban areas leads to resource strain and pollution. Low population density in rural areas may indicate better resource availability. Factors affecting density include climate, resource availability, economic opportunities, and historical settlement patterns.

key_points: World population reached 1 billion in 1804, 2 billion by 1927, and over 8 billion by 2022
Population growth rate = (birth rate - death rate) + (immigration rate - emigration rate)
Population explosion results from rapidly falling death rates while birth rates remain high
The Green Revolution increased food production but also increased dependence on fertilizers and pesticides
Overpopulation causes resource depletion, pollution, unemployment, poverty, and food insecurity
Family planning methods include natural methods, barrier methods, hormonal contraceptives, and surgical methods
Education, especially of women and girls, is the most effective long-term strategy for reducing birth rates
Rapid urbanization accompanies population growth and creates challenges for housing, sanitation, and services
Age structure (proportion of young, adult, elderly) determines future population trends
Countries with high fertility rates tend to have younger age structures and faster population growth

10|biology|Physical_Health_and_Hygiene.overview: Health is a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being, not merely the absence of disease or infirmity, as defined by the World Health Organization. Maintaining good health requires regular physical exercise, balanced nutrition, adequate sleep, personal hygiene, mental well-being, and avoidance of harmful substances. Diseases can be classified as communicable (infectious, spread from person to person) and non-communicable (lifestyle or genetic diseases that do not spread). Preventive healthcare, vaccination, proper sanitation, and healthy lifestyle choices are essential for maintaining individual and community health. Substance abuse, including tobacco, alcohol, and drugs, poses significant threats to health.

key_concepts.definition: Communicable diseases are illnesses caused by infectious microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites) that can be transmitted from one person to another or from animals to humans. They spread through direct contact, droplets, contaminated food and water, vectors (insects), or the air. Examples include tuberculosis, cholera, malaria, influenza, and AIDS. Prevention involves vaccination, hygiene, and vector control.

Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) are conditions that are not caused by infectious agents and cannot be transmitted from person to person. They include cardiovascular diseases (heart disease, stroke), cancers, diabetes, chronic respiratory diseases, and mental health disorders. NCDs are largely caused by unhealthy diet, physical inactivity, tobacco use, and alcohol consumption, making them largely preventable through lifestyle changes.

Immunity is the ability of the body to resist or overcome infections by recognizing and destroying pathogens. Innate (non-specific) immunity provides immediate defense against all pathogens (e.g., skin barrier, fever, phagocytes). Acquired (specific) immunity develops after exposure to a specific pathogen and involves lymphocytes: B-cells produce antibodies (humoral immunity) and T-cells kill infected cells (cell-mediated immunity).

Vaccination (immunization) is the administration of a vaccine containing weakened, killed, or inactivated pathogens or their components to stimulate the immune system to produce antibodies and memory cells without causing disease. If the vaccinated person later encounters the actual pathogen, memory cells trigger a rapid immune response. Vaccines have eradicated smallpox and nearly eliminated polio globally.

Personal hygiene refers to practices that maintain body cleanliness and health, including regular bathing, handwashing with soap, dental care (brushing and flossing), clean clothing, and proper food handling. Regular handwashing before eating and after using the toilet is one of the most effective ways to prevent the spread of communicable diseases. Dental hygiene prevents tooth decay and gum disease.

A balanced diet provides all essential nutrients in correct proportions: carbohydrates (energy), proteins (growth and repair), fats (energy storage and cell membranes), vitamins (metabolic regulation), minerals (various functions), and water (all cellular processes). Malnutrition includes both undernutrition (deficiency diseases like scurvy, rickets, kwashiorkor) and overnutrition (obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease).

Substance abuse is the harmful use of psychoactive substances including tobacco, alcohol, and illicit drugs. Tobacco causes lung cancer, cardiovascular disease, and COPD. Alcohol damages the liver (cirrhosis), nervous system, and contributes to accidents. Drugs like heroin and cocaine cause addiction, mental disorders, and HIV transmission through shared needles. Substance abuse has severe social and economic consequences.

Mental health is a state of well-being in which a person realizes their own abilities, can cope with normal life stresses, can work productively, and can contribute to their community. Mental health disorders include depression, anxiety disorders, schizophrenia, and bipolar disorder. Mental health is equally important as physical health and is influenced by genetics, social environment, trauma, and lifestyle factors.

key_points: WHO defines health as complete physical, mental, and social well-being, not just absence of disease
Communicable diseases are caused by pathogens and are contagious; NCDs are not contagious
The germ theory of disease was established by Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch
Active immunity is long-lasting and results from infection or vaccination; passive immunity is short-lived
Antibodies are Y-shaped proteins produced by B-lymphocytes that specifically bind to antigens
Vaccines contain antigens that stimulate immune response and create immunological memory
Handwashing with soap is one of the most effective ways to prevent spread of infectious diseases
Balanced diet should include carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, and water in correct proportions
Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy; Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets; iron deficiency causes anemia
Tobacco smoking is the leading preventable cause of cancer, heart disease, and stroke
Regular physical exercise reduces risk of cardiovascular disease, diabetes, obesity, and depression

10|biology|Pollution.overview: Pollution is the introduction of harmful contaminants into the natural environment at rates exceeding its ability to absorb or neutralize them, causing adverse changes. It poses severe threats to ecosystems, biodiversity, and human health. Pollution is classified by the medium affected: air pollution, water pollution, soil pollution, and noise pollution. Primary sources include industrial emissions, vehicle exhaust, agricultural chemicals, domestic sewage, and waste disposal. Global environmental problems like climate change, acid rain, ozone layer depletion, and loss of biodiversity are largely driven by pollution from human activities. Mitigation requires technological solutions, policy interventions, and changes in individual behavior.

key_concepts.definition: Air pollution is the presence of harmful substances (pollutants) in the atmosphere at concentrations that cause harm to humans, animals, plants, and materials. Major air pollutants include particulate matter (PM2.5, PM10), sulfur dioxide (SO2) from burning coal, nitrogen oxides (NOx) from vehicle exhaust, carbon monoxide (CO) from incomplete combustion, and volatile organic compounds (VOCs). Indoor air pollution from cooking fires causes significant health problems globally.

Water pollution is the contamination of water bodies (rivers, lakes, groundwater, oceans) with harmful substances. Sources include industrial effluents, agricultural runoff containing pesticides and fertilizers, domestic sewage, and oil spills. Water pollution causes diseases (cholera, typhoid, dysentery), harms aquatic ecosystems, and reduces potable water availability. Eutrophication, caused by excess nutrients, depletes oxygen in water bodies, killing aquatic life.

Soil pollution is the contamination of soil with hazardous chemicals or waste materials that adversely affect soil health and the organisms living in it. Causes include excessive pesticide and fertilizer use, industrial waste disposal, oil spills, and improper disposal of solid waste. Polluted soil cannot support healthy plant growth, and contaminants can enter the food chain. Heavy metals like lead, mercury, and cadmium are persistent soil pollutants.

The greenhouse effect is the natural process by which greenhouse gases (CO2, methane, water vapor, N2O) in the atmosphere trap heat from the sun and keep Earth warm. However, increased emissions of greenhouse gases from burning fossil fuels and deforestation are enhancing this effect, causing global warming. Consequences include rising sea levels, more extreme weather events, shifting climate zones, and threats to biodiversity.

The ozone layer in the stratosphere absorbs harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the sun, protecting life on Earth. Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) from refrigerators, air conditioners, and aerosol sprays react with ozone molecules, breaking them down and thinning the ozone layer. Increased UV radiation reaching Earth causes skin cancer, cataracts, and immune suppression in humans, and damages marine ecosystems. The Montreal Protocol (1987) led to phasing out CFCs globally.

Acid rain is precipitation (rain, snow, fog) that is more acidic than normal (pH below 5.6) due to dissolved sulfur dioxide (SO2) and nitrogen oxides (NOx) from burning fossil fuels. These gases react with water vapor in the atmosphere to form sulfuric acid and nitric acid. Acid rain damages forests, kills aquatic life by acidifying lakes, corrodes buildings and monuments, and affects soil chemistry.

Eutrophication is the enrichment of water bodies with nutrients (primarily nitrogen and phosphorus) from agricultural runoff and sewage, leading to excessive growth of algae and aquatic plants (algal bloom). When the algae die and decompose, bacteria consume oxygen in the process, creating oxygen-depleted (hypoxic) dead zones where fish and other aerobic organisms cannot survive. It leads to loss of aquatic biodiversity.

Biodegradable pollutants are substances that can be broken down by microorganisms into simpler, harmless compounds (e.g., food waste, paper, sewage). Non-biodegradable pollutants persist in the environment for long periods and accumulate in food chains through biomagnification (e.g., DDT, heavy metals, plastics). Biomagnification increases pollutant concentration at each trophic level, with top predators having the highest concentrations.

key_points: Four main types of pollution: air, water, soil, and noise pollution
Major air pollutants: SO2, NOx, CO, particulate matter, and hydrocarbons
Burning fossil fuels is the main source of CO2, SO2, and NOx emissions
Acid rain is caused by SO2 and NOx dissolving in rainwater to form sulfuric and nitric acids
CFCs destroy the ozone layer; the Montreal Protocol (1987) banned CFC production
Greenhouse gases (CO2, CH4, N2O, water vapor) trap heat and cause global warming
Eutrophication causes algal blooms and oxygen depletion (dead zones) in water bodies
DDT and other pesticides undergo biomagnification, accumulating to lethal concentrations in top predators
Noise pollution above 85 decibels causes hearing damage; above 120 dB causes immediate pain
The 3Rs of waste management: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle
Deforestation increases CO2 levels (less absorption) and causes soil erosion, flooding, and loss of biodiversity

10|history_civics|history_1_The_First_Wa.overview: The First War of Independence of 1857, also called the Sepoy Mutiny, was a major uprising against British East India Company rule. It began on May 10, 1857, when Indian soldiers at Meerut revolted and marched to Delhi, proclaiming Bahadur Shah Zafar as their leader. The revolt spread across northern and central India, involving not just soldiers but also peasants, landlords, and princes with grievances against British policies. Though suppressed by 1858, it had profound consequences: the East India Company was abolished, power transferred directly to the British Crown, and seeds of Indian nationalism were planted.

key_concepts.definition: A sepoy was an Indian soldier employed in the British East India Company's army. They followed military discipline imposed by British officers and served across the Indian subcontinent. Their dissatisfaction with pay, racial discrimination, and the introduction of the Enfield rifle cartridge greased with animal fat became a major trigger for the 1857 uprising.
