The Doctrine of Lapse was a policy introduced by Lord Dalhousie under which a princely state would be annexed by the British if the ruler died without a natural heir, since adopted sons were not recognized as legitimate successors. States like Satara, Nagpur, and Jhansi were annexed under this doctrine, causing widespread resentment. It was one of the major political causes of the 1857 revolt.

The Enfield rifle cartridge was a greased paper cartridge used in the new Pattern 1853 rifle. Soldiers had to bite off the end of the cartridge before loading, and rumours spread that the grease was made from cow and pig fat, offending both Hindu and Muslim sepoys. This became the immediate trigger of the 1857 revolt at Meerut.

Bahadur Shah Zafar was the last Mughal Emperor, declared the symbolic leader of the 1857 revolt by rebelling sepoys. Though elderly and lacking real power, his name gave the uprising legitimacy and a rallying point. After the revolt's suppression, he was tried by the British, exiled to Rangoon in Burma, and died there in 1862.

Nana Sahib, or Dhondu Pant, was the adopted son of the last Peshwa Baji Rao II, whose pension the British discontinued under the Doctrine of Lapse. He became one of the prominent leaders of the 1857 revolt, leading the uprising in Kanpur (Cawnpore). After the revolt's failure, he fled and his fate remained unknown.

Rani Laxmibai was the Queen of Jhansi and one of the most celebrated leaders of the 1857 revolt. After the British annexed Jhansi under the Doctrine of Lapse following her husband's death, she led fierce resistance. She died fighting the British at Gwalior in June 1858, becoming an enduring symbol of Indian resistance and patriotism.

Tatya Tope was a military commander and loyal associate of Nana Sahib who played a major role in the 1857 revolt. He organized guerrilla campaigns and assisted Rani Laxmibai at Jhansi and Gwalior. He was captured through betrayal in 1859 and executed by the British.

The Government of India Act of 1858 formally transferred governance of India from the East India Company to the British Crown. A Secretary of State for India was appointed, responsible to the British Parliament. This marked the beginning of direct British imperial rule known as the British Raj, with a Viceroy replacing the Governor-General.

key_points: The immediate cause of the 1857 revolt was the introduction of the Enfield rifle with cartridges believed to be greased with cow and pig fat, offending both Hindu and Muslim sepoys.
The revolt began on May 10, 1857, at Meerut when 85 sepoys who refused to use the cartridges were court-martialled — fellow soldiers mutinied and marched to Delhi.
Major centres of revolt included Delhi (Bahadur Shah Zafar), Kanpur (Nana Sahib), Lucknow (Begum Hazrat Mahal), Jhansi (Rani Laxmibai), and Bareilly (Khan Bahadur Khan).
Political causes included annexation of Awadh, the Doctrine of Lapse, and British interference in Indian social and religious customs.
Economic causes included drain of wealth from India, destruction of Indian handicrafts by cheap British goods, and heavy land revenue demands.
The revolt was suppressed by 1858 — Delhi fell in September 1857, Lucknow in March 1858, and Jhansi in April 1858.
As a result, the East India Company was abolished and India came under direct British Crown rule; Queen Victoria issued a proclamation granting amnesty to most rebels.
The position of Governor-General was replaced by that of Viceroy; Queen Victoria assumed the title 'Empress of India' in 1876.
The revolt exposed weaknesses of Company rule and led to major administrative, military, and social reforms in British India.
The revolt is considered by many historians as the first war of Indian independence, though others view it as primarily a military mutiny with limited popular support.

10|history_civics|history_2_Growth_of_Na.overview: Indian nationalism grew steadily in the second half of the 19th century as Indians became aware of their shared identity and the exploitative nature of British rule. Western education introduced ideas of liberty, democracy, and nationalism that Indians began applying to their own situation. The development of railways, the telegraph, and the English-language press helped unify Indians across geographic and linguistic boundaries. Economic exploitation — drain of wealth, deindustrialisation, and heavy taxation — created widespread discontent fueling desire for self-rule. The formation of the Indian National Congress in 1885 provided an organized platform for nationalist aspirations.

key_concepts.definition: Nationalism is a political ideology that holds that a people sharing common culture, language, or history should constitute an independent nation. In India, nationalism involved growing consciousness of a shared identity distinct from British colonial identity. Indian nationalism drew from both traditional Indian culture and Western liberal ideas of self-determination and democracy.

The drain of wealth theory, articulated by Dadabhai Naoroji in 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India,' argued that a large portion of India's national income was transferred to Britain without any economic return. This occurred through home charges (salaries, pensions paid to Britain), repatriated profits, and unfair trade balance. Naoroji estimated the drain amounted to crores of rupees annually and was the primary cause of India's poverty.

Western education in India, expanded through Macaulay's Minute of 1835, introduced English language, European history, and liberal political thought to Indians. Educated Indians encountered works of Rousseau, John Stuart Mill, and Edmund Burke, absorbing ideas about democracy, liberty, and citizens' rights. This educated class became the backbone of the nationalist movement, using the tools of colonialism to challenge colonial rule.

The Indian National Congress was founded in December 1885 by A.O. Hume, a retired British civil servant, with Dadabhai Naoroji and Dinshaw Wacha. Its first session was held in Bombay under the presidency of Womesh Chandra Bannerjee. Initially moderate, the INC grew into the primary vehicle for the Indian independence movement.

Dadabhai Naoroji, known as the 'Grand Old Man of India,' was an influential early nationalist and the first Asian elected to the British Parliament (Finsbury, 1892). His book 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India' exposed India's economic exploitation, and his 'drain of wealth' concept became a cornerstone of nationalist economic critique.

Indian-language and English-language newspapers played a crucial role in spreading nationalist ideas and creating political consciousness. Papers like Amrita Bazar Patrika, The Hindu, and Tilak's Kesari reported on British policies and nationalist activities. The British Vernacular Press Act of 1878 attempted to curb press freedom but was later repealed following protests.

Deindustrialisation refers to the decline of India's traditional industries, particularly textiles, under British rule. Cheap machine-made British goods flooded Indian markets while heavy duties were placed on Indian exports to Britain. This destroyed livelihoods of millions of artisans and weavers, creating widespread unemployment that fueled nationalist discontent.

The Swadeshi Movement arose as a direct response to the partition of Bengal in 1905, calling for use of Indian-made goods and boycott of British goods. It was more militant than previous nationalist activities and involved mass participation including students and women. The movement marked the transition from the Moderate era to the Extremist era in Indian politics.

key_points: Growth of nationalism in India was gradual, influenced by political, economic, cultural, and intellectual factors in the second half of the 19th century.
Western education created an educated Indian middle class exposed to liberal ideas of freedom and democracy, who began questioning colonial rule.
Dadabhai Naoroji's 'drain of wealth' theory showed how British rule economically exploited India, removing resources without adequate return.
Railways, though built for British commercial and military interests, inadvertently united India geographically and facilitated spread of nationalist ideas.
The Vernacular Press Act of 1878 attempted to muzzle press freedom but was repealed in 1881 following protests — showing nationalist resilience.
Social reform movements like Brahmo Samaj, Arya Samaj, and Ramakrishna Mission combined cultural pride with reform, nurturing national consciousness.
Political associations like Poona Sarvajanik Sabha, Indian Association (Calcutta), and Madras Mahajan Sabha preceded the INC and developed organizational experience.
The Indian National Congress was founded in December 1885 and held its first session in Bombay; early leaders were 'Moderates' believing in constitutional methods.
The Partition of Bengal in 1905 by Lord Curzon, dividing it along religious lines, was a turning point that radicalized Indian nationalist politics.
The Swadeshi Movement promoted Indian-made goods and boycott of British goods — a major escalation of nationalist activity with mass participation.

10|history_civics|history_3_First_Phase_.overview: The first phase of the Indian National Movement (1885–1905) is known as the Moderate phase, characterized by educated upper-middle-class leaders who believed in constitutional methods and the fairness of British institutions. Leaders like Dadabhai Naoroji, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, and Pherozeshah Mehta dominated early Congress sessions. The Moderates' strategy was to present petitions and resolutions, demanding greater Indian representation in legislative councils and civil service reforms. Though later criticized as ineffective, the Moderates educated Indians about their rights and laid the organizational foundation for future mass movements.

key_concepts.definition: The Moderate phase refers to the early period of the Indian National Congress characterized by constitutionalist leaders who used petitions, prayers, and protests within the legal framework to seek reforms. Leaders like Dadabhai Naoroji, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, and Pherozeshah Mehta believed the British would respond to reasoned arguments. They established the organizational basis of the nationalist movement despite being criticized for timidity by later nationalists.

Gopal Krishna Gokhale was the most prominent Moderate leader of the Indian National Congress and a social reformer who founded the Servants of India Society in 1905. Deeply respected for his integrity and scholarship, he was a powerful advocate for constitutional reform. Gokhale served as a political mentor to Mahatma Gandhi, who considered him his political guru.

Bal Gangadhar Tilak, known as 'Lokmanya,' was among the first leaders to recognize that mass mobilization was essential for independence. He broke with the Moderates and advocated Swaraj through mass action. He popularized the Ganesh festival and Shivaji festival as nationalist platforms and used his newspapers Kesari and Mahratta to spread radical ideas.

The Indian Councils Act of 1892 expanded the membership of central and provincial legislative councils and allowed some non-official Indian members. This was a concession to Moderate demands but fell far short of representative government. The Act allowed discussion of the budget but not its amendment, illustrating the limited nature of British reforms.

The Partition of Bengal was announced by Viceroy Lord Curzon in 1905, dividing Bengal into two provinces along religious lines. Indians saw it as a deliberate attempt to divide Hindus and Muslims and weaken Bengal's strong nationalist movement. It provoked massive protests and gave birth to the Swadeshi and Boycott movements, marking the end of the Moderate phase.

Pherozeshah Mehta was a prominent Moderate leader and one of the founders of the Indian National Congress. A brilliant lawyer, social reformer, and powerful orator, he served as President of Congress and shaped its early organizational structure and constitutional approach.

Lala Lajpat Rai, known as 'Punjab Kesari' (Lion of Punjab), was associated with the Arya Samaj and played a significant role in early nationalism. Though initially aligned with Congress, he became associated with the Extremist faction and advocated a more assertive approach to independence, particularly during the Swadeshi movement in Punjab.

Bipin Chandra Pal was one of the leading Extremist nationalist leaders from Bengal. A powerful orator and journalist, he advocated complete Swaraj and passive resistance against British rule. His journal New India popularized radical nationalist ideas, and he was among the first to articulate a vision of complete independence rather than gradual reform.

key_points: The Indian National Congress was founded in 1885 and from 1885–1905 was dominated by Moderate leaders who used constitutional methods like petitions, memorials, and resolutions.
Moderate demands included expansion of legislative councils, reduction of military expenditure, holding of ICS examinations in India, and reduction in land revenue.
The Moderates met annually in different cities, symbolizing the national character of the movement, though membership was largely urban and educated.
Despite criticism, Moderates succeeded in educating Indian public opinion, exposing economic exploitation, and establishing the organizational framework of the Congress.
Bal Gangadhar Tilak used popular festivals and his newspapers to mobilize mass support beyond the educated elite.
The Indian Councils Act of 1892 was a partial concession — expanded legislative councils but did not grant real representative government.
The partition of Bengal in 1905 by Lord Curzon was widely seen as a 'divide and rule' policy and provoked massive protests.
The Swadeshi Movement called for boycott of British goods and promotion of Indian industries — one of the first mass movements in Indian nationalism.
Nationalist publications like Tilak's Kesari played a crucial role in radicalizing Indian opinion beyond what Moderate methods could achieve.
The formal split between Moderates and Extremists occurred at the Surat Congress session of 1907, marking the end of the first phase.

10|history_civics|history_4_Second_Phase.overview: The second phase (1905–1916) saw a significant shift from moderate constitutionalist methods to more assertive, mass-based nationalism. The partition of Bengal triggered the Swadeshi and Boycott movements with mass participation including students and women. The Extremist faction led by Tilak, Bipin Chandra Pal, and Lala Lajpat Rai (the 'Lal Bal Pal' trio) demanded complete Swaraj through aggressive resistance. The Morley-Minto Reforms of 1909 introduced separate electorates for Muslims, a divisive step with long-term consequences. By 1916, the Lucknow Pact and the Home Rule Leagues marked a new phase of united political action.

key_concepts.definition: The Extremist phase (1905–1916) was when a more militant faction took control of Indian nationalist politics, rejecting petitions and seeking Swaraj through mass mobilization, non-cooperation, and civil disobedience. Leaders like Tilak, Pal, and Lajpat Rai believed mass pressure was necessary because the British would not grant reforms voluntarily. They drew inspiration from revolutionary nationalism in Europe and India's cultural traditions.

Lal Bal Pal refers to the trio of Extremist nationalist leaders — Lala Lajpat Rai (Lal), Bal Gangadhar Tilak (Bal), and Bipin Chandra Pal (Pal). They led the assertive nationalist movement after 1905, demanding complete Swaraj rather than gradual reforms. Their combined influence spread across Punjab, Maharashtra, and Bengal respectively, giving the movement an all-India character.

The Morley-Minto Reforms (Indian Councils Act of 1909) increased Indian representation in legislative councils and crucially introduced separate electorates for Muslims, meaning Muslims would vote only for Muslim candidates. Nationalists condemned this as a 'divide and rule' strategy, and its introduction had profound consequences for Hindu-Muslim relations leading ultimately to partition in 1947.

Separate electorate was a system introduced by the Morley-Minto Reforms under which Muslim voters could only vote for Muslim candidates in designated Muslim constituencies. Supported by the Muslim League, this institutionalized religious divisions in Indian politics. Congress leaders condemned it as divisive; Gandhi later called it 'a poison in the body politic of India.'

The Home Rule League was established in 1916 — Bal Gangadhar Tilak founded one in Belgaum and Annie Besant founded another in Madras. Modeled on the Irish Home Rule movement, they demanded self-government for India within the British Empire. The leagues organized mass membership drives and political education campaigns, marking a new phase of popular nationalist mobilization.

The Lucknow Pact of 1916 was an agreement between the Indian National Congress and the All India Muslim League, reached at a joint session in Lucknow. Congress accepted separate electorates for Muslims in exchange for Muslim League support for the demand for self-government. Though it achieved temporary Hindu-Muslim unity, it also legitimized the concept of separate Muslim political representation.

Annie Besant was an Irish socialist, Theosophist, and women's rights activist who became a leading figure in Indian nationalism. She founded the Home Rule League in 1916 and was the first woman elected President of the Indian National Congress in 1917. She championed Indian self-rule and strengthened the Theosophical Society's educational and social work in India.

The Surat Split refers to the formal division between Moderate and Extremist factions of the Indian National Congress at the 1907 Surat session. The Extremists, led by Tilak, wanted Lajpat Rai as Congress President; the Moderates insisted on Rash Behari Ghosh. The session ended in disorder and physical scuffle. The Moderates subsequently controlled Congress while the Extremists were expelled for nearly a decade.

key_points: The Partition of Bengal in 1905 was the main trigger for the second phase of Indian nationalism and the Swadeshi-Boycott movement.
The 'Lal Bal Pal' trio led the Extremist faction demanding Swaraj through mass action — boycott of British goods, promotion of Swadeshi, national education, and passive resistance.
The formal split between Moderates and Extremists occurred at the Surat Congress session in December 1907, after which Moderates controlled Congress for nearly a decade.
Tilak was tried and sentenced to 6 years imprisonment in 1908 for writing seditious articles in Kesari following the Muzaffarpur bomb case.
The Morley-Minto Reforms of 1909 introduced separate electorates for Muslims — nationalists condemned this as an attempt to divide Hindus and Muslims.
The Muslim League was founded in 1906 and initially aligned with British interests but gradually shifted toward a more nationalist position.
The year 1916 saw the Lucknow Pact — Congress and Muslim League agreeing on a joint program including demand for self-government.
The Home Rule Leagues of Tilak and Besant (both 1916) mobilized mass support for self-government within the British Empire.
This phase demonstrated that large-scale political mobilization beyond the educated elite was both possible and necessary.
The period ended with India's political scene energized by WWI and the imminent arrival of Gandhi, who would transform the movement into a genuine mass struggle.

10|history_civics|history_5_The_Muslim_L.overview: The All India Muslim League was founded in December 1906 at Dhaka, with the Nawab of Dhaka playing a key role. Its founding followed a meeting between a Muslim delegation led by the Aga Khan and Viceroy Lord Minto, where they requested separate Muslim political representation. The Muslim League initially aligned with British interests, supporting partition of Bengal and the separate electorate provision in the Morley-Minto Reforms. Over time its politics evolved — by 1916 it cooperated with Congress in the Lucknow Pact, but by the 1930s–40s under Mohammad Ali Jinnah, it increasingly demanded a separate homeland for Muslims, culminating in the creation of Pakistan in 1947.

key_concepts.definition: The All India Muslim League was a political party founded in December 1906 at Dhaka to protect the political rights and interests of Muslims in India. Its founding was motivated by concern that Muslims would be under-represented in a democratic India dominated numerically by Hindus. The League later became the primary vehicle for the demand for a separate Muslim state (Pakistan).

The Simla Deputation was a delegation of 35 Muslim leaders led by the Aga Khan who met Viceroy Lord Minto in October 1906, requesting separate electorates and reserved seats for Muslims. Minto agreed to the principle of separate Muslim representation, which was incorporated into the Morley-Minto Reforms of 1909. The deputation was later criticized by secular nationalists as deepening communal divisions.

Mohammad Ali Jinnah was a barrister who initially was a member of both Congress and the Muslim League, known as an 'ambassador of Hindu-Muslim unity.' He played a key role in the Lucknow Pact of 1916. Disillusioned with Congress over time, he became the undisputed leader of the Muslim League and the chief architect of Pakistan.

The Two-Nation Theory argued that Hindus and Muslims were two distinct nations with irreconcilable differences in culture, religion, and social customs, and could not coexist in a single democratic nation. Jinnah articulated this theory most forcefully in the 1940s. It was contested by the Congress and many Muslim scholars who believed in a composite Indian national identity.

The Lahore Resolution, also called the Pakistan Resolution, was passed at the Muslim League session in Lahore in March 1940. It demanded creation of independent states in Muslim-majority regions of northwestern and eastern India. This resolution was the turning point in the Muslim League's politics, shifting from demands for autonomy within India to outright partition.

The Communal Award was announced by British Prime Minister Ramsay MacDonald in 1932, extending separate electorates to Depressed Classes (Dalits). Gandhi strongly opposed separate electorates for Dalits and fasted in protest. The Poona Pact between Gandhi and Dr. B.R. Ambedkar replaced separate electorates with reserved seats within general electorates.

The Aga Khan (Sultan Mohammed Shah) was the spiritual leader of Ismaili Muslims and leader of the Simla Deputation of 1906. He became the first president of the All India Muslim League and was instrumental in establishing the principle that Muslims needed separate political representation in a self-governing India.

Direct Action Day was declared by Jinnah and the Muslim League on August 16, 1946, to press their demand for Pakistan. In Calcutta, it led to the 'Great Calcutta Killings' in which thousands of Hindus and Muslims were killed. The violence spread across Bengal, Bihar, and Punjab, revealing the depths of communal tension and making partition seem inevitable.

key_points: The All India Muslim League was founded on December 30, 1906, in Dhaka, with Nawab Salimullah of Dhaka as a key figure.
The founding of the League was influenced by British encouragement to create a separate Muslim political organization to counterbalance the Congress.
The Simla Deputation of 1906 secured the principle of separate Muslim electorates from Viceroy Minto, implemented in the Morley-Minto Reforms of 1909.
Initially the League supported partition of Bengal and cooperated with British policies, unlike the more anti-British Congress.
The 1916 Lucknow Pact showed Congress-League cooperation, with Congress accepting separate electorates in exchange for Muslim support for Home Rule.
Under Jinnah's leadership in the 1930s–40s, the League increasingly demanded a separate Muslim homeland.
The Lahore Resolution of March 1940 formally demanded creation of Pakistan — a definitive break with the vision of a united India.
During WWII, while Congress launched the Quit India Movement, the Muslim League cooperated with the British, strengthening its political position.
The General Elections of 1945–46 saw the Muslim League win most Muslim seats, strengthening Jinnah's claim that it was the sole representative of Indian Muslims.
The League's politics culminated in partition of India and creation of Pakistan on August 14, 1947, one day before Indian independence.

10|history_civics|history_6_Mahatma_Gand.overview: Mahatma Gandhi transformed the Indian independence movement into a genuine mass movement, mobilizing millions across class, caste, and gender boundaries. Returning from South Africa in 1915, Gandhi introduced Satyagraha (truth-force) and non-violent non-cooperation as revolutionary political methods. He launched three major national campaigns: the Non-Cooperation Movement (1920–22), the Civil Disobedience Movement (1930–34), and various regional Satyagrahas. Gandhi's connection with ordinary Indians through symbols like the charkha, his simple lifestyle, and his moral authority made him the undisputed leader of the independence movement.

key_concepts.definition: Satyagraha, meaning 'truth-force' or 'soul-force,' is a method of non-violent resistance developed by Gandhi first in South Africa and then in India. It involves willingness to suffer for truth without retaliation, seeking to transform the opponent through moral persuasion rather than coercion. Gandhi believed Satyagraha was morally superior to violence because it confronted injustice while preserving the dignity of both parties.

The Non-Cooperation Movement was launched by Gandhi in 1920 in response to the Jallianwala Bagh massacre and the Rowlatt Act. It called on Indians to boycott government schools, courts, legislative councils, foreign cloth, and government titles. Gandhi called it off in February 1922 after the Chauri Chaura incident in which a mob burned a police station killing 22 policemen, because Gandhi refused to continue a movement tainted by violence.

The Civil Disobedience Movement was launched with the famous Dandi March of March–April 1930, where Gandhi walked 385 km to the sea to make salt in defiance of the British salt monopoly. It involved deliberate breaking of colonial laws on a mass scale. The movement demonstrated the power of organized civil disobedience and led to the Gandhi-Irwin Pact of 1931.

The Dandi March was a 385 km march led by Gandhi from Sabarmati Ashram to Dandi on the Gujarat coast, completed on April 6, 1930. By making salt from seawater, Gandhi broke the British salt monopoly law which taxed a basic necessity. The Dandi March galvanized national and international attention, becoming one of history's most powerful acts of civil disobedience.

The Rowlatt Act gave the British government sweeping powers to arrest and detain individuals without trial for up to two years and to try them without jury. It was passed despite unanimous Indian opposition. Gandhi called it the 'Black Act' and organized the first nationwide political campaign against it, including a hartal (general strike) on April 6, 1919.

The Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred on April 13, 1919, in Amritsar, when Brigadier-General Reginald Dyer ordered troops to fire on a peaceful crowd gathered in a walled garden. At least 379 people were killed and over 1,000 injured (unofficial estimates are much higher). The massacre shocked Indians and permanently damaged British moral authority in India.

The Khilafat Movement was launched by Mohammad Ali and Shaukat Ali (the Ali Brothers) in 1919–20 to oppose the dismemberment of the Ottoman Caliphate after WWI. Gandhi supported the movement and linked it to the Non-Cooperation Movement, achieving a period of Hindu-Muslim unity. The movement collapsed after Turkey abolished the Caliphate in 1924.

The Gandhi-Irwin Pact was an agreement between Gandhi and Viceroy Lord Irwin in March 1931, ending the Civil Disobedience Movement. The British agreed to release political prisoners and allow Indians to make salt for personal use; Congress agreed to participate in the Second Round Table Conference. Gandhi attended but returned disappointed as it produced no substantial constitutional gains.

key_points: Gandhi returned to India from South Africa in 1915 and spent his first year traveling across India, understanding conditions before entering active politics.
Gandhi conducted his first Indian Satyagraha in Champaran, Bihar (1917), helping indigo farmers against oppressive British planters — marking his entry into national politics.
The Rowlatt Act (1919) and Jallianwala Bagh massacre (April 13, 1919) radicalized Gandhi and convinced him that cooperation with British rule was impossible.
The Non-Cooperation Movement (1920–22) called for surrender of titles, boycott of councils, courts, schools, foreign cloth, and eventually non-payment of taxes.
Gandhi called off the Non-Cooperation Movement in February 1922 after Chauri Chaura — a decision disappointing many leaders including Subhas Chandra Bose.
The Swaraj Party was formed by C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru in 1922 to pursue politics of obstruction within legislative councils.
The Lahore Congress of 1929 passed the Purna Swaraj (Complete Independence) resolution on December 31 under Jawaharlal Nehru's presidency.
The Dandi March (March 12 – April 6, 1930) launched the Civil Disobedience Movement and captured worldwide attention as a symbol of peaceful resistance.
The Gandhi-Irwin Pact of March 1931 ended the Civil Disobedience Movement; Gandhi attended the Second Round Table Conference in London but found it inconclusive.
The Civil Disobedience Movement was suspended in 1934, and Gandhi temporarily retired from active Congress politics to focus on constructive social work, particularly Harijan welfare.

10|history_civics|history_7_Quit_India_M.overview: The final phase of India's independence struggle (1939–1947) was shaped dramatically by World War II. When Britain declared war on Germany in 1939 without consulting Indian leaders, Congress provincial ministries resigned in protest. The failure of the Cripps Mission in 1942 led Gandhi to launch the Quit India Movement on August 8, 1942, with the call 'Do or Die.' The British responded with mass arrests and brutal repression. After the war, elections in 1945–46 confirmed the Congress-League divide, and negotiations ultimately resulted in the partition of India and simultaneous independence of India and Pakistan on August 14–15, 1947.

key_concepts.definition: The Quit India Movement was launched by Gandhi on August 8, 1942, at the Gowalia Tank Maidan in Bombay with the slogan 'Do or Die' (Karo ya Maro). It demanded immediate end to British rule. The British arrested Gandhi and all top Congress leaders within hours, but the movement continued through underground leadership and mass protests. It showed Indians were unwilling to support the British war effort without independence.

The Cripps Mission was sent by Winston Churchill in March 1942, led by Sir Stafford Cripps, offering dominion status after the war but not immediate independence. Congress rejected the offer because it didn't grant immediate independence and allowed provinces to opt out, implying partition. Gandhi famously dismissed it as 'a post-dated cheque on a failing bank.'

The Indian National Army was formed by Subhas Chandra Bose from Indian prisoners of war and expatriates in Southeast Asia during WWII, aiming to liberate India with Japanese help. Bose's rallying cry 'Dilli Chalo' (March to Delhi) inspired many Indians. Though the INA's military campaign failed, the INA trials of 1945 provoked massive public support and contributed to the British decision to accelerate independence.

The Cabinet Mission Plan was proposed in May 1946 by a three-member British Cabinet mission. It proposed a federal structure with a weak centre, three groups of provinces, retaining a united India. Congress initially accepted it but Nehru's clarification that Congress was free to modify the plan led Jinnah to withdraw Muslim League acceptance and declare Direct Action Day.

The Mountbatten Plan, announced on June 3, 1947, provided for partition of India into two independent dominions — India and Pakistan. Muslim-majority regions of Punjab and Bengal would be partitioned. Both Congress and the Muslim League accepted the plan. India became independent on August 15 and Pakistan on August 14, 1947.

Direct Action Day was declared by the Muslim League on August 16, 1946, to press the Pakistan demand. In Calcutta, it led to the 'Great Calcutta Killings' — thousands of Hindus and Muslims were killed over several days. Violence spread across Bengal, Bihar, and Punjab, revealing communal tensions and making partition seem inevitable.

The Radcliffe Line is the boundary drawn by Sir Cyril Radcliffe to demarcate borders between India and Pakistan in Punjab and Bengal. Completed in just 36 days and announced August 17, 1947 (two days after independence), the hastily drawn boundaries led to massive displacement and communal violence as populations found themselves on the 'wrong' side of the border.

At independence, over 560 princely states had to decide whether to accede to India or Pakistan. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, working with V.P. Menon, led integration of nearly all into India. Hyderabad was integrated by military action (Operation Polo, 1948); Junagadh's accession to Pakistan was reversed by plebiscite; Kashmir's disputed accession remains unresolved.

key_points: When Britain declared war on Germany in September 1939 without consulting Indian leaders, Congress provincial ministries resigned in protest; the Muslim League observed a 'Day of Deliverance.'
The Cripps Mission of 1942 failed because it offered only dominion status after the war and allowed provinces to opt out of the Union (implying partition).
The Quit India Resolution was passed on August 8, 1942 — the British arrested all Congress leaders the very next morning.
Despite mass arrests, the Quit India Movement continued through underground networks and mass uprisings, especially in Bihar, UP, Maharashtra, and Midnapore district of Bengal.
Subhas Chandra Bose escaped from house arrest, reached Southeast Asia through Germany, and raised the Indian National Army with the slogan 'Jai Hind.'
The Royal Indian Navy (RIN) Mutiny of 1946 showed that even colonial armed forces were no longer reliable — a key factor in the British decision to grant independence.
The Cabinet Mission Plan of 1946 proposed a united India with a three-tier federal structure but its rejection by both Congress and the League made partition almost inevitable.
Direct Action Day (August 16, 1946) led to catastrophic communal violence in Calcutta, accelerating acceptance of partition by both British and Indian leaders.
The Mountbatten Plan of June 3, 1947, provided for partition; Pakistan became independent on August 14 and India on August 15, 1947.
The partition led to one of history's largest migrations — an estimated 10–15 million people displaced and up to one million killed in communal violence.

10|history_civics|history_8_The_First_Wo.overview: The First World War (1914–1918) was a global conflict of unprecedented scale involving most of the world's great powers, organized into the Allied Powers (Britain, France, Russia, Italy, USA) and the Central Powers (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Ottoman Empire). It began with the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, triggering a cascade of treaty obligations. Devastating new technologies — machine guns, poison gas, tanks, aircraft, and submarines — resulted in massive casualties. By its end in November 1918, approximately 17 million had died, four empires were shattered, Europe's map was redrawn, and conditions were created that would lead to the Second World War.

key_concepts.definition: The Triple Alliance was a secret military pact formed in 1882 between Germany, Austria-Hungary, and Italy, agreeing to support each other if attacked by France or Russia. Italy did not join the war in 1914 (claiming it was offensive, not defensive) and later joined the Allied Powers in 1915. The alliance was one of the key structures whose activation turned a regional dispute into a world war.

The Triple Entente was an alliance between France, Russia, and Britain formed through the Entente Cordiale (1904) and Russia-France alliance. Not a formal military pact but a series of understandings, the Entente powers joined by Italy, Japan, and the USA eventually became the Allied Powers that defeated the Central Powers in 1918.

The Schlieffen Plan was Germany's military strategy for a two-front war, calling for rapid defeat of France through Belgium before turning to fight Russia. Germany's violation of Belgian neutrality brought Britain into the war, and the plan's failure to quickly defeat France led to the four-year Western Front stalemate.

Trench warfare was the dominant form of combat on the Western Front, where both sides dug hundreds of miles of parallel trenches and lived in them for years. Soldiers faced muddy, rat-infested conditions, constant artillery bombardment, disease, and the horror of going 'over the top' into no man's land. The stalemate resulted in enormous casualties for minimal territorial gains.

The Treaty of Versailles formally ended WWI between Germany and the Allied Powers. Germany was forced to accept sole war responsibility ('war guilt clause'), pay massive reparations, cede territory, and drastically reduce its military. The harsh terms created deep resentment in Germany that Hitler later exploited, making the treaty a significant cause of World War II.

The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, heir to the Austro-Hungarian throne, and his wife in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, by Gavrilo Princip, a Bosnian-Serb nationalist, was WWI's immediate trigger. Austria-Hungary blamed Serbia; Serbia's partial rejection of the ultimatum led Austria-Hungary to declare war, activating the alliance system that drew in major powers within weeks.

The League of Nations was an international organization established in 1920 by the Treaty of Versailles, intended to prevent future wars through collective security. The USA never joined (Congress rejected membership), and the League ultimately failed to prevent WWII due to lack of enforcement mechanisms and withdrawal of major members like Germany and Japan.

The mandates system placed former German and Ottoman territories under Allied administration after WWI. Britain received Palestine, Transjordan, and Iraq; France received Syria and Lebanon. The system was criticized as thinly disguised colonialism that redistributed territories from defeated powers to victorious ones.

key_points: The immediate cause of WWI was the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914; underlying causes were nationalism, imperialism, militarism, and alliance systems.
The two opposing sides were Allied Powers (Britain, France, Russia, Italy, USA) and Central Powers (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Ottoman Empire, Bulgaria).
Germany's violation of Belgian neutrality through the Schlieffen Plan brought Britain into the war on August 4, 1914.
The Western Front settled into trench warfare — stalemate, enormous casualties, and new weapons including poison gas, tanks, and aircraft.
The Battle of the Somme (1916) was one of the deadliest battles in history, with over one million casualties on both sides in a few months.
The USA entered the war in April 1917 after Germany resumed unrestricted submarine warfare and the Zimmermann Telegram (proposing a German-Mexican alliance against the USA) was revealed.
The Russian Revolution of 1917 led Russia to sign the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk with Germany, withdrawing from the war on the Eastern Front.
Germany signed the Armistice on November 11, 1918 — at the 11th hour, on the 11th day, of the 11th month.
The Treaty of Versailles (1919) blamed Germany for the war, imposed heavy reparations, territorial losses, and military restrictions.
The war destroyed four empires — German, Austro-Hungarian, Russian, and Ottoman — and redrew the map of Europe and the Middle East.

10|history_civics|history_9_Rise_of_Dict.overview: The aftermath of World War I created conditions across Europe that allowed rise of totalitarian dictatorships. Economic collapse, mass unemployment, political instability, national humiliation (in Germany), and the perceived failure of liberal democracy made extreme movements attractive to desperate populations. Fascism under Mussolini in Italy and Nazism under Hitler in Germany were radical nationalist movements that glorified the state, rejected democracy and Marxism, and promoted violence as a political tool. These ideologies led directly to World War II through aggressive foreign policies and territorial expansion.

key_concepts.definition: Fascism is a far-right authoritarian ideology that places extreme emphasis on the nation, glorifies violence and struggle, rejects parliamentary democracy, and seeks a totalitarian state. Originating in Italy under Mussolini (from Latin 'fasces' — a bundle of rods symbolizing strength through unity), fascism opposed both liberal capitalism and Marxist communism. It promoted a corporatist state replacing class conflict with national solidarity.

Nazism was the ideology of Hitler's National Socialist German Workers' Party (NSDAP), combining extreme German nationalism, virulent anti-Semitism, racial hierarchy (Aryan supremacy), territorial expansionism (Lebensraum), and totalitarian state control. Nazism was responsible for the Holocaust and the aggressive foreign policy that triggered World War II.

Benito Mussolini was the founder of Italian Fascism and dictator of Italy (1922–1943). He organized the March on Rome in 1922, forcing King Victor Emmanuel III to appoint him Prime Minister. As Il Duce, he suppressed opposition, controlled the press, and pursued aggressive imperialism in Africa. He allied with Hitler and entered World War II in 1940.

Adolf Hitler was the leader (Führer) of Nazi Germany from 1933 to 1945. Born in Austria, he served in WWI, joined the Nazi Party, and attempted the Beer Hall Putsch in 1923. Becoming Chancellor in 1933 and President in 1934, he rapidly consolidated power, rearmed Germany, launched the Holocaust, and initiated WWII. He died by suicide on April 30, 1945.

The Holocaust was the systematic, state-sponsored genocide of approximately six million Jews by the Nazi regime (1933–1945), along with millions of other victims including Roma, disabled people, Slavic peoples, and political opponents. Jews were transported to extermination camps — Auschwitz-Birkenau, Treblinka, Sobibor — where they were murdered. The Holocaust is the largest genocide of the 20th century.

Lebensraum ('living space') was a key Nazi ideology holding that Germany needed to expand eastward into Slavic territories to provide land and resources for the Aryan race. Hitler used this to justify aggression against Poland, the Soviet Union, and other Eastern European states, resulting in invasion of the USSR in 1941 and mass murder of Slavic peoples and Jews.

Anti-Semitism is prejudice against, hatred of, or discrimination against Jews as a religious or ethnic group. Under the Nazis, anti-Semitism became official state policy — the Nuremberg Laws of 1935 stripped Jews of German citizenship, prohibited marriage between Jews and non-Jews, and systematically excluded them from economic and social life. State-sponsored anti-Semitism escalated to genocide.

Totalitarianism is a system of government that seeks to control every aspect of public and private life — political, economic, social, and cultural. Both Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy were totalitarian states that abolished opposition parties, controlled the press and education, used secret police and terror to suppress dissent, and demanded complete loyalty to the state and its leader.

key_points: Conditions for totalitarian regimes were created by WWI's aftermath: economic depression, mass unemployment, political instability, and wounded national pride.
In Italy, Mussolini founded the Fascist movement in 1919, exploiting fear of communist revolution and national humiliation over the post-war peace settlement.
Mussolini's 'March on Rome' in October 1922 intimidated King Victor Emmanuel III into appointing him Prime Minister; he then dismantled democracy and established a dictatorship.
In Germany, the harsh Treaty of Versailles, hyperinflation in the 1920s, and the Great Depression of 1929 created conditions for Hitler's rise.
Hitler's Nazi Party won the 1932 elections; he became Chancellor in January 1933 and used the Reichstag Fire as pretext to pass the Enabling Act giving him dictatorial powers.
The Nuremberg Laws (1935) stripped Jews of German citizenship and began systematic legal persecution that escalated to genocide.
Hitler's foreign aggressions — Rhineland (1936), Austria (1938), Sudetenland (1938) — were appeased by Britain and France under the Munich Agreement of 1938.
The Spanish Civil War (1936–39) served as a proving ground for German and Italian military equipment and tactics, both supporting Franco's Nationalist forces.
The Rome-Berlin Axis (1936) and Anti-Comintern Pact (1936, also joined by Japan) formed the alliance that became the Axis Powers in WWII.
Fascism and Nazism glorified violence, militarism, racial superiority, and the concept of a strong leader (Duce/Führer) above the law.

10|history_civics|history_10_The_Second_.overview: The Second World War (1939–1945) was the most destructive conflict in human history, involving over 30 countries and resulting in an estimated 70–85 million deaths, more than half civilians. It began on September 1, 1939, when Nazi Germany invaded Poland, prompting Britain and France to declare war. The conflict spread to encompass Europe, North Africa, the Soviet Union, and the Asia-Pacific. The war ended with Germany's surrender on May 8, 1945 (V-E Day) and Japan's surrender on September 2, 1945 (V-J Day), following the US atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki. The war ended European colonial dominance and led to the Cold War between the USA and Soviet Union.

key_concepts.definition: Blitzkrieg ('lightning war') was Germany's military strategy of rapid, coordinated attacks using tanks, aircraft, and motorized infantry to overwhelm defenses before they could consolidate. Used successfully against Poland (1939) and France (1940), it created breakthroughs far faster than WWI trench warfare, relying on speed, surprise, and psychological shock.

The Battle of Britain was the air campaign fought by the German Luftwaffe against Britain's Royal Air Force from July to October 1940. History's first major battle fought entirely by air forces, it was intended to destroy British air power as a prelude to invasion. Britain's victory, aided by radar and superior aircraft, prevented a German invasion and was a major turning point.

Operation Barbarossa was Germany's massive invasion of the Soviet Union launched on June 22, 1941 — the largest military operation in history. Hitler's decision violated the Nazi-Soviet Non-Aggression Pact and opened a catastrophic two-front war. Initial German advances were stunning, but Soviet resistance and the harsh Russian winter led to catastrophic German defeats at Stalingrad and Kursk.

Pearl Harbor was the US naval base in Hawaii attacked by the Japanese Imperial Navy on December 7, 1941 — a surprise attack that sank or damaged 19 ships, destroyed 188 aircraft, and killed over 2,400 Americans. It brought the United States into WWII; Congress declared war on Japan the next day, and Germany and Italy declared war on the USA shortly after.

D-Day (June 6, 1944) was when Allied forces launched Operation Overlord — the largest seaborne invasion in history — landing over 150,000 troops on the beaches of Normandy, France. Planned by General Eisenhower, the successful invasion opened a major Western Front in Europe and began liberation of Western Europe from Nazi occupation.

During WWII, the Nazi regime systematically murdered approximately six million Jews and millions of others in extermination camps across occupied Europe. Jews were transported from across the continent to camps like Auschwitz-Birkenau and Treblinka and killed in gas chambers. The Holocaust is the most documented genocide in history and a defining moral catastrophe of the 20th century.

Hiroshima and Nagasaki were Japanese cities targeted by the United States with atomic bombs — Hiroshima on August 6, 1945, and Nagasaki on August 9. The bombings killed an estimated 130,000–226,000 people and caused Japan to surrender unconditionally on August 15, 1945. They remain the only use of nuclear weapons in warfare and opened the nuclear age.

The Nazi-Soviet Non-Aggression Pact, signed August 23, 1939, was a secret agreement between Hitler and Stalin in which they pledged not to attack each other and secretly divided Eastern Europe into spheres of influence. It allowed Germany to invade Poland without fearing a Soviet counterattack. Hitler violated the pact in June 1941 with Operation Barbarossa.

key_points: WWII began September 1, 1939, with Germany's invasion of Poland using Blitzkrieg tactics; Britain and France declared war on Germany on September 3.
The Nazi-Soviet Non-Aggression Pact (August 1939) allowed Germany to initially avoid a two-front war.
France fell to Germany in June 1940; Britain under Churchill held out alone through the Battle of Britain (July–October 1940).
Hitler's invasion of the Soviet Union (Operation Barbarossa, June 1941) ultimately proved disastrous — the Battle of Stalingrad (1942–43) was the decisive Eastern Front turning point.
Japan's attack on Pearl Harbor (December 7, 1941) brought the USA into the war; the USA's industrial power proved decisive for the Allied victory.
The Allied strategy of 'Germany First' focused resources on defeating Nazi Germany in Europe before concentrating fully on Japan in the Pacific.
D-Day (June 6, 1944) opened the Western Front in France; Paris was liberated in August 1944 and Allied forces crossed into Germany by early 1945.
Hitler died by suicide on April 30, 1945; Germany surrendered unconditionally on May 8, 1945 (V-E Day).
The USA dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima (August 6) and Nagasaki (August 9), 1945; Japan surrendered September 2, 1945 (V-J Day).
The war resulted in ~70–85 million deaths, the Holocaust, and reshaping of the world order with the USA and USSR as dominant superpowers, leading to the Cold War.

10|history_civics|history_11_United_Nati.overview: The United Nations (UN) was established on October 24, 1945, after WWII, with the aim of preventing future global conflicts and promoting international cooperation. The UN Charter was signed by 51 founding member nations at the San Francisco Conference on June 26, 1945. The organization replaced the failed League of Nations, granting the Security Council real enforcement powers. The UN's founding principles include sovereign equality of all member states, peaceful settlement of disputes, non-interference in internal affairs, and collective security. Today the UN has 193 member states and operates as the primary international forum for global governance.

key_concepts.definition: The UN Charter is the foundational treaty of the United Nations, signed on June 26, 1945, by 50 nations (Poland signed later, making 51). It defines the purposes and principles of the UN, establishes its main organs, and sets out member state rights and obligations. The Charter prohibits the threat or use of force against other states and authorizes the Security Council to take collective action to maintain international peace and security.

The General Assembly is the main deliberative body of the UN in which all 193 member states have equal representation with one vote each. It discusses and makes recommendations on any matter within the UN Charter, approves the UN budget, and elects members to other UN bodies. Important questions require a two-thirds majority; the General Assembly can pass resolutions but unlike the Security Council cannot issue binding decisions.

The Security Council is the UN's primary body for maintaining international peace and security, with power to adopt binding resolutions. It has 15 members — 5 permanent (USA, Russia, China, France, UK) and 10 non-permanent elected for two-year terms. Each permanent member holds veto power over substantive decisions, which has often prevented decisive action.

Veto power is the right of each of the five permanent members of the UN Security Council to block any substantive resolution, regardless of how many other members support it. A single veto prevents the Council from acting. The veto has been used hundreds of times, often by the USA and USSR/Russia during the Cold War to block resolutions unfavorable to their interests.

The International Court of Justice is the principal judicial organ of the UN, based in The Hague, Netherlands. It settles legal disputes between member states and gives advisory opinions on legal questions. The Court has 15 judges elected for nine-year terms by the General Assembly and Security Council. All UN member states are automatically parties to the ICJ statute.

The Secretariat is the administrative organ of the UN, headed by the Secretary-General who is appointed by the General Assembly on the recommendation of the Security Council for a five-year renewable term. The Secretary-General acts as chief administrative officer and can bring situations threatening international peace to the Security Council's attention.

The UDHR was adopted by the UN General Assembly on December 10, 1948, proclaiming 30 fundamental rights to which all humans are entitled regardless of nationality, race, or religion. These include the right to life, liberty, freedom from torture, freedom of expression, and right to education. Though not legally binding, the UDHR is the foundation of international human rights law.

ECOSOC is the principal body for coordinating the economic and social work of the UN and its specialized agencies. It has 54 members elected by the General Assembly for three-year terms. ECOSOC oversees the work of specialized agencies like WHO, UNESCO, and FAO and coordinates international cooperation on economic, social, cultural, educational, and health matters.

key_points: The United Nations was established on October 24, 1945 — celebrated annually as United Nations Day — with 51 founding member states.
The UN replaced the League of Nations, which had failed to prevent WWII; the UN corrected weaknesses by giving the Security Council binding enforcement powers.
The UN's four main purposes: maintaining international peace and security, developing friendly relations among nations, promoting international cooperation, and harmonizing nations' actions.
The five principal organs are: General Assembly, Security Council, International Court of Justice, Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC), and the Secretariat.
The Security Council has 5 permanent members with veto power (USA, Russia, China, France, UK) and 10 rotating non-permanent members elected for 2-year terms.
The veto power of the P5 has been controversial, often preventing the UN from acting decisively in conflicts where a P5 member has interests at stake.
India became a UN member on October 30, 1945, and has been a strong supporter of multilateralism and has contributed significantly to UN peacekeeping operations.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) is one of the UN's most significant achievements, establishing global human rights standards.
The UN has six official languages: Arabic, Chinese, English, French, Russian, and Spanish.
By 2024, the UN has 193 member states; the Vatican and Palestine have observer status.

10|history_civics|history_12_Major_Agenc.overview: The United Nations operates through a network of specialized agencies, programs, and funds addressing specific global issues from health and education to trade, finance, and refugee protection. These agencies are autonomous organizations linked to the UN through special agreements, each with its own budget, membership, and governing bodies. They work in coordination with the UN Secretariat and are overseen by ECOSOC. Major agencies include WHO, UNICEF, UNESCO, the World Bank, the IMF, and the FAO. Together these bodies form the 'UN system' implementing the organization's mandates across virtually every aspect of human development.

key_concepts.definition: The WHO is the UN's specialized agency for international public health, established in 1948 and headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland. It coordinates international responses to disease outbreaks (COVID-19, Ebola, HIV/AIDS), sets global health standards, provides technical assistance, and conducts health research. WHO played a central role in eradicating smallpox (declared in 1980) and is working toward polio eradication.

UNICEF (United Nations International Children's Emergency Fund) was established in 1946 to provide relief to children in post-war Europe and later expanded to children worldwide. It works to protect children's rights, promote education, provide nutrition and healthcare, and protect children from violence and exploitation. UNICEF operates in over 190 countries and is funded entirely by voluntary contributions.

UNESCO (United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization), established in 1945, promotes peace through international cooperation in education, science, and culture. It designates World Heritage Sites, promotes universal education, supports press freedom, and coordinates international scientific research. Its constitution states: 'Since wars begin in the minds of men, it is in the minds of men that the defences of peace must be constructed.'

The World Bank is an international financial institution providing loans and grants to developing countries for capital programs aimed at reducing poverty and supporting development. It comprises the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) and the International Development Association (IDA). The World Bank funds infrastructure, education, health, and economic reform projects in developing nations.

The IMF is an international organization of 190 member countries fostering global monetary cooperation, financial stability, and sustainable economic growth. It provides short-term loans to countries facing balance of payments crises and monitors the global economic system. The IMF is headquartered in Washington D.C.; membership in the IMF is a prerequisite for membership in the World Bank.

The FAO is a UN specialized agency, founded in 1945 and headquartered in Rome, Italy, whose mandate is to defeat hunger worldwide. It works to improve agricultural productivity, ensure food security, promote sustainable management of natural resources, and assist in rural development. Its motto is 'Fiat Panis' (Let There Be Bread).

The UNHCR (United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees) is the UN agency mandated to protect and assist refugees, asylum seekers, and stateless people worldwide. Founded in 1950, it provides emergency relief, advocates for refugee rights under the 1951 Refugee Convention, and works toward durable solutions including repatriation, local integration, and resettlement.

The ILO is the only tripartite UN agency, with representatives from governments, employers, and workers. Founded in 1919 (predating the UN) and headquartered in Geneva, it sets international labour standards, promotes workers' rights, encourages decent employment opportunities, and enhances social protection. The ILO was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1969.

key_points: The UN specialized agencies are autonomous organizations with their own budgets and governance, linked to the UN through agreements with ECOSOC.
WHO, established in 1948, coordinates global health responses and is responsible for eradicating smallpox and working toward polio eradication.
UNICEF focuses exclusively on children's welfare worldwide, working on education, nutrition, healthcare, and protection from exploitation.
UNESCO promotes education, science, and culture; it designates World Heritage Sites and promotes press freedom and universal literacy.
The World Bank and IMF, both established at the Bretton Woods Conference (1944), form the backbone of the international financial system.
FAO combats hunger and food insecurity and is headquartered in Rome, Italy.
UNHCR protects over 100 million displaced people worldwide under the 1951 Refugee Convention.
The ILO is the oldest UN agency (founded 1919), promoting workers' rights with a unique tripartite structure of governments, employers, and workers.
Other important UN agencies include the WTO (trade), UNDP (development), and UNEP (environmental protection).
India is an active member of all major UN agencies and has contributed significantly to UN peacekeeping operations globally.

10|history_civics|history_13_Non-Aligned.overview: The Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) was founded in 1961 at the Belgrade Conference as a grouping of states refusing to formally align with either the Western (NATO/USA) or Eastern (Warsaw Pact/USSR) blocs during the Cold War. India's Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru, Yugoslavia's President Josip Broz Tito, and Egypt's President Gamal Abdel Nasser were the principal founders, guided by Panchsheel (five principles of peaceful coexistence). NAM provided developing nations a collective voice in international affairs, advocating for decolonization, disarmament, and a new international economic order. India was among its founding members and most vocal advocates.

key_concepts.definition: The Non-Aligned Movement is a forum of developing world states that declared themselves not formally aligned with or against any major power bloc during the Cold War. Founded at the Belgrade Summit in 1961, it sought a 'third way' between Western capitalism and Soviet communism. NAM members supported decolonization, disarmament, economic development, and reform of the international economic order.

Panchsheel, or the Five Principles of Peaceful Coexistence, was first articulated in the Preamble to the India-China Agreement on Tibet in April 1954. The five principles are: mutual respect for territorial integrity and sovereignty; mutual non-aggression; mutual non-interference in internal affairs; equality and mutual benefit; and peaceful co-existence. Panchsheel became the philosophical foundation of NAM.

The Cold War was the period of geopolitical tension between the United States and the Soviet Union from approximately 1947 to 1991, characterized by ideological rivalry (capitalism vs. communism), arms race, proxy wars in developing countries, and constant threat of nuclear conflict, but never direct military confrontation between the superpowers.

Jawaharlal Nehru was India's first Prime Minister (1947–1964) and one of the principal architects of the Non-Aligned Movement. He articulated the philosophy of non-alignment — that newly independent nations should pursue an independent foreign policy rather than becoming pawns in the Cold War struggle. Nehru's vision was influenced by anti-imperialism, commitment to peaceful coexistence, and belief in the collective voice of newly decolonized nations.

The Bandung Conference was the first large-scale Asian-African conference held in Bandung, Indonesia in April 1955, attended by 29 countries. It was a precursor to NAM, bringing together newly independent Asian and African nations to discuss peace, anti-colonialism, and non-alignment. The conference adopted the Bandung Principles that were later incorporated into NAM.

The Belgrade Summit of 1961 was the first summit conference of the Non-Aligned Movement, held in the Yugoslav capital with 25 founding member states. Attended by Tito, Nehru, Nasser, Nkrumah (Ghana), and Sukarno (Indonesia), it formally established NAM and issued a declaration calling for peace, disarmament, and the rights of all peoples to self-determination.

The NIEO was a set of proposals put forward by developing countries through NAM and the UN General Assembly in the 1970s to reform the international economic system in their favour. Demands included better prices for raw material exports, debt relief, technology transfer, and greater voting power in the IMF and World Bank. Though never fully implemented, it influenced later discussions on development and global governance.

Decolonization refers to the process by which colonies gained independence from European powers, primarily in Asia and Africa, after World War II. The NAM was closely associated with the decolonization movement, supporting independence struggles of colonies against European powers and condemning apartheid in South Africa. By the 1970s, decolonization was largely complete, transforming UN membership.

key_points: The Non-Aligned Movement was formally founded at the Belgrade Summit in September 1961, with 25 founding members including India, Egypt, Yugoslavia, and Ghana.
The three main founders were Jawaharlal Nehru (India), Gamal Abdel Nasser (Egypt), and Josip Broz Tito (Yugoslavia) — sometimes called the 'founding trinity' of NAM.
NAM's key principles include non-alignment with either superpower bloc, peaceful coexistence, opposition to colonialism and imperialism, and support for decolonization.
Panchsheel (Five Principles of Peaceful Coexistence), first enunciated in the 1954 India-China agreement, became the philosophical basis of NAM.
The Bandung Conference (1955) of 29 Asian-African nations was the direct precursor to NAM, establishing the framework of Third World solidarity.
India was a founding member and prominent voice of NAM — Nehru's concept of non-alignment shaped India's foreign policy for decades.
NAM grew from 25 members in 1961 to over 120 member states by the Cold War's end, representing the majority of the world's nations and population.
NAM advocated for the New International Economic Order (NIEO) in the 1970s, demanding fairer terms of trade and economic relations for developing nations.
After the Cold War ended with the Soviet Union's collapse in 1991, NAM's relevance was questioned, but it continues to hold summits and advocate on development issues.
India continues to be a member of NAM, which now focuses on climate change, sustainable development, and reform of international institutions.

10|history_civics|civics_1_The_Union_Par.overview: The Union Parliament is the supreme legislative body of India, established by Part V of the Indian Constitution. It consists of two houses — the Rajya Sabha (Council of States/Upper House) and the Lok Sabha (House of the People/Lower House) — together with the President of India. Parliament derives its authority from the Constitution and exercises legislative power over subjects in the Union List and Concurrent List. Parliament not only makes laws but also controls the executive through various mechanisms, approves the national budget, amends the Constitution, and serves as the forum for national debate on major issues.

key_concepts.definition: The Lok Sabha (House of the People) is the lower house of India's Parliament and the primary legislative chamber. It has a maximum strength of 543 elected members, all directly elected by adult suffrage from single-member constituencies. The Lok Sabha has a normal term of 5 years and can be dissolved earlier by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister. It has primacy over financial legislation.

The Rajya Sabha (Council of States) is the upper house of India's Parliament and a permanent body that cannot be dissolved. It has a maximum of 250 members — 238 elected by state and union territory legislatures, and 12 nominated by the President for distinguished contributions to arts, science, literature, or social service. Members serve 6-year terms, with one-third retiring every two years.

A Money Bill (defined under Article 110) deals exclusively with taxation, appropriation of funds from the Consolidated Fund of India, and government borrowing. Money Bills can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha; the Rajya Sabha has only 14 days to return it with recommendations, which the Lok Sabha may accept or reject. The Speaker of the Lok Sabha certifies whether a bill is a Money Bill.

A joint sitting of both Houses can be convened by the President under Article 108 when there is a deadlock between the two houses over an ordinary bill. It is presided over by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha. Joint sittings have been convened only three times in Indian history — for the Dowry Prohibition Bill (1961), Banking Service Commission Bill (1978), and Prevention of Terrorism Bill (2002).

Question Hour is the first hour of each parliamentary sitting during which members may ask ministers oral or written questions about matters of public importance and government activities. Starred questions require oral answers (allowing supplementary questions); unstarred questions receive written answers. Question Hour is a crucial mechanism for parliamentary oversight of the executive.

A no-confidence motion expresses lack of confidence in the Council of Ministers. If passed by a majority in the Lok Sabha, the Prime Minister and entire Council of Ministers must resign. Only the Lok Sabha can pass a no-confidence motion — not the Rajya Sabha. At least 50 members must support its introduction.

Parliament has the power to amend the Constitution under Article 368. Most amendments require a special majority — two-thirds of members present and voting in each House, as well as a majority of total membership. Amendments affecting the federal structure additionally require ratification by at least half of all state legislatures.

Parliamentary committees are smaller bodies of MPs that perform detailed legislative and oversight work. Key committees include the Public Accounts Committee (examining government expenditure), Estimates Committee (examining budget estimates), and Committee on Public Undertakings. Standing committees examine bills and ministry activities in detail before they are considered by the full House.

key_points: Parliament of India consists of two Houses: Lok Sabha (lower house, directly elected) and Rajya Sabha (upper house, elected by state legislatures) plus the President.
The Lok Sabha has 543 elected members, all directly elected by adult franchise; its normal term is 5 years but can be dissolved earlier by the President.
The Rajya Sabha is a permanent body with maximum 250 members (238 elected, 12 nominated); members serve 6-year staggered terms.
Money Bills can only originate in the Lok Sabha; the Rajya Sabha can only make recommendations which the Lok Sabha may accept or reject within 14 days.
A joint sitting of Parliament can be called by the President to resolve a deadlock on an ordinary bill; presided over by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha.
Parliament has exclusive power to make laws on subjects in the Union List and shares power with state legislatures on the Concurrent List.
Parliament controls the executive through Question Hour, No-Confidence Motions, budget approval, and various parliamentary committees.
The Speaker of the Lok Sabha is elected by members of the Lok Sabha; the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha is the Vice-President of India.
A no-confidence motion passed by the Lok Sabha requires the Prime Minister and the entire Council of Ministers to resign.
Constitutional amendments under Article 368 generally require a special majority in both Houses; some amendments also require ratification by at least half the state legislatures.

10|history_civics|civics_2_The_Union_Exe.overview: The Union Executive of India consists of the President, the Vice-President, the Prime Minister, and the Council of Ministers. The President is the constitutional head of the Union executive and holds the highest office in India. India has a parliamentary system in which the President acts on the advice of the Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister, making the President's role largely ceremonial though constitutionally significant. The Vice-President serves as ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha and acts as President in the President's absence.

key_concepts.definition: The President is the constitutional head of the Republic of India, elected indirectly by an Electoral College comprising elected members of both Houses of Parliament and the Legislative Assemblies of all States. The President holds office for a term of 5 years and is eligible for re-election. In practice, the President acts on the advice of the Council of Ministers, but retains important discretionary powers in certain constitutional situations.

The President is elected by an Electoral College consisting of elected members of both Houses of Parliament and elected members of all State Legislative Assemblies. Votes are weighted to ensure proportional representation — each MLA's vote is worth units based on the state's population, and each MP's vote is worth the total weighted votes of all MLAs divided by total MPs.

The President has three types of veto over legislation: absolute veto (refusing assent), suspensive veto (returning the bill for reconsideration — must give assent if Parliament passes it again), and pocket veto (taking no action indefinitely). For Money Bills, the President cannot withhold assent after it is passed again by Parliament; for Constitutional Amendment Bills, assent is mandatory.

Under Article 72, the President has the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites, or remissions of punishment. This power extends to cases involving court-martials, sentences under central law, and death sentences — crucially, only the President (not the Governor) can pardon a death sentence. The President exercises this power on the advice of the Council of Ministers.

The Vice-President is the second highest constitutional office in India, serving as ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha. Elected by members of both Houses of Parliament (unlike the President, whose election also involves state legislators), the Vice-President holds office for 5 years. The Vice-President acts as President when the office falls vacant or the President is unable to perform duties.

The President can proclaim three types of emergencies: National Emergency (Article 352) due to war, external aggression, or armed rebellion; President's Rule (Article 356) when constitutional governance fails in a state; and Financial Emergency (Article 360) when India's financial stability is threatened. During a National Emergency, the President can exercise state legislative powers and fundamental rights can be suspended.

The President can be removed through impeachment under Article 61 for violation of the Constitution. The process must be initiated by either House with a 14-day notice signed by at least one-quarter of total members. The charge must be investigated by the other House, and the President stands impeached if two-thirds of total membership of each House supports the charge. No President of India has ever been impeached.

While the President generally acts on advice of the Council of Ministers, some discretion may be exercised: appointing a Prime Minister when no party has a clear majority, referring a bill back to Parliament for reconsideration once, and declining to dissolve the Lok Sabha if the Prime Minister who has lost a no-confidence vote requests dissolution.

key_points: The President is elected by an Electoral College of elected MPs and MLAs through a system of single transferable vote with proportional representation.
Presidential qualifications: Indian citizen, at least 35 years old, qualified for Lok Sabha membership, must not hold any office of profit.
The President serves a 5-year term and can be re-elected; serves as Supreme Commander of India's armed forces.
The President appoints the Prime Minister, Governors of States, judges of Supreme Court and High Courts, the Attorney General, and the Comptroller and Auditor General.
The Vice-President is elected by members of both Houses of Parliament sitting together — state legislatures do not participate.
The Vice-President as Chairman of the Rajya Sabha presides over its sessions, maintains order, and decides procedural matters.
The President's pardon power (Article 72) is the only authority that can pardon death sentences — the Governor cannot pardon death sentences.
Under Article 356 (President's Rule), the President assumes executive functions of a state if constitutional governance breaks down.
The 42nd Constitutional Amendment (1976) made the President bound to act on Cabinet advice; the 44th Amendment (1978) allows the President to return advice once but must accept it if sent again.
The President has the power to summon, prorogue, and dissolve the Lok Sabha, and to address and send messages to both Houses.

10|history_civics|civics_3_The_Prime_Min.overview: The Prime Minister is the real head of the Indian government and exercises executive power in practice, making India a parliamentary democracy where the elected executive governs. The Prime Minister is appointed by the President and must command the confidence of the Lok Sabha. The Council of Ministers, headed by the Prime Minister, is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha under Article 75(3). The Cabinet — the inner circle of the Council of Ministers — is the most powerful body in India's governmental structure, taking all major policy decisions.

key_concepts.definition: The Prime Minister is the head of government in India and the leader of the Council of Ministers. Appointed by the President, the Prime Minister must enjoy the confidence of the Lok Sabha majority. The Prime Minister advises the President on appointment of ministers, determines Cabinet composition, coordinates government activities, and is the principal link between the Cabinet and the President.

The Council of Ministers consists of all ministers in the Union Government in three categories: Cabinet Ministers (senior ministers heading important ministries), Ministers of State (with independent charge or attached to Cabinet Ministers), and Deputy Ministers (assisting Cabinet or State Ministers). The Council is appointed by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister and is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.

The Cabinet is the inner circle of the Council of Ministers, comprising senior ministers who head important departments. The most powerful executive body in India, it makes all major policy decisions, presents bills to Parliament, and coordinates government policy. Cabinet meetings are presided over by the Prime Minister, and decisions are made by consensus.

Collective responsibility (Article 75) means all ministers are jointly responsible to the Lok Sabha as a body. If the Lok Sabha passes a vote of no-confidence, all ministers must resign collectively. This ensures ministerial solidarity — a minister who disagrees with Cabinet policy must either publicly support it or resign from the Cabinet.

Individual responsibility means each minister is individually responsible to the Prime Minister and to Parliament for the actions of their own ministry. A minister can be asked to resign for personal misconduct or departmental failure even if the government retains the Lok Sabha's confidence. The President can remove a minister on the advice of the Prime Minister.
