Cholera: contaminated water/food; rice-water stools; ORS is the primary treatment — replenishing lost fluids and electrolytes saves lives.
HIV/AIDS: destroys CD4+ T cells, no cure, managed with antiretroviral therapy (ART); transmitted sexually, through blood, or mother to child.
Vaccination creates immunological memory — the immune system mounts faster, stronger responses to subsequent exposures to the same pathogen.
The 'Germ Theory of Disease' — established by Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch — is the foundation of modern infectious disease understanding.

Koch's Postulates (criteria for proving a microorganism causes a disease): pathogen found in all cases; isolated in pure culture; causes disease when introduced to healthy host; re-isolated from experimental host.
Herd immunity: when a sufficient proportion of a population is immune to a disease (through vaccination or prior infection), it protects even those who are not immune.
Antibiotic resistance is caused by misuse/overuse of antibiotics — MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) and drug-resistant TB are major public health crises.

9|biology|biology_11_Food_and_Health.overview: Nutrition and health are intimately connected — a balanced diet provides the nutrients needed for growth, energy, immune function, and prevention of disease. ICSE Class 9 covers the macronutrients (carbohydrates, proteins, fats) and micronutrients (vitamins, minerals) required by the human body, their food sources, and the consequences of deficiency. A balanced diet contains all nutrients in the right proportions. Malnutrition — from either deficiency or excess — causes serious health problems. Understanding food composition and nutritional requirements helps students make informed dietary choices.

key_concepts.definition: Carbohydrates are the primary energy source, providing 4 kcal/g. Simple carbohydrates (sugars): glucose, fructose, sucrose, lactose — quickly absorbed. Complex carbohydrates (starches): in cereals, tubers — digested slowly, providing sustained energy. Dietary fibre (cellulose): aids digestion and prevents constipation. Excess carbohydrates are converted to fat and stored in adipose tissue.

Proteins are composed of amino acids and provide 4 kcal/g. They are essential for: growth and repair of tissues, enzyme and hormone production, immune function (antibodies), and transport (haemoglobin). Complete proteins (containing all essential amino acids): animal products (meat, eggs, milk). Incomplete proteins: most plant sources. Protein deficiency causes Kwashiorkor (protein deficiency in young children) and Marasmus (both calorie and protein deficiency).

Fats provide 9 kcal/g and are the most energy-dense macronutrient. Functions: energy storage, insulation, organ protection, fat-soluble vitamin absorption, cell membrane structure. Saturated fats (solid at room temperature — butter, ghee) increase LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular disease risk. Unsaturated fats (liquid at room temperature — olive oil, fish oil) are healthier. Trans fats (hydrogenated oils) are most harmful.

Vitamins are organic compounds required in small amounts for various metabolic processes. Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) stored in body fat. Water-soluble vitamins (B complex, C) must be replenished regularly. Vitamin A (retinol): night vision, immune function; deficiency causes night blindness, xerophthalmia. Vitamin C (ascorbic acid): collagen synthesis, antioxidant; deficiency causes scurvy. Vitamin D: calcium absorption; deficiency causes rickets (children) or osteomalacia (adults).

Minerals are inorganic elements essential for body functions. Calcium: bone and teeth strength, muscle contraction, blood clotting; deficiency causes rickets, osteoporosis. Iron: haemoglobin synthesis (oxygen transport); deficiency causes iron-deficiency anaemia. Iodine: thyroid hormone synthesis; deficiency causes goitre and cretinism. Sodium: fluid balance, nerve transmission. Potassium: heart function, muscle contraction.

A balanced diet contains all essential nutrients in the correct proportions to meet the body's needs without excess or deficiency. Components: carbohydrates (45–65% of calories), proteins (10–35%), fats (20–35%), vitamins, minerals, water, and dietary fibre. Nutritional requirements vary with age, sex, activity level, and physiological state (pregnancy, lactation). The Indian Plate recommends cereals, pulses, vegetables, fruits, dairy, and proteins in appropriate proportions.

Malnutrition encompasses both undernutrition (deficiency of calories and/or nutrients) and overnutrition (excess calories leading to obesity). Undernutrition conditions: Marasmus (calorie and protein deficiency — wasting), Kwashiorkor (protein deficiency — swollen belly, edema), anaemia, vitamin deficiency diseases. Overnutrition leads to obesity, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and metabolic syndrome.

Water constitutes 60% of body weight and is essential for all metabolic reactions, temperature regulation, transport of nutrients and waste, and joint lubrication. The recommended intake is 2–3 litres per day. Dietary fibre (roughage) — cellulose, pectin, lignin from plant sources — is not digested but adds bulk to faeces, prevents constipation, reduces risk of colorectal cancer, and helps maintain healthy blood sugar and cholesterol levels.

key_points: Macronutrients provide energy: carbohydrates and proteins = 4 kcal/g; fats = 9 kcal/g.
Essential amino acids cannot be synthesized by the body and must come from diet (isoleucine, leucine, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, threonine, tryptophan, valine).
Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are stored in the liver and body fat; excess can cause toxicity. Water-soluble vitamins (B and C) are excreted in urine; regular intake needed.
Vitamin A: sources — liver, egg yolk, dairy, yellow/orange vegetables; deficiency — night blindness, xerophthalmia.
Vitamin C: sources — citrus fruits, amla, guava; deficiency — scurvy (bleeding gums, poor wound healing).
Vitamin D: synthesized by skin on UV exposure; sources — fish liver oil, fortified milk; deficiency — rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults).
Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional deficiency worldwide, causing microcytic hypochromic anaemia — pale skin, fatigue, breathlessness.
Iodine deficiency: goitre (enlarged thyroid gland) in adults; cretinism (stunted physical and mental development) in children born to iodine-deficient mothers.
Calcium sources: dairy products, green leafy vegetables, ragi; works with vitamin D for absorption.
A balanced diet prevents nutritional deficiency diseases; dietary diversity is the best strategy for ensuring adequate intake of all nutrients.

9|biology|biology_12_Health_and_Hygiene.overview: Health is defined by the World Health Organization as a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being, not merely the absence of disease. Hygiene refers to practices that maintain health and prevent the spread of disease. Personal hygiene, food hygiene, environmental hygiene, and community sanitation are all essential components of preventive healthcare. Understanding the relationship between hygiene, sanitation, and disease transmission helps individuals and communities adopt behaviors that protect health and reduce the burden of infectious diseases.

key_concepts.definition: The World Health Organization (WHO) defines health as 'a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity.' This holistic definition recognizes that true health encompasses physical fitness, mental wellness, and positive social relationships. It challenges the narrow 'disease-absence' model and emphasizes preventive and holistic approaches to healthcare.

Personal hygiene includes practices that maintain the cleanliness and health of an individual's body: regular handwashing (especially before eating, after using the toilet, and after contact with potentially contaminated surfaces), daily bathing, dental care, clean clothing, nail trimming, and safe food handling. Handwashing with soap is one of the most effective and inexpensive public health measures to prevent infection transmission.

Food hygiene involves safe handling, preparation, and storage of food to prevent foodborne illness. Principles include: cooking food to the right temperature to kill pathogens, storing food at safe temperatures (below 5°C or above 60°C), preventing cross-contamination between raw and cooked food, washing fruits and vegetables, and using safe drinking water. Poor food hygiene causes cholera, typhoid, salmonellosis, and food poisoning.

Environmental hygiene addresses the relationship between the physical environment and health. It includes: proper disposal of solid waste and sewage, prevention of waterlogging and stagnant water (mosquito breeding sites), access to clean and safe drinking water, proper sewage treatment, air quality management, and control of environmental pollutants. Environmental hygiene reduces vector-borne, waterborne, and airborne diseases.

Sanitation refers to the provision of facilities and services for the safe disposal of human urine and faeces. Adequate sanitation prevents fecal-oral transmission of diseases. Open defecation contaminates water and soil, spreading diseases like cholera, typhoid, and helminthic infections. The Swachh Bharat Mission in India aimed to achieve universal sanitation coverage by constructing toilets and ending open defecation.

National immunization programs provide vaccines to protect populations against infectious diseases. India's Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) provides free vaccines against: tuberculosis (BCG), polio (OPV), diphtheria, pertussis, tetanus (DPT), hepatitis B, measles, and others. Immunization is the most cost-effective public health intervention, having eradicated smallpox globally (1980) and significantly reducing polio, measles, and other diseases.

Access to safe, clean drinking water free from pathogens and chemical contaminants is essential for health. Waterborne diseases (cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A, amoebic dysentery) spread through contaminated water. Water purification methods: boiling (kills pathogens), chlorination (disinfection), filtration (removes particles), UV treatment, and reverse osmosis. The right to safe water is recognized as a basic human right.

Community health refers to the health of a population in a defined area. Community health activities include: disease surveillance and reporting, mass immunization campaigns, health education, maternal and child health programs, control of endemic diseases, and management of environmental health hazards. Community health workers (ASHA workers in India) play a crucial role in connecting communities with healthcare services.

key_points: WHO definition of health (1948): 'complete physical, mental and social well-being, not merely the absence of disease' — health is multidimensional.
Handwashing with soap and water for 20 seconds is among the most effective single public health measures to reduce infectious disease transmission.
The chain of infection: pathogen → source/reservoir → mode of transmission → portal of entry → susceptible host — breaking any link prevents disease.
Waterborne disease prevention: drink boiled or purified water, avoid open defecation near water sources, proper sewage treatment.
Food safety: cook thoroughly, refrigerate promptly, wash hands and surfaces, separate raw and cooked food, use safe water and raw materials.
India's immunization program (UIP) provides vaccines free at birth and during the first years of life against 12 serious diseases.
Smallpox was eradicated globally in 1977–80 through worldwide vaccination — the first disease to be completely eradicated by human effort.
Polio is nearly eradicated globally thanks to oral polio vaccine (OPV); India was declared polio-free in 2014.
Vector control measures: eliminating mosquito breeding sites (stagnant water), using insecticide-treated nets, spraying insecticides, draining swamps.
Open defecation is a major public health hazard — human feces contain pathogens that contaminate water and soil, causing diseases affecting millions, especially children.

9|physics|Measurements_and_Experimentati.overview: Length is the most fundamental measurement in physics, and choosing the right instrument determines the precision of the result. This chapter covers the instruments used across different length scales — metre rule for everyday distances, vernier calipers for millimetre precision, and micrometer screw gauge for sub-millimetre precision. Understanding how to read these instruments correctly, including least count and zero error, is an essential practical laboratory skill.

key_concepts.definition: Measures lengths from a few centimetres up to 1 m. Least count = 0.1 cm = 1 mm. Used for measuring lengths of objects, distances between points. The eye must be perpendicular to the scale to avoid parallax error.

Measures external and internal diameters, depths, and lengths more precisely than a metre rule. Least count = 0.1 mm = 0.01 cm. Has a main scale and a sliding vernier scale; the reading is the main scale reading plus (coinciding vernier division × least count).
Least count = 1 main scale division - 1 vernier scale division = 1 mm - (9/10) mm = 0.1 mm for a 10-division vernier. The least count is the smallest measurement the instrument can reliably make.

When jaws are fully closed, if the vernier zero is to the right of the main scale zero: positive zero error (subtract from reading). If to the left: negative zero error (add to reading). Corrected reading = observed reading - zero error.

Measures small lengths such as wire diameter, thickness of glass, etc. to 0.01 mm precision. Consists of a thimble (barrel) and sleeve. Least count = pitch/number of divisions = 0.5 mm/50 = 0.01 mm. Reading = sleeve reading + thimble reading.
The distance the thimble advances per complete revolution = 0.5 mm for a standard micrometer. The thimble has 50 divisions, so each division = 0.5/50 = 0.01 mm.

The apparent shift in position of a reading when the eye is not perpendicular to the scale. Eliminated by ensuring the eye is directly above (or level with) the scale marker. Parallax is not instrument error — it is reading error.

Accuracy: how close a measurement is to the true value. Precision: how reproducible the measurement is. An instrument can be precise (consistent readings) but inaccurate (systematic offset from true value due to zero error).

laws_and_formulas.name: Least Count (Vernier)
laws_and_formulas.formula: LC = 1 MSD - 1 VSD = 1 mm / n (for n-division vernier)
laws_and_formulas.description: Smallest measurable length with a vernier caliper (typically 0.1 mm).

laws_and_formulas.name: Micrometer Reading
laws_and_formulas.description: Total reading from a micrometer screw gauge.

key_points: Metre rule: LC = 1 mm. Vernier: LC = 0.1 mm. Micrometer: LC = 0.01 mm.
Vernier reading = main scale + (coinciding VSD number × LC).
Micrometer reading = sleeve (main) scale + thimble reading.
Positive zero error: subtract from reading. Negative zero error: add to reading.
Always check and record zero error before taking measurements.
Parallax error: eye must be perpendicular to scale.
Micrometer pitch = 0.5 mm; thimble has 50 divisions → LC = 0.01 mm.
Vernier calipers: measures internal/external diameters and depths.
Precision of result is limited by the least count of the instrument used.
Zero error is a systematic error — it affects all readings by the same fixed amount.

9|physics|Measurements_and_Experimentati.overview: Mass and weight are distinct quantities often confused in everyday language but treated precisely in physics. Mass is the amount of matter in an object (measured in kilograms), while weight is the gravitational force acting on that mass (measured in newtons). This chapter covers instruments for measuring mass (beam balance, spring balance), establishes the formula W = mg, and explores how weight varies with location while mass remains constant.

key_concepts.definition: The quantity of matter in an object. A scalar quantity measured in kilograms (kg). Mass is constant regardless of location — it does not change whether an object is on Earth, the Moon, or in space. Mass is an intrinsic property of matter.

The gravitational force acting on an object: W = mg. A vector quantity measured in newtons (N). Weight varies with location — on the Moon (g ≈ 1.6 m/s²), a 60 kg person weighs only ~96 N, compared to ~588 N on Earth (g ≈ 9.8 m/s²).

Measures mass by comparing the unknown mass against known standard masses placed in the other pan until the beam is level. Since both pans experience the same gravitational acceleration, a beam balance measures mass correctly regardless of g. Works on the Moon.

Measures weight (force) by the extension of a calibrated spring. Reads directly in newtons (or kilograms-force). Since it uses Hooke's Law (extension ∝ force), it measures weight and would give different readings on the Moon vs. Earth.
Weight W (N) = mass m (kg) × gravitational acceleration g (m/s²). On Earth's surface, g ≈ 9.8 m/s² ≈ 10 m/s² (approximate). On Moon, g ≈ 1.6 m/s²; Jupiter, g ≈ 24.8 m/s².

The resistance of a body to change in its state of motion or rest. Directly proportional to mass. A more massive object is harder to accelerate — mass is the measure of inertia. This is distinct from weight, which depends on gravity.

The acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface ≈ 9.8 m/s². Varies slightly with altitude (decreases) and latitude (slightly higher at poles due to Earth's shape and rotation). In problems, g = 10 m/s² is commonly used.

A physical balance (chemical balance) with fine pans and precision masses measures to 0.001 g. A top-pan electronic balance converts force on the pan directly to a digital mass reading using a load cell.

laws_and_formulas.name: Weight Formula
laws_and_formulas.description: Weight (N) = mass (kg) × gravitational acceleration (m/s²). On Earth, g ≈ 9.8 m/s² or 10 m/s².

key_points: Mass: amount of matter, measured in kg, constant everywhere.
Weight: gravitational force, measured in N, varies with location.
W = mg; on Earth g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (use 10 for rough calculations).
Beam balance measures mass (compares, independent of g).
Spring balance measures weight/force (depends on g).
On Moon (g = g_Earth/6): weight is 1/6 but mass is unchanged.
Inertia is measured by mass — not weight.
A 1 kg mass has a weight of approximately 9.8 N on Earth.
SI unit of force (including weight) is the newton (N).
Electronic balances are spring-balance type — they display mass but measure force.

9|physics|Measurements_and_Experimentati.overview: Time is one of the seven SI base quantities, and its accurate measurement underpins all of dynamics, kinematics, and wave physics. This chapter covers instruments for measuring time intervals, from simple stopwatches to more precise electronic timers. The period of a simple pendulum provides a consistent time standard, and understanding the factors affecting it — length and gravity, not amplitude or mass — is a key result in the chapter.

key_concepts.definition: A mass (bob) on a string of negligible mass attached to a fixed pivot, swinging through small arcs. For small amplitudes (< 10°), the motion is approximately simple harmonic and the period is independent of amplitude and bob mass.
T = 2π√(L/g). The period depends only on the length L of the pendulum and g (gravitational acceleration). Doubling L increases T by √2. A longer pendulum swings more slowly.

The maximum displacement from the mean position. For a simple pendulum, the period is independent of amplitude (for small oscillations). This is the isochronous property — all small swings take the same time.

Measure the time for 20 complete oscillations using a stopwatch, then divide by 20. Taking multiple oscillations reduces the percentage error in timing. A complete oscillation is one full back-and-forth swing.

A stopwatch (manual or electronic) measures time to ±0.1 s (mechanical) or ±0.01 s (electronic). A ticker timer makes 50 dots per second on a tape attached to a moving trolley, used to analyse motion in lab experiments.

Period increases with longer string (T ∝ √L). Period decreases where g is larger (pole vs. equator). Period is INDEPENDENT of: (1) mass/material of bob, (2) amplitude (for small angles). This fact is frequently tested.
A pendulum with a period of exactly 2 seconds (T = 2 s), so each half-swing takes 1 second. Length L = g/π² ≈ 9.8/9.87 ≈ 0.993 m ≈ 1 m on Earth. Used historically in precision clocks.

Light gates and data loggers measure time intervals to millisecond precision by detecting when an object interrupts a light beam. Eliminates human reaction time error (≈0.2–0.3 s for a stopwatch start/stop).

laws_and_formulas.name: Period of Simple Pendulum
laws_and_formulas.description: Period T (s) in terms of length L (m) and g (m/s²). Valid for small amplitudes only.

laws_and_formulas.name: Frequency of Pendulum
laws_and_formulas.description: Frequency (Hz) is the reciprocal of the period (s).

key_points: T = 2π√(L/g) — period depends on L and g only.
Period is independent of amplitude (for small angles) and mass of bob.
Longer pendulum → longer period; larger g → shorter period.
Measure time for 20 swings, divide by 20 → reduces timing error.
Seconds pendulum: T = 2 s, L ≈ 1 m on Earth.
Stopwatch precision: manual ≈ 0.1 s; electronic ≈ 0.01 s.
Ticker timer: 50 dots/second → time between consecutive dots = 0.02 s.
One complete oscillation = one full back-and-forth swing (not just half).
Human reaction time error ≈ 0.2–0.3 s — minimised by timing many oscillations.
f = 1/T: higher frequency = shorter period.

9|physics|Motion_in_One_Dimension_Scalar.overview: Physical quantities are classified as scalars (having only magnitude) or vectors (having both magnitude and direction). This distinction is fundamental in physics — adding two displacements of 3 m and 4 m gives 7 m only if they are in the same direction; if perpendicular, the result is 5 m. This chapter establishes the vocabulary for kinematics and sets the stage for all of vector mechanics in higher physics.

key_concepts.definition: A physical quantity fully described by a magnitude (number and unit) alone. Examples: distance (5 m), speed (30 km/h), mass (70 kg), time (10 s), temperature (300 K), energy (500 J). Scalars obey ordinary algebra.

A physical quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Examples: displacement (5 m North), velocity (30 km/h East), acceleration (10 m/s² downward), force (20 N at 30°). Represented by an arrow (length ∝ magnitude, arrow direction = physical direction).

Distance: total path length travelled (scalar). Displacement: the straight-line distance and direction from start to finish (vector). A runner completing one lap returns to the start: distance = 400 m; displacement = 0 m.

Speed: rate of change of distance, scalar = distance/time. Velocity: rate of change of displacement, vector = displacement/time. A car moving in a circle at constant speed has changing velocity (direction changes).

Vectors in the same direction: add magnitudes. Vectors in opposite directions: subtract. Perpendicular vectors: use Pythagoras — resultant = √(a² + b²). For other angles: use the parallelogram or triangle law.

The single vector equivalent to the combined effect of two or more vectors. For perpendicular vectors: magnitude = √(a²+b²); direction = arctan(b/a) from the first vector. The resultant is the hypotenuse of the right-angled triangle.

A vector equal in magnitude but opposite in direction to the original. If displacement A = 5 m East, then -A = 5 m West. Subtraction of vectors: A - B = A + (-B). Subtracting a vector means adding its negative.

Aeroplane velocity + wind velocity = resultant ground velocity (vector sum). A river current + swimmer's velocity = resultant path across river. These parallelogram/triangle constructions are key problem-solving tools.

laws_and_formulas.name: Resultant of Perpendicular Vectors
laws_and_formulas.description: Magnitude and direction of the resultant of two perpendicular vectors a and b.

key_points: Scalar: magnitude only — distance, speed, mass, time, temperature.
Vector: magnitude + direction — displacement, velocity, acceleration, force.
Distance is always positive; displacement can be negative (opposite direction).
Speed = distance/time; velocity = displacement/time.
Vectors in same direction: add magnitudes. Opposite: subtract. Perpendicular: Pythagoras.
Resultant of 3 m + 4 m (perpendicular) = 5 m (not 7 m).
A body moving at constant speed in a circle has constant speed but continuously changing velocity.
Vector subtraction: A - B = A + (-B); reverse B's direction then add.
Represent vectors as arrows: length = magnitude, direction = physical direction.
The net effect of all vectors is called the resultant.

9|physics|Motion_in_One_Dimension_Equati.overview: When acceleration is uniform (constant), three equations relate displacement, velocity, acceleration, and time. Derived from the definitions of velocity and acceleration, these equations are the core toolkit for solving kinematics problems: falling bodies, projectiles (vertical component), car braking distances, and any uniformly accelerating system. The velocity-time graph provides a visual and analytical framework for all these situations.

key_concepts.definition: Acceleration that remains constant in magnitude and direction. The velocity changes by the same amount each second. Example: a freely falling body (ignoring air resistance) accelerates at g = 9.8 m/s² downward regardless of its current speed.

Final velocity = initial velocity + (acceleration × time). Derived from the definition of acceleration: a = (v-u)/t → v = u + at. Gives final velocity after time t of uniform acceleration a from initial velocity u.

Displacement = (initial velocity × time) + ½(acceleration × time²). Derived from the area under the velocity-time graph (trapezoid = rectangle + triangle). For a body starting from rest (u=0): s = ½at².
Derived by eliminating t from the first two equations. Useful when time is not given or not required. For a freely falling body from rest: v² = 2gh, so v = √(2gh).

For uniform acceleration: a straight line with slope = acceleration. Area under the graph = displacement. A horizontal line → zero acceleration (constant velocity). Downward slope → deceleration (negative acceleration).

Motion under gravity alone with no air resistance. Acceleration = g = 9.8 m/s² ≈ 10 m/s² downward. u = 0 for a body dropped from rest. For a body thrown upward: v = 0 at maximum height, then accelerates downward at g.

Negative acceleration — the object is slowing down. If upward is positive, a body thrown upward has acceleration -g (pointing opposite to motion). Deceleration is not a separate concept; it is acceleration in the direction opposing motion.
Using downward as positive with u = 0: v = gt; h = ½gt²; v² = 2gh. Time to fall height h: t = √(2h/g). Impact velocity from height h: v = √(2gh).

laws_and_formulas.name: First Equation of Motion
laws_and_formulas.description: Final velocity in terms of initial velocity, acceleration, and time.

laws_and_formulas.name: Second Equation of Motion
laws_and_formulas.description: Displacement in terms of initial velocity, acceleration, and time.

laws_and_formulas.name: Third Equation of Motion
laws_and_formulas.description: Final velocity in terms of initial velocity, acceleration, and displacement.

key_points: Three equations: v=u+at; s=ut+½at²; v²=u²+2as — memorise all three.
These apply only when acceleration is uniform (constant).
Free fall: a = g = 9.8 m/s² (downward); u = 0 if dropped from rest.
Dropped from height h: t = √(2h/g); impact speed v = √(2gh).
v-t graph: slope = acceleration; area = displacement.
Deceleration: a is negative (in the direction opposing motion).
At maximum height of thrown body: v = 0 (instantaneously at rest).
Time up = time down for vertical throw (ignoring air resistance).
s = average velocity × time = (u+v)/2 × t (fourth useful relation).
Identify the four quantities — any three known gives the fourth from one of the three equations.

9|physics|Motion_in_One_Dimension_Accele.overview: All objects near the Earth's surface accelerate at the same rate when falling freely under gravity, regardless of their mass. This remarkable result — first demonstrated by Galileo — contradicts intuition but follows from Newton's second law combined with the law of universal gravitation. This chapter establishes the value of g, its variation with altitude, and how g is measured experimentally using free-fall or a simple pendulum.

key_concepts.definition: The acceleration of any freely falling body near Earth's surface: g ≈ 9.8 m/s² ≈ 10 m/s² (directed downward). All objects, regardless of mass, experience the same g in the absence of air resistance — proved by Galileo's Leaning Tower experiment.

key_concepts.term: Why All Objects Fall at the Same Rate
key_concepts.definition: Gravitational force ∝ mass (F = mg). From Newton's 2nd Law: a = F/m = mg/m = g. The mass cancels — acceleration is independent of mass. A heavier object has more gravitational pull but also more inertia, exactly cancelling out.

g decreases with increasing altitude above Earth's surface (g ∝ 1/r² where r is distance from Earth's centre). At the surface, g ≈ 9.8 m/s²; at high altitude, g is smaller. In orbit, the satellite is in continuous free fall (apparent weightlessness).

g is slightly larger at the poles than at the equator. Two reasons: (1) Earth is slightly flattened at poles → closer to centre → larger g; (2) centrifugal effect of Earth's rotation reduces apparent g slightly at equator.
Drop an object and measure the time t to fall height h. Using h = ½gt² → g = 2h/t². Requires accurate measurement of both h and t. Electronic timers improve accuracy over a stopwatch.
From T = 2π√(L/g), rearranging: g = 4π²L/T². Measure L precisely and time many oscillations to find T accurately. This is a practical and accurate method at school level.

A person in free fall (or in orbit) experiences weightlessness — they feel no contact force. Their weight still exists (gravity acts), but there is no normal force from the floor. Astronauts in orbit are in continuous free fall.

Galileo demonstrated experimentally (c. 1590) that all objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass. This contradicted Aristotle's view that heavier objects fall faster. Galileo used inclined planes to slow down motion and measure it accurately.

laws_and_formulas.name: Free Fall Equations
laws_and_formulas.description: Equations for freely falling body from rest, with downward positive and g ≈ 9.8 m/s².

laws_and_formulas.name: g from Pendulum
laws_and_formulas.description: Calculate g by measuring pendulum length L and period T.

key_points: g ≈ 9.8 m/s² ≈ 10 m/s² downward at Earth's surface.
All bodies fall with the same acceleration g regardless of mass (no air resistance).
F = mg: gravity ∝ mass; a = F/m = g — mass cancels.
g decreases with altitude (further from Earth's centre).
g slightly higher at poles than equator.
Measure g: free fall (g = 2h/t²) or pendulum (g = 4π²L/T²).
In free fall from rest: h = ½gt², v = gt, v = √(2gh).
Weightlessness = free fall with no supporting surface (not zero gravity).
Galileo disproved Aristotle: mass does NOT determine fall rate.
Time to reach maximum height = time to fall back to same level (symmetric trajectory).

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Newton_first_la.overview: Newton's First Law — the Law of Inertia — states that every body continues in its state of rest or uniform motion in a straight line unless acted upon by an external net force. This was revolutionary: it replaced the Aristotelian idea that forces are needed to maintain motion. Inertia explains why passengers lurch forward when a bus brakes suddenly, and it defines what a force fundamentally is — an agent of change in motion.

key_concepts.definition: A body remains at rest or moves with uniform velocity in a straight line unless acted upon by a net external force. Also called the Law of Inertia. It defines what a 'force' does: it changes the state of motion.

The resistance of an object to any change in its state of rest or uniform motion. Mass is the measure of inertia — a more massive object is harder to set in motion or stop. Inertia has no units (it is a property, not a quantity).

The vector sum of all forces acting on a body. If net force = 0, the body stays at rest or moves with constant velocity (Newton's 1st Law). A moving body doesn't need a force to keep moving — only to change its motion.

A body is in equilibrium when the net force on it is zero. Static equilibrium: body is at rest. Dynamic equilibrium: body moves with constant velocity. Examples: a book on a table (static); a parachutist falling at terminal velocity (dynamic).

Passengers thrown forward when a bus stops suddenly: they tend to continue in their original motion. Coins stacked on a table fly forward when the tablecloth is pulled quickly. Fruit falls from a tree when the branch is shaken (the fruit tends to stay still).

key_concepts.term: Friction as a Force Change Agent
key_concepts.definition: Without friction, a sliding object on a frictionless surface would slide forever (Newton's 1st Law). Friction is the external force that decelerates real-world sliding objects. This is why a ball rolled on grass stops but Newton's ideal object would not.

Aristotle: objects need a continuous force to maintain motion. Newton: objects need a force only to change their motion. Newton was right — demonstrated by objects sliding on frictionless surfaces (ice, air tracks) that continue moving without applied force.

In a collision, the car decelerates rapidly. Without a seat belt, a passenger's inertia carries them forward at the original speed — Newton's 1st Law. Seat belts apply a backward force on the passenger, decelerating them with the car.

key_points: A body stays at rest or in uniform motion unless a net force acts on it.
Inertia = resistance to change in motion; measured by mass.
Net force = 0 → equilibrium (static or dynamic).
A body moving at constant velocity has zero net force (not zero force — forces balance).
Heavier object has greater inertia — harder to start/stop.
Aristotle was wrong: no force needed to maintain constant velocity on a frictionless surface.
Passengers lurch forward when bus brakes: inertia carries them forward.
Seat belts, headrests, crumple zones all relate to Newton's 1st Law.
An object in orbit has a net force (gravity) — it is NOT in equilibrium (it is accelerating).
Newton's 1st Law is a special case of Newton's 2nd Law (when F = 0 → a = 0).

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Newton_second_l.overview: Newton's Second Law quantifies the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration: F = ma. It is the most powerful and widely used law in classical mechanics. From this single equation, we can calculate the force needed to accelerate a rocket, the tension in a lift cable, the braking force to stop a car, and countless other applications. The law also defines the SI unit of force: 1 newton = 1 kg·m/s².

key_concepts.definition: The net force acting on a body is directly proportional to its acceleration and acts in the direction of the acceleration: F = ma. Net force F (N), mass m (kg), acceleration a (m/s²). The law applies to the net (resultant) force, not individual forces.

1 N = 1 kg·m/s². One newton is the force that gives a 1 kg mass an acceleration of 1 m/s². Defined by Newton's 2nd Law: N = kg × m/s². This makes Newton's 2nd Law an equation of definition as well as physics.

Weight W = mg is the force (F) due to gravity; m is the mass; g is the acceleration due to gravity. Newton's 2nd Law: a = F/m. For free fall: a = W/m = mg/m = g — confirming that all objects fall with the same acceleration.

In problems, first identify all forces on the body (including weight, normal force, friction, applied force, tension). The net force F_net = F_applied - F_friction (for horizontal motion). Then a = F_net/m.

For a system of connected bodies (Atwood's machine, blocks on a surface with a hanging mass), treat the system as a whole to find acceleration: F_net = (total mass) × a. Then analyse individual bodies to find tensions.
F = ma = m(Δv/Δt) = Δ(mv)/Δt = rate of change of momentum. This form is more general (applies even when mass changes). Impulse = FΔt = Δ(mv) — useful for impact problems.

Newton's 2nd Law gives instantaneous acceleration for a given net force. If force is constant, acceleration is constant (and kinematic equations apply). If force varies, acceleration varies and we cannot use the three equations directly.

Lift problems: N - mg = ma (accelerating up) or mg - N = ma (accelerating down). Tension in tow-rope: T = ma (for the towed mass alone). Rocket propulsion: thrust - weight = ma (net upward). All use F_net = ma.

laws_and_formulas.name: Newton's Second Law
laws_and_formulas.formula: F = ma (F in N, m in kg, a in m/s²)
laws_and_formulas.description: Net force equals mass times acceleration. The cornerstone of classical mechanics.

laws_and_formulas.name: Weight as a Force
laws_and_formulas.description: Special case: the net force on a freely falling body is its weight mg, giving acceleration g.

key_points: F = ma — net force, not any single force.
1 N = the force to accelerate 1 kg at 1 m/s².
Acceleration is in the same direction as the net force.
Doubling force → doubles acceleration (if mass fixed). Doubling mass → halves acceleration (if force fixed).
For a system: a = net force / total mass; then find internal tensions individually.
Lift accelerating up: N > mg (feel heavier). Lift decelerating down: N > mg too.
Lift in free fall: N = 0 (weightlessness).
Free fall: F = mg, a = g — mass cancels, confirming all objects fall equally.
F = Δp/Δt — more general form (impulse-momentum); F = ma is a special case (constant mass).
Always draw a free-body diagram before writing F = ma.

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Newton_third_la.overview: Newton's Third Law states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. This law explains rocket propulsion, the recoil of a gun, how we walk, and how boats are rowed. Crucially, action and reaction forces act on different objects and therefore never cancel each other. Understanding this distinction resolves many common misconceptions about force pairs and equilibrium.

key_concepts.definition: When body A exerts a force on body B, body B exerts an equal and opposite force on body A. Forces always occur in pairs. The action and reaction are equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and act on different bodies.

Examples: Earth pulls you down (weight, action) → you pull Earth up (reaction). Your foot pushes backward on the ground (action) → ground pushes your foot forward (friction, reaction — allows walking). Rocket exhausts gas backward → gas pushes rocket forward.

Action and reaction act on different bodies. When you push a wall, the wall pushes back on you — but the wall's force acts on YOU, not on the wall itself. Forces that cancel each other act on the same body — those are balanced forces, not Newton's 3rd Law pairs.

Swimmer pushes water backward (action) → water pushes swimmer forward (reaction). Oar pushes water backward → water pushes boat forward. The force on the water and the force on the swimmer/boat are the Newton's 3rd Law pair.

Hot gas is expelled backward at high speed (action) → gas exerts equal and opposite force forward on the rocket (reaction). No external medium needed — rockets work in space. This is why a balloon flies erratically when released: air exhausts one way, balloon goes the other.

The weight of a book (Earth pulling book down) and the normal force of the table (table pushing book up) do NOT form a Newton's 3rd Law pair — they act on the same body (the book) and happen to be equal only when in equilibrium. The 3rd Law pair of the book's weight is the book pulling Earth upward.

When a gun fires a bullet forward, the gun recoils backward. The force on the bullet and the force on the gun are equal and opposite (3rd Law pair). The gun recoils more slowly than the bullet moves because the gun is more massive: same force, F=ma.

All contact forces (normal, friction, tension) arise from molecular-level electromagnetic repulsion between surfaces and are Newton's 3rd Law pairs between the two bodies in contact. The normal force of the floor on you is the reaction to your weight pressing on the floor.

key_points: Every action has an equal, opposite reaction — acting on a DIFFERENT body.
Action-reaction pairs are always: same magnitude, same line, opposite direction, different objects.
They never cancel because they act on different objects.
Walking: foot pushes back on ground → ground pushes foot forward (friction = reaction).
Rocket: gas exits backward → gas pushes rocket forward.
Gun recoil: bullet forward → gun backward (same force, larger recoil speed for lighter gun ... wait: lighter gun has LESS mass so higher recoil speed — but force is same).
Book on table: weight acts on book (Earth→book); Normal force acts on book (table→book) — NOT a 3rd Law pair. The pair of weight is the book→Earth pull.
3rd Law pair of normal force (table→book): book pushing table down.
The pair of forces in a collision are always equal and opposite — Newton's 3rd Law.
In an isolated system, 3rd Law pairs sum to zero net force on the system → momentum is conserved.

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Momentum.overview: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity — it measures the 'quantity of motion' of a body. Newton's original formulation of his second law was in terms of rate of change of momentum. The principle of conservation of momentum states that in an isolated system (no external forces), total momentum is constant. This fundamental law governs collisions, explosions, and all interactions between bodies and is a direct consequence of Newton's 3rd Law.

key_concepts.definition: p = mv. Mass × velocity; a vector quantity in the direction of velocity. SI unit: kg·m/s = N·s. Momentum measures how hard it is to stop a moving body. A truck moving slowly and a cricket ball moving fast may have similar momenta.

The total momentum of an isolated system remains constant if no external net force acts. m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ (for a two-body collision). Derived from Newton's 3rd Law: the forces in a collision are equal and opposite, so impulses on each body are equal and opposite → total momentum unchanged.

Impulse = F × Δt = change in momentum (Δp = mΔv). Measured in N·s = kg·m/s. The same change in momentum can be achieved with a large force for a short time or a small force for a long time. Airbags increase the time of deceleration, reducing the force.

A collision in which both momentum AND kinetic energy are conserved. In a perfectly elastic collision, the bodies bounce off each other with no loss of energy. Billiard balls and gas molecules approximate elastic collisions.

A collision in which momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is NOT (some is converted to heat, sound, or deformation). Most real collisions are inelastic. A perfectly inelastic collision is when the bodies stick together and move as one (maximum KE loss).
When two bodies stick together: m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = (m₁+m₂)v_common. Momentum is conserved; solve for v_common.
Before a gun fires, total momentum = 0. After: bullet (mass m, velocity +v) + gun (mass M, recoil velocity V): mv + MV = 0 → V = -mv/M. Gun recoils in opposite direction with speed mv/M.
F = Δp/Δt = rate of change of momentum. This is Newton's original and more general statement. For constant mass: Δp = mΔv = m(at) → F = ma. For varying mass (rockets), use F = Δp/Δt directly.

laws_and_formulas.name: Momentum
laws_and_formulas.description: Momentum = mass × velocity; a vector quantity.

laws_and_formulas.name: Conservation of Momentum
laws_and_formulas.description: Total momentum before collision = total momentum after (no external forces).

laws_and_formulas.name: Impulse
laws_and_formulas.description: Impulse equals the change in momentum; unit: N·s = kg·m/s.

key_points: p = mv — vector in direction of velocity; unit kg·m/s.
Conservation of momentum: m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ (no external forces).
Impulse = FΔt = Δp — larger time of impact means smaller force for same Δp.
Elastic: both momentum and KE conserved. Inelastic: only momentum conserved.
Perfectly inelastic: bodies stick together; maximum KE loss.
Recoil: total initial momentum = 0 → bullet and gun momenta are equal and opposite.
F = Δp/Δt — Newton's 2nd Law in more general (momentum) form.
Airbags reduce injury by increasing impact time → reducing force.
Momentum is conserved in all collisions (if no external forces); KE is NOT always conserved.
Gun recoil speed = (mass of bullet / mass of gun) × bullet speed.

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Friction.overview: Friction is the force that opposes relative motion (or tendency of motion) between surfaces in contact. It arises from microscopic irregularities and molecular adhesion between surfaces. Friction has both essential benefits (enabling walking, brakes, writing) and costs (energy loss, wear). This chapter distinguishes static friction from kinetic friction, establishes the laws of friction, and explores factors that affect friction and methods to reduce it.

key_concepts.definition: The friction force preventing relative motion between stationary surfaces. It adjusts to match the applied force up to a maximum value (limiting friction). The object does not move until the applied force exceeds the maximum static friction.

The friction force acting when surfaces slide against each other. Kinetic friction is approximately constant and less than the maximum static friction. Once motion starts, the friction force drops from the limiting static value to the kinetic value.

1. Friction acts parallel to the surfaces in contact, opposing relative motion. 2. The magnitude of kinetic friction = μₖN (where N = normal reaction and μₖ = coefficient of kinetic friction). 3. Friction is independent of area of contact (for flat surfaces). 4. Friction is independent of speed.
μ = friction force / normal reaction = F/N. Dimensionless (no units). Values: rubber on concrete μ ≈ 0.8; steel on steel μ ≈ 0.6; ice on steel μ ≈ 0.03. A higher μ means more friction.

The perpendicular contact force from the surface. For a horizontal surface: N = weight = mg. For an inclined plane: N = mg cosθ. Friction force = μN, so friction depends on the component of weight perpendicular to the surface.

Walking (foot grips the ground), brakes (pads grip disc/drum), belts and pulleys (power transmission), writing (pen on paper), matches igniting. Without friction, we cannot walk, steer vehicles, or hold objects.
Energy dissipated as heat in engines, brakes, and bearings. Wear and tear of machine parts. Noise. Friction reduces efficiency of machines. In engines, up to 20% of fuel energy is lost to friction.

Lubrication (oil, grease) creates a thin fluid layer between surfaces. Polishing reduces surface roughness. Ball bearings replace sliding friction with rolling friction (much lower). Streamlining reduces air resistance (fluid friction).

laws_and_formulas.name: Friction Force
laws_and_formulas.formula: f = μN (μ = coefficient of friction, N = normal reaction)
laws_and_formulas.description: Friction force equals the product of the coefficient of friction and the normal reaction.

key_points: Friction opposes relative motion (or tendency of motion) between surfaces.
Static friction ≥ kinetic friction; maximum static friction > kinetic friction.
f = μN — friction depends on μ (surface nature) and N (normal force).
Friction is INDEPENDENT of area of contact and speed of sliding.
N = mg for horizontal surface; N = mg cosθ for incline at angle θ.
Higher normal force → higher friction (pressing harder on brakes → more stopping force).
Advantages: walking, braking, writing. Disadvantages: energy loss, wear.
Reduce friction with: lubrication, polishing, ball bearings, air cushions.
Rolling friction < sliding friction (ball bearings work on this principle).
Friction always acts opposite to the direction of motion (or intended motion).

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Work_and_energy.overview: Work is done when a force moves its point of application. Energy is the capacity to do work. The work-energy theorem establishes that the net work done on a body equals the change in its kinetic energy. The law of conservation of energy — energy can neither be created nor destroyed, only converted from one form to another — is one of the most profound principles in all of physics and underpins thermodynamics, chemistry, and biology.

key_concepts.definition: W = F × d × cos θ, where θ is the angle between force and displacement. Measured in joules (J). Work is done only if the force has a component in the direction of motion. No work is done if force is perpendicular to displacement (e.g., centripetal force).
The SI unit of work and energy. 1 J = 1 N × 1 m = 1 kg·m²/s². One joule is the work done by a force of 1 N moving an object 1 m in the direction of the force.
Energy of motion: KE = ½mv². Measured in joules. Depends on mass and the square of speed — doubling speed quadruples KE. KE is always positive (since it depends on v²).

Energy stored due to position or configuration. Gravitational PE = mgh (height h above reference level). Elastic PE stored in a stretched/compressed spring = ½kx². PE is relative to a chosen reference level.
Net work done on a body = change in kinetic energy: W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mu². This directly links kinematics (velocity) with dynamics (force × distance).

Energy can be converted from one form to another but the total energy in an isolated system remains constant. In free fall: KE gained = PE lost. For a pendulum: total mechanical energy (KE + PE) is constant (ignoring air resistance).
Total mechanical energy = KE + PE = ½mv² + mgh. In the absence of friction and air resistance, mechanical energy is conserved. When friction acts, mechanical energy decreases (converted to heat).
Power = rate of doing work = work/time = W/t. SI unit: watt (W) = joule/second (J/s). Also P = Fv (for constant force and velocity). 1 kilowatt (kW) = 1000 W; 1 horsepower ≈ 746 W.

laws_and_formulas.name: Work
laws_and_formulas.description: Work done by force F over displacement d at angle θ to the displacement direction.

laws_and_formulas.name: Kinetic Energy
laws_and_formulas.description: Energy of motion; proportional to mass and square of speed.

laws_and_formulas.name: Gravitational Potential Energy
laws_and_formulas.description: Energy due to height h above reference level, in gravitational field g.

laws_and_formulas.name: Power
laws_and_formulas.description: Rate of energy transfer (watts). Also equals force × velocity for constant velocity.

key_points: W = Fd cosθ — only the component of force in the direction of motion does work.
No work if θ = 90° (centripetal force, normal force).
Work-Energy Theorem: W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mu².
KE = ½mv² — quadruples when speed doubles.
PE = mgh — lost as object falls; gained as it rises.
Conservation of energy: KE + PE = constant (no friction).
At bottom of swing: KE maximum, PE minimum. At top: KE minimum, PE maximum.
P = W/t = Fv; unit watt (W) = J/s.
Efficiency = useful output / total input (expressed as a fraction or %).
Energy forms: kinetic, potential (gravitational, elastic, chemical), heat, light, sound, electrical, nuclear.

9|physics|Laws_of_Motion_Circular_motion.overview: An object moving in a circle at constant speed is continuously changing direction, meaning it is continuously accelerating. This centripetal acceleration is always directed toward the centre of the circle. The centripetal force — the net inward force producing this acceleration — is provided by different agents in different situations: gravity for orbiting planets, tension for a stone on a string, friction for a turning car. Understanding circular motion is essential for planets, satellites, rotating machinery, and fairground rides.

key_concepts.definition: Motion in a circle at constant speed. Although speed is constant, velocity is not (direction changes continuously). Therefore the body is always accelerating. The acceleration is centripetal — always pointing toward the centre.

a = v²/r = ω²r. Always directed toward the centre of the circle. 'Centripetal' means 'centre-seeking'. It is not a separate type of acceleration — it is the direction of the net acceleration for circular motion.

F = mv²/r. The net inward force required to keep a body moving in a circle. It is not a new type of force — it is provided by whichever physical force(s) are directed toward the centre: gravity, tension, friction, normal reaction, etc.

Stone on a string: tension. Car turning: friction between tyres and road. Planet orbiting Sun: gravitational attraction. Electron in orbit: electrostatic attraction (older model). Clothes in spin dryer: the drum walls push inward.

From the rotating frame, objects appear to experience an outward 'centrifugal force'. This is a pseudo-force — it is not real but arises from being in a non-inertial (accelerating) frame. In the lab frame, what happens is the inward force is insufficient, and the object flies outward (tangentially).
ω = angle turned / time = 2π/T = 2πf. Unit: rad/s. The relationship between linear speed and angular velocity: v = ωr. For one revolution: angle = 2π radians; if T is the period, ω = 2π/T.
T = time for one complete revolution; f = 1/T = number of revolutions per second (Hz). Speed v = circumference/T = 2πr/T. These link circular motion to the kinematics of periodic motion.

Roads are banked (tilted inward at bends) so that the inward component of the normal reaction provides centripetal force. This reduces dependence on friction for turning and allows vehicles to turn safely at higher speeds.

laws_and_formulas.name: Centripetal Force
laws_and_formulas.description: The net inward force required for circular motion at radius r and speed v.

laws_and_formulas.name: Centripetal Acceleration
laws_and_formulas.description: Acceleration of a body in uniform circular motion — always toward centre.

laws_and_formulas.name: Angular Velocity
laws_and_formulas.description: Angular velocity in rad/s; linear speed = angular velocity × radius.

key_points: Constant speed in a circle ≠ zero acceleration (direction changes).
Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r, always toward centre.
Centripetal force: F = mv²/r — the net inward force (provided by tension, gravity, friction, etc.).
Centrifugal force is a pseudo-force — not real in the lab frame.
If the providing force disappears, the object moves tangentially (off in a straight line).
v = 2πr/T; ω = 2π/T; v = ωr.
Increasing speed or decreasing radius requires larger centripetal force.
Planet in circular orbit: centripetal force = gravitational force.
Banking of roads: normal reaction component provides centripetal force without relying on friction.
A car turning too fast: friction < centripetal force needed → car skids outward.

9|physics|Heat_and_Energy_Thermal_expans.overview: Most substances expand when heated because thermal energy increases the kinetic energy of molecules, which vibrate more and push each other further apart. Solids expand least; liquids more; gases most. The coefficients of linear, superficial, and cubical expansion of solids define the rate of expansion. Thermal expansion has critical practical importance: gaps in railway tracks, expansion joints in bridges, the bimetallic strip in thermostats, and the anomalous expansion of water near 4°C.

key_concepts.definition: A solid rod of original length L₀ at temperature θ₁ expands by ΔL when heated to θ₂: ΔL = αL₀Δθ, where α is the coefficient of linear expansion (unit: per °C or per K). New length L = L₀(1 + αΔθ).

α = fractional change in length per degree change in temperature = ΔL/(L₀ × Δθ). Unit: per °C (or per K). Typical values: steel 11×10⁻⁶/°C; brass 19×10⁻⁶/°C; aluminium 24×10⁻⁶/°C. α is very small — expansion is not visible in short rods over small temperature ranges.

For a solid: coefficient of superficial (area) expansion β ≈ 2α; coefficient of cubical (volume) expansion γ ≈ 3α. For liquids and gases, only cubical expansion is relevant. Gases expand enormously more than solids or liquids.

Water contracts (unusually) from 0°C to 4°C, reaching maximum density at 4°C. Above 4°C it expands normally. This is why lakes freeze from the surface down — 4°C water sinks, keeping the bottom warmer, allowing aquatic life to survive winter.

Bimetallic strip: two metals with different α values bonded together bend when heated. Used in thermostats, fire alarms, circuit breakers. Railway tracks have expansion gaps. Bridges have roller supports. Telephone wires sag in summer (expanded) and are tight in winter (contracted).

Liquids expand more than solids at the same temperature rise. Apparent expansion: what is observed when a liquid in a container is heated (expansion of container subtracts). Real expansion = apparent expansion + expansion of container.
Gases expand far more than solids or liquids. The cubical expansion coefficient of an ideal gas at 0°C is γ = 1/273 per °C. This forms the basis of the gas laws studied in a separate chapter.

If a solid is prevented from expanding when heated, internal compressive stress builds up. This can buckle railway tracks or crack structures. Expansion joints and gaps in structures prevent thermal stress damage.

laws_and_formulas.name: Linear Expansion
laws_and_formulas.description: Expansion of a solid rod; α is the coefficient of linear expansion (per °C).

laws_and_formulas.name: Cubical Expansion
laws_and_formulas.description: Volume expansion of a solid; coefficient of cubical expansion ≈ 3× linear coefficient.

key_points: Most substances expand on heating — molecules vibrate more and push apart.
Solids expand least; liquids more; gases most.
α (linear) is very small: ~10⁻⁶/°C for metals.
β (superficial) ≈ 2α; γ (cubical) ≈ 3α.
Anomalous expansion of water: maximum density at 4°C; ice floats.
Water 0°C→4°C: contracts (density increases); 4°C and above: expands normally.
Bimetallic strip: different expansion rates → bending on heating.
Expansion gaps in railway tracks and bridges prevent thermal damage.
Real expansion of liquid = apparent expansion + expansion of container vessel.
Thermal stress occurs when expansion is prevented — can buckle structures.

9|physics|Heat_and_Energy_Calorimetry.overview: Calorimetry is the science of measuring heat energy transferred during physical and chemical processes. The heat capacity of a body tells us how much energy is needed to raise its temperature by 1°C, while specific heat capacity is an intrinsic property of the material. The method of mixtures uses conservation of energy: heat lost by hot body = heat gained by cold body. These principles underpin thermodynamics, cooking, climate science, and materials engineering.

key_concepts.definition: Heat capacity C of a body = heat required to raise its temperature by 1°C. C = Q/Δθ. Unit: J/°C or J/K. Depends on the mass and material of the body. A large block of copper has greater heat capacity than a small block of copper.

The heat required to raise 1 kg of a substance by 1°C: c = Q/(mΔθ). Unit: J/(kg·°C). Water has a very high specific heat capacity (4200 J/kg°C), making it an excellent coolant and heat store. c is a material property, not dependent on mass.

Heat energy transferred Q (J) = mass m (kg) × specific heat capacity c (J/kg°C) × temperature change Δθ (°C). If temperature increases, heat is absorbed (Q positive). If temperature decreases, heat is released (Q negative).

Heat lost by hot body = heat gained by cold body (assuming no heat loss to surroundings). m₁c₁(θ₁ - θ_f) = m₂c₂(θ_f - θ₂), where θ_f is the final equilibrium temperature. Used to find specific heat capacity of unknown materials.

A vessel (typically copper or aluminium) used to measure heat exchanges. Its own heat capacity must be accounted for in calculations: heat gained by calorimeter = m_cal × c_cal × Δθ. This is the 'water equivalent' of the calorimeter.

The mass of water that has the same heat capacity as the calorimeter. W = m_cal × c_cal / c_water. If 0.1 kg copper calorimeter (c_Cu = 400 J/kg°C): W = 0.1 × 400/4200 ≈ 0.0095 kg of water equivalent.

When a substance changes state (melts or boils) at constant temperature, heat is absorbed/released without a temperature change. This 'latent heat' is studied in detail separately but is relevant when mixtures involve phase changes (e.g., ice added to water).
Heat lost to the surroundings (addressed by lagging the calorimeter). Incomplete mixing. The thermometer's own heat capacity. Evaporation cooling. Good experimental technique minimises these.

laws_and_formulas.name: Heat Equation
laws_and_formulas.description: Heat energy absorbed/released by mass m of a substance with SHC c over temperature change Δθ.

laws_and_formulas.name: Method of Mixtures
laws_and_formulas.formula: Heat lost = Heat gained: m₁c₁Δθ₁ = m₂c₂Δθ₂
laws_and_formulas.description: Conservation of energy for heat exchange (no external gains or losses).

key_points: Q = mcΔθ — the fundamental calorimetry equation.
c for water = 4200 J/(kg·°C) — highest among common substances.
Heat capacity C = mc (for a specific body); specific heat capacity c is a material property.
Method of mixtures: heat lost by hot body = heat gained by cold body.
Always include the calorimeter's heat capacity in calculations.
Water equivalent of calorimeter = m_cal × c_cal / c_water.
Temperature change Δθ = |T_final - T_initial| (always positive for heat absorbed).
Higher specific heat capacity → more energy to change temperature by 1°C.
Water is used as a coolant (cars, power stations) because of its high c.
Sea breeze / land breeze: land heats/cools faster than sea (lower specific heat capacity).

9|physics|Heat_and_Energy_Latent_heat.overview: When a substance changes state — melting or boiling — heat is absorbed or released at constant temperature. This 'hidden' heat (latent heat) does not raise the temperature; instead it changes the arrangement of molecules. The specific latent heat of fusion (melting) and vaporisation (boiling) determine how much energy is involved per kilogram. These concepts explain why ice cools drinks more effectively than cold water, and why sweating cools the body.

key_concepts.definition: The heat energy absorbed or released during a change of state at constant temperature. 'Latent' means 'hidden' — no temperature change occurs. The energy goes into changing the bonding arrangement of molecules rather than increasing their kinetic energy.
The heat energy required to convert 1 kg of a solid to liquid at its melting point (or released during freezing). For ice/water: Lf = 336,000 J/kg = 336 kJ/kg. Q = mLf for melting.

The heat energy required to convert 1 kg of liquid to gas at its boiling point (or released during condensation). For water: Lv = 2,260,000 J/kg = 2260 kJ/kg. Much larger than Lf — bonds are more completely broken in vaporisation.

A graph of temperature vs. heat added for ice → water → steam: (1) ice warms to 0°C; (2) melting at 0°C (flat region, Q = mLf); (3) water warms to 100°C; (4) boiling at 100°C (long flat region, Q = mLv); (5) steam warms above 100°C. Flat portions = latent heat.
Heat for change of state = mass × specific latent heat. For melting: Q = mLf. For vaporisation: Q = mLv. No temperature change term (Δθ) because temperature stays constant during the state change.

key_concepts.term: Ice vs. Cold Water for Cooling
key_concepts.definition: Ice at 0°C cools a drink more effectively than water at 0°C because: (1) the ice absorbs latent heat (mLf) as it melts, in addition to (2) the melted cold water absorbing heat as it warms. The latent heat absorption is substantial: 336 kJ/kg.

The body cools by evaporating sweat from the skin. Each kilogram of water evaporated removes ~2260 kJ from the body. This is far more effective at cooling than simply having water on the skin without evaporation.

Steam at 100°C causes more severe burns than boiling water at 100°C. When steam condenses on skin, it releases its latent heat of vaporisation (2260 kJ/kg) in addition to the heat lost as it cools from 100°C. Total energy transferred is much greater.

laws_and_formulas.name: Heat for Change of State
laws_and_formulas.formula: Q = mL (L = specific latent heat in J/kg)
laws_and_formulas.description: Heat absorbed or released during melting (L = Lf) or vaporisation (L = Lv) at constant temperature.

key_points: Latent heat: heat absorbed/released at constant temperature during state change.
Q = mL (no Δθ term); Q = mcΔθ (no state change).
Lf (ice) = 336 kJ/kg; Lv (water) = 2260 kJ/kg.
Lv >> Lf because more energy is needed to completely separate molecules in vaporisation.
Flat portions of heating curve = latent heat; sloped portions = sensible heat.
Ice at 0°C cools better than water at 0°C: ice absorbs Lf as it melts.
Steam burns more severely than boiling water: releases Lv on condensation.
Sweating cools body by latent heat of vaporisation of water.
Condensation releases the same energy as evaporation absorbs (reversible process).
Refrigerators use the latent heat of vaporisation of a refrigerant to absorb heat from the interior.

9|physics|Heat_and_Energy_Heat_transfer_.overview: Heat can be transferred by three mechanisms: conduction (through solid contact — molecule-to-molecule vibration), convection (through fluid movement — hot fluid rises, cool fluid sinks), and radiation (electromagnetic waves requiring no medium). Understanding which mechanism dominates in a given situation guides the design of insulators, heaters, cooking vessels, solar collectors, and countless thermal engineering applications.

key_concepts.definition: Heat transfer through a material by direct collision of molecules and (in metals) by free electrons. Energy passes from hot to cold along the material without any bulk movement of the material itself. Metals are good conductors (free electrons transfer energy rapidly); non-metals and fluids are poor conductors (insulators).

A measure of how well a material conducts heat. Metals have high thermal conductivity (copper: 400 W/m·K; silver: 429 W/m·K). Non-metals much lower (glass: 1 W/m·K; wood: 0.1 W/m·K; air: 0.025 W/m·K). Air is an excellent insulator — trapped air reduces conduction.

Heat transfer in fluids (liquids or gases) by the actual movement of the fluid. Warm fluid is less dense → rises; cool fluid is denser → sinks → forms a convection current. Examples: household radiators, sea breezes, atmospheric circulation, ocean currents.

Circular currents in a fluid driven by density differences due to temperature. Hot fluid rises, cools, becomes denser, sinks, is reheated and rises again. In the atmosphere: warm air rises near the equator, cold air sinks at the poles → global wind patterns.

Heat transfer by electromagnetic waves (infrared radiation) requiring no medium. Can travel through vacuum (explaining how the Sun heats Earth). All objects emit and absorb radiation; hotter objects emit more. Speed = speed of light (3×10⁸ m/s).

Black, dull, rough surfaces are the best absorbers and emitters of radiation. White, shiny, smooth surfaces are the poorest absorbers and emitters (best reflectors). A matt black body is the ideal 'black body' radiator.

Thermos flask: silvered walls (reduce radiation), vacuum (eliminate conduction and convection in the gap), insulating stopper. Solar panel: black surface (absorbs radiation). Building insulation: trapped air (poor conductor). Double-glazing: air gap prevents conduction.

Glass (or atmospheric CO₂ and H₂O) is transparent to visible light but absorbs re-emitted infrared radiation from the ground. This traps heat — the greenhouse effect. Essential for life on Earth but enhanced by excess CO₂ from fossil fuels, causing global warming.

key_points: Three heat transfer mechanisms: conduction (solids), convection (fluids), radiation (all — no medium needed).
Conduction: molecule-to-molecule; free electrons make metals good conductors.
Metals: good conductors. Non-metals and air: good insulators (poor conductors).
Convection: hot fluid rises (less dense); cool fluid sinks (more dense) → convection current.
Radiation: infrared EM waves; no medium needed; travels at speed of light.
Black, dull surfaces: best absorbers and emitters. Shiny, white: best reflectors.
Thermos flask prevents all three types: vacuum (conduction/convection), silvering (radiation).
Sea breeze (day): land heats faster → hot air rises over land → cool air from sea moves in.
Land breeze (night): sea cools slower → hot air rises over sea → cool air from land moves in.
Greenhouse effect: atmosphere traps infrared re-radiation from Earth's surface.

9|physics|Light_Refraction_of_light.overview: When light passes from one transparent medium to another, it bends at the interface — this is refraction. Refraction occurs because light changes speed as it crosses media with different optical densities. Snell's Law relates the angles of incidence and refraction to the refractive indices of the media. Refraction explains the apparent bending of a straw in water, mirages, rainbow formation, and is the operating principle of lenses and optical instruments.

key_concepts.definition: The change in direction of a light ray when it passes from one transparent medium to another of different optical density. Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium (speed decreases) and away from the normal when entering a less dense medium (speed increases).

n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂, where n₁, n₂ are refractive indices and θ₁, θ₂ are angles of incidence and refraction (from normal). Also written as: sin i / sin r = n₂/n₁ = constant for a given pair of media.

n = speed of light in vacuum / speed of light in medium = c/v. A dimensionless quantity ≥ 1 for all media. Water n ≈ 1.33; glass n ≈ 1.5; diamond n ≈ 2.42. Higher n → slower light → greater bending toward normal.

key_concepts.term: Critical Angle and Total Internal Reflection
key_concepts.definition: When light travels from a denser to a less dense medium, at the critical angle C: sin C = n₂/n₁ = 1/n (for air as medium 2). For angles > C, all light is reflected back (total internal reflection). Used in optical fibres and diamond brilliance.

A straw appears bent in water (bending toward normal at air-water interface). A pool appears shallower than it is (apparent depth = real depth / n). Mirages (total internal reflection in air layers). Stars twinkle (atmospheric refraction). Atmospheric refraction also makes the Sun visible just before it rises and after it sets.

Apparent depth = Real depth / n. A swimming pool of real depth 3 m with water (n = 1.33) appears to be 3/1.33 ≈ 2.25 m deep. This is because refraction at the surface bends rays from the bottom, making them appear to come from a shallower point.

A thin glass or plastic fibre that transmits light by total internal reflection. Light enters one end and bounces along the fibre without escaping. Used in telecommunications (internet, phone cables) and endoscopes (medical imaging inside the body).

A glass prism refracts white light twice (at entry and exit). Different wavelengths refract at slightly different angles → the spectrum: red (least deviation) to violet (most deviation). This dispersion into colours is because the refractive index of glass depends slightly on wavelength.

laws_and_formulas.name: Snell's Law
laws_and_formulas.formula: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂
laws_and_formulas.description: Relates angles of incidence and refraction to refractive indices of the two media.

laws_and_formulas.name: Refractive Index
laws_and_formulas.formula: n = c/v = sin i / sin r (from air to medium)
laws_and_formulas.description: Ratio of speed of light in vacuum to speed in the medium; also ratio of sines of angles.

laws_and_formulas.name: Critical Angle
laws_and_formulas.formula: sin C = 1/n (for light going from medium to air)
laws_and_formulas.description: Angle of incidence above which total internal reflection occurs.

key_points: Light bends toward normal: entering denser medium. Away: entering less dense medium.
Snell's Law: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂.
n = c/v = sin i / sin r (from air).
Higher n → slower light in medium.
Critical angle C: sin C = 1/n; total internal reflection occurs for angles > C.
Apparent depth = real depth / n (pool looks shallower).
Optical fibres use total internal reflection to transmit light pulses.
Prism disperses white light: ROYGBIV (red deviates least, violet most).
Stars twinkle due to atmospheric refraction varying with turbulence.
Diamond's high refractive index (n ≈ 2.42) gives it brilliance via total internal reflection.

9|physics|Sound_Nature_of_sound.overview: Sound is a mechanical, longitudinal wave produced by the vibration of matter and propagated through a material medium (solid, liquid, or gas). It cannot travel through a vacuum. Sound waves consist of compressions and rarefactions — alternating regions of high and low pressure. The characteristics of sound — loudness, pitch, and quality — are determined by amplitude, frequency, and waveform respectively. Understanding sound is fundamental to acoustics, music, medical imaging, and sonar.

key_concepts.definition: Sound travels as compressions (regions of high pressure) and rarefactions (regions of low pressure) in the direction of propagation. Particles of the medium vibrate parallel to the wave direction. Longitudinal waves require a material medium — unlike transverse electromagnetic waves.

Frequency = number of vibrations per second; unit: hertz (Hz). Higher frequency → higher pitch. Human hearing range: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz. Below 20 Hz: infrasound (used by elephants). Above 20,000 Hz: ultrasound (used by bats, dolphins, medical imaging).

Amplitude = maximum displacement of a particle from its mean position. Greater amplitude → more energy → louder sound. Loudness is measured in decibels (dB). Every 10 dB increase represents a tenfold increase in intensity.

Speed of sound depends on the medium: faster in denser, more rigid media. In air (20°C): ≈340 m/s. In water: ≈1500 m/s. In steel: ≈5000 m/s. In solids > liquids > gases for rigidity reason. Speed increases with temperature in gases.

The distance between two successive compressions (or rarefactions). λ = v/f (wavelength = speed/frequency). For a 340 Hz sound in air: λ = 340/340 = 1 m. Higher frequency → shorter wavelength (for same speed).

v = fλ. Speed = frequency × wavelength. This is the universal wave equation applying to all types of waves — sound, light, water waves. For sound in air at 340 m/s: f and λ are inversely proportional.

A reflected sound heard as a distinct repetition of the original. For an echo to be heard distinctly, the reflected sound must arrive at least 0.1 s after the original (threshold of human hearing for distinguishing two sounds). Minimum reflecting distance = 340 × 0.1/2 = 17 m.

The quality of sound distinguishing two sounds of the same pitch and loudness from different instruments. Determined by the number and relative intensity of overtones (harmonics) present. A violin and flute playing the same note sound different due to different timbre.

laws_and_formulas.name: Wave Equation
laws_and_formulas.description: Speed of sound = frequency × wavelength. Applies to all waves.

laws_and_formulas.name: Echo Minimum Distance
laws_and_formulas.formula: d = v × t / 2 (for echo after time t)
laws_and_formulas.description: Distance to reflecting surface when echo is heard after time t.

key_points: Sound: longitudinal mechanical wave — needs a medium (cannot travel in vacuum).
Compressions (high pressure) and rarefactions (low pressure) alternate along the wave direction.
Frequency → pitch; amplitude → loudness; waveform → quality (timbre).
Human hearing: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz; below: infrasound; above: ultrasound.
Speed in air ≈ 340 m/s; in water ≈ 1500 m/s; in steel ≈ 5000 m/s.
Speed: solids > liquids > gases (rigidity); also increases with temperature in gases.
v = fλ — wave equation.
Echo: heard distinctly if reflected sound arrives ≥ 0.1 s later → minimum wall distance = 17 m.
Thunder heard after lightning → d = v × t (sound travels 340 m per second).
Reverberation: multiple echoes in enclosed spaces; managed with sound-absorbing materials.

9|physics|Sound_Infrasound_and_ultrasoun.overview: Sound beyond the range of human hearing — infrasound (below 20 Hz) and ultrasound (above 20,000 Hz) — has extensive natural and technological applications. Ultrasound is particularly useful because it can penetrate soft tissues, reflect off boundaries between different densities, and be focused with precision. Sonar, medical ultrasonography, industrial non-destructive testing, and echolocation by animals all exploit ultrasound. Infrasound is used by large animals for long-range communication.

key_concepts.definition: Sound below the human hearing threshold. Produced naturally by earthquakes, volcanoes, ocean waves, and large animals. Elephants communicate using infrasound (rumbles at 14-35 Hz) detectable up to several kilometres. Infrasound can cause physiological effects: discomfort, disorientation, resonance in body organs.

Sound above the human hearing threshold. Produced by piezoelectric crystals (which convert electrical to mechanical vibration and vice versa). Used in sonar, echolocation, medical imaging, cleaning, and non-destructive testing.

Uses ultrasound pulses to measure distance underwater: emit pulse → detect echo → d = v × t/2. Used to map the ocean floor, locate submarines, detect shoals of fish, and survey underwater structures. Speed of sound in seawater ≈ 1500 m/s.

Bats, dolphins, and other animals emit ultrasound pulses and interpret the returning echoes to navigate and locate prey. Bats can detect insects smaller than 1 mm using frequencies of 20–200 kHz. The short wavelength gives fine spatial resolution.

A probe emits ultrasound pulses that partially reflect at tissue boundaries (different acoustic impedances). The reflected pulses are detected and processed to produce a 2D image. Used for imaging organs, foetal development (non-ionising, safe), blood flow (Doppler ultrasound), and detecting tumours.

Ultrasound is passed through a material (metal, concrete); any internal cracks or voids create partial reflections that are detected. The material is not damaged. Used in aerospace (aircraft wing inspection), construction, and pipeline inspection.

Objects are submerged in a liquid bath through which ultrasound is passed. The high-frequency vibrations create microbubbles that collapse violently (cavitation), dislodging dirt and contaminants from surfaces. Used to clean jewellery, surgical instruments, and optical components.

Shorter wavelength → better resolution (can detect smaller features). Non-ionising (unlike X-rays) → safer for patients, especially pregnant women. Reflects at tissue boundaries → different tissue densities visible. Can be focused into a narrow beam.

laws_and_formulas.name: Sonar Distance
laws_and_formulas.description: Depth or distance of reflecting object: half of (speed × echo time).

key_points: Infrasound: < 20 Hz; ultrasound: > 20,000 Hz — both beyond human hearing.
Ultrasound produced by piezoelectric crystals (electrical → mechanical vibration).
Sonar: d = vt/2 (echo time t, speed v in water ≈ 1500 m/s).
