Bats: 20–200 kHz for echolocation; dolphins: up to 150 kHz.
Medical ultrasonography: images formed from reflected ultrasound at tissue boundaries.
Ultrasound is non-ionising → safer than X-rays for foetal imaging.
Shorter wavelength → finer resolution in imaging.
NDT: ultrasound detects internal cracks without damaging the material.
Ultrasonic cleaning: cavitation bubbles dislodge contaminants.
Elephants communicate in infrasound (14–35 Hz) over long distances.

9|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Simp.overview: Electric circuits are the pathways along which electric charge flows. A simple circuit consists of a source of EMF (battery), conducting wires, and components (resistors, bulbs, switches). Current is the rate of flow of charge, voltage (potential difference) is the energy per unit charge, and resistance opposes current flow. Ohm's Law relates these three quantities. Understanding circuits is the foundation for all of electronics, electrical engineering, and power systems.

key_concepts.definition: The rate of flow of electric charge: I = Q/t. Unit: ampere (A). 1 A = 1 coulomb of charge passing a point per second. Conventional current direction: from positive to negative terminal (opposite to electron flow). Measured with an ammeter connected in series.

The work done per unit charge in moving charge from one point to another: V = W/Q. Unit: volt (V). 1 V = 1 joule per coulomb (1 J/C). Measured with a voltmeter connected in parallel across a component.
Opposition to the flow of electric current. R = V/I. Unit: ohm (Ω). 1 Ω = 1 V/A. Resistance depends on the material, length, cross-sectional area, and temperature of the conductor.

For a conductor at constant temperature: V = IR. The current through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across it (provided physical conditions, especially temperature, are constant). An ohmic conductor obeys this linear V-I relationship.

Components connected end-to-end in a single loop. Same current through each component. Total voltage = sum of individual voltages (V = V₁ + V₂ + ...). Total resistance = sum of individual resistances (R_total = R₁ + R₂ + ...).

Components connected between the same two nodes. Same voltage across each component. Total current = sum of branch currents (I = I₁ + I₂ + ...). Reciprocal of total resistance = sum of reciprocals: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + ...

The energy supplied per unit charge by a source (battery, generator): EMF = energy/charge. Unit: volt. EMF drives the current around the circuit. Terminal voltage < EMF when current flows, due to internal resistance: V_terminal = EMF - Ir.

P = VI = I²R = V²/R. Unit: watt (W). Energy dissipated = Pt = VIt (unit: joule). 1 kilowatt-hour (kWh) = 3.6×10⁶ J (the unit on electricity bills). The cost of electricity = energy in kWh × price per kWh.

laws_and_formulas.name: Ohm's Law
laws_and_formulas.formula: V = IR (V in volts, I in amperes, R in ohms)
laws_and_formulas.description: Relationship between voltage, current, and resistance at constant temperature.

laws_and_formulas.name: Series Resistance
laws_and_formulas.description: Total resistance in a series circuit.

laws_and_formulas.name: Parallel Resistance
laws_and_formulas.description: Total resistance in a parallel circuit (always less than smallest branch resistance).

laws_and_formulas.name: Electrical Power
laws_and_formulas.description: Power dissipated in a component.

key_points: I = Q/t; V = W/Q; R = V/I — the three fundamental definitions.
Ohm's Law: V = IR (constant temperature required for ohmic conductors).
Series: same current; voltages add; resistances add.
Parallel: same voltage; currents add; 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + ...
Parallel total resistance < smallest individual resistance.
Ammeter: in series (low resistance). Voltmeter: in parallel (high resistance).
P = VI = I²R = V²/R.
Energy = Pt = VIt; unit: joule (J); commercial unit: kWh (= 3.6 MJ).
EMF = work done per coulomb by source; terminal voltage = EMF - Ir (I = current, r = internal resistance).
Fuses and circuit breakers protect circuits from excessive current.

9|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Heat.overview: When electric current flows through a resistance, electrical energy is converted to heat energy. This heating effect — the Joule heating or Joule effect — is the operating principle of electric heaters, toasters, incandescent bulbs, and fuses. The amount of heat produced depends on the current, resistance, and time. While Joule heating is useful in heaters, it is unwanted energy loss in power transmission lines, which is why high-voltage (low-current) transmission is preferred.

key_concepts.definition: The heat produced in a resistor is H = I²Rt. Where H is heat energy (J), I is current (A), R is resistance (Ω), and t is time (s). Heat is proportional to the square of the current — doubling the current quadruples the heat produced.

Electric heater: nichrome wire (high resistance, high melting point, does not oxidise). Electric bulb: tungsten filament at ~2500°C emits visible light. Fuse: thin wire of low melting point alloy (lead-tin) melts when current exceeds rated value, breaking the circuit.

A safety device: a thin wire with a specific melting point that melts (burns out) when current exceeds its rated value (amp rating). This breaks the circuit, preventing damage to appliances and reducing fire risk. The fuse is connected in series in the live wire.

Replaces fuses in modern installations. Automatically trips (opens) when current exceeds a set value. Can be reset (unlike a fuse which must be replaced). Uses electromagnetic or bimetallic strip mechanism.
P = I²R = V²/R = VI. The power determines the rate of heat production. A 2 kW heater produces 2000 J every second. Rating of electrical appliances (wattage) specifies their power consumption.

An electric heater converts nearly all electrical energy to heat → efficiency ≈ 100%. An incandescent bulb: ~5% of electrical energy becomes light, ~95% becomes heat → very inefficient. LEDs are far more efficient (>50% light output).

Power lost in transmission lines = I²R. For a given power P = VI: higher voltage → lower current → less I²R loss. This is why electricity is transmitted at high voltages (132 kV, 400 kV) and stepped down at substations using transformers.

Choose the fuse with the lowest rated current that is still above the appliance's normal operating current. A 3 A fuse for a lamp; 13 A for a heater. Using too high a fuse allows excessive current before the fuse blows — dangerous.

laws_and_formulas.name: Joule's Law (Heat)
laws_and_formulas.description: Heat produced in a resistor carrying current I through resistance R for time t.

laws_and_formulas.name: Electrical Energy
laws_and_formulas.description: Electrical energy consumed equals power multiplied by time.

key_points: H = I²Rt — Joule heating proportional to I², R, and t.
Double the current → four times the heat (H ∝ I²).
Nichrome: used in heaters (high R, high melting point, doesn't oxidise).
Tungsten: used in bulbs (very high melting point ~3422°C, glows at 2500°C).
Fuse: melts when I exceeds rated value → breaks circuit. Connected in series in live wire.
MCB: electromagnetic trip; can be reset; replaces fuses in modern buildings.
Transmission lines: P_loss = I²R; use high V → low I → less loss.
Electric heater ≈ 100% efficient; incandescent bulb ≈ 5% efficient; LED ≈ 50%+ efficient.
Energy cost = power (kW) × time (h) = kWh; multiply by tariff rate to get cost.
Fuse rating: just above normal operating current of the appliance.

9|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Magn.overview: A current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it — the magnetic effect of current discovered by Oersted in 1820. This electromagnetic link between electricity and magnetism is the foundation of motors, generators, transformers, and electromagnets. The magnetic field pattern around a straight wire, a solenoid, and a coil can be predicted using the right-hand rule, and the force on a current in a field is described by Fleming's Left-Hand Rule.

key_concepts.definition: Hans Christian Oersted (1820) discovered that a current-carrying wire deflects a nearby compass needle, demonstrating that an electric current produces a magnetic field. This was the first evidence that electricity and magnetism are related phenomena.

key_concepts.term: Magnetic Field of a Straight Wire
key_concepts.definition: A straight current-carrying wire produces concentric circular magnetic field lines around it. Right-Hand Rule (thumb rule): point the right thumb in the direction of current; the curling fingers show the direction of the magnetic field circles.

A coil of wire wound in a helix. When current flows, the magnetic field inside is uniform and parallel to the axis — similar to a bar magnet. The magnetic field strength increases with: number of turns per unit length, current, and placing an iron core inside.

A solenoid with a soft iron core. The iron core is magnetised by the solenoid's field, greatly amplifying the total magnetic field. The magnetic effect stops when the current is switched off (soft iron does not retain magnetism). Used in cranes, MRI scanners, electric bells, doorbells.

key_concepts.term: Force on a Current in a Magnetic Field
key_concepts.definition: A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force (the motor effect). Fleming's Left-Hand Rule: hold left hand with thumb, index finger, and middle finger mutually perpendicular; index = field direction (B), middle = current direction (I), thumb = force/motion direction (F).

A device that converts electrical energy to mechanical (rotational) energy using the motor effect. A coil carrying current in a magnetic field experiences a torque. The commutator reverses current in the coil every half turn, maintaining the direction of rotation.

Of a solenoid: (1) current through the wire — more current, stronger field; (2) number of turns — more turns, stronger field; (3) iron core — soft iron dramatically amplifies the field; (4) compressing the coil (increasing turns per unit length).

A solenoid acts like a bar magnet: it has a north and south pole, attracts magnetic materials, and its field lines exit from the north pole. The end from which field lines emerge is the north pole (determined by right-hand rule for the solenoid).

key_points: Oersted (1820): current produces magnetic field — the first connection between electricity and magnetism.
Straight wire field: concentric circles; right-hand thumb rule gives direction.
Solenoid field: uniform inside, like a bar magnet; right-hand grip rule for poles.
Electromagnet = solenoid + soft iron core; amplifies field greatly.
Motor effect: force on current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.
Fleming's Left-Hand Rule: index = B (field), middle = I (current), thumb = F (force).
DC motor: converts electrical → mechanical energy; commutator reverses current.
Stronger electromagnet: more turns, more current, softer iron core.
Soft iron: easily magnetised and demagnetised (used in electromagnets, transformer cores).
Hard steel: retains magnetism (used in permanent magnets).

9|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Hous.overview: Household electricity is alternating current (AC) supplied at 220–240 V, 50 Hz in most countries. The three-pin plug, live-neutral-earth wiring system, fuses, circuit breakers, and earthing are all safety features that protect people and appliances. Understanding the wiring in a house — ring main circuits, lighting circuits, and the distribution board — is essential practical knowledge and directly relevant to electrical safety.

key_concepts.definition: Direct current (DC): current flows in one direction (batteries). Alternating current (AC): current periodically reverses direction. Household AC: 50 Hz means 50 complete cycles per second; 220 V is the RMS (root mean square) voltage, equivalent to 220 V DC in terms of heating effect.

Live (brown/red): carries current at high voltage. Neutral (blue/black): return path, at low voltage. Earth (green-yellow/green): safety conductor connected to Earth. Fuse is fitted in the live wire. Earth is connected to the metal casing of appliances.

The earth wire connects the metal case of an appliance to the ground. If a live wire touches the case, a large current flows through the low-resistance earth wire, blowing the fuse and cutting off the power before the user can receive a shock.

Fuse (in the plug): melts when current exceeds rated value, protecting the appliance and cable. Main circuit breaker (in the distribution box): automatically trips for the whole household circuit. RCDs (Residual Current Devices) trip within 30 ms when there is a small earth leakage current — very sensitive.

The most common wiring arrangement for power sockets. The live wire forms a ring starting and ending at the fuse box. Each socket is connected to two points on the ring, effectively connecting it in parallel with the ring — providing lower resistance and allowing high currents.

Lights are connected in parallel from a radial (spur) circuit, not a ring. Each light can be switched independently. Parallel connection means failure of one bulb doesn't affect others, and each bulb receives the full supply voltage.

Appliances that are double insulated (marked with a square-within-square symbol) have two layers of insulating material between live parts and the outer casing. No earth wire is needed as the user cannot touch any live metal part. Example: hairdryers, power drills.

Never overload a socket (too many devices → excess current → overheating). Never touch live wires or wet appliances. Fuses must be correctly rated. Earth wire must be connected. Turn off at the mains before repairs. Water and electricity are extremely dangerous together.

laws_and_formulas.name: Household Energy Cost
laws_and_formulas.description: Calculate electricity cost from power rating, usage time, and unit price.

key_points: AC household supply: 220–240 V, 50 Hz in most countries.
Three wires: live (brown) = high V; neutral (blue) = return; earth (green-yellow) = safety.
Fuse in the live wire — melts to break circuit if current is too high.
Earth wire connects metal casing to ground — prevents electric shock.
RCD trips in < 30 ms on earth leakage — most sensitive safety device.
Ring main: sockets in parallel around a ring circuit.
Parallel wiring: each device gets full voltage; one failure doesn't affect others.
Double-insulated appliances: two insulation layers — no earth wire needed.
Never overload sockets; never use water near electricity.
1 unit of electricity = 1 kWh; cost = kWh × unit price.
